official of the Transport Owners Association, Deacon Bright Osasume, said in
the past, when customs officers were manning roadblocks at strategic
locations in the state, illegally acquired guns and other contraband goods
were not in mass circulation in the state.
"No transporter then will carry your goods without knowing the contents
and what the law is saying about those goods. Since the customs stopped
mounting roadblocks in Edo State, every person is now free to move anything
from Lagos to Onitsha, Warri, Port Harcourt or even Lagos to Abuja and other
parts of the East and South-South. Edo has now turned to a dumping ground of
all those things, you know you must pass through Benin to all those places."
He blamed government for dismantling the customs checkpoints in the
state which he said has given room for the massive movement of contraband
and other prohibited goods into the state which he said is responsible for
the high rate of crime in the area.
When Weekly Trust visited the major business areas in Benin City, only
Uyi Technical Company and two others were seen selling new refrigerators
with about five new compressors said to be in stock for several months
without patronage as against the numerous stores that sell second hand
fridges and compressors in the country.
A store attendant where second hand fridges, compressors, televisions,
computers and other electronics are being sold, Mrs. Franca Ezeala, told
this reporter that they sell an average of ten fridges a week and that they
sell to even local and state governments offices as well as corporate
bodies, companies and individuals.
However, at number 98C Mission Road, where new fridges are sold, Mr. Ken
Omono, the store attendant told this reporter that the last time they sold
new fridges was before the April general elections when they supplied two to
the state government. He attributed the poor sales to the massive stock of
tokunbo fridges and the poor economic state of Nigerians.
An economists in a private firm, ALSFAT Finances, Mr. Sunday Dimowo said
the major mistake government did in it's bid to curtail the entry and
circulation of contraband goods was for allowing government agencies and
officials to patronize the contraband goods themselves. "Tell me right from
the president to the governors, senators to even councillors, ministers,
commissioners and chairmen of local government, who is not using the
imported guinea brocade? Who among them is using the brocade manufactured in
Zaria or Kano? Look at their wives, who among them is using locally made
fabrics? Go to their dinning table, what you see there are imported
biscuits. The government is now buying tokunbo vehicles as official
cars. Then why should the poor man be punished for doing the same?"
Mr. Dimowo warned that the government should put it's house in order by
cleansing itself before attacking poor traders in the market whom he said,
use their life savings in buying the goods that the customs arrest and
destroy or auction.
The National President of Motor Dealers Association of Nigeria (MODAN),
Elder Benjamin Ughumu, said the issue of tokunbo vehicles should not be an
issue in this country at all, adding that "the customs have sealed up the
border against tokunbo cars and all the items they listed as contraband. You
cannot bring in a vehicle to this country now except through the seaport. If
there is anybody that brings in a vehicle illegally into Nigeria, that may
be the smugglers or customs officials. We are not smugglers."
He said it will amount to a violation of his members rights to free
trade, if the customs decided to raid their warehouses and impound any
vehicle that passed through customs into the country.
"There is no bush path or illegal route you can enter Nigeria with a
vehicle and not a motorcycle without meeting the customs. If they allow
smugglers to bring in vehicles illegally, they must not come and disturb
innocent dealers thereafter."
On the issue of motor dealers forging customs clearance papers, he said
his association does not support illegality. "I doubt if there is anything
like that happening. But if anybody is arrested for forgery and it is
established, I am sorry, MODAN may not have anything to do about that. The
person should clear himself." The Managing Director of a multi-national oil
palm producing company in Edo State, Presco Oil, and member of the
Presidential Committee on Oil Palm Development, Mr. Phil Vandebeeck, told
newsmen that the N50 billion the federal government intend to release was
for enhancement of local production of vegetable oil.
He pointed out that the oil palm producing companies in the country have
the capacity to produce enough oil for both local consumption and export. He
said Presco, which produces 50 tonnes of oil per day, is now increasing its
production to 70 tonnes per day.
He said there is no need for the importation of vegetable oil from any
country.
When contacted for comments, the Customs Area Comptroller of the state,
Dr. Hussaini Suleiman Kangiwa, said the issue of manning the country's
border by security operatives is very difficult due to the porous nature of
the border and the large size of the country.
"When people travel to border towns like Banki/Amchide in Borno State
where the two towns of Banki in Nigeria and Amchide in Cameroun have merged
together, they will understand what we are saying. There are hotels, houses
and shops built along the borderline. You fine such houses or hotels with
two doors, one in Nigeria and the other in Cameroun. When the Nigeria
authorities say no movement or business after 12 midnight, the hotels close
the gates leading to Nigeria and open the Cameroun gates and business
continues and vice-versa. How do you man such places when the people are one
and they eat from the same pot?"
He said the customs doesn't enact laws but they only enforce laws
relevant to their duties, adding, "every item listed as contraband, has a
reason why its importation is banned. The gas in those used compressors is
harmful to human health. Used Cars of over eight years are very weak and
old. At the slightest accident, you find that they are completely damaged
and that affects the passengers in the vehicle. With all the cooking oil we
produce in this country and the surplus bottled water and biscuits, what is
the need of importing other ones. We should not allow Nigeria to be turned
into a dumping ground for all sub-standard and over used products. We should
be seen to be exporting all these things".
He listed the contraband goods as rethreaded used tyres, cars of more
than eight years, gaming machines, cement in bags, vegetable oil in bulks,
used refrigerators, used air conditioners, used compressors, fabrics and
textile materials.
He mentioned other goods as poultry products, cassava and cassava
products, tooth pick in any form, table drinking water, biscuits, spaghetti,
fruit juice, paints, sugar confectionaries, exercise books, envelopes, beer
and toilet rolls.
He also warned against the exportation of maize, timber, raw hides and
skins, scrap metals, unprocessed rubber latex and humps, Artifacts and
antiquities as well as wild animals like crocodile, elephants, lizards,
eagle, monkey, zebra and lions are all prohibited.
He said most of the local industries stopped production because the
markets for their local products, which he described as goods for the
Nigerian environment, have been taken over by "unnecessary imported goods
that are mostly substandard and not good for both our economy and the
environment." He advised used motor dealers to ensure that they buy and sell
only vehicles that passed through the approved entry ports, with authentic
import and customs clearance documents and advised that the vehicles must be
within the approved specification of not more than eight years old for
cars.
"The law is there and there is just no compromise. It is left for the
dealers or smugglers or whatever they call themselves, to operate within the
ambit of the laws or have themselves to blame. Nobody will harass anyone
that does his or her business as specified by the law. When you do anything
different, it is your own business and you are doing that at your own risk
and at your own cost."
For those dealing in contraband goods, he adviced them to keep away from
such businesses and find other things doing before the law catches up with
them. "We can only advise, warn and guide before one is arrested. When you
are arrested, you must be punished according to the law and that is all."
"Look, smuggling activity alone or the illegal importation of contraband is
enough to ruin the economy of a country no matter how rich it may be. These
smugglers use the much needed hard currency to go and bring those
things. They bring them into the country illegally without the government
gaining a kobo from it. When they bring those goods, they deprive the local
manufacturers of similar goods from selling theirs. These local
manufacturers who pay tax to government, employ Nigerians to work for them
and help in developing the economy are now forced to close business." He
said that government's decision to fish out these illegally smuggled
contraband goods even from the hinterland is in the interest of both the
economy and security of the nation. "The effect of this illegal smuggling of
contraband into the country may be partially responsible for the poor
economic situation in the country since a lot of factories have been forced
to close down. Nigerians should expose them and their warehouses to the
authorities."

Original_Search_Title: Abuja Commodity Exchange Begins Stakeholders Meetings
Headline: Nigeria: Abuja Commodity Exchange Begins Stakeholders Meetings
Body_Text:
Kaduna — The management of the Abuja Securities and Commodity Exchange, ASCE,
will soon visit relevant stakeholders in Kaduna, Ibadan and Akure in
pursuance of its programme of strategy interactive meetings across the
country.
A statement signed by the spokesman of the exchange, Chris Echikwu, said
the ASCE management team, which will be led by its managing director, Mallam
Yusuf Abdurrahim, will meet with the members of the National Cotton
Association of Nigeria in Kaduna and the Cocoa Association of Nigeria in
Akure.
"During the inter active sessions, the produce farmers and merchants
will be intimated with the salutary objectives of the federal government's
decision to establish a commodity exchange in Nigeria as well as the
benefits that will accrue to them by its operations," the statement
added.
The commodity producers will also be expected to highlight the problems
militating against their operations, including unfavourable government
policies as well as the way forward in arresting identified problems.
The ASCE team will also visit the Cocoa Research Institute of Nigeria
and the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA), both in
Ibadan, with a view to harnessing their inputs into solving the problems
facing the production of some agricultural commodities in the country. The
agro-produce to be assessed during the visit includes Cocoa, Tea, Coffee,
Cassava and Soya-beans, among others.
The next phase of the stakeholders meetings, which will commence next
year, will cover other commodities including Bitumen, Barytes, Kaolin and
Palm kernel, it said.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers of Ghana, 'Ayekoo'
Headline: Ghana: Farmers of Ghana, 'Ayekoo'
Body_Text:
AS THE country celebrates this year's Farmers Day, The Chronicle doffs
its hat to all the long-suffering farmers of Ghana in recognition of the
immense sacrifices they have made in feeding the nation as well as earning
us scarce foreign exchange.
We have them to thank for the recent stability in staple food prices
which has cushioned workers from the brunt of low wages and salaries and the
astronomical increases in tariffs for the utilities.
Indeed, had it not been for the abundance of food, many workers and
citizens would have been unable to eat even one square meal a day when
prices of fuel skyrocketed early this year.
Despite the numerous constraints they face: high farm labour costs,
expensive agricultural inputs, lifting of subsidies in the agricultural
sector and other limiting factors, farmers have continued to struggle on,
using the little available help they can get - and all this without a murmur
of complaint.
What The Chronicle is not happy about, however, is the way farmers are
taken for granted and the non-chalance that has attended their noble
efforts.
Take the cocoa sector for instance. When the NPP government, in its
wisdom, decided to embark on free spraying of insecticides in all
cocoa-producing areas, many citizens applauded them for a decision
well-taken. Here, at last, was a government that was apparently serious
about making the country regain its former status as the world's leading
cocoa producer.
And truly, we are gradually clawing back our lost world market share in
cocoa production. Projected figures for this season indicate that we will
harvest over 500,000 tonnes. With the political trouble in La Cote d'Ivoire,
it may even be possible for us to take the lead in cocoa production.
How then, one may ask, can the hard work of our farmers be brought to
nought because of bad planning by officials in the cocoa sector? The log-jam
of cocoa haulage trucks at the Tema harbour, and presumably at Takoradi as
well, unable to off-load bags of processed beans ready for export, does not
speak well of a nation desirous of regaining her lost image and sends the
wrong signals to our farmers.
Are these public servants saying that plans were not made to take care
of the projected increases in this year's harvest? If this much could not be
done, what about when other cash crops also see increased production?
The government has embarked on Presidential Special initiatives (PSI) to
increase production in cassava, cotton, salt, and others. Are these also to
founder on the lack of initiative and poor work ethics of urban dwellers
living comfortably on the sweat of our hard-pressed farmers?
Surely, farmers deserve better. If the government cannot subsidise
agricultural inputs (even while our so-called developmental partners in the
West continue to fund their farmers), then the least it could do is to
facilitate the work of farmers who toil in the blazing sun day in and day
out.
The Chronicle believes that giving yearly awards to the "Best Farmer" is
all well and good. However, it would be of far more benefit to the country
when every farmer's effort is recognised - not quantitatively but
qualitatively.
When each and every farmer realises that the "big people" in the cities
appreciate their worth and do so not only in words but in deeds, then they
will also continue to brave the odds to feed this nation.
Let our farmers realise that their work can give them happiness. Only
then will this country move forward.
"Ayekoo," farmers of Ghana! God bless you all.

Original_Search_Title: Sunflower Making Lives Brighter
Headline: Kenya: Sunflower Making Lives Brighter
Body_Text:
Nairobi — As a young boy growing up in Kotur village t in Western Province,
Patrick Emoto recalls, he and his peers had a lot of work to do, threshing
millet and sorghum after harvest-time. There were large plantations of
cassava, and potatoes and cassava as well, and cotton provided a dependable
source of income. The Teso, who inhabit the area, lived a fairly comfortable
life. Today, although millet, sorghum and cassava are still the grown in the
area, a lot has changed since Emoto's childhood.
Then, the cotton industry grew steadily weaker and eventually
collapsed. Unreliable rainfall and plagues that cleared livestock followed,
leading to severe malnutrition in the area. Then about two years ago, an
elderly mzungu (white man) entered the scene with flower seeds in one hand
and beans in the other," says Emoto.
"He explained that the beans were drought-resistant and more nutritious
than our local varieties. Besides, both the beans and the flowers were ready
sources of money, and he had a ready market. All we had to do was grow the
crops in large enough quantities.
Several months later, whole locations have taken up soya bean and
sunflower growing. The effects are remarkable: the people look a lot
healthier, and shopping centres that resembled ghost towns have stirred back
to life.
At the Emoto homestead behind Kotur market, we found children picking
seedsout of the compact sunflower heads. Inside his grass thatched house
stood sacks of soya beans.
"When you return, you will probably find that I have replaced this
grass-thatching on my roof with corrugated iron sheets, which I am planning
to buy with the receipts from my crop this season, " said Emoto with a lot
of hope.
"Thanks to Jim Cheatle and his Association for Better land Husbandry
(ABLH), I now dream of doing such a thing" says Emoto, who is in his early
40s.
To facilitate marketing, ABLH encourages farmers to organise themselves
into self-help groups or clusters known as farmers action associations
(FAA). Besides Tesoland, ABLH is active in Bungoma, Lugari, Kakamega and
Vihiga Districts, where farmers have seen their fortunes change through
intensive farming methods and collective marketing of their produce.
Forty-two- year-old Charles Muoshi, of Isiekuti Village in Vihiga
Districtfound relief in ABLH after wasting years on jobs that earned him a
pittance.
"They assessed the soil on my land soil and found it suitable for soya
and other beans. They recommended vegetables such as onions, pepper, egg
plants, spinach and leeks, among others, for the Indian market. I tried my
hand at it and have never looked back," he says.
To attract better prices, he was advised to practise organic
farming. Then to hone his skills, ABLH took him for a course at the Kenya
Institute of Organic Farming.
Today he is a leading member of the Isiekuti Organic Farming Youth Group
and makes an average of sh4,000 every month from the farm. Gone are the days
when he agonised over poorly yielding maize. "We use money from the sale of
the beans and vegetables to buy maize. Now I don't need to get a job," he
says.
When Eliazar Likhaya retired from the civil service after 40 years, he
returned tohis ancestral land close to the abandoned Rosterman Gold Mines
near Kakamega town.
"I planted maize for subsistence and kept a few cows for milk. But I had
no reliable source of income and the little that I had saved quickly fizzled
out. Life was not exciting - until ABLH declared our soil suitable for the
growing of soya beans.
"They came to us with a package that included supply of certified seed
on credit to be settled after harvest, along with a ready market for the
produce. I was among the first farmers to take up the offer," he says.
"Other farmers soon came forward and we formed Rosterman Farmers Action
Association. He has an acre under soya beans and is confident that the crop
will do well. "I made my debut last season with half an acre, and the
returns encouraged me to devote more land to the crop," he explains.
ABLH also initiated a food processing branch, Farmers Own company which,
unfortunately did not do well. that produced a variety of products ranging
from including snack bars, jam, honey and tonutria mchuzi mix- an
alternative to the popular Royco Mchuzi Mix. The Farmers Own Company churned
out produced healthy foods devoid of chemical additives, which made them
popular with consumers. In addition, the food processing company provided
farmers with an added value througha bonus derived fromdepending on the
number of cartons and sachets sold. This prompted the company to shift its
focus from production to to marketing for producer groups.
By so doing, says Cheatle, ABLH has managed to involve thousands of
peasants, drastically transforming their lives.
"Each producer's annual income level has risen from about Sh4,000 to at
least sh30,000," he says.
As a person who has experienced poverty, Cheatle can empathise with the
local people. "With my family going through a financial crisis, I left
school at 17 in search of a living and did anything that could give me an
income," he recalls.
After many years doing odd jobs, Cheatle joined a bank, where he says he
quickly developed a feel for numbers and honed his basic entrepreneurial
skills.
"Nine years later, with two small but successful businesses under my
belt and a little cash in the bank, I decided to further my education and
train as a teacher," he said.
He studied geography at Birmingham University and studying soil science
at the University of Aberdeen in Scotland.
The opportunity to work in Africa first came with an offer in 1970 to
lecture in Geography at the University of Malawi.
By 1980, Cheatle was head of Soil Survey Chemistry Laboratories in
Zambia.
"I worked on issues such as the utility of rock phosphate and soil
fertility maintenance in small-holder systems, learning the importance of
affordable and adoptable methods based based on organic recycling of manure
and compost.
He worked with the Kenya Agricultural Research Institute (KARI) on a
soils' research project before going into private consultancy, which
culminated in ABLH, which he helped found in 1994.

Original_Search_Title: Not Surprised More Ugandans Are Poor
Headline: Uganda: Not Surprised More Ugandans Are Poor
Body_Text:
Kampala — It is now official - the World Bank is admitting more Ugandans are
getting poorer as was reported in The East African issues of December 1 -
7.
This might be news to the bank, which has spent the last 15 years
trumpeting Uganda as 'Africa's success story' but not to the average Ugandan
whose living conditions hardly bear out the annual good news
statistics.
Years of experimenting with the IMF/World Bank's much criticised
policies (cut social spending, privatise and liberalise everything all the
while pumping in the loans) have yielded nothing.
This is hardly surprising because these policies have never succeeded in
lifting a single country out of poverty anywhere in the world. Countries
that have succeeded (mainly in Asia) have had to pick themselves up by their
own bootstraps.
The bank's Mr Suhashan Canagarajah told The East African last week that
there was need to understand why poverty was not falling yet the economy was
growing. Growing economy? Falling poverty?
Anybody who has been watching Uganda's economy since the Movement
government tossed aside its so-called "Ten Point Programme" and
whole-heartedly embraced the bank's policies can only shake his head in
bewilderment.
Despite the bold assurances that the economy has galloped full throttle
between the 1980s and mid 1990s during which time President Yoweri
Museveni's catchphrase was that Uganda's growth rate was second only to
China's, signs of stagnation were everywhere.
Kampala got dirtier and increasingly took on the tired look of a
ramshackled city every passing year. Sugar and salt, though plentiful, once
again became luxuries for many rural families.
The peasants in the countryside, experts of silent rebellion, quit all
pretence of growing the coffee and cotton that paid our import bill and the
salaries of civil servants to become petty merchants in local trading
centres - the exodus from the land had started.
Those who stayed on the land just grew their own food with a bit of
cassava and potatoes for the market to enable them buy necessities.
There is yet another reason why Canagarajah need not scratch his head to
understand why the ranks of the poor have swollen.
How could Ugandans have gotten richer when companies that employed
thousands were sold off for a song to the politically connected only for
their assets to be promptly stripped?
Remember the fate of Uganda Transport Company, Printpak, Foods and
Beverages, Uganda Hotels, Uganda Grain Millers, Uganda Co-operative
Transport Union etc.
The bank's supporters might point to the companies the nation has gotten
out of its liberalisation policy - MTN, Celtel and Tri-Star as
examples.
But slaughtering one's cow and buying another to replace it hardly
represents an increase in one's fortunes.
Yet others would argue, yes there are a couple or so of shiny new
buildings in Kampala and one might add there should be a lot more. After
all, 17 years is a long time.
The World Bank and Finance Ministry it seems have stubbornly refused to
acknowledge that much of the so-called economic growth that seems evident
from the increased number of second hand Japanese cars on Kampala roads and
the construction boom are the result of donor money that has flooded the
country in recent years to address the adverse effects of the bank's
policies, the incessant wars the government has indulged in and continues to
fight, and of course the Aids pandemic.
Talking about the wars, what the bank and the Ministry of Finance has
not bothered to do is calculate the actual cost of the war in northern
Uganda - now in its 17th year - to this economy in terms of lost lives and
output, direct expenses, impact on foreign investment and tourism. And one
must not forget the war in western Uganda and Congo.
In this mix, add the unprecedented and systematic corruption - a more
appropriate term perhaps should be state inspired looting - this nation has
witnessed.
(Uganda is perennially listed among the top ten most corrupt nations in
the world on Transparency International's corruption rating
index). Corruption and poverty are bedfellows - existence of one is a sign
of presence of the other.
Meaningful production, which is what it takes to lift any nation out of
poverty has never taken off during Museveni's rule, perhaps because there
has never been any original, systematic effort by government to revive
commercialised agriculture.
Whatever little has happened has been done by private business people
(for example Mukwano and simsim in Lango, and Aga Sekalala and vanilla in
Mukono), donors agencies like USaid and NGOs.
The Movement government and the World Bank have of course always been
quick to claim the accolades at the slightest sign of success.
Lately, the country is witnessing a disjointed- some might say
opportunistic- attempt to latch onto international poverty fighting
initiatives like the African Growth Opportunities Act (Agoa). Ugandans can
expect scanty salvation from these.
For example, Agoa as a serious poverty escape door for poor countries
has come in for considerable criticism from the experts - one of which is
the initiative's sustainability.
What needs to be done to make a serious dent in poverty in Uganda is not
yet another study looking into its causes.
But rather for Museveni's government to roll up its sleeves and come up
with an original strategy to deal with poverty and the will to execute it-
just like the Mahathirs of this world did done for their countries.
As The East African's Rasna Warah wrote this week, the World Bank like
any other bank at the end of the day is here primarily to make money for its
owners (the rich nations) rather than develop this impoverished
country.
The real sad news though is, if Uganda's growing poor cannot count on
the bank, they apparently cannot do so on this government either whose
energies at the moment seem to be devoted towards securing Museveni yet
another shot at the presidency.
Mr Musoke is a lecturer & communication consultant attached to
Makerere University's Mass Communication Department.

Original_Search_Title: Kufuor Targets $1,000 Per Capital Income in 10 Years
Headline: Ghana: Kufuor Targets $1,000 Per Capital Income in 10 Years
Body_Text:
Accra — The President, John Agyekum Kufuor has charged the Ghana Investor,
Advisory Council (GIAC) to work hard to bring up innovative ideas that would
eventually enable Ghana attain a per capital income of $1,000.
We need necessary domestic and foreign investment, which will enable us
achieve growth in the range of 8-8.5 % per annum, "to enable us attain that
per capital vision, the President told the Council members.
He gave the charge while addressing the fourth GIAC meeting at Akosombo
in the Eastern Region. While Foreign Direct Investments (FDI) has gone down
worldwide, investments into Ghana are going up slowly, but steadily the
President said.
The Ashanti/AngloGold merger has created the largest gold mining company
in the world and will bring $500 million into Ghana. Also Newmont, currently
the biggest gold mining company in the world is investing over $400 million
in three mines in Ghana.
Further, Goldfields Ghana Limited is investing over $160 million in
their Tarkwa and Daman mines.
That according to the President would raise Goldfield's current total
investment from $400 million to $560 million. The Ghana investment Promotion
Council (GIPC) has this year alone recorded 108 new investments, which are
altogether bringing in initial investment capital of $89.86 million as at
the end of the third quarter, the President disclosed.
That compares with 138 projects, which brought in a total of $65.3
million for the whole of 2002. Over the last two years government effort has
jumped cocoa output from 350,000 metric tones and expects it to reach
500,000 metric tones by the end of year.
That boost at expectation has been made possible by two factors,
President Kufuor told the Council.
These are the mass spraying of farms undertaken by government and the
increase in the producer price of cocoa, which currently stands at 67% of
the world market price. The President also stated the first export of
garments to the USA under the Africa Growth and Opportunities Act (AGOA)
adding that there has also been a trial export of cassava starch.
Both fall under the President's Special Initiative (PSI) set up by
government to promote job-creation, especially in the rural areas and also
increase export revenues.
President Kufuor also disclosed that government projected an overall
budget deficit of 2.7% of Gross Domestic Product but the deficit currently
stands at 0.6 of GDP as far and it is expected to go down further.

Original_Search_Title: Food Security 'Under Control'
Headline: Mozambique: Food Security 'Under Control'
Body_Text:
Maputo — The food security situation is "serious, but under control" in 40 of
Mozambique's 128 districts and "reasonable" in the rest of the country,
Deputy Agriculture Minister Joao Carrilho said on Wednesday.
Addressing the country's parliament, the Assembly of the Republic,
during a question and answer session, Carrilho said that those 40 districts
had been hit either by prolonged drought or by abnormal rains. A total of
230,000 hectares of crops had been lost.
The last survey, in August, found that 659,000 people were facing food
insecurity, and that a further 250,000 were at risk of joining them.
In the vulnerable areas, food was available, Carrilho said, either
through free distribution, through food-for-work schemes, or income
generating activities which provided people with the money to buy food.
Despite the natural disasters that hit parts of the country,
agricultural production had been recovering from the floods of 2000 and
2001. In the 2003 harvest, 1.8 million tonnes of cereals were produced, a
figure last attained in 1998. For the first time, cassava production
exceeded six million tonnes.
Surpluses built up, and Carrilho noted that "today Mozambique is
exporting seeds to Zimbabwe, whereas in the past Zimbabwe was a net supplier
of seeds to us".
Public Works Minister Roberto White told the deputies that the
government is certain to meet its five year plan target of providing safe
drinking water to 40 per cent of the rural population.
"With the conclusion of the building of the 1,153 water sources
programmed for this year, the rural water supply coverage rate will reach
39.3 per cent, a figure that guarantees we will reach the targets in the
five year plan", said White.
But he warned that it was no good just drilling new wells or boreholes,
which ceased to be operational after a time, and were not repaired.
"We have to correct defects to do with the poor management of water
sources by the communities, and the insufficient transfer of technical
capacity, in order to guarantee routine repair and maintenance", said
White.
"We are trying to strengthen the management bodies", he continued,
"namely the water and sanitation committees, and the maintenance
groups".
The Minister wanted to see greater involvement by community authorities
in water matters, and a sustainable network to market spare parts for pumps,
which would involve the district administrations, community and religious
bodies, and private companies.
As for urban water supply, the challenge was on a gigantic scale, since
the urban population is growing by over 10 per cent a year.
In seven cities, the management of water supply has been farmed out to
the private consortium "Aguas de Mocambique" (Waters of Mozambique). White
thought this strategy was working since the rate of coverage by the public
network had risen from 29 per cent of the population of those cities in 2000
to 36 per cent now.
"In general, we are beginning to see an improvement in the services
provided, expressed in an increase in the number of hours per day water is
pumped, and a substantial improvement in water quality".

Original_Search_Title: An Alternative Staple Crop That Can Also Kill
Headline: Southern Africa: An Alternative Staple Crop That Can Also Kill
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Countries of the Southern Africa Development Community are faced with a
dilemma. A number of them, faced with famine, are considering empowering
communities to adopt cassava as a staple crop due to its resilience to
adverse weather conditions. But, as AANA Correspondent, Hobbs Gama, reports,
experts have issued a reminder that the crop has poisonous substances, which
can only be minimised through a series of procedures that many communities
are not very familiar with.
Member countries of southern Africa are becoming wary on the issue of
famine and food insecurity, having learnt the hard way from acute droughts
and food shortages that hit the region in recent years.
Scientists and researchers have joined hands to identify alternative
food crops for diversification.
Cassava, which can be consumed in various forms - as a raw snack,
boiled, or processed into flour for baking of confectionaries and also taken
as hard porridge, has for a long time been promoted as an optional food,
particularly during such hard times. The crop is also drought
resistant.
For the several weeks, the Southern Africa Root Research Network
(SARRNET) and the Initiative for Development and Equity Agriculture (IDEAA),
have been carrying out programmes to utilise simple technology and
popularise cassava as a cash crop and an alternative to maize, which is
widely consumed as the staple food across the region.
The organisations are keen on encouraging communities to adopt good
methods of processing cassava, in order to limit a wide variety of side
effects associated with the root crop.
Indeed, experts have in the past exposed that cassava possesses
poisonous substances that present an enormous challenge to its promotion as
an alternative staple crop, particularly to communities that do not have
much exposure to it .
It is said that continuous consumption of cassava may have adverse
effects on people's health, that could result in death in extreme
cases.
Studies have shown that monotonous ingestion of some species of cassava
could lead to chronic health problems such as "nutritional neuropathies,
endemic goitre, and upper motor neuron disease".
Further, the studies indicate that raw or insufficiently processed
cassava may cause headaches, nausea, loss of appetite, vomiting and
collapse, among other symptoms.
According to Prof Emmanuel Maduagwu, a biochemical and environmental
toxicologist, who is the head of biochemistry at the College of Medicine of
the University of Malawi, cassava contains toxic elements, such as cyanide,
"one of the most lethal poisons ever known to man".
There are two main cyanide species of cassava - the sweet variety, said
to have low cyanide content, and the bitter variety, which contains high
levels of the substance. Interestingly, the bitter variety is usually the
most preferred by producers of the crop. "Most growers prefer the bitter
variety as it is high yielding and has higher amount of carbohydrates," says
Maduagwu.
Accordingly, he warns that eating raw cassava, particularly of this
variety, as many people do, particularly women, exposes the consumer to
great health risk.
"Cassava cyanide is minimised by processing the crop before consumption,
as such, eating raw cassava means eating cyanide," he says.
He explains that people who do not suffer immediate effects of eating
raw cassava are those who either have high nutritional levels or who do not
consume the food on continuous basis.
"An acute attack of cyanide can be avoided by taking high protein diets,
which detoxify the cyanide. Lack of protein foods makes the body vulnerable
to the toxin," he adds.
The experienced biochemists warns that a wide variation of cyanide has
been found even among the "so-called" sweet varieties. Cyanide in cassava
has cumulative effects that can reach higher levels as the crop
matures.
South African High Commissioner to Malawi, Nthutang Seleka expressed
fears that the issue of cassava cyanide posed serious dilemma to all
countries within the Southern Africa Development Community (SADC) region,
coming at a time when the root crop was being espoused as an optional staple
food, due to its characteristics of resilience, which makes it survive harsh
climatic conditions.
"Surely, we are going to be affected in this part of Africa, although in
the meantime, cassava has not reached a stage to cater as a substitute,"
said Seleka.
He observed that processing of cassava was a painstaking undertaking
that made people unwilling to adopt it as an alternative staple crop.
"The public would have to be educated on the importance of not following
shortcuts when processing it into an alternative food substance," he
advised.
Turning cassava into flour involves peeling the fresh tubers, soaking
them for at least three days, drying, and then grinding.
However, people have a tendency of soaking the crop for only a short
time, and this contributes very little to the reduction of cyanide.
Communities familiar with the crop have traditional methods of boiling
the crop for consumption, and have done so since time immemorial with little
or no reported cases of serious poisoning.
An official from the Ministry of Agriculture, Jonathan Mkumbira,
commended communities along the shores of Lake Malawi, who already take
cassava as a staple food and yet no side-effects have ever been registered
among them. "Because they are patient and process it well - there are no
health problems despite their growing the high yielding bitter type," said
Mkumbira.
As Malawi continues to figure out a way out of the dilemma, the World
Health Organisation (WHO) was a few weeks ago busy investigating 60 cases of
paralysis in Pemba islands near Zanzibar, which occurred in September as a
result of wrong cassava consumption.

Original_Search_Title: Shell to Work With USAID for Improved Development in Niger Delta
Headline: Nigeria: Shell to Work With USAID for Improved Development in Niger Delta
Body_Text:
Asaba — THE management of SHELL Petroleum Development Company (SPDC) says it is
currently working out a long term strategy aimed at bringing lasting peace
to the people of Niger Delta. New Chairman/ Managing Director of the company
Mr. Chris Finlayson stated this in Asaba weekend when he led a team of
management staff of the company on courtesy visit to Governor James Ibori at
the Government House Asaba.
He however expressed regret over the continued hostility, which rival
groups now use heavy weapons to fight.
According to him, "the greatest shock was to see the degree of heavy
weaponry which has now filled the Delta". The Shell boss said that "in the
short term, we are making some progress but in the long term, we are
refocusing our community development to making them to be a sustainable
community development via the investment we are making not only in the short
term but we know we had problems in the past with building things and they
are not working and then we get almost negative things.
"But we really want to make some of that investment and as you may have
seen, we have signed agreement with USAID and with Africa. To me, there is
need to bring in international aid agencies with their abilities to set up
country wide programmes and with them and local NGOs something good would
happen". But the agreement with USAID he said, has been centred on
commercialization of cassava farming"
The United States Agency for International Development (USAID) and Shell
are to partner in a 20 million dollars (about N2.8 billion) development
programme in Nigeria. A statement from Abuja said the agreement was
announced in Washington recently.

Original_Search_Title: Africa Gears Up for Green Revolution
Headline: South Africa: Africa Gears Up for Green Revolution
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — SPECIALISTS in African agriculture begin a three-day meeting outside
Pretoria today to try to draw lessons from the continent's food production
successes and usher in a green revolution.
African ministers of agriculture will consider the findings of the
conference as part of the New Partnership for Africa's Development (Nepad)
plan for the sector. This is part of a wider effort to compensate for the
decline in foreign aid to African agriculture, which has fallen 40% since
1980.
The organisers the Nepad secretariat, two German development agencies,
and the Washingtonbased International Food Policy Research Institute, said
yesterday that the successes had not been sufficient to prevent a fall in
food production over the past 40 years, but there was still a great deal
that could be learned.
Success, for the purpose of the conference, is being defined as a
significant and durable change in agriculture which boosts incomes and
reduces poverty and/or improves the environment.
The cases that will be examined are those of cassava in west Africa,
dairy production in east Africa, horticultural exports from Kenya and Côte
d'Ivoire, maize in east and southern Africa, cotton in west Africa, and two
cases of managing natural resources in a sustainable manner.
Cassava was introduced by Portuguese traders and with its rapid spread
has become a staple in much of west Africa. Milk production has become the
fastest-growing income source for small farmers in Kenya.
The conference will also look at the roles of new technologies such as
that of the new breed of rice, specially created for dry African
conditions. Nerica rice combines the hardiness of African rice in its
ability to resist drought and certain diseases with the high yields of the
Asian varieties.
However, the conference will also stress that any green revolution will
require improved education, social services, infrastructure, and more
efficient markets.

Original_Search_Title: Ghana's Problem is Brain-Wastage - Prof.
Headline: Ghana's Problem is Brain-Wastage - Prof.
Body_Text:
Konongo — Rev. Professor Sarfo Kantanka of the Faculty of Agriculture of the Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology (KNUST) has said that the problem of Ghana is not brain drain but brain wastage.
According to him, "we don't have a problem with brain drain. The problem facing the country is brain wastage".
Rev. Prof. Kantanka made the observation at a mini-Durbar held at Konongo-Odumasi on Monday as part of activities marking the Golden Jubilee celebration of Konongo-Odumasi Secondary School (Great KOSS).
Speaking on the theme: "Rural Poverty and Quality Education, which way forward," he identified lack of skills as avenues for employment which are problems of government.
He explained that, the problem of brain-wastage is more of attitude rather that poverty". He lamented the apathy of some rural and urban dwellers in committing themselves to the education of their children, even to the extent of refusing to contribute to the welfare of schools and teachers.
Rev. Prof. Kantanka said the farm produce from rural areas is the same used by urban dwellers that pay for them. He noted that with commitment, a rural dweller could make a daily contribution of ¢2000 for his/her ward of Primary Five.
The daily saving could yield ¢3.5million by the time the child or ward was ready for Secondary School education, the old boy of Great KOSS suggested.
With a plea, he said, "We have a task and a responsibility to our children to prevent the brain-wastage".
To the university don, who is also a Minister of the Methodist Church and Presiding Member of the Asante Akim South District Assembly, "nations are not built through remittances the money is instead used to build and train for development".
He appealed to the chiefs and people of the two Districts to start caring for and educating their own children, because a country's major resource is its people.
Rev.Prof. Kantanka, who is credited for research into the development of potatoes and cassava varieties, disclosed that a headmaster had to beg his (Prof's) father when he obtained Distinction before he was sent to Secondary School.
The academician cum Man of God said, to prevent a JSS Leaver from Krofa (where the Professor hails from) with an aggregate of 19 from" brain-wasting", he sponsored his enrolment into Secondary School where he is doing very well.
The Board chairman of Great KOSS, Nana Owusu Prah explained to the students that the school is "a factory for producing human beings . their quality will speak for itself". He said past students did not have the facilities they were enjoying today and exhorted them to take their studies seriously.
Mr. George Kwame Frimpong, District Chief Executive for Asante Akim North, said it was ironical that we tend to keep our cemeteries clean while our cities and towns are replete with filth and mosquitoes which are carriers of disease and death.
Frimpong, who was honoured by the chiefs and people of Domeabra on Sunday with the title of Osabarima conferred on him, advised the students to practice total abstinence since that was the only cure for AIDS.
The mini-Durbar, attended by the school's headmaster, Mr. K. Ameyaw Yamoah, was graced by Dr.Lord Fenteng, DCE for Asante Akim South and some chiefs including including the chief of Konongo, Nana Batafo Acheampong Nti II and a representative of Nana Kofi Akoi, chief of Odumasi.
President J. A. Kufuor is expected to address the school on Saturday, November 29 to climax activities marking the 50th anniversary of the founding of the school.

Original_Search_Title: Food Security in Bugesera Worrying, Fews Net Warns
Headline: Rwanda: Food Security in Bugesera Worrying, Fews Net Warns
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Food security in Bugesera, central southern Rwanda, is worrying as households there are depleting cassava reserves and many have not planted new cassava because of lack of planting material and insufficient rainfall, the Famine Early Warning Systems Network (FEWS NET) has warned.
In its 18-22 November food security bulletin, FEWS NET reported that a long dry spell in the second half of November had caused the main crop of the season, beans, to wither and in some cases, to dry up.
"In [Bugesera's] seven and eight sectors of Nyamata and Gashora districts respectively, home to approximately 100,000 people, rains were too late and too insufficient to allow beans planting," FEWS NET said.
It said that rainfall in the region had been generally erratic, resulting in poor harvests in the last two seasons.
Cassava is the crop that contributes the most to food and incomes in Bugesera, FEWS NET reported. "The current food insecurity in area is therefore likely to persist and even to worsen for at least up to June 2004," it reported.
In the districts of Cyarubare, Nyarubuye, Rusumo and Mirenge of Kibungo Province, and in pockets in central and southern-eastern Umutara Province, food insecurity had also worsened due to unseasonably dry conditions, FEWS NET said.
It added that the government of Rwanda was trying to correct this problem, by providing emergency food supplies to the most affected areas and that the intervention bolstered the aid already provided through ongoing programmes such as the UN World Food Programme's school feeding, assistance to nutrition centres and Food for Work (FFW) activities.
FEWS Net said that although rains resumed in the last week of November, giving hope for at least some harvest of beans and cultivation of more sweet potatoes, an estimated 300,000 people in central and eastern parts of the country could need food and non-food aid in the December 2003-June 2004 period.
FEWS NET, UN agencies and NGOs active in the region are scheduled to conduct a joint crop assessment mission in January 2004 on the food security situation.
FEWS NET is a USAID-funded activity that collaborates with international, national, and regional partners to provide early warning and vulnerability information on emerging or evolving food security issues. For further information, go to http://www.fews.net.

Original_Search_Title: Annual Inflation Drops to 19%
Headline: Zambia: Annual Inflation Drops to 19%
Body_Text:
Lusaka — THE annual rate of inflation has fallen to 19.1 per cent for November
from the 21.1 per cent level recorded in October, the Central Statistical
Office (CSO) has announced.
Releasing the November Consumer Price Index (CPI) yesterday, CSO
director Dr. Buleti Nsemukila said the decline in the annual rate of
inflation was largely due to the decrease in the food index.
Dr. Nsemukila said the food index, which showed increases in September
and October, had recorded a decrease in November.
The monthly inflation rate was recorded at 2 per cent as at November
2003, representing an increase of 0.2 of a percentage point on the October
rate of 1.8 per cent.
Monthly inflation rates for the metropolitan low, high income and
non-metropolitan groups were recorded at 2.1, 2.2 and 1.8 per cent
respectively.
For the CPI, the price decreases observed for mealie meal and cereals in
the month of October have continued in the month of November.
The monthly food index was recorded at 1.9 per cent in November 2003,
0.5 of a percentage lower than 2.4 per cent recorded for October 2003.
Annual food inflation stood at 16.6 per cent as at November 2003,
decreasing by 4.4 percentage points on the October rate of 21 per cent.
The food index was influenced by the continued decline in the prices of
mealie meal, maize grain and other cereals as well as some vegetables.
Price increases were observed in meat, fish, kapenta, oils and fats,
milk and milk products and other processed food commodities.
However, these price increases were not high enough to influence the
rate of inflation in the positive direction.
There were price decreases in the cost of mealie meal, maize, rice,
flour, bread, samp, millet and sorghum, cassava meal, some vegetables and
fruits.
The non-food index recorded a monthly increase of 2.2 per cent as at
November, a percentage point higher than the rate observed in October.
The annual non-food index increased by 22.1 per cent in November, 0.8 of
a percentage point higher than the October increase.
Moderate price increases were observed in some non-food sectors.
Some non-food sectors that contributed to the slight increase in the
non-food index are increases in furniture, medical care, telecommunications
and personal care.
Price decreases were observed in the cost of air transport, vehicles and
hotel accommodation.
The drop in the cost of these services is attributed to the current
lower foreign exchange rate prevailing on the foreign exchange market
compared to that recorded in October.
Meanwhile, the average price of a 25 kg bag of roller meal decreased by
7.6 per cent, from K25,367 in October to K23,449 in November 2003, while
breakfast meal decreased by 1.1 per cent, from K31,130 in October to K30,793
in November 2003.
Maize grain measured in a 20-litre tin also registered a decrease, from
K11,993 in October 2003 to K11,537 in November, reflecting a decrease of 3.8
per cent.
The CPI covers three areas categorized as metropolitan low, metropolitan
high and non-metropolitan groups.
The metropolitan low income group covers low income households in the
urban areas while the metropolitan high income group covers high income
households in the urban areas. The non-metropolitan group covers households
in rural areas

Original_Search_Title: Douala's Breadbaskets
Headline: Cameroon: Douala's Breadbaskets
Body_Text:
Grand Hangar, Rail and Bonassama markets play an important role in the daily live of Douala inhabitants.
Despite the natural demarcation (Wouri River) separating Bonaberi from other neighbourhoods in Douala, many factors still link them up. One of these is trade which brings the three main markets, Grandhangar, Rail, and Bonassama together. The markets play an important role in the daily live of the inhabitants. Apart from the sale of agricultural products which is predominant, these markets have three major characteristics. First, they are each situated along one or two long streets leading to the main quarters. Secondly, the railway cuts across and thirdly, part of the markets extend to the main roads. At the Grandhangar market, a former train station for trains on transit from Kumba to Douala during the booming days of the Cameroon Railway Corporation, the sales of vegetables, fresh fish, "Njanga" from the Wouri River and cocoyams popularly known as "mamy and ibo coco" is predominant.
As early as 5:30 a.m everyday, hundreds of women from Limbe, Buea, Mile 17, Loum, Pendja, etc, throng the road-side markets to supply farm produce not only to "bayam sallams" (women from other markets like Diedo, Bonamoussadi and the central market), but also to restaurant owners. According to one of the buyers, Mbuoe Fany, restaurant proprietor at the Diedo round about, vegetables and cocoyams sold at the Grandhangar markets are cheap and of good quality especially during the early hours of the day when freshly brought from the farms. An opinion shared by Mrs Gladys Kem, "achu" specialist, who believes that, there is a great difference in taste and price between footstuffs cultivated West of the Mungo and those from some parts of the Littoral Province cultivated using a lot of fertiliser. She adds that the reason behind the influx of women at the Grandhangar market to get the products themselves is because those who retail foodstuff in markets in Douala are not honest. For example, some of them mix cassava with corn before selling as pap, some mix other leaves with "eru" before selling in order to make a lot of profit.
This is however not the case at the railway market popularly known as "Marché de rail". This foodstuff market which began with the sale of second hand dresses and shoes is today indispensible for Bonaberi as well as Douala inhabitants. This market has its own speciality; the sale of spices especially for African dishes. People travel from far-off to get them. Some of the spices sold here are for the praparation of dishes like corn fufu and eru, achu, mbongo, nkwi, coki, etc.
At the banks of River Wouri is the Bonassama market. Though popularly known for the sale of "mbounga", the market also attracks many customers especially those in transit who come to purchase foodstuffs like, plantains, cocoyams, potatoes, cassava, fruits, cooking ingredients, fresh fish, second hand shoes and dresses, fishing and farming equipment.
Bonaberi is also endowed with industries involved in the fabrication of soap, plastic, yogurt, eggs, artificial hair, aluminium ports in addition to its hosting of one of the main slaughter houses in Douala.

Original_Search_Title: Donors Boost Mengo Agriculture
Headline: Uganda: Donors Boost Mengo Agriculture
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Buganda government through its ministry of agriculture will this
year get $1,181,524 (sh 2.4b) to promote a number of projects.
Presenting the kingdom's budget recently, the finance minister
Eng. J.B. Walusimbi said USAID will support the promotion of banana juice
production with $334,000 (about sh668m).
Through the UNDP, the Kabaka's government will get about sh100m for the
Bio Fuel project while SIDA will offer about sh178.4m for fish farming.
The Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) will support the kingdom
with sh400m to improve cassava production by supplying cassava resistant
breeds. The UNDP will in addition to banana juice production, offer about
sh1bn for planting and maintaining forests. The Buganda Cultural and
Development Foundation (BUCADEF) a department which promotes agriculture,
nutrition, sanitation and development in the kingdom, will get
sh1,183,636,818 from various donors to carry out its programmes.
Other projects to be developed include fish farming in Kyaggwe, Mukono
where three ponds will be rehabilitated at a cost of sh60m.

Original_Search_Title: Kapeka Farmers Reap Big From Salim Saleh's Land
Headline: Uganda: Kapeka Farmers Reap Big From Salim Saleh's Land
Body_Text:
Kampala — Farmers in Kapeeka subcounty in Nakaseke, Luweero, have made a fortune following a series of bumper harvests on free land which Lt Gen Salim Saleh lent them to do farming, two years ago.
Saleh lent out 150 acres of tilled farm land to UPDF veterans, LCs and youths. Each of the beneficiaries was allocated two acres near the UPDF heros' cemetery, near Kapeeka town.
The New Vision learnt recently that several of the beneficiaries have constructed houses, bought cars or started businesses using the money they got from crops harvested from Saleh's land.
The beneficiaries planted mostly cassava, maize and rice which did well on the newly opened fertile land. The LC3 chairman, Christopher Kiwanuka bought a car from the sale of his cassava which he planted in 2001. Other farmers have built new houses.

Original_Search_Title: 16 Rescued in Gulu
Headline: Uganda: 16 Rescued in Gulu
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Uganda Peoples Defence Forces (UPDF) on Thursday rescued 16
abductees and recovered looted goods from the LRA rebels after an assault on
a rebel hideout in Aswa River Valley, Palaro sub-county.
UPDF 4th Division spokesman Lt. Paddy Ankunda said the Lord's Resistance
Army (LRA) rebels fled in disarray towards Kitgum district.
The items recovered included dry meat, cassava chips and cooking
utensils.
Meanwhile, three LRA fighters, including LRA vice chairman Vincent
Otti's wife, surrendered to the UPDF in Kitgum district.
Area-based UPDF 503 brigade commander, Lt. Col. Samuel Kiwanuka, said
the trio were at the headquarters.
Gulu district officials said the district has been relatively peaceful
for months save for last week's LRA incursion into Aswa county.

Original_Search_Title: 2007: I Have No Presidential Ambition - Benue State Governor Akume
Headline: Nigeria: 2007: I Have No Presidential Ambition - Benue State Governor Akume
Body_Text:
Lagos — Benue State Governor, Mr George Akume, has said he is not nursing a
presidential ambition, pledging however, that he will support a friend of
Benue for the office in 2007.
He spoke at a thanksgiving service organised for him by the Nongo U
Kristu U Sudan Ken Tiv, NKST church, Asukunya in Tarka council area of the
state.
The governor called on Nigerians to support the survival of democracy
adding that no other form of government can bring more dividents than under
a democratic era.
He disclosed that the state government will establish three cassava
processing industries, one in each of the three Senatorial zones of the
state to process the product for export.
Governor Akume enjoined the people of the state to intensify the
production of cash crops such as beniseed, rice, and cassava, adding that
the export market for such crops was enormous.
According to him, the federal government has banned the importation of
rice, and other crops that can be produced in the country noting that the
people could cash in on the ban because according to him the state
government has also concluded plans to establish a tomatoe paste
company.
He enjoined the Tarka community to reciprocate the respect they have
received from the people of the state who had elevated their son, Senator J
S Tarka in the past, and have made him Governor in the current democratic
dispensation.
Governor Akume stated that this could be achieved by eschewing violence
and promoting the ideals of good neighbourliness, dialogue and peace.
He enjoined youth to prepare for a better future by conducting
themselves responsibly and shunning those he described as people with
sinister motives who recruit them for thuggery while their children are in
safe haven elsewhere.

Original_Search_Title: Congo: Military Chiefs Analyse Situation Along Border
Headline: Angola: Congo: Military Chiefs Analyse Situation Along Border
Body_Text:
Cabinda — Matters related to the current situation along the Angola, Congo
Brazzaville and the Democratic Republic of Congo's common border will be
analysed on Tuesday, in far northern Cabinda province, by the three
countries' military delegations.
To participate in the meeting, is in Cabinda city the vice Chief of the
Angolan Armed Forces, General Geraldo Sachipengo "Nunda", who heads the
Angolan team.
In oil-rich Cabinda province, the Angolan Armed Forces have been
carrying out for several months military operations against some guerilla of
FLEC, with a view to guarantee a political and military stability in this
region of Angola.
Cabinda province is bordered to the West with the Atlantic Ocean, North
with Congo Brazzaville and in East and South with the Democratic Republic of
Congo.
It has an area of 7.280 square kilometers and its population is
estimated at 170.000 inhabitants.
Oil, phosphate, uranium, wood, potassium and gold are its primary
natural resources.
The local population dedicates mostly in the cultivation of maize,
coffee, cocoa, cassava and in the production of palm oil.

Original_Search_Title: A Royal Father's Development Drive for His Kingdom
Headline: Nigeria: A Royal Father's Development Drive for His Kingdom
Body_Text:
Lagos — For His Royal Majesty, the Dein of Agbor Kingdom, Delta State,
Kiagborekuzi 1, everything must be done to ensure the development of every
facet of his kingdom. The task is enormous, but it must be done, whatever it
would take to do just that.
The 26-year-old royal father, who was the star guest at the opening of
the Belgian Week, at Eko Le Meridien Hotel, last week, had expressed concern
at the neglect his people had suffered over the years and the need to
reverse the trend.
The traditional ruler, who was resplendent and stole attention at the
occasion, also bore his mind on the problem of youths' unrest, saying that
it was a fallout of the neglect being suffered by the people.
Agbor, his main domain, he said, deserves to have good network of roads,
and otherl infrastructures, among others.
"It is rather unfortunate that Delta State has been in the news for the
wrong reasons due to the restiveness in the Warri axis of the oil rich
state. The state, however, is only 12 years old, so, we ask for patience and
understanding on the part of the public as the state goes through what might
be referred to as teething problems", he said, pointing out that inspite of
the restiveness, Delta State remains one of the best states in the country
for investment, naming the limitless areas, yearning for investment in the
state to include the agro and agro-allied sectors, marine, non-metallic
minerals, polymer, mining and mineral processing, wood, craft, packaging,
and textiles.
"The state, in addition to being the largest source of oil and gas in
the country, is blessed with many more natural resources and raw materials
that can make it a haven for investors. We have oil palm, kaolin, lignite,
silica, clay, cassava, ethanol, spices and tomato, the government and people
of Delta State would willingly provide land, necessary infrastructure and
conducive atmosphere for prospective investors. So, we invite them to come
and take advantage of these resources and hospitality of Delta State to
invest there", he said.
With the Belgium community in Nigeria and guests present now all ears,
listening to him, Kiagborekuzi launched into a brief history of his Kingdom,
now known as Agbor:
"Agbor Kingdom is one of the oldest Kingdoms in Nigeria, having had her
first King, Ogele, in 72BC. We presently sit on the throne of our third
dynasty, the Dein Dynasty, which started in 1270 AD as a hereditary
rulership. That is why it was possible for us to be crowned in 1979, when we
were barely two and half years old. This earned us a place in the Guinness
Book of Records as the youngest crowned monarch in the world at that time",
he said.
The second dynasty in Agbor, he said, was between 600-9000 AD, a period
in which the palace of the first dynasty, the Ogele dynasty, was moved.
He explained that Agbor was related by blood and marriage to Ile-Ife,
stating that history had it that it was in Oduduwa land that Agbor's first
King found himself on entering the present day Nigeria and indeed was given
his first wife from there to make the Queen of Agbor then a Yoruba
woman.
Agbor, he said, was also blood related to the Binis, who are
incidentally related to Oduduwa. He said the present dynasty and Kings of
Benin and Agbor were blood brothers, explaining that Agbor moved from the
present day Benin to the Niger River basin.
"This can clearly be seen on examination of the present rulers in the
geographical area, who got their independence from Europeans. The royal
families of these rulers are commonly referred to as Nmu-Dein, which means
children of Dein", he said, pointing out that in the earliest times, the Oba
of Benin and the Dein of Agbor (Obi of Agbor then), were the recognised and
most powerful kings in that axis of the country.
"Agbor has always been involved in the government of this country. At a
point, our grandfather, His Majesty, Obi Obike A. Gbenoba, while on the
throne, served as Minister along with the Ooni of Ife and Oba of Benin", he
further explained, saying that Agbor has two indigenous languages, the Oza
and Agbor, to make for similarities between the two Kingdoms.
Closely related to that, he said, is the entire Delta State, which he
described as the melting pot of ethnic and cultural diversity, making for a
very rich and exciting young state, noting that there were many similarities
between Nigeria and Belgium.
He said in Europe, Belgium was at the heart of activities as Nigeria was
to Africa.
He, however, said that Nigeria has a lot to learn from the Belgian
experience such as clear constitutional policies and the acceptance and
involvement of the monarchy in governance.
He said he was committeed to fostering better relations between Nigeria
and the outside world, and increased international trade to enhance
Nigeria's fortune.
He consequently called on President Olusegun Obasanjo to continue his
fight against corruption and entrench transparency in governance.
He called for urgent attention to be given to the plight of the youths
in the Niger-Delta, especially as it concerns unemployment, which he blamed
for idleness and the often recourse to militancy and attendant civil unrest
in the area. He also called for infrastructural development in the area of
road rehabilitation, provision of pipe borne water, and rural
electrification, among others to make life easier for all while advising the
youths to shun vices, and educate themselves, show respect to their parents,
and put their lives in God's hand.

Original_Search_Title: No Other Form of Govt Will Suffice - Akume
Headline: Nigeria: No Other Form of Govt Will Suffice - Akume
Body_Text:
Benue — GOVERNOR George Akume, of Benue State says he nurses Presidential
ambition, pledging however, that he will support a friend of Benue for the
office in 2007.
He spoke at a thanksgiving service organised for him by the Nongo U
Kristu U Sudan Ken Tiv, NKST church, Asukunya in Tarka council area of the
state.
The governor asked Nigerians to support the survival of democracy adding
that no other form of government can bring more dividends than a democratic
era. He disclosed that the state government will establish three cassava
processing industries, one in each of the three Senatorial zones of the
state to process the product for export.
Governor Akume enjoined the people of the state to intensify the
production of cash crops such as bean seed, rice, and cassava, adding that
the export market for such crops was enormous.
According to him, the federal government has banned the importation of
rice, and other crops that can be produced in the country noting that the
people could cash in on the ban assuring that the state government has also
concluded plans to establish a tomatoe paste company.
He enjoined the Tarka community to reciprocate the respect they have
received from the people of the state who had elevated their son, Senator J
S Tarka in the past, and have made him Governor in the current democratic
dispensation.
Governor Akume stated that they could do this by eschewing violence and
promoting the ideals of good neighbourliness, dialogue and peace.
He enjoined youths to prepare for a better future by conducting
themselves responsibly and shunning those he described as people with
sinister motives who recruit them for thuggery while their children are in
safe haven elsewhere.
The governor has also called for the dualization of the
Keffi-Akwanga-Lafia-Makurdi-Enugu road.
The Governor who made the call when the Senate Committee on works in the
North Central Zone, led by Senator Sa'aad Mohammed paid him a courtesy call
said the construction would ease flow of traffic in the areas affected.
He said the Federal government must do more on the roads in the State
especially the Katsina-Ala-Zaki-Biam-Buruku road, Makurdi-Yandev road,
Adoka-Ugbokpo-Oweto and the Gboko-Ameladu road.
Mr. Akume informed the committee of his personal contact with the
Presidency, over the Katsina-Ala-Zaki-Biam-Buruku road which was a link to
the food basket area of the State, and the mobilization of the contractor
for the Adoka-Ugbokpo-Oweto road.
He acknowledged the need for a maintenance agency for the federal roads,
and identified with the Senate Committee's decision to get first hand
information on the State of Federal roads.
Earlier, the Chairman Senate Sub-Committee on Works in the Zone, Senator
Mohammed said the committee was in the State to discuss with the Governor
and see the condition of federal roads, or those under repairs as well as
other requests of the State.
In his comment, the Senator representing Bayelsa-West Senatorial
district, Senator John Brambaifa, said they would ensure that their
assessments of Federal roads are implemented, saying the result of the visit
will soon be seen.

Original_Search_Title: Speaker: Women's Role in Pre-Colonial Africa Highly Esteemed
Headline: Africa: Speaker: Women's Role in Pre-Colonial Africa Highly Esteemed
Body_Text:
Yaounde — Speaking to some 200 African women meeting Nov. 17-22 in conjunction
with the All Africa Conference of Churches' 8th Assembly here, the
Rev. Mrs. Bridget Ben-Naimah of the Evangelical Presbyterian Church of Ghana
reminded her audience of the significant status of pre- colonial women.
"Their roles were highly esteemed," she said in her address, entitled
"Enhancing Women's Leadership Roles in Church and Society."
"Queen Mothers had specific roles that were highly respected which could
not be compared to any other institutional role in society.  Female army
commanders were able to lead their societies to war against their enemies,"
she said, citing the example of Yaa Asantewaa who led the Ashantis of Ghana
to war.
In African Traditional Religion, she spoke of the priestesses who were
highly revered for their spiritual powers and contributions towards "the
maintenance of sanity in the social lives of people.  An example is the
Fetish Priestess who was/is in charge of the 'Akonode' shrine found among
the Lateh People of Ghana."
She also referred to women leaders whose role was to ensure that girls
were brought up to continue traditional roles in housekeeping and rearing of
children Their input was significant, she said, when it came to giving
orientations to adolescent girls and initiating them into womanhood.
It was, she said, colonization and formal education coupled with
Christianity that established gender roles at the expense of sweeping
women's leadership talents under the carpet.  She also blamed the early
Christian missionaries for actively imposing their views on African cultures
which resulted in the denigration of the role of women.
Referring to major religions that see women in a secondary position in
relation to men, she added that women are commonly regarded as a potential
source of social and moral disorder.  As in the case of the Fall of
humanity, where Eve is identified as the cause of Adam's disobedience, women
are often regarded as a potential source of social and moral disorder.
"As one can see, early African Christianity, instead of enhancing
women's leadership, rather suppressed it," she said.
While the colonial era brought with it development systems meant to
ensure social stability, she said, this often resulted in the Western
mindset that only men were capable of engaging in the public sector.
This resulted in women being dependent on men, which was further
enforced by Western missionary influence, and led to bias toward wage
earners, inadequate educational qualification and lack of usual community
support.
"In the urban areas, women were deprived of the kin and community
support, both materially and psychologically, which, in the past, allowed
them to direct events in their lives," said Mrs. Ben-Naimah.
Other setbacks to women leadership advancement include early marriage,
despotic systems and patriarchy, she said.
Mrs. Ben-Naimah related the story of a man in the late 1970's who
convinced the management committee of a village communal farm in Tanzania to
turn over to him his estranged wife's annual earning to him even though he
himself had been living and working in another village with his second wife.
He wanted to control his polygamous household by having all his family needs
met and allowing her a small portion on which to exist.
She also cited a situation in southern Ivory Coast where it is said that
men control highly profitable export crops such as coffee and cocoa, while
women have established control over their own food crops such as yams,
cassava and vegetables grown for household consumption.
"Agricultural extension and commercialization of the men's export crops
is institutionalized and effective while little government attention is paid
to women's crops so that this sector produces far below its potential," she
said.
The wrong interpretation of scripture - particularly in St Paul's
letters, Mrs. Ben-Naimah said, has also played its part in preventing women
from taking part in both church and society, as they were often taken out of
context.
"Sometimes ignorance about the context within which Paul spoke has
resulted in women having a negative self image which does not promote the
enhancement of leadership in any way," she said.

Original_Search_Title: Delta to Shift Focus From Oil to Agric
Headline: Nigeria: Delta to Shift Focus From Oil to Agric
Body_Text:
Lagos — Governor James Ibori of Delta State, has said his government was
determined to discourage over reliance on oil and focus more on
agriculture.
He equally stated that in achieving this goal, deliberate efforts were
made by his government in the last four years towards boosting mass food
production, adding that the effort also gave birth to an integrated
agricultural project tagged "Songhai Delta".
Represented yesterday in Asaba by his Senior Special Assistant on Media
and Publicity, Mr. Abel Oshevire, the governor said "Songhai Delta which is
the first of such integrated farming project in the country, is intended to
be replicated in all local governments in the state with a view to taking
Deltans back to the land, as well as provide employment for the youth in
particular and the teeming rural population in general."
According to Oshevire, the state was also embarking on the production of
Cassava, Palm Oil, and rubber on massive scale and other agricultural
produce for which the state was a leader before the advent of oil, adding
that the state was also going into the production of shrimps for both local
consumption and export.
"The state government has come to realise that the discovery of oil has
become an albatross to the state and Deltans. Rather than bring development,
and improve the lives of the people, oil has only brought Deltans poverty,
under-development, agony and self-destruction," the senior special assistant
declared.
He stressed that it was only sensible therefore, for the state
government and the people to revert to their traditional means of
livelihood, which according to him was agriculture.

Original_Search_Title: A Long Way From Reducing Extreme Poverty
Headline: Ghana: A Long Way From Reducing Extreme Poverty
Body_Text:
Accra — Ghana's rural communities constitute 60 percent of the national
population. And out of this figure, about 70 percent earn their incomes from
agriculture-related activities.
Yet most rural communities lack basic infrastructure like portable
water, modern health facilities, roads, electricity and schools. If
agriculture flourishes, it automatically goes to improve the quality of life
for the majority of the rural population.
At the same time, 900 million people worldwide, about 75 percent of the
1.2 billion extremely poor live in the rural areas. Rural poverty must
therefore be given priority if the Million Development Goals (MDG)
particularly those related to poverty reduction are to be met.
In 2000 at the Millennium Summit, governments committed themselves to
reduce by half the proportion of people living in extreme poverty by 2015 in
their countries.
It is in the view of this that a two-day seminar organized by the World
Bank and the International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD) on the
theme "Fighting poverty and promoting growth and employment, the role of
agricultural and rural development" took place in Accra this week to find
workable solutions to alleviating poverty among the rural folks through
improvement in agriculture.
Yaw Osafo-Maafo, Minister of Finance and Economic Planning, said there
is enough paper work and consultancy activities on factors hindering growth
in agriculture, therefore stressing, time has come for the implementation of
these findings.
"We need to change our mindset - we must measure our success in
improving agriculture on the field of production. We need to spend our
resources as direct inputs in form of improved seeds, equipment and
subsidies to farmers and not over-emphasize overseas training trips or over
staffed Project Management Units at headquarters," he said.
What is lacking, the Minister said, is the vision to implement practical
measures to improve not only rural agriculture, but also the overall
development of these communities. "Effective arrangement for farming at the
micro level, SME level and large-scale continues to be serious bottleneck in
our development of agriculture", he added.
Osafo-Maafo said the Ghana Poverty Reduction Strategy (GPRS) has
outlined several measures, which has the potential to improve agriculture
and rural development. These are improved land administration, small-scale
irrigation schemes, increased budgetary allocation in support of agriculture
economy, improved road infrastructure, electricity, health and education
facilities amongst others.
