He conceded that the rapid increase in cocoa yield largely because of
the mass spraying exercise clearly shows that bold and practical initiatives
are what is needed to facilitate economic growth in the country.
Osafo-Maafo entreated the country's development partners to reduce the
money spent on consultancy work for feasibility studies and rather put these
monies in the hands of the poor farmers to increase production and alleviate
their plight.
"We must intervene directly in agriculture production and pay attention
to the farmers and the inputs the farmer requires to support production"
adding " there is the need to make concerted effort to provide enhanced
support to some agricultural products in the non-cocoa grass land and
savannah areas of this country in order to effectively improve the incomes
of the rural poor, he told the development partners."
Courage Quashigah Food and Agriculture Minister noted that increased
productivity could only be achieved through the adoption of improved
technologies for effective and efficient use of resources.
He expressed concern that most assistance gotten from the country's
donor and development partners are rather channeled into areas which do not
directly end up in higher agricultural productivity - to reduce rural
poverty.
Quashigah said "in modern times, agriculture is not just producing what
we are capable of producing and looking for someone to sell it to, but it is
more of understanding what people want and producing it for them," hence the
development of the Food and Agricultural Sector Development Policy
(FASDEP).
"Our policy objective therefore concentrates on a selected number of
agricultural products to ensure significance increase in their production
and ensure the processing of at least 80 percent of the raw materials
locally into products that will earn high returns in the domestic and
external markets', he said.
He said that the key areas are infrastructure development; enhancement
of the capacity of operators such as researchers, processors and producers
in the agricultural sector; and facilitate the delivery of improved
financial services. The rest are the development, dissemination and adoption
of appropriate technology and promotion of selected commodities and improved
access to markets.
The Agriculture Minister said the sector could experience a sustainable
growth of about six percent by 2007 if requisite resources to implement the
MOFA strategies are made available and on time.
He commended IFAD for supporting four projects currently in the sector,
saying that the Village Infrastructure Project (VIP) one of IFAD's supported
programs has helped in the fabrication of simple machines for processing
cassava and rice.
"The latest is the fabrication of windmills for pumping water to enhance
MOFA's programme of development of underground water for irrigation," he
added.
The President of IFAD, Lennart Bage said that in an attempt to help
improve the living standards of the rural poor through enhanced agriculture,
"the rural poor need to be listened to, since poverty cannot be eradicated
without the full participation of the poor themselves".
Outlining factors that must be present for any meaningful poverty
reduction to take place, he said, "the poor need security for land and
water, they need an effective access to markets both at the local and
international scene, access to finance - a place to save and to borrow; and
access to technology and research".
The IFAD boss conceded that achieving the Millennium Development Goals
(MDG) that is halving poverty levels in the country would not be easy given
the depth of the problem.
The Vice Chancellor of the University of Ghana, Legon, Kwadwo Asenso
Okyere on the topic the "Macro Environment and Overview of Agriculture's
Contributed to the Economy" said though there seems to be some stability on
the macro environment front, more efforts must be made to consolidate these
fundamentals to benefit agriculture and the rural people.
"Low inflation is the biggest [threat] to the poor who are mostly in
agriculture in the rural areas and so the objective of bringing inflation
down to single digits should be pursued vigorously, " he added.

Original_Search_Title: USAID, Shell to Execute N2.6bn Devt Projects
Headline: Nigeria: USAID, Shell to Execute N2.6bn Devt Projects
Body_Text:
Abuja — United States Agency for International Development, (USAID) and Shell
Petroleum Development Company of Nigeria, (SPDDC), have announced a
partnership worth N2.6 billion ($20million), for the execution of several
development projects in the country.
SPDDC will dedicate N1.95 billion over the next five years, while USAID
will contribute N650 million for three main projects that include a cassava
project, the prevention and treatment of Malaria and support for the shrimp
industry.
A statement from USAID quoted its administrator, Mr. Andrew Natsios and
Chairman, committee of managing directors of Royal Dutch/Shell Group of
Companies, Sir Philip Watts as having made the disclosure in Washington
D.C. United State of America.
The announcement follows the signing of a five-year $20 million
Memorandum of Understanding in September.
The cassava programme, which is the first project slated for execution
before the end of the year is estimated to cost N546 million. It will
provide greater income for cassava farmers in 11 states through technology
transfer, identification of commercial markets for cassava such as livestock
feed and use in the baking and textile industries.
According to the statement, 'the International Institute of Tropical
Agriculture,Ibadan, Oyo State will carry out the implementation of the
cassava project over a four-year period.'
The statement further quoted Sir Philip as stating that Shell already
spends about N7.8 billion in the Niger-Delta region on well-established
social investment programmes. He also disclosed that there are plans to
implement on a new N45 billion five-year programme of development and
capacity building throughout Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: $5bn Targeted From Cassava Export - Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: $5bn Targeted From Cassava Export - Obasanjo
Body_Text:
Abuja — Nigeria hopes to generate $5 billion in revenue annually, from the
export of cassava and related products, President Olusegun Obasanjo has
said.
Obasanjo, who spoke yesterday in Abuja at the start of the second
Agricultural Summit hosted by the Nigeria Economic Summit Group (NESG) said
the Federal Government was targeting between seven and 10 per cent sustained
growth rate annually in the agricultural sector in order to ensure long-term
food security and alleviation of poverty.
The president, who was represented at the occasion by the Minister for
Agriculture, Chief Bamidele Dada, said two delegations of the Federal
Government led by Chief Audu Ogbeh, the Presidential Adviser on Food
Security and Agriculture and the Minister of State of agriculture were in
Brazil, Columbia, the Netherlands and the Scandinavian countries
respectively to explore the market opportunities for the export of
cassava.
He said the visits proved that there were a lot of prospects to achieve
the target as the private sectors in the countries visited expressed
interest to buy cassava from Nigeria.
"Right now Nigeria is the largest producer of cassava tubers. We produce
about 35 - 40 million metric tones of cassava annually and we have
instructions that within the next three years we could increase the
production to 150 million metric tones of cassava, "Bamidele later told
THISDAY.
He said cassava had become an important product world wide especially in
developed countries where it is used for poultry feeds. "If we are able to
access the export market to produce cassava pillets, cassava chips of the
world market quality, we can start to earn part of this projected amount,"
Bamidele stated.
He said the visits to Denmark, Sweden, UK and Netherlands revealed that
there were tremendous potentials for the use of cassava for industrial
starch, livestock feeds, production of ethanol and some derivatives for
pharmaceuticals.
"What we need to do is to establish appropriate agro- industries to make
use of these cassava raw tubers which we are producing so that we can export
these products as import substitutes then we will be able to meet the needs
of other African countries as well as European and other developed
countries. So, within the next five to 10 years we will start realising
these targets," he emphasised.
Bamidele disclosed that during the tour, a number of equipment producers
expressed interest to visit Nigeria and partner with the country's
entrepreneurs to explore business opportunities in the agricultural sector,
especially in the processing of cassava.
Obasanjo said the blue print for agricultural development recently
approved by the Federal Executive Council (FEC) was intended to boost food
production and alleviate poverty. He added that the launching of the
National Agricultural Development Fund with an initial take-off fund of N10
billion was one of the measures put in place to increase food
production.
He said the sector will also benefit from 25 per cent of the Sugar
Development levy and one percent of tax accruing to the Federal Government
from the sale of petroleum products as well as the dedication of 16 per cent
of national capital budget to agriculture as part of the measures to ensure
the financing needs of the sector were met.
The president announced that Nigeria had entered into an agreement with
China to provide technicians to help the country's farmers. Already, the
first batch of the technicians had arrived the country and redeployed to the
states to help farmers in the field. He said the last batch of 500
technicians will arrive the country by March next year.
"Agriculture remains a potent tool with which the twin goals of poverty
reduction and food security can be achieved in the short term. The sector
therefore has been incorporated as a major sector in fulfilling the
requirements of the on -going NEPAD initiative," he told the summit
attendants drawn from financial institutions, donor agencies, farmers and
private investors in the sector.
Speaking on the theme of this year's summit "Competitive Agriculture:
The Pathway to Economic Transformation," Chairman of the NESG, Alhaji
Mohamed Hayatu-deen, said it was no longer a matter of debate that
agriculture and food security were basic factors for the economic
transformation of the country.
"We already know from our own history and those of developed economies
that the strength, capacity and sustainability of any economy is dependent
on the extent of agriculture and food security," the chairman stated. He
added that the cycle of economic instability in Nigeria was directly related
to what he called the continuous neglect of agriculture.

Original_Search_Title: FG Releases N310m to Combat Cassava Mosaic Virus
Headline: Nigeria: FG Releases N310m to Combat Cassava Mosaic Virus
Body_Text:
The federal government has released the sum of N310 million to combat
cassava virus in the Eastern part of the country.
Agriculture and Rural Development Minister, Mallam Adamu Bello,
disclosed this when he declared open the first national conference on
Nigerian cassava which took place in Abuja.
Represented by his Minister of State, Mr Bamidele Dada, the minister
also disclosed that resistant varieties to the mosaic virus are being
rapidly multiplied and distributed to all parts of the country.
The minister said that efforts were being intensified to increase the
production of cassava. According to him, cassava production is going on in
79 sites of the special programme for food security in the country while
processing into gari and flour is being undertaken in 49 sites through the
provision and installation of processing equipment.
Mallam Bello also explained that his ministry is actively involved in
the sensitisation of stakeholders for increased cassava production and
processing. Joint trade missions, he said, are already being undertaken with
members drawn from the public and private sectors with a view to attracting
potential investors in production and processing as well as sourcing market
for cassava products.
He assured genuine local and foreign investors of conducive business
environment and proposed incentives which include concessionary interest
rate on loans and advances from designated banks such as the Nigeria
Agricultural, Cooperative and Rural Development Bank; provision of planting
materials/machinery and equipment at subsidised rates of 25 per cent zero of
minimal import duty for importation on processing machinery/equipment;
adequate remuneration for products through guaranteed minimum prices;
adequate funding for research and development to enable high yields through
multiplication of new, tested and disease resistant varieties and
consideration for tax holiday for new investors in cassava production and
processing.
A statement from the chief press secretary to the minister, Mr Tope
Ajakaiye, said the conference which brought together all stakeholders in the
cassava production processing and marketing in Nigeria has the theme:
Boosting cassava production for generating growth and reducing poverty in
cassava production, processing and marketing, and was coordinated by Trend
Solution Consultancy Limited with the collaboration of the Federal Ministry
of Agriculture and Rural Development.

Original_Search_Title: Uganda's Camps: 'Children Will Curse Us'
Headline: Uganda's Camps: 'Children Will Curse Us'
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Dickson Otto's dream is to move out of the Pagak Internally Displaced
Persons (IDP) camp where he has lived for eight years. He wants to return
home to lead a normal life like his fellow Ugandans in the rest of the
country.
"If it is possible, we should be allowed to return home, because we have
stayed for too long in this camp," he says in his appeal to whoever cares to
listen.
Otto's dream is not different from that of majority of the approximately
13,000 IDPs in Pagak camp, located in Lamogi sub county, Kilak County, 24 km
northwest of Gulu town.
Otto, a native of Gulu, has lived in the camp since it was established
in 1996. He and his fellow villagers, who were successful farmers, now
depend on food aid. He remembers the fertile fields he used to cultivate
back home, where the yields gave them enough food for their families and a
host of other social and economic benefits.
"We have none of the things associated with a proper family setting like
privacy, liberty and a sense of ownership. Here, we even lack basic
necessities like food," he told The EastAfrican in Pagak recently.
The Catholic Archbishop of Gulu, John Baptist Odama, says: "Our concerns
have not changed, but the situation has got to change.
"People have even married in the camps and their children will curse us
for having failed to change their lives. Yet the situation is worsening
instead of improving."
Odama, who is the chairman of the Acholi Religious Leaders Peace
Initiative, attributes the failure to end this conflict to "lack of trust
among the warring parties."
Pagak is one of the original camps where displaced people were settled
in 1996. It also hosts IDPs from the smaller camps of Kaladima and Guruguru,
which were established by the government in 2000 and were relocated from
their original locations to Pagak in 2002 because of the deteriorating
security in their areas.
The camp has a population of 5,161 males and 5,882 females, making 2,743
households. Kaladima has 817 male and 887 female inmates, with 430
households. Guruguru has 2,511 male and 2,548 female IDPs making 993
households.
There are four primary schools in the camp and three of them belong to
IDPs relocated from Kaladima and Guruguru.
Due to lack of a health unit in the camp, patients have to travel to
Awere health unit, about 5km away, for treatment.
The accommodation facilities in the camps have been described by the
civil society organisations operating in the Acholi sub-region as "makeshift
shelters dotted all over the northern and eastern parts of the country."
The IDP "protected camps," with the assistance of non-governmental
organisations, offer some relief food to the displaced people but basic
social services remain inadequate leading to poor education and health
conditions. Lack of safe water and sanitation facilities worsens the
situation.
The "protected" life in the camps has also led to a total breakdown of
the traditional Acholi, economic, social and cultural structures.
According to the UN's World Food Programme (WFP), the only humanitarian
agency reaching all the camps in the two regions, life in the IDP camps is
appalling.
An average camp is approximately one square mile in area with an
estimated population of 15,000 people living in crammed small huts with
inadequate space and limited facilities like water, latrines, schools and
health facilities.
"The prolonged displacement in this type of living environment has
exposed many IDPs to increasing levels of vulnerability," the WFP notes in a
statement.
The plight of the people was highlighted in the WFP Emergency Food Needs
Assessment (EFNA) 2001 report.
The displacement has hindered the majority of IDPs from producing enough
food for their families, hence greatly affecting the food security situation
in the district and making them dependent on food aid, which is not
sustainable in the long run.
Since these camps receive reliable rainfall, the people are engaged in
small-scale agriculture growing rice, cassava, simsim, finger millet,
groundnuts and sweet potatoes.
The head of the Gulu WFP sub-office, Pedro Amolat, told The EastAfrican:
"People top up their livelihood through petty trade in charcoal, fire wood,
vegetables and casual labour. The camp market is usually deserted with few
items on sale."
NGOs and other donors have been scaling down their operations in the
district since June 2002, following deterioration of security. Those
remaining operate mainly within Gulu municipality.
According to the latest population census, Gulu has 472,496 inhabitants,
236,434 of them male and 243,062 female. The insurgency has resulted in the
displacement of approximately 416,254 people of the district population into
33 IDP camps since 1996.
Since June 2002, approximately 29,000 tonnes of food have been
distributed to the affected population.
WFP provides food assistance to over 1.2 million IDPs in northern and
eastern Uganda using armed military escorts. In the eastern region, there
are 305,899 displaced persons from Kumi, Soroti, Katakwi and
Kaberamaido. There are 928,215 from the districts of Gulu, Kitgum, Pader and
Lira in northern Uganda.
When the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) stepped up attacks on the camps in
June last year, WFP responded by increasing its rations from meeting 30 per
cent of the daily minimum nutritional requirements to 100 per cent.
In January this year, WFP assisted the Ministry of Health and the Gulu
District Department for Health Services to carry out a rapid nutritional
assessment in two IDP camps in Gulu, at Anaka and Pabbo. The results showed
a "serious situation" in terms of global acute malnutrition, with 31 per
cent for Anaka and 18 per cent for Pabbo.
WFP also started the school feeding programme (SFP), which is meant to
increase attendance in schools especially of disadvantaged pupils and
enhance the learning capacity of the children in the programme.
SFP is supported under the USAid-led Global School Feeding Initiative
(GSFI) providing one cooked meal (lunch) per day to 79,000 primary school
children in Gulu and Kitgum currently.
Before the emergency, approximately 70,000 school children in 145
primary schools were receiving WFP assistance through SFP, with
participating schools being mostly from the camps or close proximity to the
camps.
The insurgency disrupted the SFP as most schools were closed and risk of
looting and abductions at school escalated. WFP is now focusing on the
displaced camps in the municipality and their host schools.
"The rationale is that children from the displaced schools in the
municipality have come to town due to the insecurity and are hence uprooted
from their former setting", Amolat said. "Since they or their parents are
not assisted by WFP through GFD, the SFP intervention is strategically
encouraging the children to continue their education while displaced in
town."
SFP has since July resumed operations in the camps after an assessed
reduced risk in continuing with the programme. It is expected to benefit
120,000 primary school pupils in 172 primary schools and an estimated 10,000
pre-school children.
The head teacher of Pagak Primary School, John Fred Okema, has his own
reservations about food distribution in camps. He says the programme
interferes with his classes on the days when there is food distribution
every month. "No one cares for classes during the programme. Every pupil's
mind is on the food programme, so they abandon classes," he says.
Food needs of the displaced persons have continued to rise in the face
of intensified insurgency by the LRA since June 2002. "Although the
programming process is flexible to accommodate both relief and recovery
needs, the current unpredictable LRA insurgency remains a challenge to the
programme," cautions WFP.
"Donor response has been good. Food and cash pledges and confirmation
have been received," said Amolat. "WFP will therefore be able to meet its
food requirement to feed the desperately hungry and poor people in the camps
through to December this year."
WFP now encourages NGO communities to provide non-food items to
complement food resources to IDPs.
The northern conflict started in 1986 when former soldiers of the Uganda
National Liberation Army (UNLA) regrouped themselves and started a guerilla
movement called the Uganda People's Democratic Army (UPDA) against the NRM/A
government for alleged excesses committed when they captured northern
Uganda.
Several rebel groups emerged during the same period and following the
1988 peace accord between a UPDA faction and the government, the LRA
eventually became the main armed opposition group in the region.
An NGO called Gulu Civil Society says the government has used the
military option with intervals of concurrent peace initiatives as a strategy
to address the conflict without any predictable end in sight. It has
appealed to the international community to take a more active and positive
direct role, and "work towards expeditious resolution of the conflict
through meaningful dialogue.
"Human rights violations are common as a result of the nearly two
decades of the internecine conflict," says the civil society.
Access to camps remains a major concern for aid agencies. Regular
attacks, ambushes, abductions and killings by LRA rebels have resulted in
increased insecurity.
WFP has included Teso region among the world's hunger spots. It says
places with severe food shortage in the region are increasing requiring
particular attention.
The army has been unable to provide adequate protection to civilian and
humanitarian organisations. Humanitarian agencies have been unable to access
the majority of the IDP camps because of the rampant insecurity across the
sub-region.
Gulu Civil Society believes that a solution based on military
intervention is inappropriate. "This is evidenced by the numerous failed
attempts by the government to end the conflict through military
means. Instead, these efforts have been counter-productive as they have led
to increased suffering of civilians at the hands of rebels."
The NGO argues that whereas the international community is doing a good
job in funding relief operations in the north, additional efforts to bring
an end to the conflict through peaceful means are required. They want the
donor community to act as mediators.
Unicef estimates that 18,000 adults and about 10,000 children were
abducted between 1990 and 2001. Since the child-rescue military intervention
"Operation Iron Fist" in 2002, around 8,000 children and the same number of
adults have been abducted by the LRA.
Most of the adults were released but more than half of the children have
not been accounted for to date and are believed to either have died or still
be in captivity.

Original_Search_Title: Country Selected to Export Items to US Market
Headline: Ghana: Country Selected to Export Items to US Market
Body_Text:
Ghana has been selected as the only country in sub-Saharan Africa to
export Afro-centric (locally woven garments) items to the United States
market under the African Growth and Opportunity Act (AGOA).
Ghana attained this status after the sector Ministry had sent various
samples of Ghanaian folklore garments and textiles to the United States for
verification, last year.
The items included, batik, tie and dye, Kente, Joromi, Kaftan, smock,
table mats and other textile furnishings.
Dr Gordon Asare-Kyeremateng, AGOA Adviser, announced this in Tamale on
Friday when he addressed a one-day seminar to launch the American Chamber of
Commerce AGOA Help Desk.
He said by attaining this position, Ghanaian exportable items such as
garments and textiles were likely to increase the volume of items to the US
market.
About 60 representatives from the business community in the Northern
Region took part in the seminar, which had the theme: "Taking advantage of
the AGOA initiative".
The forum was to create the awareness and opportunities of AGOA to the
business communities in the Northern, Upper East and Upper West Regions for
them to get involved in the activities of the initiative.
Dr Asare-Kyeremateng called on the business community in the Northern
Region to take advantage of the opportunities around them to explore and be
part of AGOA and derive optimum benefit from the initiative. Mr Iddrisu
Adam, Tamale Metropolitan Chief Executive called on the participants to make
good use of the seminar and position themselves in the right perspective to
benefit from AGOA.
He said, for instance, that cashew, groundnut, sheanut, cottonseed, yam
and cassava, which were produced in the region, could be exported to the
US. Mr Adam said the Metropolitan Assembly was ready to support businessmen
in the region to promote their businesses.
The seminar covered areas such as "background information on AGOA",
product coverage and eligibility", performance under AGOA" and "export
planning.

Original_Search_Title: Improvement in Gender Relations
Headline: Ghana: Improvement in Gender Relations
Body_Text:
Accra — It is a cold wet Saturday morning at the CIBA market, Mallam junction in Accra West. Different types, shapes and colours of cars drive in and park in front of the market mostly driven by men.
From observation, it appears most of the men are either from the high class or at least the middle class in society.
While some of the men appear a bit uncomfortable in a market area, others concentrate on reading the weekend papers as they wait for their wives to do the week's shopping.
"I am very happy whenever my husband drives me to the market," says Kate Aboagye a regular shopper at the market and resident of McCarthy Hill.
"Throughout the week, we hardly talk since we go to work in different cars, but at the weekend he offers to drive me to the market and everywhere I decide to go so I can also have some rest," she says.
Aboagye says her husband,a veterinary doctor by profession is becoming more and more helpful.
Sometimes as I do the shopping he offers to buy fruits for the family close to where the cars are parked. "At first when I did not even have a car of my own, he was not bothered about how I managed to get foodstuffs and groceries home for the week.
He used to help all morning on Saturdays, wake up around 10 am and demand a sumptuous meal.
Marriage
Asked how Aboagye became a changed man, Aboagye says after a marriage seminar at church where a renowned speaker asked men to help their wives at home, since the Bible says women are rather supposed to be helpers and should not bear it all alone her husband changed.
Aboagye is not the only man whose wife is grateful to him for at least driving him to the market, and helping her fill the boot with tubers of yam, bunches of plantain, cassava, meat, fish etc for the family of six for the whole week.
Quartey, a designer who lives at Weija Estates carries his wife's basket and follows her to do the shopping.
My wife is an Ashanti woman who does not know much about the different types of fish from the sea, I make sure I go to the fish sellers with her to select my favourite and then help her with the rest of the shopping.
But why does Quartey carry his wife's shopping baskets- something some Ghanaian men will consider a taboo?
"I believe I am physically stronger than my wife, and as such I should carry the heavy load of cassava, yams and plantains, besides, you women these days say we should help you, so I am helping her."
According to Quartey, it is not an act of wickedness on the part of men to allow their wives to do additional house chores all alone. "Most of us men are actually ignorant of the workload of women, until I started escorting my wife to the market, I did not know how difficult it was to shop for the family," he said adding, "I thought it was a very easy task, but now following her from stall to stall amazes me, that beyond my wife's great cooking art is another personality I did not know of, her bargaining prowess is amazing and sometimes I can't believe I married such a gem."
Quartey says accompanying his wife to the market on Saturdays has brought them closer together and their marriage is now stronger than before.
Kwame Dodzi owns a large furniture shop. He prefers to come to the market alone and do the weekly shopping.
"My wife has just had a baby and our househelp has run away, since I cannot take care of the little girl for even 30 minutes, I would rather do the shopping," he said.
Dodzi said when he started visiting the market at first he was a bit embarrassed because he had never gone shopping at a market all his life.
"But here I saw a lot of rich elderly men accompanying their wives especially at the fresh fish sellers place, I then realised I am even too young to feel bad about the situation.
He said the hospitality of the traders mostly women has overwhelmed him.
As soon as they see me, they rush to get my basket asking "Dada wope dien?, literally meaning "Daddy what do you wish to buy ?"
I have realized helping my wife has brought me a lot of peace, because the issue who was to go shopping was a big one until I offered to do it.
My wife is so grateful and appreciative of my help and has promised to tell our little girl when she grows up all that I am doing," Dodzi says.
There are some women who drive to the market all alone to do the weekly shopping.
Houseboys and professional drivers also come along.
Monique Yakubu a banker says her husband still enjoys her Saturday morning naps and would not drive her to the market. " He does not care about the campaign for men to help their wives which I think most of the men you find here are trying to adhere to, but he is adamant."
Monique does not drive and has to depend on public transport back to Gbawe where she lives. There are no porters at the CIBA market and a shopper has to carry her own goods around especially if there is no car.
Partners
"I suffer a lot before I get the goods I buy home, but my partner does not care," she said adding, "I am not as fortunate as some of the other women you find here whose husbands have at least given them the comfort of riding in a car to the market, it saves you a lot of energy, you know," Monique said.
Speaking on a TV show popularly known as Love Web on TV3 last week, a renowned Marriage counsellor Opanin Kwadwo Kyere said churches and other religious bodies have a greater role to play in changing the thinking of men especially when it comes to helping their wives in the home as well as helping them attain higher heights in life.
He said even though times are changing, there are some men who still think, housekeeping is mainly for women and do nothing to help them.
This he said has created a lot of sexual problems in marriages.
"When the woman works from dawn to dusk and she is tired and the man had just rested the whole day, there are bound to be differences in their attitude towards sex.
The United Nations has among many laws passed a law called the Convention on the Elimination of all forms of Discrimination Against Women (CEDAW).
It is to ensure that the rights of all women are recognized and respected.
The law says "all human beings men and women alike, have rights that cannot be taken away from them, except where the law permits."
CEDAW also says all women should be treated with dignity and honour," adding, "all countries should ensure that men and women alike enjoy equal rights with regards to social, cultural and economic matters as well as political activities."
The 1992 Constitution of Ghana provides that all human beings are equal before the law and people should not be treated differently because they are male or female.
From the CIBA market it is clear that the campaign and public education to improve gender relations and the elimination of discrimination is progressing somewhat.
If women are to have access to higher education, participate in social activities among others, their workload must be reduced and this is what some men are doing to help their wives.
As a result of cultural norms, the changes may not be as rapid as activists may want, but it is refreshing no matter how small to observe that some progress is being made.

Original_Search_Title: 12 Kidnapped Oil Workers Released; Shell Aids Small Enterprises
Headline: Nigeria: 12 Kidnapped Oil Workers Released
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Shell plans N2.8bn lift for small enterprises
The 12 oil workers abducted last week by Ijaw militants have been
released. The workers who were employees of two contractors working for
Chevron Nigeria Limited were part of a convoy traveling with supplies from
Warri to Escravos, when they were kidnapped at gun point near Bennet
Island.
In a press statement signed by the General Manager, Public and
Government Affairs of Chevron Nigeria Limited, Mr. Sola Omole, the company
said quick and resolute intervention of the Delta State Government Security
Council "resulted in the release of the hostages without any loss of lives."
The kidnap saga was the latest in the safety worries for oil producing
companies operating in the Niger Delta. Last June, about 100 oil workers
were held hostage on drilling rigs for nearly two weeks, offshore Rivers
State.
Violence and attacks on workers as well as oil facilities in the Niger
Delta, have heightened recently leading to the loss of over 40 per cent of
Nigeria's oil production last July.
Chevron, Nigeria's third biggest oil producer, had shut in 140,000
barrels per day (bpd) of oil in the last four months following the closure
of its production facilities due to violence in the Warri area of the Niger
Delta.
Chevron said it would not re-open the shut facilities until the area is
certified safe for workers.
Last week, the Permanent Secretary in the Ministry of Petroleum
Resources, Ms Ama Pepple urged both Chevron and Shell not to abandon onshore
operation due to increasing restiveness in the Niger Delta.
Meanwhile, the Shell Petroleum Development Company of Nigeria Limited
(SPDC) will soon launch a $20 million sustainable development project in
Nigeria, aimed at building capacity in small and medium scale
enterprises.
SPDC said in a press statement issued at the weekend that the programme
will be executed in partnership with the United States Agency for
International Development (USAID).
Under the partnership agreement, Shell will contribute $15 million over
the next five years while the USAID will contribute $5 million. "The
partnership's programmes will aim to help build capacity and opportunity for
Nigerians in the strategic areas of agriculture, health, and small and
medium size enterprise," said Shell.
It added that the plan will focus on food security through cassava
cultivation support programme; the prevention of malaria; and supporting the
export shrimp industry.
The cassava project is expected to take off by the end of this year, and
to be implemented by the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture in
Ibadan, Oyo State, for farmers in 11 states across the country.
"The programme will improve technology transfer to address cassava
mosaic disease and to develop cassava processing," the statement said.
"It will also help identify further commercial markets within industry
for cassava, such as ethanol production, livestock feed and use in
baking. In addition to being a staple food, starch from cassava is already
used in industries including textile manufacturing," it added.
Shell is Nigeria's biggest oil producer, accounting for more than 40
percent of the country's output. The company like other multinational oil
producers, have been accused of doing very little to uplift the living
standard of people in the oil producing communities of the Niger Delta.
Shell Chairman, Sir Philip Watts however, said the company was already
spending about $60 million annually in the Niger Delta on its
well-established social investment programme.
"I am proud that Shell will now be able to extend this important work
with this partnership with USAID. I am confident that our programmes
together will make a significant contribution to socio-economic development
in Nigeria and in the Niger Delta in particular," said Watts.

Original_Search_Title: Shell Backs Health and Food Security Projects in Nigeria with Africare and USAID
Headline: Nigeria: Shell Backs Health and Food Security Projects in Nigeria with Africare and USAID
Body_Text:
Washington, DC — The Shell group of companies has pledged $18.5 million for health and
development projects in Nigeria. The money will be invested through two
"social partnerships" - an anti-malaria program with Africare, a private,
charitable organisation based in Washington, DC, and a three-pronged food
security and anti-malaria effort with the United States Agency for
International Development.
The aid agency will contribute $5 million through its Global Development
Alliance, which facilitates public/private partnerships. Africare will
provide $1.1 million for the anti-malaria effort.
The announcement was made by Shell chairman Sir Philip Watts during a
breakfast sponsored by the Council on Foreign Relations and the Center for
Strategic and International Studies. Shell is investing $2 billion annually
in Africa, a large proportion of which is in Nigeria, where its production
has recently risen to one million barrels per day - a landmark the company
says it expects to sustain.
The three initiatives for the Shell/USAID effort aim to revive
production of a Nigerian staple food by introducing mosaic-resistant
cassava; to reduce malaria, which kills an estimated 300,000 Nigerians a
year; and to build shrimp exports to create jobs and income for rural
communities. Watts said the initiatives, for which Shell is providing $15
million, aim to intervene at the level of basic human need. He said, for
example, that thirty million people in Nigeria grow or distribute
cassava.
Follow us on WhatsApp | LinkedIn for the latest headlines
"Investing in people is perhaps the single most important factor in
achieving long-term economic growth," said Andrew S. Natsios, USAID
administrator. He said his agency is providing about $65 million annually in
Nigeria for development assistance that supports democracy and governance,
agriculture, education, health and HIV/Aids.
Julius Coles, the Africare president, called malaria one of Africa's
most serious health problems, "in some cases a more important killer than
HIV/Aids." The three-year malaria control campaign, to which Shell is giving
$3.4 million alongside Africare's contribution, will focus on selected
communities in six Nigerian states in the oil-producing Niger Delta region
along the country's Atlantic coast. The rivers and mangrove forests of the
area, home to an estimated 10 million people, provide a fertile breeding
ground for the mosquitoes that carry the malaria parasite, he said.
"Prevention methods will be stressed and will include increased
availability of insecticide mosquito nets, early recognition of danger signs
followed by rapid treatment, and improved access to effective and affordable
anti-malaria drugs," Coles said.
Watts said that his corporate group, which has been strongly challenged
in Nigeria as well as South Africa by human rights and environmental
activists, has added two new commitments to its business principles since
the mid-1990s - "to contribute to sustainable development and to support
fundamental human rights." He said Shell is spending $60 million annually in
the Niger Delta on social programs and that Shell's managers have "learned
to start with a conversation that asks: 'What do you need in this area and
how can we help'?"
What is most important, he said, is "not whether you make mistakes but
whether you acknowledge it, whether you learn from it, whether you do
something about it and change and improve the way you do business."  In
addition, he said, "we learned that having a third party is useful" as a
neutral broker between the company and the community.
"Africa is truly a test case," Watts said, of whether international
business can be a "creator of wealth and promoter of progress." Africa-wide,
Shell operates in 38 countries, employees 10,000 people, and has $6 billion
in assets. Supporting education and training is particularly critical, Watts
said, on a continent where once-robust institutions of higher learning can
no longer equip libraries and scientific laboratories. Empowering women
through training, micro-credit and infrastructure that can help them develop
businesses is also seen as a central goal.
In response to the problem of HIV/Aids, Watts said companies in the
Shell group have become pro-active in responding to "this catastrophic
situation we have on our hands". The company is working with UNAIDS to
develop predictive "global Aids scenarios" as a powerful communication tool
for increasing awareness among policy makers and the public generally, he
said.
Watts, who also chairs the World Business Council for Sustainable
Development, said supporting sustainable African development is both "a
moral imperative for business" and a strategic necessity. Citing Shell's
initiatives to develop alternative sources of energy, he said petroleum and
related products will remain essential to Africa's quest for
prosperity. "I'm not a bit embarrassed about leading a fossil fuel company,"
he said. For the near future, energy will come largely from fossil fuels,
and "without energy, there is no development."
Watts, whose formal title is chairman of the Committee of Managing
Directors of the Royal Dutch/Shell Group of Companies, has been involved in
Africa personally since arriving in Sierra Leone as a teacher more than
three decades ago. When he became head of the Shell group, he took the
unusual step of making sub-Saharan Africa part of his office's
responsibility. "The head doesn't usually have a regional portfolio to look
after," Watts acknowledged, but he said that the decision signals both a
corporate and an individual priority.

Original_Search_Title: USAID, Shell in $20 Million Partnership for Development in Niger
Headline: Nigeria: USAID, Shell in $20 Million Partnership for Development in Niger
Body_Text:
THE United States Agency for International Development (USAID) and Shell
Petroleum Development Company of Nigeria Limited (SPDC) announced yesterday
the setting up of a joint $20 million sustainable development project in
Nigeria.
The agreement was announced in Washington DC by Andrew S. Natsios, USAID
Administrator, and Sir Philip Watts, Chairman of the Committee of Managing
Directors of the Royal Dutch/Shell Group of Companies.
The partnership is the largest so far under USAID's new business model,
the Global Development Alliance, which promotes public-private alliances to
implement sustainable development programmes around the world. By partnering
with the private sector, developing country governments and non-governmental
organisations, USAID is able to extend its reach and effectiveness in
rsponding to new global challenges.
Shell will contribute some $15 million over the next five years to the
partnership and USAID will contirbute $5 million. The partnership's
programmes will aim to help build capacity and opporutnity for Nigeirnas in
the strategic areas of agriculture, health, and small and medium size
enterpise.
USAID and Shell plan to focus their work on food security through a
cassava cultivation supprot programme, the prevention of malaria; and
supporting the export shrimp industry.
The cassava project is expected to be the first to commence, with
start-up by the end of 2003. Its aim is to provide greater income for
cassava farmers in 11 Nigeian states. The progrmame will improve technolgy
transfer to address cassava mosaic disease and to devleop cassava
processing. It will also help identify further commerical markets within
industry for cassava, such as ethanol produciton, livestock feed and use in
baking. In addition to being a staple food, starch from cassava is already
used in industries including textile manufacturing.
The cassava proejct will be implemented by the International Institue of
tropical Agricurlrue in Ibadna, an internaitonal agricultural research
centre which has successfully implemented USAID and other cassava
programmes. The institute is also a key partner with the government of
Nigeia in the implemention of the Nigerian Presidential Cassava
Initiative.
Sir Philip said yesterday: "Shell already spends about $60 million
annually in the Niger Delta on its well-establisehd social investment
programme. I am proud that Shell will now he able to extend this important
work with this partnerhsip with USAID. I am confident that our programmes
together will make a significant contribution to socio-economic developemnt
in Nigeria and in the Niger Delta in particular." Mr. Natsios said:
"Investing in people is perhaps the single most important factor in
achieving long-term economic growth. USAID is proud to partner Shell, who
have been working in Nigeria for over 60 years. USAID will continue to
support developemnt efforts and capacity building wherever opportunities
exist worldwide."

Original_Search_Title: Pain And Agony in IDP Camps
Headline: Uganda: Pain And Agony in IDP Camps
Body_Text:
Kampala — She went to look for food, but she never came back. She was abducted,
tortured and brutally murdered by her captors, the Lord's Resistance Army
(LRA) rebels.
Joyce Amongin 33, a mother of three, was killed in cold blood last week
while on her way to pick some cassava from her abandoned garden in Acowa
sub-county, Katakwi district.
Five-months pregnant, Amongin was stabbed several times in the head and
in the back with a bayonet. Her naked, decomposing body bore five stab
wounds in the head, four on the skull and one deep wound near the right ear
lobe.
The deceased's swollen neck bore bruises inflicted by a blunt
object. Amongin's ordeal began on the morning of October 31, 2003, when she
and her co-wife, Jessica Aguti, 37, sneaked out of the Internally Displaced
Peoples (IDPs) camp in Acowa to go and look for food.
Inspite of insecurity in the area, they braved the scorching sun and
travelled to their home in Asinge village, 16 kms away from the camp. It is
said that for three consecutive days, the two and their nine siblings had
not eaten food.
There was nothing to eat, and the situation had run out of control. They
had earlier asked their husband, Mr Vincent Ongole, 35, an Arrow Group
section commander, to seek permission so he could escort them to their
village.
But in light of 'standing orders' he could not be allowed to accompany
them. That sealed Amongin's fate.
She is one of the many case of people who have lost their lives at the
hands of marauding LRA rebels. They have abducted, killed, and displaced
thousands of people in the Teso sub-region.
Her co-wife, Aguti, who survived by a whisker after escaping, recounted
what had transpired in the bush.
"We were walking, when suddenly two rebels jumped into the road. Two
behind and two infront. 'Cungi cungi (stop stop)' they ordered us. We were
terrified," she said.
"They removed all our clothes and led us to the bush where we were made
to sit down.
They asked us whether we were married to soldiers. They also wanted to
know the number of soldiers in the camps. We said we were not wives of
soldiers."
Aguti recalls how the deceased was first hit with the nozzle of the gun
and she fell face down.
"You know she was pregnant, but she was hit so hard with the gun that
she fainted," she said as she struggled to hold back tears that were by now
welling up in her eyes.
Aguti managed to escape when an army chopper and the ground soldiers
engaged the rebels.
"When they saw the chopper, they told all of us to hide. That is when I
managed to escape.
My colleague couldn't. She was killed. She was killed in the cruellest
manner. Her death has been haunting me since and I don't know how I will get
out of it," Aguti said as she broke down in tears.
The night of June 15, 2003 will never be erased out of people's minds -
in Katakwi district.
It marked the LRA incursion into the region. One old man, Ezekiel Opiro,
from Obalanga sub-county, from where the rebels entered the district,
recalls how the information about the LRA attack filtered into the area but
was ignored by the residents.
"We got information about the impending attack, but people ignored
it. Nobody, including me could believe it. Two weeks later, they attacked
Obalanga," Opiro, in his late 60s said.
"They came as friends, but they turned out to be the worst enemies on
earth."
Speaking with bits of spittle escaping from the corner of his mouth, the
white haired father of nine, is one among thousands of people camped in
Acowa sub-county headquarters.
Opiro recalls how the rebels shared drinks and played football with the
locals of the area before they began abducting young children under the age
of 12.
Acowa IDP camp, is one of the biggest camps in Katakwi district with a
population of about 30,000 IDPs. Other camps are in Amuria trading centre,
Orungo and Morungatuny sub-counties respectively.
With poor sanitation and lack of drugs, residents have suffered
starvation and death, sparking off relentless pain and tears.
The LC III chairman Acowa sub-county, Mr Joseph Okwi, said between four
to five people, mostly children, die in the camp daily.
"Our biggest problems here are insecurity, lack of water, drugs and poor
sanitation. But the biggest problem is water," he said.
With barely one borehole within reach, most IDPs have to rely on
unprotected water sources, which include dams and tadpole-infested
streams.
Statistics from the camps indicate that 99 percent of the deaths are due
to diarrhoea. Since June this year, over 100 people have died in Acowa camp
alone.
Malaria, measles and pneumonia have also dealt a blow on the IDPs. Every
other day, IDP residents wake up to the sounds of wailing from bereaved
families.
In most cases the bodies are buried within the camps, while in other
cases, soldiers escort the bodies for burial in the nearby
villages. Carrying dead bodies to bury is the order of the day in the
camps.
Elizabeth Ariyo, 21, is one of the widows in Asamuk camp whose husband,
Mr Lawrence Epudu fell prey to the ruthless rebels.
Epudu, a former LC III chairman of Asamuk sub-county in Katakwi, was
shot at point blank range in the presence of his wife. Ms Ariyo, cuddling
her four months old baby, narrated how the rebels invaded their home in the
wee hours of the night.
"They stripped us naked. I was ordered to sit next to my husband's
body. He had died and was still bleeding," said Ariyo, who was nine months
pregnant.
She recalls that a female rebel, carrying a kid on her back, saved
her. "He pointed a knife at my stomach, but a woman soldier raised her hand
and pleaded with the rebel not to kill me. It was terrifying," Ariyo said,
her hands trembling with fear.
"Then they started kicking my husband's body, one by one, as they
left".
Life in the IDP camps is full of misery, pain and agony.
The IDPs are wallowing in abject poverty and disease. Unhappy faces are
the commonest sight - across the camps.
Mothers carrying malnourished children on their laps sit in small groups
contemplating their next move.
In one hut, a little boy wore a tattered vest that barely reached his
navel. His thin legs struck out of his bloated belly like spindles. He could
neither stand nor talk.
He just lay there on the dusty floor, his eyes rolling deep inside their
sockets. But life has to continue. His mother, Ms Grace Apolot said her son
has been bed ridden for over one-month.
The World Food Programme (WFP) is currently providing relief food to the
districts in the region. Three NGOs, Action Aid, Christian Children's Fund
and the Red Cross are distributing food in the districts of Katakwi, Soroti,
and Kaberamaido.
Action Aid, which covers Katakwi, has distributed tonnes of relief food
in the area for the last one month.
However, the relief effort is like a drop in the ocean in light of the
catastrophe. Last week, it supplied beans and maize to Acowa sub-county
camp.
But the food was not enough for the IDPs whose number is ever
increasing.
To compound the problem, insecurity hampers the distribution of relief
food. Action Aid volunteers say they sometimes have to spend between three
to four days in an area to distribute food.
Teso sub-region, which was steadily recovering from the traumatic
effects of the Uganda People's Army (UPA) insurgency in the late 1980s, has
again become a region where hope is in short supply.
As humanitarian aid trickles into the region, the future of more than
150,000 IDPs in Katakwi district lies in balance.

Original_Search_Title: Shell Announces Partnerships With Aid Agencies In Africa
Headline: Nigeria: Shell Announces Partnerships With Aid Agencies In Africa
Body_Text:
Washington, DC —  Shell and Africare form partnership to launch $4.5 million health
care programme in Nigeria
The Shell Petroleum Development Company of Nigeria Limited (Shell) and
Africare today announced the signing of a partnership agreement to establish
a $4.5 million health care programme in Nigeria.
Shell will contribute $3.4 million of the programme's costs over the
next three years, with Africare contributing $1.1 million.
Shell and Africare's programme will focus on reducing child and maternal
mortality from malaria and will cooperate with the Federal Government's Roll
Back Malaria programme.
Shell and Africare will concentrate on the six core Niger Delta
states. The partners will begin by distributing mosquito nets impregnated
with insecticides. The programme will progress to developing local capacity
to manufacture and impregnate nets in the Niger Delta, creating business
opportunities for small and medium scale enterprises.
Sir Philip Watts, Chairman of the Committee of Managing Directors of the
Royal Dutch/Shell Group of Companies, said today: "Malaria is a devastating
disease in Africa. Through this partnership, Shell and Africare will help to
end this scourge, and at the same time support the development of
sustainable business opportunities in the Niger Delta. Africare has
considerable healthcare expertise and shares Shell's long-standing
commitment to playing a key part in improving the socio-economic well-being
of the people of the Niger Delta, and Nigeria as a whole."
Dr. Julius Coles, President of Africare, said: "Addressing the health
needs of the people of the Niger Delta is fundamental to enabling
development of the area.  Africare is proud to partner with Shell in this
important work."
Chris Finlayson, Chairman and Managing Director of Shell Petroleum
Development Company of Nigeria Limited, said: "Health care has been an
important focus of Shell's social investment programmes in the Niger Delta
for decades. Joining hands with Africare will help us to expand the scope of
this work."
Dr. Chinwe Effiong, Africare Nigeria Country Representative, said
"Africare is pleased to be able to boost its development programmes in
Nigeria through this partnership with Shell.  Our programme will fit well
with the government's 'roll back malaria' campaign and contribute to
reducing childhood and maternal mortality."
USAID and Shell form partnership to launch $20 million Nigerian
Development Project
The United States Agency for International Development (USAID) and the
Shell Petroleum Development Company of Nigeria Limited (Shell) today
announced they are to be partners in a $20 million sustainable development
project in Nigeria.
The agreement was announced in Washington DC by Andrew S. Natsios, USAID
Administrator, and Sir Philip Watts, Chairman of the Committee of Managing
Directors of the Royal Dutch/Shell Group of Companies.
The partnership is the largest so far under USAID's new business model,
the Global Development Alliance, which promotes public-private alliances to
implement sustainable development programmes around the world. By partnering
with the private sector, developing country governments and non-governmental
organizations, USAID is able to extend its reach and effectiveness in
responding to new global challenges.
Shell will contribute some $15 million over the next five years to the
partnership and USAID will contribute $5 million. The partnership's
programmes will aim to help build capacity and opportunity for Nigerians in
the strategic areas of agriculture, health, and small and medium size
enterprise.
USAID and Shell plan to focus their work on food security through a
cassava cultivation support programme; the prevention of malaria; and
supporting the export shrimp industry.
The cassava project is expected to be the first to commence, with
start-up by the end of 2003. Its aim is to provide greater income for
cassava farmers in 11 Nigerian states. The programme will improve technology
transfer to address cassava mosaic disease and to develop cassava
processing. It will also help identify further commercial markets within
industry for cassava, such as ethanol production, livestock feed and use in
baking. In addition to being a staple food, starch from cassava is already
used in industries including textile manufacturing.
The cassava project will be implemented by the International Institute
of Tropical Agriculture in Ibadan, an international agricultural research
centre which has successfully implemented USAID and other cassava
programmes.  The institute is also a key partner with the Government of
Nigeria in the implementation of the Nigerian Presidential Cassava
Initiative.
Sir Philip said today: "Shell already spends about $60 million annually
in the Niger Delta on its well-established social investment programme. I am
proud that Shell will now be able to extend this important work with this
partnership with USAID. I am confident that our programmes together will
make a significant contribution to socio-economic development in Nigeria and
in the Niger Delta in particular."
Mr Natsios said: "Investing in people is perhaps the single most
important factor in achieving long-term economic growth. USAID is proud to
partner Shell, which has been working in Nigeria for over 60 years. USAID
will continue to support development efforts and capacity building wherever
opportunities exist worldwide."
USAID provides $65 million annually in development assistance to Nigeria
in the areas of democracy and governance, economics and agriculture,
education, health and HIV/AIDS.
The Shell Petroleum Development Company of Nigeria Limited was
incorporated in 1937 and is the oldest and largest producer of oil and gas
in Nigeria. Shell in Nigeria spends some $60 million each year on social
investment, assisting communities to develop agriculture and social
infrastructure including community health and educational facilities.

Original_Search_Title: Samfya: a Sleeping Giant Yet to Arise
Headline: Zambia: Samfya: a Sleeping Giant Yet to Arise
Body_Text:
THE most prominent feature as you reach Samfya is the expanse of water
which greets the visitor.
Built on the shores of Lake Bangweulu and spreading into the hinterland,
Samfya is a ruggedly beautiful place which has everything rolled in
one.
Much has already been said and written about this cinderella district of
Luapula Province, but also more that is new has taken place in the district
over the last decade that the district is worth another look.
Unlike many other rural districts, Samfya can boast good roads and
telecommunication infrastructure but what it has in abundance are natural
resources that make it one of Zambia's most promising future tourist
destinations. All that is lacking, the first-time visitor feels, is a good
marketing promotion and a bit more economic investment.
It is one of the areas in Luapula Province which, apart from tourism,
has great potential for industrial development though so far no serious
investor has taken advantage of that.
The district has two-thirds of its total surface area covered with
water, making it a low-lying, sleeping giant.
There is a total oversight in terms of tourism promotion by local
marketing agents who would be expected to ve working over-time to attract
potential and serious developers to the district.
One cannot believe that the shores of Lake Bangweulu provide one of the
most natural sandy beaches in the world.
Here, holiday beach hotels, chalets including fast food facilities, can
be constructed in partnership with local and foreign investors to offer
first class service to tourists and fun-seeking locals.
Samfya fact file:
Total surface Area: Approximately 10,000 square km.
Population: 168,863 as per the year 2000 national census.
: 172,370 according to the 2003 district health board survey.
Annual Population growth rate: 3.4 per cent.
Households: 27,800 out of which 25,000 are farming families.
Annual rainfall: 1,200 mm to 1,500mm.
Distance: From the capital city, Lusaka - 696 km;
From the provincial headquarters, Mansa-80 km.
The district is divided into two segments with that covered in swamp
spanning about 5,000 square km while, 2,500 square km is open water and the
rest, dry land.
Samfya's water provides livelihood to more than 50,000 beneficiaries who
live near the lake shores and are engaged mostly in fishing or related
activies.
The only missing aspect since independence in 1964, is the establishment
of an ice plant for the booming fish industry as well as the lack of a fish
canning factory to process the product for export and provide employment to
the locals.
Twenty-one-year-old Alex Kalumbu who has been engaged in fishing since
his teen days, is surprised and unimpressed that up to now, no investor has
seen the need to set up a fish canning plant by the lakeside.
Mr Kalumbu said there was a big market in the Great Lakes region which
consumed a lot of Zambian fresh water fish.
"At least many of us who have spent our lives fishing can find
employment in such plants and earn a decent living," said Mr Kalumbu, just
returning from an early morning fishing expedition on the lake.
Lake Bangweulu itself is well-known for the largest population of what
the locals call "human" crocodiles (large crocs that are believed to be
inhabited by human spirits) that terrorise fishermen and residents.
Locals say this year alone, more than 70 lives have been lost on and
near the lake.
Many of these reptiles have been shot dead by police and wildlife
officers while others, had "refused" to die even when shot at close
range.
"There is a very big population of human crocodiles in the lake. Many of
them do not die even when shot at close range," says Samfya council
messenger Keleya Mwansa who acted as a guide to this author and his
colleagues from other media institutions who recently went to cover
Constitutional Review Commission (CRC) submissions in the district.
One crocodile, about six metres long and weighing more than the normal
weight of a fairly sized sports car, was shot dead last month when it
attacked residents on shores of the lake.
The crocodile mauled a fisherman in a canoe and seriously wounded
another who was fishing on the shores, before it was gunned down by
police.
The reptile was dragged to the central police station grounds where it
was doused in kerosene and set ablaze attracting a huge crowd of curious
onlookers.
There are about six traditional rulers on the wetlands namely; Senior
Chief Kalimankonde, chiefs Mulongwe, Mbulu, Kasomalunga, Nsamba and Bwalya
Mponda who live with their subjects right in the Bangweulu basin and heavily
dependent on fishing as their mainstay. There is also Chief Mwansakombe on
the mainland.
There is also mixed farming mainly of maize, groundnuts, cassava and
sweet potatoes but not on a very large scale. Trading mainly in fish, has
taken centre stage.
It is this fish business that has greatly threatened the population of
most species in Lake Bangweulu whose figures have declined drastically in
the past three decades.
If you are a first-time visitor arriving in Samfya at night, it is quite
easy to mistake the night-time activities on Lake Bangweulu for a small,
well-lit bustling city of Europe or America.
Fishermen paddling "deep" on the lake, light up dozens of lanterns all
night long to catch small kapenta-like fish commonly known as chisense.
Chisense which has not been commercially exploited for the past 20
years, has now emerged as a major profitable fish in the industry.
It is caught on a daily basis, year-in and year-out to meet
ever-increasing high demand in Lusaka and the Copperbelt where it fetches
good money.
Even with the introduction of the Fisheries Act in 1974, which was aimed
at regulating and forestalling a drop fish numbers in the lake, people have
continued to use indiscriminate methods of fishing such as explosives,
spears, poisoning, using long baskets known as imyono and lately, even
mosquito nets.
These primitive fishing methods have had a great effect on the
population of most fish species of the lake which have declined both in size
and numbers.
One of the fish species which is now threatened with extinction is the
Anchinoglanis Occidentalis or popularly known as Imbowa in the local
language.
There is also no deliberate policy by Government to ensure that the
local community engaged in other profitable activities during the annual
fish ban.
As is the case now, it is difficult for the local community to survive
during the three months fish ban period. The temptation to breach the ban is
simply irristible.
During a recent tour of the district by the Constitutional Review
Commission (CRC), vice-chairman General Kingsley Chinkuli, observed that the
district would catapult to prosperity if there was support from Government
and other stakeholders including foreign investors.
General Chinkuli who was speaking when he paid a courtesy call on the
district administrator (DA) Cosmas Chola, said focus should be on the
development of the water transport system to promote tourism on Chilubi,
Mbabala and Chishi islands.
"Government should help in bringing about investment to the area
including water transport to boost tourism," Gen Chinkuli pointed out.
Mr Chola said the Bangweulu Water Transport Authority was in urgent need
of at least three medium-sized 65-seat passenger boats to handle the
traffic.
"We only need about three 65 passenger boats from Government to improve
the transport system on the lake and help to promote tourism," he said.
The only competitor, the Post Boat run by the Zambia Postal Services
Corporation (Zampost) has its own problems due to low traffic as motor boats
which were faster, had taken advantage of the situation.
But the Post Boat has maintained and sustained operations because its
main line of business is to deliver mail to the islands on the lake.
This year, in a deliberate move to promote tourism, the District Health
Management Board has planned a boat cruise on Christmas day to avail
tourists a chance to enjoy the beautiful scenery of Lake Bangweulu.
New lodges such as Samfya Beach on the lake shores, Twesheko, Villax and
other hospitality businesses which were supplemented by Government rest
houses, have emerged to provide decent accommodation to tourists.
Investors are now being challenged to set up facilities such as boating,
angling and rafting clubs along side night clubs and other social
infrastructure along the lakeside which spots beautiful clear water and
white sandy beaches.
Samfya district has abundant wildlife, especially in the Kalasamukonso
Game Management Area (GMA) and is the only place in the world where Black
Lechwe are found.
But the GMA faces a lot of challenges in terms of rampant poaching and
encroachment by villagers.
Permanent settlements in the swamps and flood plains are also a source
of worry and have contributed much to the declining Black Lechwe
population.
The area is difficult for wildlife police officers to manage
effectively.
Currently, one officer covers an area of approximately 167 square km
mainly on foot.
Samfya district has no Government hospital to cater for rising
population but is served by 25 rural health centres.
The serious cases have to be referred to the either of the two mission
hospitals in the area; Lubwe on the shores of Lake Chifunabuli adjacent to
Lake Bangweulu and another at Kasaba but they are far from the
district.
It has become increasingly difficult for Samfya residents to travel to
these medical institutions due to the lack of reliable transport, or high
transport costs where it is available.
Samfya district administrative officer Fred Kosamu said the area only
has a rural health centre stage II for the township population which also
acts as a first referral hospital to serve the swamps and southern areas of
the district.
These institutions have no reliable facilities such as x-ray, surgical
and other advanced laboratory requirements.
"There is an urgent need for this district to have a Government hospital
which can serve the ever increasing population," Mr Kosamu said.

Original_Search_Title: Government's Vision for Farmers - Vice President
Headline: Ghana: Government's Vision for Farmers - Vice President
Body_Text:
The Vice President, Alhaji Aliu Mahama has stated that the NPP
government is committed to a long term goal of achieving food security,
poverty reduction, wealth creation and providing raw materials for local
industries and commodities for the domestic and export market.
He said the government has encouraged and promoted Agribusiness through
the implementation of the President's Special Initiative programmes on
Cassava, Oil Palm ,Rubber and cotton.
The Vice President said this at the opening ceremony of the 2nd Ghana
International Food and Agriculture Trade Fair (AGRIFEX ?03) which was
organised by the Ministry of Agriculture in collaboration with Ghana Trade
Fair in Accra under the theme "Processing and Packaging Ghana's rich
Agricultural Products for Food Security and Export."
He said we need to take serious view of standard processing and
packaging of agro-based products to protect our entrepreneurs against losses
through badly processed and packaged products for exports.
Alhaji Aliu Mahama said the government is determined to address issues
related to post ?production in the agricultural sector with the active
participation of the private sector in harvesting, storage, haulage,
processing ,packaging and improvement of new product for market
expansion.
He added that the NPP government is ready to develop rural
agro-enterprise to improve market opportunities to impact positively on the
economic and social benefit of our rural areas.
The Vice President expressed concern about how some farmers continue to
regard farming as a way of life rather than as a business.
The Minister of Food and Agriculture ,Major Courage Quashigah said the
aims for this year?s AGRIFEX is to improve the image of the Ghanaian farmer
and fisherman so as to persuade the educated ,particularly the youth into
the agricultural sector and to educate farmers and fishermen on modern and
profitable method of farming.
He said his Ministry has set up a post harvest working group in August
this year with representatives of stakeholders in the production,
processing, packaging and market chain.
The chairman of the ceremony Nana Daniel Afari added that the old image
of a farmer returning from a farm carrying a baby at the back and goods must
be replaced with a farmer returning from a farm with a Nissan Pick Up
carrying loads.

Original_Search_Title: Group Seeks Improved Standard, Infrastructure
Headline: Nigeria: Group Seeks Improved Standard, Infrastructure
Body_Text:
Cassava — Concerned stakeholders in the cassava processing sector of the agriculture industry, drawn from the six geo-political zones in Nigeria, have deliberated and made suggestions on the improvement of the vital and potential foreign-exchange earning sector.
In a communique drafted at the end of a National Cassava Processing and Export Workshop, held recently at Abuja, the group spear-headed by Erste Graceland Nigeria Limited, a private sector initiator noted the daily waste and losses incurred in the cultivation of cassava by peasant farmers and large-scale industrialists in Nigeria, and the need created by absence of an enabling environment for harnessing the vast potentials of a product labeled as 'white gold'.
At the forum with the theme "Cassava Revolution Moving from Words to Action", the group sought to improve the situation by committing foreign investors to themselves and the Nigerian stakeholders on the cassava sector; to mitigate and effectively sensitise and mobilise the nation to benefit technically, economically and socially from the above process.
In the statement, jointly signed by Chidi Ulelu of Erste Graceland, Wole Ogunubi (South-West), Theresa Nwanu of North-East, and Mallam Abba Aliyu of North-East, among others; participants at the forum emphasised an urgent need for immediate institution of a social networking group of cassava producers cooperative in Nigeria.
"Suitable land grant policy should be enacted by the Federal and state governments to enable cassava farmers have access to cheap or free land, further articulation of the opportunities of cassava production in Nigeria and urgent support of the private sector initiative in various specific ways", they stated.
Among other requests and suggestions proffered by the group included land clearing facilities to enhance productivity; provision of research grants to enable members of the cooperative who are experts in improved breed multiplication to enhance the quality of cuttings and meet the imminent challenge that will be posed by expected need for increase in production.
Further demands included provision of inorganic fertilisers to farmers at minimal cost or highly subsidised rates and encouraging the use of organic fertilisers which are both environment-friendly, and capable of attracting better internal prices; setting up a joint action committee with members of the cassava cooperative and stake holders who will monitor the banks implementation of the S.M.E.I's funds, while creating appropriate legislative and policy framework channeling percentages of same funds to only cassava related projects found viable by open standard.
The group also called for the establishment of research laboratories and standardisation institutions in the six geo-political zones of Nigeria; distribution of smaller processing equipment for food grade starch, spray starch, ethanol, citric acid, glucose, derivatives and others; and re-activation of the Agricultural guarantee schemes while henceforth involving private sector representatives in every loan/ grant disbursement process targeted at farmers.
"Starch and starch derivatives export should also be channeled towards the A.G.O.A Market. Government should support special schemes developed and targeted at women which have flexibility and special funding characteristics aimed at making it easier for women to access loans and grants; and increase the current micro-credit agro-bank schemes ceiling from N250,000 to N750,000 reflecting the reality of the times while making sure the community banks are not left out in the agro-loan schemes", the group said.
Government and all concerned stakeholders were urged to adopt worldwide standards for exportable cassava products, and set up monitoring agencies at ports of entry; enforce the mandate of IITA, FIIRO, RMRDC, NEXIM, BOI and other allied agencies to produce cassava, perfect processing and provide funding as and at when due;
and encourage young school secondary and post-secondary graduates who embrace cassava planting with solid sources of maintenance for at least the first twelve months;
It further called for the urgent address on the issue of lack of proper statistics in the areas of production and import of cassava products while monitoring all future activities in this areas; and development of world standard industrial and modified starch processes, using every available means and will to break into world markets so as to provide ready market avenues for farmers' products;
The Federal government was also enjoined to encourage and stimulate indigenous technology for the production of standard cassava production and processing equipment of international standard to reduce cost and discourage importation.
The forum witnessed the gathering of all stakeholders and potential foreign investors, who all deliberated on the potentials of cassava processing and export earning. Other future fora were equally planned on the crucial topic.

Original_Search_Title: Stop Using NGOs As Fronts, Mumba Warns Politicians
Headline: Zambia: Stop Using NGOs As Fronts, Mumba Warns Politicians
Body_Text:
VICE-President Nevers Mumba has warned politicians against using
non-government organisations (NGOs) as fronts to destabilise
Government.
Dr Mumba said people who wanted to enter the political arena should be
bold enough to do so openly.
Speaking at a function to commemorate Programme Against Malnutrition
(PAM)'s 10th anniversary in Lusaka yesterday, Dr Mumba said there was a
tendency by NGOs to use foreign funding to cause anarchy in a nation.
"Do not be afraid to participate in politics. If you feel you have
something to contribute to politics and you stand on what you believe, come
out in the open," he said.
He commended PAM for remaining committed to the cause of fighting
poverty and uplifting the standard of the vulnerable despite the massive
donor funding it was receiving.
He said there was a temptation by many NGOs to use their economic power
for destabilisation and most often, to advance the causes of their foreign
financiers.
"Interestingly, such NGOs agitating destabilisation will not be seen
when trouble comes," he said.
He said PAM had demonstrated the capacity, professional proficiency and
accountability in running large-scale projects to alleviate poverty, for
disaster mitigation and to increase food security.
Under the Food Security Pack, PAM facilitated 128,500 households with
agricultural inputs and it was estimated that over 80,000 tonnes of cereal,
20,000 tonnes of legumes and 140,000 tonnes of cassava and sweet potatoes
would be produced this season.
PAM was also implementing the Small-holder Access to Processing,
Extension and Seeds (Shapes) and the World Bank-funded Emergency Drought
Recovery Project.
Dr Mumba said he was happy to learn that PAM was planning to focus on
