fighting malnutrition and HIV/AIDS both of which had devastating impact on
agriculture.
And Norwegian ambassador to Zambia Halvard Lesteberg said his Government
had provided $7 million to PAM since 1995.
He said the food security situation in Zambia had been difficult and
continued to be precarious.
"The challenge of simultaneously pursuing social and economic
development, and battling the food insecurity is really a difficult task,"
he said.
He said hasty emergency interventions were often detrimental to
long-term development efforts, hence the need for NGOs like PAM to
assist.
Red Cross Society secretary general Sam Phiri urged Government to grant
tax exemption to humanitarian organisations.

Original_Search_Title: There's a Temptation By NGOs to Destabilise Govts - Mumbaa
Headline: Zambia: There's a Temptation By NGOs to Destabilise Govts - Mumbaa
Body_Text:
Lusaka — THERE is a temptation by non-governmental organisations in Africa to
destabilise governments, charged Vice-President Nevers Mumba yesterday.
Vice-President Nevers Mumba and PAM Executive director Drinah Nyirenda
cutting a cake to mark PAM's 10 th anniversary yesterday.
Officiating at PAM's (Prevention Against Malnutrition) 10th anniversary,
Vice-President Mumba said life was an event of choices in which those that
wasted their lives to alleviate problems of the poor were remembered.
He said in developing Africa, there was temptation amongst NGOs that
received more money than government to use their economic power as a means
to destabilise governments.
"They use economic means at the influence of foreign power as has
happened in some African countries to destabilise government,"
Vice-President Mumba said. "Once destabilisation comes, those NGOs won't be
there to resolve the conflict they have created or mitigate suffering among
the citizens."
Vice-President Mumba said PAM had proved itself as a local, Zambian, NGO
with a true vision of disaster management and recovery programmes of the
vulnerable but viable families.
He said no person should hide in NGOs for them to achieve ulterior
motives.
Vice-President Mumba said in the last ten years, PAM had grown from
relief food distribution and recovery programmes in disaster areas to
include development projects aimed at poverty alleviation.
He said the food security pack, one of their programmes, had in the
2002/2003 season seen 128,500 households that received agricultural inputs
harvest 80,000 metric tonnes of cereal, 20,000 metric tonnes of legumes and
140,000 metric tonnes of cassava.
Vice-President Mumba said this production secured some one million rural
persons with food and that such projects assisted government in its
obligation to reduce hunger by 2015.
He said during the 1991/1992 drought disaster, PAM delivered 250,000
metric tonnes of relief food to 2.5 million beneficiaries.
Vice-President Mumba said PAM had had an eventful decade of
existence.
"As we all pursue the Millennium Development Goals, the Poverty
Reduction Strategy Programme and the main economic growth strategies that
are aimed at moving this nation from its highly indebted poor country status
to a wealthy nation, PAM has a major role to play," he said.
Vice-President Mumba said life had a tendency to remember those that
empowered others and not those that accumulated wealth for themselves.
"We are all a sum total of choices we make. We exist for others," he
said.
Norwegian Ambassador Halvard Lesteberg said the overall Norway support
to PAM was approximately US $7 million since 1995.
He said food insecurity affected men and women but that it affected most
severely the weaker part of the society.
Ambassador Lesteberg said he was pleased that PAM had been in the hands
of competent and dedicated women leaders and that most successful households
under PAM programmes were female headed.
"Women historically have a better record in understanding and responding
to such type of suffering," Ambassador Lesteberg said. "It is therefore only
right that women are targeted in PAM programmes, that increases the chances
of sustainable impacts."
Since inception, women served as executive directors of PAM starting
with Fridah Luhila and now Drinah Nyirenda.
Ambassador Lesteberg said Zambia's food security had been difficult for
a long time and that the situation is still fragile.
He said dealing with food insecurity, both on a national level and also
for more geographically isolated pockets particularly vulnerable groups was
a challenge for many developing countries.
"In case of Zambia this may seem to be a paradox, Zambia being rich in
both fertile land and water," he said.
Ambassador Lesteberg said food insecurity was closely linked to the
general poverty level.
He said the challenge of simultaneously pursuing social and economic
development and battling the food insecurity was really a difficult
task.
Ambassador Lesteberg said it was well known that hastily conceived
emergency interventions in many cases were detrimental to long term
developmental efforts.
He said such dilemma had to be properly addressed.
Ambassador Lesteberg said PAM was a tool for interventions that
addressed immediate and near future food security concerns while at the same
time invested resources to contribute to permanently improved food
security.
He said PAM's programmes had enabled food insecure families address
immediate high risk and near future problems and had consolidated higher
food production, better and balanced nutrition.
"Both increased production volumes, diversification and market
development must go hand in hand," he said.
PAM board chairman Bishop John Mambo said since establishment on
November 11, 1993, PAM had become an umbrella NGO linking many rural NGOs to
government and donor resources.
He said currently there were 100-partner led NGOs that PAM provided
capacity and that were contributing with PAM on various projects.
Bishop Mambo said PAM had gradually transformed itself to refocus more
on drought rehabilitation and development oriented non-food relief and rural
poverty alleviation programmes.
He said these activities included seed distribution, fertiliser, seed
production and distribution, food processing and preservation and
utilisation through use of government and donor resources.
Bishop Mambo said nothing was greater than love, faith and charity.
He said PAM had lost many of its members during the course of duty.
Bishop Mambo said PAM lost its board chairman Bishop Paul Lungu in a
road traffic accident on his way from a board meeting in Lusaka to Monze
where he was based.
"I don't know why he was not honoured this October because he died at a
time of difficulties," he said.
Bishop Mambo said Zambians were ready to work hard if they were assisted
with resources.
He said agriculture minister Mundia Sikatana did not need to resign
because there was a bumper harvest.
"You can only resign because you have poor marketing policies," said
Bishop Mambo.

Original_Search_Title: Big Boost for PSI On Cassava
Headline: Ghana: Big Boost for PSI On Cassava
Body_Text:
Koforidua — THE EASTERN Regional director of the Ministry of Food and Agriculture (MoFA) has disclosed that the Presidential Special Initiative (PSI) on Cassava in the region has received a boost this year as there has been an increase in the level of production.
He said, as compared to last year, which saw the cultivation of 184 acres of farmland, cultivation this year has increased to 901.5 as at last month.
The Regional director of MoFA, Mr. Kweku Amoo-Baffoe, disclosed this to The Chronicle in an interview at Koforidua, on Monday.
Currently, seven districts in the region are benefiting from this initiative.
The districts are Fanteakwa, Yilo Krobo and Suhum/Kraboa/Coaltar. The rest are Akuapem North, Manya Krobo, Asuogyaman and Birim South districts.
Mr. Amoo-Baffoe said 276 farmers have so far received cassava-planting materials from MoFA.
Aside this, other groups and individuals are also cutting and planting materials, which he said, keep the farmers in constant supply of raw materials.
Touching on the mass cocoa spraying exercise, the Regional director of MoFA said there has been considerable increase in cocoa production.
He attributed this to the mass spraying exercise, a disease and pest control project as well as the application of fertilizers in the cultivation of cocoa.
For instance, the use of a hi-tech type of fertilizer for the cultivation of cocoa has increased production from an initial three bags during harvest to between ten and 16 bags, he explained.
Amoo-Baffoe said the increase in the producer price of cocoa has encouraged more farmers to increase their production levels and also made them to adopt the hybrid type of cocoa which is high yielding and able to withstand pests and diseases.
He expressed optimism that, with the current trend in production levels, Ghana would soon regain her former position as the largest producer of cocoa on the world market.
The Regional director advised farmers to develop a farm maintenance culture on their farms as a means of ensuring high crop production.

Original_Search_Title: NARO Boss Advises Researchers
Headline: Uganda: NARO Boss Advises Researchers
Body_Text:
Kampala — PROF Frederick Kayanja, the NARO board chairman has called on
researchers in agriculture to be sensitive to farmers' needs and come up
with technologies which increase crop production and improve the existing
crop varieties.
Kayanja, who is also the Vice Chancellor of Mbarara University of
Science and Technology, was addressing senior managers of NARO during a
five-day training workshop conducted at Mukono district agricultural
research and development centre, recently.
"You should be sensitive to the environment and farmers' needs to be
able to contribute effectively to the modernisation of agriculture and
eradicate poverty," he said.
Speaking at the function, the Director General of National Agricultural
Research Organisation (NARO), George William Otim commended agricultural
researchers for the hard work in fighting the cassava mosaic and the water
hyacinth.
"Agricultural scientists have been dealing with quality assurance for a
long time," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Help Us Rebuild Our Hospital, Liberian Bishop Asks
Headline: Liberia: Help Us Rebuild Our Hospital, Liberian Bishop Asks
Body_Text:
Washington, DC — With peacekeepers deployed and an interim government in place in
Liberia, how much is the humanitarian situation improving? United Methodist
Bishop John Innis, who leads one of the country's largest churches, says the
key to the creation of peace and stability lies in the rural areas. Innis
was born in northern Liberia and spent a decade working in rural regions,
primarily as the director and a teacher at the Camphor Mission Station. He
graduated from the University of Liberia and Saint Paul School of Theology
in Kansas City, Missouri and worked for the United Methodist Committee on
Relief in the United States and West Africa before his election to the
episcopacy in 2000. There are some 170,000 United Methodists in Liberia and
600 churches. United Methodist University in Monrovia has about 1,000
students.
While in Washington, DC this month to take part in a United Methodist
Council of Bishops meeting, Innis was interviewed by AllAfrica about
conditions in his country.
What has changed since the major fighting ended?
Life in Monrovia is returning to normal, and we can see signs of hope
returning to our people. They have to believe that their lives will return
to normal, that their homes will be rebuilt, that psychologically they can
recover from what they have experienced during this terrible period we have
gone through. We're very hopeful that peace is really on its way.
Keep up with the latest headlines on WhatsApp | LinkedIn
But we want to see peace enjoyed by all of our people. In rural areas,
there are still many pockets of trouble. People demanding money and food
from the residents in those villages and towns. The conditions there are not
pleasant at all, for the most part.
Doesn't having the United Nations peacekeeping force in Liberia make
a difference?
Yes, but peace and security in Monrovia does not mean security for the
whole country. Our concern is that the peacekeepers get to those remote
areas and help people there. Of course, many rural people have had to flee
into the cities, and they are not used to that. Urban life is horrible for
them. They want to go back, when it is safe for them to go. If our people
can stopped being harassed, they will go back to their farms and grow
cassava, rice, potatoes, peppers and begin to supply the country again with
food.
You spoke about Monrovia. What about Buchanan (the country's
second-largest city)?
I was in Buchanan a few weeks ago. The young rebels are still
masquerading around the town with their guns. I spoke to some of them and
asked: 'How can you lead us if you have no education?' I shared my own
struggles with them - how I came from the village and had to hold down odd
jobs to get an education. I told them that they can come to people like us
and say 'we want to go to school' and we will try help to help them.
As soon as disarmament occurs, I said they should drop the guns. The
United Methodist Church and all the churches, along with international
groups, need to work together to see that those young people have a future
without the guns. Education is the key to transformation for our country. We
have to prioritize education.
Churches play a big role in the life of Liberians. How were churches
impacted by the war?
We lost everything in the fighting. Our schools, our churches, our
orphanages were damaged. Many pastors lost their homes. Look at Ganta
Hospital in the north. That wonderful hospital was vandalized, badly
damaged. Just a few years ago, some $1.5 million was invested in that
facility, which serves over a half million people in the northern part of
the country. The nurses' school connected with the hospital became a
college, one of the best nursing schools in Liberia. There was a pre-natal
clinic, a center to feed malnourished children an eye clinic, and 75 beds
for people requiring to be hospitalized.
That hospital has been very important for that area, and not just for
Liberians. People came there from Guinea and from Côte d'Ivoire, and, at
times, from Monrovia for treatment. It has to be rebuilt.
I understand that damage occurred throughout much of the larger
mission facility in Ganta, which includes a primary and secondary school, a
demonstration farm, vocational training facilities, and a leprosy and
tuberculosis rehabilitation unit.  The United Methodist Board of Global
Missions in New York has issued an appeal to help restore the hospital and
the rest of the mission. What steps need to be taken to resume operations
there?
We'd like for Liberians from that area to be able to go back and begin
cleaning up the dirt and destruction that has accumulated, to begin making
repairs. We want to reopen early in 2004, but before that can happen we need
to patch up the buildings, fix the plumbing and power and water systems. We
think it is going to cost $200,000 or more.
This is a priority for us. We also have orphans who need care, and other
urgent needs. We hope our United Methodists friends here in the United
States, all our friends, can find a way to help us. We are determined to
help ourselves. But we can use a hand!
RELATED: Bishop's Appeal for Liberia United Methodist
Empowerment Foundation

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture Gets New Impetus
Headline: Nigeria: Agriculture Gets New Impetus
Body_Text:
Makurdi — The Benue State Government has announced plans to make agricultural
production a priority, with particular emphasis on the production of
cassava, soybeans, yams, just as it wold acquire a processing technology to
add value the yields and safeguard against wastage.
The new policy thrust is coming just as farmers in the state have
suggested new fertiliser distribution measures to curb the incidence of
diversion, which had so far denied most farmers the opportunity of applying
fertilisers on their crops this year.
Other areas to receive priority attention include the development of
fish farms, which the government has so far approved the sum of N2 million
to kick start.
Already the state Governor, Mr George Akume, has made two foreign trips
to Brazil and Europe in line with his policy decision towards
technology-driven agriculture in the state. The trips were to seek better
ways of production and processing of cassava.
Hon David Onah, Animal and Forest Resources Commissioner said the fish
development project would focus primarily on production of fingerlings,
which would be sold to fish farmers in the state.
The policy according to the commissioner is to encourage the production
of fish to meet the protein needs of the people of the state.
Similarly, farmers in the state have suggested the distribution of
fertilisers through the wards to combat the problem of diversion that
characterised fertiliser distribution in the state. The diversion palava had
led to the suspension of the Permanent Secretary in the Ministry of
Agriculture, Dr Yusuf Omojoh, said to have perpetrated illegalities.

Original_Search_Title: Interview Campaign to End Sale of Unsafe Milk
Headline: Kenya: Interview Campaign to End Sale of Unsafe Milk
Body_Text:
Nairobi —  Since the liberalisation of the dairy sector in the early 1990s
dozens of processing units have proliferated along with explosion of milk
hawking. Kenya Dairy Board (KDB) recently launched a campaign to promote
consumption of safe raw milk and products. BusinessWeek staff writer Muna
Wahome interviewed the Board's managing director Machira Gichohi.
What exactly is this campaign all about?
There is a huge informal raw milk trade sector. These traders are ill
equipped in safe handling of milk and products.
This has raised serious public health concerns especially with regard to
the spread of diseases like tuberculosis, brucellosis, typhoid and
diarrhoea.
Notably some of these traders have been known to engage in unscrupulous
practices such as adding water, wheat or cassava flour, margarine and even
harmful chemicals to the milk.
It is in this regard that the Board has come up with a campaign to
sensitize the consumers about the dangers associated with consumption of
milk of uncertain quality.
The said opening up of the market has obviously had some negative
impact on the market. How are you dealing with this?
The advent of liberalisation opened the way to private entrepreneurs who
had interest in the large milk trade hitherto under the original Kenya
Co-operative Creameries monopoly. The scale of trade varied from small scale
- less than 100 litres per day - to the large scale, over 100,000 litres per
day.
The diversity in the volumes handled necessitated the introduction of
various licence categories by the Board; to date these are used as means of
regulating the trade.
Our new licence categories have included: milk bar licence, cottage
industry licence, mini-dairy and processor licence. In addition, the Board
has strengthened its field outlets through deployment of more qualified
personnel, timely supply of equipment and increased collaboration with
stakeholders.
How does the safe milk campaign gel with the industry code of
practice developed by stakeholders last year?
The campaign is based on the industry code of practice for production,
handling, processing and distribution of milk and milk products. Basically,
this whole promotion is therefore an attempt at promoting compliance with
the industry code.
One of the major issues in the industry between producers and
processors is the non-payment for supply on time. Is the Board doing
something about it?
The Board has come out boldly to ensure that the industry is sustainable
for the mutual benefits of all players. Soon, the Board will introduce a
clause with effect from January next year that will compel all processors to
provide an indemnity (in form of bank guarantee) covering three months.
This provision will be used to settle outstanding payments to producers
in case there is a default.
How well is the Board placed to play its regulatory role and also
assist industry players to build their human, technical and financial
capacity?
Our is a small organisation with a lean staff and financial
base. However, we are working with stakeholders and employing modern
technologies to enhance outreach and efficiency.
How have you approached the issue of glut in milk production,
especially during the rainy season and what plans have been put in place to
stop milk from going to waste at such times?
The Board has just concluded a study on the management of surplus milk
during the wet season and creation of a strategic reserve for use in the dry
seasons.
The Board has also conducted wide-ranging consultations among
stakeholders and particularly with the parent ministry and the new Kenya
Co-operative Creameries (KCC). The proposals are being reviewed at various
levels of government and by stakeholders, and are expected to win their full
support.
In your view, how can small-scale milk co-operatives be leveraged to
increase production and market access?
Kenya Dairy Board has held several meetings in various parts of the
country with small scale farmers. The main issue would be to encourage
small-scale producers to form groups that address the marketing of their
products. We are encouraging such groups to register as Self Help Groups or
Cooperatives who deal in transportation, cooling and marketing of milk.
With the establishment of consistent marketing channels of milk,
smallholder farmers will be able to sustain and even improve their
production.

Original_Search_Title: Poor Policies Souring Kenya's Dairy Sector
Headline: Kenya: Poor Policies Souring Kenya's Dairy Sector
Body_Text:
Nairobi — THE FUTURE of Kenya's dairy industry remains gloomy despite recent
government efforts to streamline the sector.
Insiders predict that by 2005, the country could become one of the top
importers of milk, despite being among the top milk producing and per capita
consumption countries in Africa, surpassed only by Botswana, Sudan and
Mauritania.
The country lacks technical and institutional policies to turn around a
sector, said to be one of the most developed in East and Southern
African. Even the increase in numbers of commercial milk processors - 30 at
the moment - has not matched the demand for milk for both industrial use and
human consumption currently at 2.5 billion litres per annum.
A report by the Institute of Policy Analysis and Research (IPAR) says
that the dairy sector, which accounts for about 10 per cent of the country's
gross domestic product, has the potential to create close to a million jobs
and improve significantly the incomes of smallholder diary farmers, who make
up about 80 per cent of milk producers in the country, if Kenya puts in
place the necessary institutional and technical policies to guide the
sector.
The IPAR report titled, Liberalisation and Policy Agenda for the Dairy
Sector compiled by Dr David Omiti and Mathew Muma, said since the
liberalisation of the sector in 1992, dairy policies have focused on the
declining large-scale production, and ignored the smallholder producers and
traders, who produce more than 80 per cent of the 2.8 billion litres of milk
produced and sold in Kenya annually.
This is against the backdrop of a sector that boasts of a herd of
approximately 13 million dairy cattle - nine million zebu and 3.6 million
grade cattle. These statistics do not, however, include eight million dairy
goats and 800,000 dairy camels.
William Njoroge, a senior dairy official at the Dairy Board of Kenya
said that, although Kenya produced over 2.8 billion litres of milk annually,
it processes between 600,000 and 800,000 litres only, way below its one
million litres capacity per annum. The remaining is either consumed raw or
goes to waste.
He attributes the latter to inadequate information about market and
pricing and the reduced role of farmer training institutions through the
introduction of cost sharing.
The report calls for corrective policies to create technical and
entrepreneurial capacity to commercialise animal breeding services to avoid
wastage and improve market access but adds that "we should expect frequent
and serious milk shortfalls from 2005."
From a public policy perspective, this has critical implications on food
security, malnutrition, commercial imports and foreign exchange
reserves.
It is estimated that over 150 million litres of milk, worth about Ksh3
billion went to waste last year, which IPAR attributes to poor
transportation.
Wastage reduces per capita consumption in Kenya from between 200 and 300
litres as recommended by the Food and Agriculture Organisation to 80-100
litres per annum.
According to Mr Njoroge, the liberalisation of the industry did not take
into account the role of the informal dairy market in Kenya.
The IPAR report says, "like transportation in the 1970s, contemporary
research suggests that hawking offers considerable employment and
income-earning opportunities if accompanied by supportive policy."
While productivity had risen since the industry embraced the free market
concept, the increase in output did not result in income growth and social
welfare improvement.
The report titled, Liberalisation and Policy Agenda for the Dairy
Sector, compiled by Dr David Omiti and Mathew Muma,notes that to meet the
demand that is expected to rise in 2005, the country's animal breeding
policy must be revised to improve production.
The report says for the past 10 years, "The artificial insemination
services have been in disarray following government divesture and
introduction of cost-sharing schemes."
In a major departure from the commonly held view that milk sold by
small-scale traders is a health risk to consumers, IPAR says 50 per cent of
the milk sold through informal markets, mainly to hotels and restaurants, is
safe and hygienic. Instead, the report says, large-scale producers, who
transport milk long distances, are to blame for the adulteration of milk
with solids - contaminated cassava or wheat flour and antibiotics.
But Mr Njoroge, disagreed and said that in the absence of organised
marketing of milk, small-scale traders contribute to the low quality milk
sold in the market.
"We look forward to the revival of the Kenya Co-operative Creameries,
(KCC) which used to process about 300,000 litres per day," said Mr
Njoroge.
Investment
9 million zebu and 3.6 million grade cattle
30 commercial milk processing plants
2.5 billion litres per of milk demanded per annum
2.8 billion litres milk produced annually

Original_Search_Title: Marauding Elephants Invade Kilifi
Headline: Kenya: Marauding Elephants Invade Kilifi
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Twenty marauding elephants on Friday night invaded farms in Kilifi
District.
The elephants destroyed crops worth thousands of shillings, at Kaembeni
and Kahingoni villages in Vitengeni division.
Speaking to the Press yesterday, the residents called on the Kenya
Wildlife Service to address the issue.
The residents said the elephants have destroyed crops and threatened
their lives.
Speaking at Matano Manne trading centre, they said the elephants had
destroyed maize, cassava, coconut trees, bananas and paw paws among
crops.
The residents further said that they carry out their activities amidst
fear of being attacked.
Led by Mwahera ward councillor Byron Changawa Charo the residents said
the jumbos have terrorised them for the last two months.
They said they would kill the elephants if a solution is not found
soon.
A farmer Baray Baya said the elephants invaded his farm last Friday and
destroyed cassavas.
The elephants are believed to have strayed from the Arabuko Sokoke
forest reserve. Vitengeni District Officer Wilson Kimaiyo confirmed the
incidents. He said the Provincial Administration and KWS would take drastic
measures to relocate the elephants.

Original_Search_Title: Our HIPC Status Over?
Headline: Ghana: Our HIPC Status Over?
Body_Text:
Our HlPC Status is going to be over, possibly in nine months' time, the
President assures us.
We definitely must have crossed the HIPC line, and we genuinely must
congratulate ourselves for having been able to manage our HIPC economy so
well so far. HIPC cannot be forever. It is only a measure to give us some
breathing space to help rejuvenate ourselves for a purposeful economic
management.
We also need to congratulate the brains behind the HIPC Thing whose
management plans have been so successful thus far. They are proving to us
that any economic system however bankrupt can be managed for results, which
are what we are seeing now as HIPC is about to bow out possibly about June
2004.
The Economic System has prodigiously been enriched by donor magnanimity,
which brought to us endless donations like donations mourners offer at
funeral celebrations in our social system. And the gifts were both in cash
and in kind, including in some instances, cancellation of debts. We must
thank them all for having helped us gone out of our HIPC-ness.
As we are saying, China came in to see us with gifts and cancellation of
debts. Japan, one of the countries to approve of our going HIPC has been
with us even before the beginning of our HIPC days. Japan has never ever
been tired of helping us. They come with rice donation and technical support
on our rice farms. And the road construction works from Mallam to Yamoransa
are their gift.
And Spain wouldn't wish to be out done on the donor list. They are in to
build facilities for water supply particularly for the people of Koforidua
where our Prof .Emeritus King rules his people with "Statistical"
finesse. You see he used to call himself Prof right from his undergraduate
days to be rightly deserved of a donation of Professorship by a new
University in his domain. Well, Spain's donation draws another donation to
enrich us.
And the British People have been here. Their aids have been
regular. Their DFID Budget of 50 Million Pounds Sterling over the next many
years gives all the confidence of supply of education materials to our Basic
Schools. Their DFID endeavour sits in other fields like water supply waste
management, and moral support in all things of the economy.
Ghana has been their child since 1844, through 1901 to 1957 when the
Colonial bonds had to be broken. With the chains off, their soft hands of
friendship have always been with us where their aid is needed most.
And the United States of America has been among our HIPC Sympathisers in
the big way. The USAID has been here to promote quality education in the
Basic Schools. US AGOA support has been outstanding in the industrial front,
where garment manufacture and export are on the ascendancy.
AGOA (Africa's Growth and Opportunity Act) is already reeking in the
necessary profits yearly to fill our coffers. We can afford to pull off our
HIPC Coat, and live confidently on our AGOA earnings, thanks to America,
thanks to Prez. George Bush.
And Prez George Bush is surely adding new dimensions to the NEPAD
Initiative, which is to reward exclusively the first few nations of Africa
which would be adjudged first in good governance, where the rule of law
rules. Ghana is the obvious target, which should quickly step forward to
receive America's NEPAD Prize for good governance!
The proof is not far to seek. We are the first to give meaning to NEPAD
by setting up a whole Ministry for NEPAD and Regional Cooperation. Again we
are the first to submit our selves for peer Review Appraisal to prove our
good governance credentials.
The HIPC harvest has been plentiful. To date we have already embarked
upon plans to reduce poverty. We are rehabilitating, and building new Basic
Schools as well as reaching the thirsty with borehole water. HIPC money too
has hit the rural roads to open them up through due processes of
rehabilitation!
We must sincerely thank all nations and the IMF/World Bank People whose
donations and rare initiatives have been us almost off our HIPC
tentacles. It is a relief. We are no longer going to be dictated to, to do
the "impossible" with the management of our finances. We should be mature
now as masters of our own fate and responsibly.
But we are going to lose great many favours in budget support for
example. We hear Donor Support of our Budget has been in the region of 6O%
of the total annual Budget. But when the President says we are off the HIPC
facility, he must be certain of the gains we have in reserve which has been
bagged to date.
But if that is not the case, we should plead for some extension. We need
more money to meet demands for increase in workers' wages, where HIPC still
resides. We need money as seed money for the National Health Insurance
Scheme which our Workers's Leadership loves but shy away the necessary
sacrifice for it.
Well, our industrial boon too soon will be able to take care of all
that. The President is saying just that when he declared the other day,
courtesy the Ghanaian Times of 14.10.03 that there is wealth in the soil
which we must go for. No doubt, his Ayensu Starch Initiative is on
course. The Cassava element in there is named "Kufuor Bankye. Indeed. It is
the crop of the day, among our farmers.
And the cocoa fashion is on. Our cocoa yields shoot us to the Second
Spot among nations that produce cocoa. Too soon, we may regain our top
position with the development of new brand of Cocoa that takes only 18
months to bear fruits.
The Amazon type of Cocoa is still here. Our research farmers should be
able to give us high yielding Cocoa still in the days ahead of us. It will
make all the difference when HIPC finally leaves us.
HIPC is not going to go with our soil which bears the gold bearing rocks
whose richness Randgold cannot just stand and store. It wants a joint
venture with Ashanti Gold and it is prepared to pay Twenty-two times the
basic single share Ashanti Gold now pays. There is wealth in the soil
indeed.
Obuasi gold, in particular of the Ashanti Gold Enterprise is almost
inexhaustible. It appears to grow richer and richer by every gold-bearing
rock we blast from its underground mines. It is not only deep, but
horizontally so which our geologists wouldn't disclose how long for a
Thousand Pounds Sterling! It is the exclusive gift of Odomankoma, lodged for
us in the soil.
Indeed our Economic Resources are considerable, which should be
sufficient for us to say good bye to HIPC. But we should need to know in
real terms our total indebtedness in the entire Recovery Programme to
appreciate better the hard task ahead of us when HIPC finally bows out.
Of great interest will be our repayment schedules year by year which
should be pitched against our earning potency in the economic management
prowess will need to be rehearsed to us for our continued confidence in the
system. It is a necessary request.
In any case1we wish to be assured that with the HIPC Coat off, the
entire recovery processes have been a success, and that after HIPC, there
would be no other devise to come to bother us. Then HIPC off, and hurrah!!!
But wait a minute. Can't our HIPC experience which has been generous
lead us into another generous expression of goodwill with our IMF people for
a total cancellation of our debts. That would be wonderful. Good Governance
under President Kufuor should be able to earn us that too! Things have
changed much with our politics for the better!
Our politics since Independence has been the bane of the Economy, which
has been used recklessly for political ends instead of the promotion of the
economy. On that score, we became famous internationally, but easily
bankrupt at home. Soon we became the object of hatred in high international
political circles much to our ruin. We were hunted out, as it were, and
captured, and destroyed, because we were talking too much politically.
And with the collapse of our (in) famous political kingdom, built first
around Socialist ideals, through to the unaligned block of nations, we lost
almost everything of our importance as a people. The Economy already has
been destroyed, and with it, the very soul of the nation.
Meanwhile, we toppled one government after the other in search of our
lost selves, in vain. In our helplessness, we turned more corrupt and
pocketed what little money is left with us.
Indeed we became so madly infested with corruption that we felt the
answer to our corruption selves could be found in killing people and
demolishing market places, which we did with impunity.
In the end we became so poor that nothing could save the Economy but
some sort of Economic Recovery Prograrrme programmed for us by IMF/World
Bank People. Unfortunately that rescue package met with some fundamental
problems. We reached a Constitutional Rule to see if some political sanity
could help in the recovery processes. We were mistaken in the mean
time.
Bad politics and bad governance persisted till lately we had a change of
the political course, and a change in the Economic management endeavour. We
had to accept to go HIPC in order to stay alive as a People with bankrupt
economy, which is fast coming back to its own, fortunately.
Our return to normalcy should not make us swollen-headed. Hard economic
decisions have to be taken if we have to repair and maintain our once
shattered political and economic fortunes. We expect the necessary
Initiatives from the direction of the Government who should march with us on
the road to full recovery.
In the meantime, we will miss HIPC, a very close friend, whose requiem
we cherish to sing, any way!

Original_Search_Title: Contraband Owners Get 2-Week Disposal Deadline
Headline: Nigeria: Contraband Owners Get 2-Week Disposal Deadline
Body_Text:
The Nigeria Customs Service (NCS) has given supermarkets and shop owners
in the country a 2-week deadline to dispose of contraband or risk
seizure.
The News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) recalls that the federal government had
in August, last year banned the importation of some products, food items and
other consumables into the country.
"We are inundated with reports of flagrant display and sale of banned
items in supermarkets, shops and market places across the country," a
statement issued by the NCS today in Abuja, said.
The statement, signed by the comptroller-general, Alhaji Ahmed Mustapha,
and made available to NAN, stated that the service would on expiration of
the ultimatum raid such shops nationwide.
Mustapha said that the enforcement team of the service would confiscate
the banned items "where ever found" and also prosecute the offenders.
He, directed the customs area controllers to use the local media in
their locality to educate and warn members of the public on the impeding
action.
The customs boss, also advised all customs formations nationwide to
ensure strict compliance with the directive.
NAN recalls that the service had recently raided warehouses in some
popular markets in Lagos and Kano and impounded contraband textiles and
other items valued at millions of Naira.
Among items outlawed were sorghum, millet, wheat flour, gypsum,
vegetable oil in bulk, mosquito repellent coils, rethreaded or used tyres,
gaming machines and second-hand clothes.
Others are used fridges and air-conditioning units, used compressors,
bagged cement, vehicles of any kind through the land borders, vehicles that
are above eight years from the date of manufacture, textiles of any kind
through the land borders, printed fabric (Ankara) and frozen poultry.
Also banned were cassava, cassava products, containerised goods through
the land borders, fruit juice in retail packs, table water
(sparkling/non-sparkling), toothpicks, spaghetti, noodles, biscuits of any
type and drugs and other regulated products through the land borders.
The government had also banned the importation of sugar, all kinds of
canned and bottled beer, toilet rolls, confectioneries, exercise books and
envelopes, Barytes and Bentonites with effect from July 1, 2003.
NAN learnt that the ban was intended by government to boost local
production, enhance the performance of the national economy and safeguard
the health of the citizenry.

Original_Search_Title: Budget 2004: the Time for Truth
Headline: Nigeria: Vanguard Comment:- Budget 2004: the Time for Truth
Body_Text:
Politicians are well-known for exaggerating their achievements while
downplaying their failures. Thus, it was understandable that President
Obasanjo in his 2004 budget address to the nation talked of an estimated 5%
growth of the Gross Domestic Product based on projected agriculture sector
growth of 7% per annum. The President also claimed that the GDP growth for
2002 was 4%.
We are aware that the President during his first term had presented four
budgets promising 5% GDP growth rate each time and failing to reach that
target each and every time. We are also aware that the figure for last year
was widely estimated at less than the 4% which the President claims in his
2004 budget address.
What makes the President's claim for 2003 more disturbing is that it
comes at the same time that the Chairman of the National Economic
Intelligence Committee (NEIC) was announcing that the reforms embarked upon
by the Obasanjo administration have so far failed to deliver the promised
string of benefits to the people. Specifically, the NEIC Chairman pointed
out that GDP growth this year will barely hit the 3.75% mark as opposed to
the 5% the President is claiming. The Manufacturers Association of Nigeria
(MAN) is also on record as predicting a GDP growth for this year of less
than 4%.
Matters are not helped when the President based his estimates on the 7
per cent growth in agriculture without taking into account that oil is the
mainstay of the Nigerian economy and also without taking into consideration
sectors that might have grown less than 3% such as manufacturing and
services.
Vanguard will like to remind the government that the elections are over;
the time for truth is here. If the economy grows at less than 5%, the
government should be honest enough to say so. As it is, 5% is far below the
7% that must be achieved and sustained for poverty reduction to occur and
for the living standards of the average Nigerian to improve. For progress to
take place, we need reliable figures, not political estimates which are not
realistic. As the old adage goes, the farmer who after planting one hundred
yam tubers claims to have planted two hundred will, at harvest time, reap
one hundred yams followed by one hundred bundles of lies. Nigeria cannot
afford that.

Original_Search_Title: Christmas Fever Grips Yaounde
Headline: Cameroon: Christmas Fever Grips Yaounde
Body_Text:
Most markets now appear busier than usual as the population prepares for Christmas.
The month of December is a special period world wide, not only for the celebration of Christmas but as an end of a year. Money or no money, markets during this period are jammed with goods and people.
This festive fever is once more around. Markets in the nation's Capital get thicker each day. Buyers and sellers have intensified their activities in a bid to prepare for Christmas. Hundreds of people have engaged in businesses that are of peak demand to the population. Toys, decorative materials, fashion dresses and food stuffs are some of the most sought for goods. In Yaounde, for instance, toys and children dresses flood most markets. Affordable toys and dresses can be found in the Mokolo and Central markets. Cartons of toys are displayed in almost all corners in the market. Many buyers are seen purchasing either toys or dresses for their children.
Mokolo market is a case in point where many buyers, especially women, scramble for second hand dresses (okrikah). According to a buyer, Valerie, they are affordable because of the cheap price. Most big shops in Mokolo and the Central market have not only displayed new items in and out of their shops, but have set a festive tone with attractive music and decorations.
There is high competition amongst traders as many shop owners are doing every thing to attract customers. While some have announced discount in prices of goods, others hire young men and women to persuade customers for them. Others have even gone in for animation groups to animate in front of their shops thereby attracting customers. According to the proprietor of one super market in Mokolo, there is need to attract customers because the competition amongst traders is very high. He offers a packet of detergent to every customer who buys from the super market.
At the Mfoundi market, still in Yaounde, food stuff dominates sales as usual. One can quickly see yams, rice and chicken, etc, as the are displayed almost in all corners of the market. Most traders especially in the Melen market exceed their closing time as they sell late into the night. Here one can find very cheap under wears, dresses and shoes for less than 500francs.
With the crowd around markets, pedestrians and car owners can hardly have their way near market avenues. To this effect, police men are on guard to evacuate those selling along the road especially in the Mokolo and Elig-Edzoa markets.

Original_Search_Title: Petrol Sells for N200 in Calabar
Headline: Nigeria: Petrol Sells for N200 in Calabar
Body_Text:
Calabar — PETROL now sells for N200 per litre at the black market in
Calabar, Cross River State, following acute scarcity of the commodity that
has hit the city for over two weeks now. Scarcity of the product has slowed
down movement of commuters who wait for hours for comercial buses and
motorcycles (alalok). No reason has been given for the dry filling
stations.Long queues have returned to the few filling station with petrol as
motorists spend several hours waiting for their turn to buy the
commodity.
Consequently, transport fares have doubled as buses which ply the city
centre (Calabar Road) to the outskirts of Calabar (8 miles) now collect N60
as fares as against N30 or N40 before the scarcity. Similarly, commercial
motorcyclists, alaloks now charge as much as between N80 and N100 or more
for the same trip, while they demand between N30 and N40 for a short trip
within the city.
The effect of the fuel scarcity has led to a hike in the prices of
foodstuffs, causing the traders to sell a small bunch of unripe plantain for
between N150 and N200, while ripe ones go for between N300 and
N400. Tomatoes and pepper are not left out as traders now sell five pieces
of pepper for N20 and same number of tomatoes for N50. Furthermore, five
tiny tubers of yam sell for between N800 and N900 while rice is sold at
different prices depending on where the commodity was produced. Local rice
and imported bags of rice are priced at between N3000 and N5000. And when
the items are purchased, it was also learnt, kerosene or firewood to cook
them is another issue housewives have to contend with.
A bottle of kerosene now sells for N60 within Calabar city but the same
bottle of kerosene is sold at N80 at the outskirts of the city and firewood
which may prepare a pot of soup goes for between N20 and N30. All these are
blamed on the scarcity of petrol in the state.

Original_Search_Title: Road Safety Wagons Go West
Headline: Cameroon: Road Safety Wagons Go West
Body_Text:
After Yaounde-Douala, Transport Minister targets the Bafoussam-Bamenda Axis.
Statistics from the Ministry of Transport show that human errors are responsible for 70 percent of road accidents on major inter-urban highways in the country. These road mishaps which characterise the Christmas and New Year season have largely been attributed to reckless driving caused by alcoholism, excess speed, fatique and road worthlessness of vehicles. Road users' attitudes on the highways was also identified as a major contributor. It is against this background that the Ministry of Transport launched a four week sensitisation campaigns to ensure an accident-free festive period in the country. The crusade which began on the Yaounde-Douala axis, two weeks ago, moved to the Yaounde-Bafoussam-Bamenda highways, last Tuesday. The call remained unique: "the protection of lives on our highways is everyman's duty, not only the government, the Transport Ministry or drivers," the Minister of Transport, John B Ndeh, preached from Ebebda through Banakane, Bafossam to Bamenda. At each stop, he handed over the campaign trumpet to his administrative and judicial collaborators.
Traffic policemen on the Yaounde-Bafoussam axis say the sensitisation campaigns have made an impact within that zone. Police officials told journalists that there has been a marked improvement on road worthiness of most vehicles, the rate of overloading and less incidence of violation of road safety controls. The situation on Bafoussam-Bamenda lane was, however, not the same. Many a vehicle rattled and swallowed up the road with smoke, while others were grossly over loaded. A Toyota Hilux transporting bunches of plantains, coco-yams, firewood and over 25 passengers in the same cabin from Bouda to Bamenda su mulsulted, a few hundred metres, ahead of the minister's road safety convoy. The car was derailed by its weight on a sharp bend. The driver successfully dashed into the bush, leaving many wounded and no document to identify him.
The road safety campaign field report show that, apart from recklessness, many other factors contribute to the rampant road accidents in the country. Driver complain of very low and irregular salaries, which induce them to overloading for extra allowances. They also said numerous "extortionist barriers" erected on the highways by the police, gendarmery and councils cause drivers to speed excessively in order to recover the sum lost, as bribe, to these road safety officials. Once the officials are satisfied with the booty, drivers can go on any speed. The loading condition and the road worthiness of the vehicles become a minor matter. A check on the activities of these officials may reduce the rate of these mishaps.
Drivers also hold that many of their colleagues operate with faked documents. They revealed that most driving licences are bought from senior authorities in the Ministry of Transport and ficticious driving institutions. Reacting this, the Director of Land Transport, Mr Josue Youmba said, the rule stipulates that a professional driver must pass through a driving institute and be awarded licence only by a jury which constitutes experts from several ministries and the civil society. He said the list of candidates selected for the driving test must be published one week before the jury meets. This is to check fraud. Qualified driving schools, Mr Youmba said, must be approved by the Transport Ministry after having shown proves of qualified trained staff, satisfactory infrastructure and equipment. He said to check corruption and maintain efficiency, routine controls are carried out in these institutions every year.

Original_Search_Title: Hausa Community Applauds Odili Leadership
Headline: Nigeria: Hausa Community Applauds Odili Leadership
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — The Sarkin Hausawa of Rivers State, Alhaji Isa Madaki, has attributed
the good relation between the diverse religious groups in the state to the
broad-minded policy of the state government.
The community leader who spoke to Daily Trust in Port Harcourt said that
unlike other states where religious problems intermittently occur, the
non-indigenes of Rivers State have always felt at home under all
circumstances.
Alhaji Madaki observed that despite the uncanny attitude towards the
non-indigenous Muslims communities from the leader of the Islamic Council in
the state, the Hausa community has always placed peace and brotherly love as
watchwords since their religious preaches such qualities and not
discrimination and disorder.
He said that the community which has existed for over a hundred years
with its leadership since 1972 has always lived in harmony with the people
of the state and any government of the day.
"The government has provided a free environment for the non-indigenes to
live in the state as if it were theirs. The freedom is sometimes more than
that attainable in their states of origin. That is why foreign investor
whether from outside the country or outside the state prefer this state for
investments."
"Hausa hawkers walk in the streets from 6.00am to about 10.00pm selling
their wares in trucks, wheel-barrows or on their heads without problems,"
the Sarki maintained.
"Apart from the N1,500 annual registration fee, I move freely selling my
yams on the wheel-barrow. And have done this business for ten years and have
never been harassed on the way," 11 years, an indigene of Jigawa State
confirmed.
The Sarki added that no Hausa store has been destroyed in the demolition
exercise and that the governor has been the man protecting the interest of
the non-indigenes.
"The Governor has been doing a lot for us that is why we voted for him
in the last elections. We wish him greater and higher political position in
the future dispensation.
Alhaji Madaki, however, advised the non-indigenes especially the Hausa
community in the state to be law abiding in the discharged of their
businesses.

Original_Search_Title: 'The Media Should Help AMA Enforce By-Laws'
Headline: Ghana: 'The Media Should Help AMA Enforce By-Laws'
Body_Text:
The Accra Metropolitan Assembly, AMA last weekend called on the media to
help them enforce the existing by-laws that bars hawkers and traders from
selling on ceremonial streets and pavements.
The AMA for the past four weeks has initiated an 'Action Plan' which
makes Saturday's clean-ups mandatory for the AMA Task Force and
traders.
Speaking in an interview with ADM, the Acting Director of Waste
Management, AMA, Mr. Laryea said AMA has put the necessary logistics in
place to keep the Capital City clean. The only thing left is the enforcement
of the by-laws. This has been in existence since 1960. The media can
therefore play a vital role by helping AMA in championing the education of
these by-laws.
Since the interception of the 'Action Plan' AMA has made all efforts to
stop traders from selling on the said places. Twenty-one traders have been
summoned, each paid ¢6million and ¢2million respectively.
The AMA Task Force last Saturday inspected the Ashiedu Keteke
Sub-Metro. The Task Force indulged the traders in a clean-up exercise but
only a hand full on them turned up.
The chairman of Ashiedu Keteke Sub-Mero, Mr. Sammuel Tetteh, said AMA is
not interested in summoning traders. 'AMA summons traders to serve as
warning to others who might fall foul to the by-laws'.
The Chairman said traders selling on the streets do not pay tolls. The
traders without proper locations would be sacked from where they are.
He said Yam sellers, cooking oil sellers and onion sellers would be
relocated. Traders like those on the streets have abandoned their
locations. Action taken have not worked because 'AMA has been cowed down by
public sympathy'.
As to whether was AMA wining the 'war' on sanitation he said yes but to
the disadvantage of the general public' The chairman said traders are not
cooperating in the clean-up exercise.

Original_Search_Title: A Nation's Talent for Bungling
Headline: Nigeria: A Nation's Talent for Bungling
Body_Text:
Lagos — IN the wake of his recent bitter experience with Nigeria's sports
administrators, Englishman Bryan Robson had scathing albeit profound words
on the country. Robson, the ex-Manchester United star player had virtually
been appointed coach of Nigeria's senior soccer squad ahead of the African
Nations Cup championship next month before Sports Minister, Musa Mohammed
truncated the deal at the eleventh hour.
In the words of the "nearly" Eagles coach, until Nigeria sheds its
"Third World mentality," it will almost be impossible for it to actualise
its potentials and win a major soccer laurel.
The Englishman said his comments were not borne out of
bitterness. Maybe. He will, of course, not be saying the whole truth if he
said he was not bitter and disappointed. But even at that, any such
bitterness in him, which will be quite natural, hardly nullifies the
profundity of his observations. If it takes bitterness to say the haunting
truth about a people's legendary culture of tardiness, so be it. All it
needs to confirm the penetrating import of Robson's comment is to lift it
beyond the confines of sports. If that is done and his views still hold
relevance, then the issue of whether he spoke out of bitterness becomes
substantially immaterial.
In more ways than one, Nigeria as unably personified by its consistent
leadership and governments seem determined to cling on to haphazard and
incompetent ways. These are what Robson classified as Third World
mentality. The manifestations of this mentality pervades the country's
administrative landscape.
In its sundry dimensions, the Third World mentality finds expression in
planlessness, corrosive viciousness, myopia, primitive accumulation,
inferiority complex, lack of self worth, raw power exhibition and an
incapacitating disdain for research and scientific methods. Of course, there
are various other forms. For the purpose of this discussion here, we remain
limited to the realms of sports, soccer to be particular.
In less than one month, the African Nations Cup tournament will kick off
in Tunisia. Robson noted that all the top and best hotels in Tunis had been
reserved by other nations where teams will be at the championship. As usual
Nigeria's sports administrators (a misnomer of a tag, really) are yet to
concern themselves with such details. Eventually, the Nigerian team will be
settled in some inauspicious, dingy location; maybe in the middle of a red
light district in Tunis. Still, Nigerians will expect their team to
concentrate and contend for the trophy. Indeed, the setting is nothing
new. It has become the way of the country. In the immediate past edition of
the same championship in Mali, such other contending teams as Cameroon,
Senegal, South Africa etc were quartered by their respective sports
administrators in very secluded and conducive locations in the host
city. Not so with Nigeria's team. They were quartered in the middle of a
burstling market area, the equivalent of Oyingbo in Lagos. Such is the
attitude Robson was referring to. It hardly makes for
success. Instructively, it still takes a white coach or manager to get our
sports administrators to walk away from such a debasing, miserable
disposition. Talk of a Third World mentality!
There are, of course, the matters of appropriate feeding arrangement and
tuning up matches for the team, as well as the critical task of intelligence
on their opponents' programme. None of these obviously constitutes the
present concern of Nigeria's sports administrators. Yet you can bet that at
the appropriate time a loaded plane of federal government delegation
featuring representatives from the presidency, ministers, governors, their
wives, their concubines and sundry ministry officials and party thugs,
otherwise called stalwarts will head to Tunis, there to ginger the maligned
soccer squad to win their matches. That is the way of Nigeria, a classical
Third World attitude.
As it concerns the hiring or non-hiring of Robson, the real issue is not
that he was not eventually hired. Of course, it was hopeless to set about
scouting for and engaging a coach for a team barely two months to a major
tournament. But where was the sports minister all the while that the
leadership of the Nigeria Football Association (NFA) was negotiating the
Robson deal? Could the minister and his ministry not have moved earlier to
save the country, the NFA and all the international embarrassment of
quashing the Robson deal very late along the line? Obviously, it is the
power wielding thing, the ultimate manifestation of Third World
mentality.
Then, there is the lack of self worth or inferiority complex
dimension. In the thick of the English Premiership League at the moment
stars a young man whose name is Shola Ameobi. He plays for Newcastle
Football Club. As his name goes, Ameobi could be a Nigerian, one way or
another. It is not clear here where he was born and who his parents
are. Shola Ameobi does score goals for his club. He is a hardworking player,
but not one with any exquisite talent, the type national and team coaches go
all to get. Ameobi has been waiting for a call-up to the English national
team. Well, the call has not come. The coaches of the English national side
obviously rate him lower than what they need.
The reports in the media recently are that Shola Ameobi will be willing
to play for Nigeria. Mark that! If the wait for England fails, Nigeria can
be managed. Going by the inferiority complex that often comes to play in
Nigeria's official acts when relating with things foreign, coach Christian
Chukwu's bloated list may soon accommodate Shola Ameobi. If that development
occurs, it will only be following a pattern. It will even be far better than
the case of John Fashanu. Fashanu if you can recall, rejected calls to play
for Nigeria. He too was waiting for an England call up. John was even far
less talented than Shola Ameobi. But he had brawn. Then he was playing for
Wimbledon. Two Nigerians at heart, John Chiedozie and Tunji Banjo did answer
the call of the Nigerian national team from their base in England. In later
years another British Nigerian, Reuben Agboola followed suite and played for
the Eagles. England did call up Fashanu, but it was not a remarkable career
for him thence. But at least he actualised his dream of playing for
England.
Some years after his playing career, what did Nigerians get? First, John
Fashanu was made what they called a sports ambassador for Nigeria. Then he
was hurled into the NFA as member. Oh yes, it does get that pitiable in
Nigeria. That is the Third World mentality that Bryan Robson talked
about. It is most unlikely that any character with Fashanu's profile would
have been made ambassador of any variety, not the list in sports in say,
Cameroon or for that matter in any society with self worth. But then, this
is Nigeria. And this is why the country will never get anywhere until it
sheds its Third World mentality that does pervade its existence in numerous
forms. The pathetic thing about Nigeria is that people who watch while
cocoyam is being sown often wake the next day to expect and pray that the
yield will be a healthy yam. That sure goes beyond Third World mentality. It
is inane.

Original_Search_Title: Governor Ibrahim Idris, Please Save Koton-Karfe From Oil Thieves
Headline: Nigeria: Governor Ibrahim Idris, Please Save Koton-Karfe From Oil Thieves
Body_Text:
For nearly four years now, oil thieves and bunkerers have been operating
at Okpaka and Jamata villages both near Koton-Karfe town, bunkering oil
(NNPC) installations in these areas. It is estimated that an average of
20,000 litres of petrol and diesel are being carted away every night.
What bothers one is the consequence of oil spillage into our rivers and
fire disaster that may occur. Fire disasters with devastating extensive
consequences that can gut the entire Koton-Karfe and Gegu-Beki communities
not to talk of over 10 human settlements along the pipelines. Also about 7
petrol stations were built on the pipelines. Remember the Jesse fire inferno
where 1,000 people were burnt to death. Charred human bodies littered
everywhere like bunch of yams. In recent times there has been increases of
fire incidences at homes as a result of petrol stock at homes as transit
points.
The impunity with which the vandals operate opens the way to doubts s to
whether any effort is being made to apprehend them. This, in spite of armed
policemen on guard there.
There is active evidence that the police in Koton-Karfe and Lokoja (HQS)
are fully collaborators in these crimes. In the past vigilante groups make
arrests but promptly released as soon as they get to the police HQS at
Lokoja.
A community-based organisation, Club '98 has severally written and
visited NNPC Abaji Pumping Station and the police on these crimes but no
action. The club further organised public awareness campaign on the dangers
of these acts and attendant effect on the communities. But the illegal
trade, exploration on the "national cake" continued to wax stronger.
Furthermore, considering the dangers of oil disasters we expect NNPC to
have a fallback plan to contain emergencies such as these theft and where it
is carried out. Is it not possible that the vandals are operating as a
syndicate with the collusion of police and NNPC guards as insider? The Crime
is on the increase because there is no deterrence.
The only saving grace now is the Governor of Kogi State, Alhaji Ibrahim
Idris, appealing to take drastic steps with the federal authorities to deal,
arrest all those found. The rate at which these thieves are brandishing
their cars with stolen oil money, embarrassing, harassing and brutalising
everyone are common traits now. The Governor will be doing good by saving
innocent lives in Koton-Karfe and Gegu-Beki (where over 80 per cent of these
criminals are) from incidence associated with oil bunkering. As the govenor
used to say, that Koton-Karfe is his second home therefore please do
something to prevent the place from burning down from the activities of
shrouded criminals. The traditional rulers, elders could not exact their
orders anymore
Salisu Mamman Isa, Kogi Local Government Secretariat, Koton-Karfe,
Kogi State.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Maintains Upward Trend
Headline: Nigeria: Inflation Maintains Upward Trend
Body_Text:
Prices of goods and services as measured by the Consumer Price Index
(CPI) maintained an upward trend in the month of October rising by 0.8 per
cent to 114.4 from 113.4 in September.
The Consumer Price Index (CPI) report, just released by the Federal
Office Statistics (FOS) revealed that the rise in the index was occasioned
by increase in prices of alcoholic beverages, clothing materials,
maintenance repair, gas and firewood, transport expenses, books and
stationary.
The report said that the urban index fell by 0.9 per cent while the
rural index rose by 1.9 per cent when compared with September 2003
figures.
FOS stated that the average consumer price level went up by 23.6 per
cent in October, 2003 which is higher than 18.4 per cent observed in
September when making a similar comparison. It also noted that the urban and
rural indices increased by 25.0 and 22.9 per cent respectively during this
period.
FOS reported that the percentage change in the average CPI for the
twelve months period ending October, 2003 over the average of the CPI for
the previous twelve months was 12.3. This according to the report means that
a fixed market price of consumer goods and services purchased with N100.00
during the twelve months period ending October, 2002 sold for N112.30 on the
average for the corresponding period ending October, 2003.
The report also indicated a rise when compared to 10.7 per cent obtained
by making a similar comparison in September, 2003.
Invariably the Composite Food Index (CFI) took the downward trend by 0.2
per cent in October, 2003.The decline, FOS remarked was due to the decreases
in the prices of maize, grains, oils and fat, potatoes, and yams tuber.
The level of the CPI was higher than the corresponding level a year ago
by 13.2 per cent, it stressed that the average annual rate of increase in
the index was 5.0 per cent for the twelve months period ending October,
2003. In the all item less farm produce index which excludes the prices of
agricultural produce rose by 1.7 per cent in October, 2003 when compared
with September, 2003 figure.
Continuing, FOS said that in the twelve months to October, 2003, the
index rose by 27.8 per cent, while the average annual rate of increase of
the index was put at 17.4 per cent for the twelve months period to October,
2003 .

Original_Search_Title: Niger Indigenes Ask IBB to Run in 2007
Headline: Nigeria: Niger Indigenes Ask IBB to Run in 2007
Body_Text:
Lagos — Clamour for former President Ibrahim Babangida to return to active
politics has received a boost with eminent indigenes of Niger State calling
on him to consider running for president in 2007.
Over 100 of the elders drawn from all 42 local government areas of the
state made the appeal during a visit to Gen. Banbangida's Hilltop Villa in
Minna Thursday, December 4, 2003.
But Babangida, short of giving his consent, said the visit and the
appeal had "strengthened my resolve to be a participant in the process of
nation building. I will not disappoint you."
In a terse response, the retired general added: "Somebody must have a
base. Niger is my state; it is my home. Unless I have the support base in my
home, any other thing that I wish to do may be eroded a little. I cannot
just say that I don't recognise the importance of Nigeria. If I want to do
anything at all, I now have the support of my people."
Led by Alhaji Mohammed Egba Enagi, Commissioner for Revenue Mobilisation
and Fiscal Commission and Chairman of the Elders Forum, the indigenes told
Babangida that the socio-economic conditions in the country had declined to
unbearable level, hence their decision to ask him to run.
Alhaji Enagi said: "At the moment we are not happy with the situation in
the country. What we have come to do is to ask you to come away from the
sideline and come to the center of the Nigerian political situation and do
something to save the people because they are suffering."
He decried the steep rise in the prices of petroleum products and
government's inability to pay pensioners, including himself. "As Obasanjo
himself did admit, Nigerians are suffering. I am now having to pay more than
twice what I used to pay for fuel three months ago. My pension hasn't
increased. See the number of people on the streets waiting for their
pensions. Soldiers who fought to defend this country not being paid their
pensions.
He spoke of Minna farmers bringing their produce to the market without
people to buy from them "because there is no money in circulation. Those
yams are going rotten. The backbone of every economy in this world is
agriculture. Europe subsidises agriculture. America subsidises
agriculture. Whom are we responding to?"
"The suffering and complications now are that we have to look for
somebody who can handle the situation very, very well," Enagi said.
Senator Idris Kuta, a member of the Elders Forum who was also at
Babangida's residence for the occasion, said he was fully in support of the
former military president's contesting for the highest office on the land
come 2007. He pointedly asked President Olusegun Obasanjo to back
Babangida.
His words: "With the loyalty and support Babangida has given to
Obasanjo, I believe Obasanjo would be the main person that would dictate who
becomes the president of Nigeria in 2007. And anybody that is not loyal to
him may not get his support. I believe IBB has given Obasanjo the support he
deserves and desires, and Obasanjo should reciprocate by supporting
him".
Citing Babangida's records in road dualisation, rural development
through the defunct DFRRI and petroleum products distribution, Senator Kuta
said, the general who "stepped aside" in 1993 was the only Nigerian leader
who "really touched the lives of Nigerians."
Prominent Niger indigenes in the delegation were Sir. T. Y. Madaki,
former military governor of Katsina State and commander of Brigade of
Guards, General Fidelis Makka, former military governor of Benue State,
Group Captain Peter Gana (rtd) former Managing Director of the now bankrupt
Nigeria Airways, Ambassador J.T. Kolo, traditional rulers, businessmen and
women leaders.
Others were the former DIG of Police senator Nuhu Aliyu, Alhaji Bawa
Rijau, Alhaji. Alhassan Kpaki, Alhaji. Liman Doko, Alhaji. Akuye, Professor
Salka and Hajia Ramatu.

Original_Search_Title: Queen Meets Villagers in Karu, Visits BBC Project
Headline: Nigeria: Queen Meets Villagers in Karu, Visits BBC Project
Body_Text:
Abuja — Her Majesty, Queen Elizabeth II, yesterday got the feel of a typical
African village when she paid a visit to Karu Village, a local government in
Nassarawa State.
The village setting at the Local Government Headquarters was also the
site for a BBC project titled "Voices" which is a three year national public
education project made by Nigerians, in Nigeria for Nigerians in English,
Hausa, Ibo and Yoruba languages."
The Queen who arrived Karu at 10.30 am in a white Range Rover with
diplomatic registration number plates was received by Nasarawa State
Governor Abdullahi Adamu.
He was also joined to receive the Queen at Karu by other Governors,
amongst whom are George Akume and Bukola Saraki of Benue and Kwara States
respectively. Mrs. Folashade Randle, who as a four-year old Folashade Lawson
presented the Queen with flowers in 1956 during her first trip to Nigeria,
was also there to put four year old Hania Phillip Adadu through the same
steps.
Several cultural troupes including Alago from Doma Local Government,
Nassarawa State, and Jarawa from Plateau State and Swange from Benue State
entertained the British Monarch.
The Queen led by Governor Adamu walked around the village meeting locals
from Karu environs, displaying their wares including tomatoes, pepper and
yams.
Adamu in a speech at the occasion said the Commonwealth has never lost
its relevance in contemporary issues. He said the Commonwealth is becoming
stronger and continues to be a loud voice in the world.

Original_Search_Title: Queen Elizabeth Arrives Ahead of Chogm
Headline: Nigeria: Queen Elizabeth Arrives Ahead of Chogm
Body_Text:
Abuja — QUEEN Elizabeth II of England arrived in Abuja yesterday, two days ahead of the Commonwealth summit, on her first visit since Nigeria's independence.President Olusegun Obasanjo met the Queen at the Nnamdi Azikiwe International Airport on the first day of a state visit which will include tomorrow's ceremony to mark the opening of the Commonwealth Heads of Goverment Meeting (CHOGM).
Descending from a British Airways jet into stifling 40 degree heat in a simple white, knee-length dress and matching hat, the Queen chatted warmly with Obasanjo, himself clad in agbada. She inspected a Nigerian Army detachment to the sounds of a 21-gun artillery salute, before setting off in a motorcade of black Mercedes for the half-hour drive into Abuja.
She was due to make a speech at a banquet thrown in her hon-
our last night by Obasanjo, who has had a 1,500-seat hall constructed specially to welcome his royal visitor, back in Nigeria for the first time since 1956.
"The Queen is delighted to be returning to a democratic Nigeria, and she's going to have particular pleasure to come out as head of the Commonwealth," her spokeswoman, Penny Russell-Smith, told reporters Tuesday. "Her main aim is to ensure that she meets as many of the Nigerian people as she can, within the time she is here," she added.
Some 47 years have passed since the Queen last came to Nigeria, during which time it has suffered through civil war, military dictatorship and corrupt rule, still to emerge in the 21st century as the world's biggest black nation.
With Nigerian and British security forces on alert for a possible terror attack, the Queen's visit will be limited to Abuja.
"We're not so much worried about the threat from within Nigeria, but what might come from outside," said a western diplomat, referring to the threat posed by radical Islamist movements such as Osama bin Laden's al-Qaeda network. Today, however, the Duke of Edinburgh will make a day trip to Lagos to visit old friends at the Lekki Conservation Centre and inspect a British-funded project for HIV/AIDS sufferers.
At the same time, the Queen will visit the village of New Karu, just half-an-hour outside Abuja, to visit the set of a new BBC radio soap opera built to look like an African village market.
This visit has raised some eyebrows in Nigeria, and attracted amused commentary in the British press, as it was initially promoted as Her Majesty's opportunity to meet "real Nigerians" in a typical setting.
In fact, the market is a film set and yesterday the "villagers" were practising their lines for a carefully choreographed walk-about in which she will see BBC employees pounding yams and staffing pumpkin-seed stalls.
"This was seen as a good compromise at the time," said British High Commission spokesman, Paul Edwards, ackowledging that some would be disappointed that the Queen would not be able to establish more personal contacts.
Aside from New Karu's new radio studio and mock market, Her Majesty will also attend two reciprocal state dinners, and address the 52 arriving leaders of the Commonwealth member states, mainly former British colonies.
The Commonwealth summit will open tomorrow and carry through until Monday.
Nigeria Police Force says it has drafted tens of thousands of officers into the capital from surrounding states to protect both the state visit and CHOGM, as the world nervously awaits its next terror attack.
They will be equipped with their standard issue assault rifles and tear gas grenades, as well as 10,000 vehicles and 1,000 horses "for crowd control and to add colour to the ceremonies," said police spokesman, Mr. Chris Olakpe.
The royal couple are due to leave Nigeria on Saturday.

Original_Search_Title: Aso Rock Capital of Corruption
Headline: Nigeria: Aso Rock Capital of Corruption
Body_Text:
Chief Gani Fawehinmi, the stormy petrel of Nigeria's legal and
political systems, speaks on the state of the nation
On National Conscience Party the last elections and his future I have
been leading the party since inception as a human rights group in 1994. It
metamorphosed into a political party in September that year and I have been
leading it since then. Now, I think the time is ripe for me to hand over to
the younger generation. I have been thinking of that very seriously, and
very soon I will make up my mind on it.  Maybe the party needs a new
strategy to be more relevant to 2007. I will make up my mind before the end
of December. 2007 is very important and if the party is going to participate
meaningfully, I think it is time for me to hands off.
You will recall our long drawn battle for registration. INEC first
called us to discuss the guidelines for registration. We challenged those
guidelines in court. In June while the matter was still pending in court,
they decided to recognise some parties and excluded the NCP. In July we won
the case, but they went to the Supreme Court and also lost there in
November. We were registered in December and they put some fresh hurdles
asking us to pay some money. They knew we would not be able to pay. So
another battle started afresh, since the money was running to over N350
million. The battle was still on until 6 February when judgement was
delivered and we won. Really we contested under difficult situations. We had
to revive the machinery of the party. So, reviving in March for an election
in April was difficult. We were far behind financially, logistically and
otherwise. So partly we achieved that result because they did not want us to
participate from the beginning.
Secondly, we all knew all that happened. There was no election. The
government of the day used money and unconstitutional means to make people
vote against their conscience. Even after voting, they still manipulated the
results. Let me give you one example. On their website, I was recorded to
have scored only eight votes in the whole of Ogun State. In one ward in
Ipokia alone in the same state, the document given to us by INEC recorded
786 votes for me. Yet in the whole of the state, they recorded eight votes
for me. But they made it round for everybody.
The candidate for Peoples Mandate Party, Arthur Nwankwo, was also given
