eight votes, so also was Balarabe Musa, candidate of People's Redemption
Party. When officers of the European Union visited me here, it was then it
struck me that may be they were just recognising the radicals from the three
geo-political zones by the eight votes they allocated to each of us! In the
same Ogun State, 1.5 million people registered to vote.
Both the presidential and governorship elections took place the same
date. Gbenga Daniels, who got elected as governor got 411,000 votes.
Obasanjo got 1.3 million votes. They belong to the same party. So, where
did the balance of his votes come from? He got less than that in Lagos
State. You will then see that the election was fake and fraudulent.
Again, they took advantage of the fact that we are poor. We did not have
polling agents to man the polls for us. To man Lagos alone, we would need
close to N20 million, giving N100 to each agent. I financed my own
campaign. Each NCP candidate financed his or her own campaign. There was no
money coming from anywhere until after the polls when INEC decided to
distribute three million naira to each party. Now when you compare this with
Mr. President who got contribution of N5.6 billion with about N2 billion
coming from the corporate world, some came from the construction industry
and even from abroad , These are contrary to the electoral act, under which
you are not supposed to spend more than N20 on each voter.
They flouted the law and we were hamstrung because we were poor.
Besides, many things happened between the polling booths and the
collating centres. In some voting centres in Lagos, we were leading, but by
the time the results came out, we were scoring zero.
For 2007 if we really want to be relevant and to prevent a re-occurrence
of the fraud, NCP has to look for money. In looking for money, we must be
careful because I have fought so many battles against corruption in this
country. I do not think it will be morally right to ask my party to start
looking for money, because that is contrary to my convictions, and because I
may not even know where to get money. However, if the party must survive in
2007 and be free to collect money from people who may want to subscribe to
its fortunes in the future, I think it is better I resign as its leader. I
have fought many battles and stepped on so many toes, that I think it will
not be within my moral position to start collecting money from people whose
position I cannot vouch for. I think the time has come for me to resume my
one-man squad rather than leading a political party.
On The Congress of Political Parties
There has never been an alliance between NCP and any other party. When
the battle for registration started, we acted jointly and rejected the so
called guidelines. That was the beginning of the platform that became
Congress of Nigerian Political Parties, CNPP. You wouldn't expect an
alliance between the NCP and AD or ANPP. No, and for obvious reasons.
The only party that got very close to NCP was the PRP which has fought
so many ideological battles as well. There was no basis for an alliance
between NCP and those parties. CNPP was just a platform to fight against
obnoxious guildelines and not an alliance. When the elections were held and
we knew they were rigged, we just decided that CNPP should continue the
fight against the fraud. There could not have been an alliance without
compromise and contribution from the parties. The NCP is distinct from the
others. It is an ideological party and we intend to keep it so.
On Privatisation
There are certain things that ought to be done which are not being
done.
And things that we don't want are now being done. For instance the
privatization agenda. We don't want it. It is against the constitution of
the country particularly as it is being done. The constitution declares that
the economic sector shall be operated and managed by the federal government
and if it is going to be otherwise, it must be passed by the National
Assembly. Today everything has been sold in the name of
privatisation. Everything is being reversed in the oil sector.
Our heritage is being sold. Our lives are being sold. Our moral standing
and future are being sold. Everything is being sold. We have lost our
dignity.
If the basis for selling all these is because they are being badly
managed and administered, then I say the first place or thing to be
privatised is Aso Rock, because that is the seat of corruption. That is the
seat of incompetence. That is the seat of maladministration.  Next, sell the
police and the army. You are not doing these, but what our leaders have
laboured for are what you are selling. Obasanjo has disgraced and debased
us. All over the world now, Nigerians don't count for anything.
The National Carrier There is no National carrier. All these airlines
and companies are just treating us the way they want. They humiliate us in
their planes and give us all sorts of sub-standard food and so on because we
don't have a national carrier. Ghana has one. Cameroon and others also
have. America, France, England all have. You may say that substantial parts
of their assets and operation have been privatized. But they have National
carriers.
If you debase the past of the country and humiliate the present, then
where is the hope for the future? The National Conscience Party knows that
privatization is an evil agenda and when we come to power, we shall reverse
those decisions. Those who have bought those things are just wasting their
time.
The Refineries and fuel prices They even said they are selling our
refineries. We have four of them. If they are producing at optimal level,
they have capacity for 750,000 barrels per day. At the moment, they cannot
refine up to 120,000 barrels per day because Obasanjo wants it that
way. They destroyed Kaduna refinery and gave the job to one of their cohorts
and running dogs of the PDP. The day it was to be test-run it caught
fire. So nobody knows whether or not any money was expended on it.
They just don't want to build new refineries so that we can continue to
suffer. Obasanjo in five years has not laid the foundation of one refinery,
for obvious reasons. He wants to be importing so that they can continue to
make money through foreign exchange, while Nigerians suffer.
Liberia has a refinery. Who built it there? They take our crude oil to
that place to refine and when they bring it back to Nigeria they say it is
imported. This is not acceptable.
The volume of corruption being perpetrated in Aso Rock is crying to high
heavens for revenge. If democracy will not bring that revenge, God will
intervene to bring it about. And they will pay for it effectively.
Nobody should dispute or show anger when God intervenes. I doubt whether
this democracy can bring the reforms. I doubt if this democracy will last. I
doubt whether it is not collapsing. I doubt whether it is not moribound
now.
It is clear that their agenda is to punish the Nigerian people. That is
ungodly. The federal government has not laid the foundation of a single
refinery in the past four and half years. Instead, they keep juggling the
figures and asking us to pay more for the fuel you have imported, when the
facilities here are not utilized and they have not even created avenues for
better opportunities for us to refine our fuel at home. That is not what
obtains in other places.
Take Iran as example. Iran has nine refineries. Its allocation from OPEC
is about 4.8 million barrels per day. It refines 2.6 million barrels
everyday to satisfy its home consumption and export. The same thing for
America. It imports 60 per cent of her own needs and produces 40 per cent at
home. In the last 56 years, the price of one litre of petrol in USA has
never reached the unit of their currecy which is one dollar. In fact, it has
not reached 60 cents. Today there are two grades of petrol in America. The
premium grade is 30 cents per litre while the low grade is forty cents per
litre, and that also includes tax. Conversely, in Nigeria we started with
145,000 barrels in 1956, today it is over three million barrels per
day. Between 1956 and 1978 we had fuel stability for eight years at eight
kobo per litre. It was the same Obasanjo who, in 1978, told Nigerians that
they were enjoying too much of oil. He increased it by 60 per cent. It went
to 15 kobo. Between 1966 and 93, a period of 27 years, the price of fuel per
litre did not reach one naira. Shagari increased it to 20 kobo. Buhari did
not increase it.
Babangida increased it to 39 kobo, then it went to the dual regime of 42
kobo and 60 kobo. By the time he was leaving in 1993, he left it at 70
kobo.
Now between 1993 and 2003, we are at 40,45,50 or even 60 naira depending
on where you are buying. And they are tossing us here and there. Move from
Lagos to Abeokuta, it is different. Move from there to Edo or Kaduna. It is
different_e. Ah ah in the same country? One government, one oil? Tell me,
why is Obasanjo treating us like this?  He increased it in 2000. Then in
2001 he did not even address the nation or say happy new year. We just went
to buy fuel and saw that he had increased it.
What type of government is this? Look at his arrogance, obstinacy and
his crude behaviour. Look at the unpresidential attitude of a man who told
NLC he would crush them, the way he crushed ASUU. This is a president who
was asking journalists 'who are the masses?' I think the earlier the man is
flushed out of this country's leadership the better for this country, by all
means.
This man is a danger to our progress. He is an impediment to our
vision.
And a disaster to our psyche. In any shape or form, Obasanjo has to go
if this country must move forward.
On Corruption
He has institutionalised corruption in Nigeria. Look at the people he
just appointed into government.
Look at Kupolokun. The report of his corruption was everywhere in
1993.
He was charged and flushed out of the NNPC for corruption and
mismanagement. You now brought him back to come and run or ruin NNPC.
All these must not continue. What type of anti corruption war are they
waging? A minister of the Federal Republic went to the National Assembly and
said two senators demanded a bribe of N54 million from him.
None of them was dragged to court. The same thing for the airways
probe.
Many directors were accused of corruption. They were not investigated or
brought to court. Nothing has been done so far. The corruption in the land
today is more than how it was under Abacha. Yet nobody is talking about
it. Where is Justice Mustapha Akanbi? Let him tell us now about the sanctity
of his commission. All they can parade are the small guys in his commission,
when the truly corrupt people are walking away free.
Let me tell you two things that were done abroad recently. On 13
November, the former directors and chairman of Elf oil were probed, charged
and convicted for using 430 million dollars to bribe African leaders. This
happened in France, because it was against the laws of their land and
because they are sincere about fighting corruption.  Look at Enron in
America. Where are the examples in Nigeria? You can steal the soul of
Nigeria and get away with it as long as you are close to Obasanjo and
continue to play the sychophant. If you are not close to him, steal one kobo
and you will be hunted.
This Obasanjo is a disaster. His regime is the most corrupt. Look at
COJA. What is known is just a tip of the iceberg of the corruption in
COJA. Unless we have a government that will stretch out this regime, we will
never know the level of corruption in this regime. I wonder what is
wrong. This country is not moving a step forward under Obasanjo. Except for
the propaganda we hear on privatisation, nothing is happening.
Education
Nine million students left primary school last year. Only 120,000 could
be absorbed into institutions of higher learning. The rest will be running
helter skelter all over the country. We will continue to have high rate of
robbery, and crime of the highest order as long as employment is not
generated in this country. This is a regime that caters only for
itself. This democracy is a government by the government, for the
government. This is a shame on all of us. It is a shame for a president who
does not care about breaches of the constitution.
Local government election The moment they finished rigging their own
election, they said 'oh let us cover our tracks so that they will not know
how we rigged elections.' So they decided not to conduct the local
government election. They would not have been able to rig at the local
government level. So they set up a technical committee and started
appointing caretakers to run the local governments. It is against section
seven of the constitution. That is the only place where the word
"guaranteed" is mentioned in the entire constitution.  Even your human
rights are not guaranteed by the constitution. The presidential system is
not guaranteed. But about local government it says "a system of
democratically elected government is hereby guaranteed". That is what
Obasanjo has now destroyed.
Last week they paid N30 billion to local government run by illegal and
unconstitutional caretaker committees. That type of money being paid to an
incompetent and illegal committee! How can you be giving the money of the
people to an unelected body? That is enough for this man to leave office. He
has breached the foundamental laws of the country. But who will remove him?
Will Mantu and Wabara have the courage to remove him?
Wabara was not elected. Mantu was not. We knew how they juggled the
whole thing. If they see Obasanjo carrying 'Ghana must go' they will turn
the other eye, because all of them are carrying 'Ghana must go,' in a way
that is so obscene and sad.
Value of the naira.
They are destroying the economy. The pride of a nation is its
currency.
Destroy the currency and you destroy the pride of the nation. I am not
surprised why small businesses are collapsing. They will continue to
collapse in this situation. Obasanjo came to power with N82 to a
dollar.
Today, it is N154 to one dollar. He came to power with N122 to a
pound.
Today, it is N222 to one pound. What do you want to bring from abroad
that will not be expensive?
Can you imagine a country making more than 50 million dollars every day
from a single product? And yet the more the country makes the poorer it
becomes and the weaker the value of its currency. Something is wrong.
One, corruption is doing well in the country. Even under Abacha things
were not this bad. Obasanjo cannot organise anything because he is not an
organizer. Obasanjo is a simple brute. He makes his way through what I will
call insane behaviour, bribery and all sorts of unwholesome behaviour.
Now when you look at the currency and its free fall, you will see that
it is neither capitalist nor socialist. We deceive ourselves if we think we
have a capitalist economy. Between 2001 and 2002, the American Federal
Reserves Bank intervened twelve times to reduce the cost of funds. Today in
America the bank rate is just one per cent.  That broadens the economic
horizon for proper development. In America, the Americans are participants
in the economic development of their country.
It is not so in Nigeria. Here Nigerians are observers, not
participants.
I am just looking at Okonjo Iweala, all these professors and people
talking about economic growth. They are talking nonsense, bullshit.
You must know where you are going before you bring in economists and
other experts. As a leader you must have a vision of what you want before
you bring in experts to help you achieve them. Not that you ask experts to
formulate a philosophy and vision for you as a president.
This is madness. I don't even know what is happeaning today. It is a mad
house. The European Union is also intervening in the banking rate. And when
a company is collapsing, the government of that country comes to the aid of
the company. Germany has done that.  America also did it after 9/11. The
same America subsidises agriculture by 10 billion dollars every year. Here,
we allow Nigerians to suffer. We don't have any regard for the home
industry.  So the cost of fund is so high. But the World Bank cronnies like
Okonjo-Iweala and all those characters will tell you the economy is
growing. Let them learn from Lee Kuan Yew and his economic miracles.
Your people must be the greatest investors, not foreigners. You must
make the atmosphere conducive rather than travelling all over the place that
you are looking for investors. For 165 times now, Obasanjo has stepped out
of the country to look for investors. He goes all over the place and says he
is developing Nigeria. He does not know how people are suffering in Ogun
State, because he doesn't visit. But he can tell you every dot on the map of
Japan. He can tell you every road junction on the map of Germany. He can
tell you where the highways in New York are.
Shouldn't this man understand one thing, that charity begins at home?
He must learn from Henry Ford, an American and automobile engineer.  The
greatest investors are your people. Must he be looking for foreign investors
so that they can give him a cut abroad while we suffer at home? This man
says he is developing the country, but he doesn't know how people eat or pay
the school fees of their children.
This man is just jumping up and down and wasting time, to the extent
that even his wife says she has a pet project. When you look at what that
woman is doing, you will know that it is an illegality and an abomination to
democracy. Launching this and that everywhere and you don't even know where
the money is coming from. She said people are donating. Who are those
people? Is it those who are taking money from the government contractors_e
there is no doubt about it. They are the people donating to the so called
charity organization that the woman is flaunting about. Where are the
programmes of Maryam Babangida?  Where are those of Maryam Abacha? Those of
Stella will also die immediately her husband leaves office. You are using
your office to get public money from contractors and you say it is a
charitable act.  There is nothing charitable about that.
Who knows this woman in the constitution? This woman does not exist as
far as Nigerian constitution is concerned. There is no position for the
first lady. I have read the constitution, I have read every corner and every
full stop and there is no word about first lady. No function is given to
Mrs. Obasanjo as far as the constitution is concerned. So for her to be
launching all these things and be pretending to us that she is taking money
from donors! Who are the donors? I think the lady should be called to
order. Now, when I revert to my one-man crusade to defend the Nigerian
people! I think I would soon start because I am being inhibited by the fact
that I'm in a political party and there are certain things I would love to
do by going to court and doing many things, but because I'm in a party I
will have to refer to them before doing it.
Funding of Education
When you look at the neglect of the Nigerian society by the regime,
education, after food is very important.  Without it, no nation can
develop. Why I first of all mentioned food is because of the importance of
food, because without food, nobody could study. Between 1965-1990, Germany
has been devoting 24 per cent of her fiscal allocation to education. Japan
has been devoting 30 per cent to education. Canada has been devoting 31 per
cent to education.  That is why they are so highly technically
developed. Germany has no single oil. It has coal, but the coal is not as
large as what we have in Enugu. Japan is a clear example of a country that
has all the negative features, typhoon, earthquake, volcano and not a single
drop of oil. But Japan few years ago beat America by almost one trillion to
the second richest country in the world. Nigeria, you want to develop to be
a power? Please you must devote 20-26 per cent of your fiscal allocation to
education. Not only at the federal level but also at the state and local
government levels because we need close to N450 billion to turn education
around in this country. But Obasanjo has not shown seriousness. Last year,
he gave education 1.8 per cent. Can you compare that to the UNESCO standard
of 26 per cent?  And so ASUU, which Nigerian people did not quite understand
insisted on the standards. But Nigerians said oh let our people go to
school.  "Oh! Let them go to school". But ASUU is saying you are coming to
school to learn, but you are not learning. Look at the state of graduates in
Nigeria today. Can you imagine students doing courses on computer without
seeing computers? Can you teach them where you have more than 500 students
in one classroom with some peeping through the window?
On Health Look, my son had an accident here, he was in Igbobi, Igbobi
has no single spinal cord surgeon, no neuro-surgeon. Igbobi has no Magnetic
Ressonance Imaging, MIR, which would detect the type of injury from where
you go straight to do your operation. Igbobi has no single scanning
machine. Don't shake your head, it is not only in Igbobi, the whole of Lagos
has no MIR. Go and ask Dr. Arigbabu in LUTH. He is the oldest
neuro-surgeon. In the whole of Ibadan, no MIR, no single MIR.
They have only one neuro-surgeon. I can't remember the name now, but
he's also the provost. No MIR, which is the height of medicine to be able to
know where to operate but they don't have it. National Hospital in Abuja has
no functional MIR, the one they had broke down two years ago, and right now
it's not functioning. In the whole of this country, they have a semblance of
it in Maiduguri; I was able to know this because of the investigation
carried out. On this Mohammed thing, I am going to fight the health problem
of this country with my heart, with my soul and with everything I have. The
Nigerian government is not interested in our health. How much does it cost
to have an MIR? Between 675,000 and 700,000 pounds sterling. It is not up to
what one minister would steal under Obasanjo. It is not up to what Obasanjo
would spend in one second illegally. Yet the health of our people is being
seriously ignored. You go to Igbobi again, you have an accident, you would
buy your own bed sheet, your own pillow, and your water. In fact they just
leave you there to rot away. And that is the situation all over the
country. So what is the purpose of governance? What is the purpose of
government, if you don't look after the health of the people, the education,
the interest of the business people!
On Interest Rate and Business
Let me tell you interest rate for loan is between 25-30 per cent as of
today.
Who do you want to sell to after generating your own electricity?
Providing for your own water, you want to sell to a man, not just one
man, they are many like that, earning N5, 000 minimum wage. If you divide it
by 250, that is about 21 pounds per month. That is not what an English
person would get for four hours because minimum wage there is about
£4.5 pounds. So who do you want to sell to? To Nigerians who are
cash-strapped, who are earning 21 pounds per month. And maybe by next week
it would be 19 pounds per month because of depreciation.  When I came into
this country in 1965, my brother bought ram for three pounds.
Today, I have to buy ram for N20, 000. There is no excuse for that type
of depreciation because we are making money. There is no justification for
that. Nigeria is like a man who makes one million naira a month.
After taking the money he goes into an hotel, takes a suite for that
night, calls all the prostitutes and tells them to take whatever they want,
wine, champagne e.t.c. After spending all, maybe N250, 000 and they steal
another N250, 000 from him in his state of stupor and finally, he goes home
with only N5, 000. The children are waiting at home. Daddy! Daddy! We have
not seen you; we have not paid our school fees! The wife is crying,
everybody is crying and all he tells them is 'please, be patient with me,
bear with me.' Such a person deserves a divorce. Obasanjo deserves a removal
by the Nigerian people, this man has to go, he is a disaster and some of
these Yoruba that are saying he is our man, let me tell them they will bear
the consequences in future.
All Nigerians would say yes! You are loud mouth people, you talk too
much, you are the people agitating. When you put your man there for eight
years, what did he do? He failed the nation, corruption was at its highest,
poverty was at its highest, unemployment was at its highest, everything went
down the drain, education was comatose, health was bad.
On Yoruba's Refusal to Support Him.
But that was what he's trying to do. He is trying to buy the Yoruba
interest by saying I appoint this from Yoruba land, appoint that, but I
always tell people you may put all your relatives and tribesmen in office,
they cater for themselves, they don't cater for the masses.  When you go to
the market and meet a Yoruba woman who is selling garri and meet another one
who is selling yam and you meet another one who sells beans and you say
please, I am Yoruba, please help me out! The traders would tell you they
bought their commodities with money. You would get out of that place empty
handed. So poverty does not know Yoruba from Igbo.
The Niger Delta Question
We took Adaka Boro, arrested him and convicted him for treason. He was
only released when the civil war broke out because they wanted him to fight
for Nigeria. Nobody actually knows who killed him from behind, whether it
was the Nigerian army or the Biafran troops. Suddenly in April 1993, an
intellectual who even helped the federal government to bring back part of
the Niger Delta, Bonny province and the rest back to the Nigerian state
during the civil war was framed, Ken Saro-Wiwa. They lied against Ken. I
knew Ken, he had no single gun, he was always using branches of leaves as
demonstration tools with his people. He never used violence. But Nigeria
lied against him, tried him in the court of law, killed him, poured acid on
him. Why? Because he said the injustice was too much. They are hewers of
wood and drawers of water and they deserve a fair deal and the good things
of life.
In November 1999, Obasanjo, under the pretext that 13 soldiers were
missing, bombarded Odi and 2,750 were massacred. More than 1,000 houses were
destroyed including that of a man who served the federal government for 35
years. His entire pension was put on the house.  Each time people raise
their anger, their justifiable anger, we maltreat them, we kill them, we
slaughter them. That has to stop because their cause is right.
Our cause is wrong and what is wrong cannot defeat what is right, it can
only do so temporarily. So we have started with NDDC bill, OMPADEC
e.t.c. All these cannot work. What is the way out?
The Solution
The first thing is that, that is why I said that their problem is part
of the problem of Nigeria. We need a sovereign national conference.
Number one, let the people talk, let them express their problems, let
them proffer solutions. You can't dictate to them, create 10,000 more
commissions. They will continue to fail to meet the needs of the
people.
Not just a conference where government would set up committees or
conference of civil societies, no no no.
The solution to the conference is simple, use what you have to get what
you need. We have 774 LGAs. Many of them are from the Delta. Let each local
community represent one delegate to the conference not on political party
basis. Anybody from Niger Delta would be representing Niger Delta. Now there
are those organisations and groups who would not contest election, you have
the Obas, Emirs, Obis, and Amayanabos e.t.c.
There are also in the Niger Delta, the professionals, the lawyers, the
journalists, accountants, organized labours and students. Also in the Niger
Delta, we have police, don't forget that Adaka Boro was a policeman. He was
a student leader at the University of Nigeria.
Let police, Army, Airforce and Navy bring their representatives, let
Nigeria be a beehive of discussion in a properly organised sovereign
national conference. It may be 5,000 or 10,000. Let us meet and discuss
anything under the sun. And this conference would determine the fate of
Nigeria and at the end of the day, the document would be given to Nigerian
people in a referendum and it would be the first time we will have a
referendum in Nigeria since the amalgamation in 1914.
If we want to adopt the Switzerland example or the American style, let
it be said in the constitution. I must tell you that I don't buy the system
of presidential system. Who is calling Obasanjo to order?  Nobody.
Imposing a presidential system like we have now and allowing Obasanjo to
be behaving like a mad man is not acceptable to this country. In a
parliamentary system of government, you see what is happening to Britain,
that is why Britain is able to be what it is, they discuss idea in the
parliament. They gave the Irish people power through the parliament. They
gave the Scottish people power and they have their own Assembly. So why
don't we give the Niger Delta their autonomy within the contest of Nigeria?
Are we operating a federalism? No. We have a unitary system in place now. So
we need a conference to reach this accord, but certainly not a unitary form
of government or a presidential, because it is alien to us. Today, National
Assembly cannot probe anybody but in a parliamentary system, the question
would be thrown! Man, can you tell us why you put Kupolokun, who was retired
in 1993 for fraud as the new Group Managing Director of NNPC?  The press
would be there, no gagging and nobody would come out with a stupid N1
billion suit and be talking nonsense in Aso Rock that he doesn't know
COJA. You go to the person and say how come Obasanjo is involved in COJA?
But nobody would say that in the National Assembly.  Nobody would say that
in the Senate. So please let us have a sovereign national conference. If
there is a conference, I will fight in Ondo to be a representative of my
area or come to Lagos and tell people put me there. I am going for economic
benefit. The SNC is not a tribal issue, not for tribal jingoist, it is for
lawful masses.
So, sovereign national conference will solve the Niger Delta
problem.
Revenue Allocation
Now to the peculiar issues of special treatment, I don't understand why
we are saying that federal government should take 46 per cent of revenue
allocation, 33 per cent for state, 20 per cent for local government. I would
stop at that, it is madness. The whole money comes from this Niger Delta and
as Abiola used to say, you can't shave my hair behind me. You can only do it
when I am there. People are bound to be angry all the time you are sharing
their money behind them and you don't even remember them. Okay, identify the
problem in the Niger Delta. You see I took time to study the situation when
I was a lead counsel for Ken. One, unemployment is too high. One out of ten
is unemployed, so it is a special area. Why don't you then say any group of
people who wants to come together, there are doctors that want to establish
a hospital, you can give all the doctors special incentive to establish in
the area. Do you remember that Yenogoa had just one petrol station few years
ago, where Nigeria is making money from?  Maybe now, they may have added
five or six to the number. So when you talk of the infrastructure, you can
ask the business men to come in and you provide the enabling
environment. So, instead of 46, 33, 20 per cent, maybe 40, 25, 15 and the
rest to the Delta for the next five years and ensure that it is properly
implemented and allocated to the state government in those areas.
So by the time you take that special interest and special allocation of
funds, then you galvanize the interest of the people and they become
participants in the economic revival of Niger Delta. They say the devil
finds job for the idle mind. But when most of their people are employed,
they go to school and can satisfy their educational aspiration, then the
whole crisis is curbed.
Insecurity in the country.
We would be making the biggest mistake if we think that by employing one
million policemen, the problem would be solved. Security is physical alone,
it is also social. In England and America, what they say is that they don't
want to drive the society into the precipice.  They pay social security to
keep body and soul together so that you won't think of any mischief or
havoc. That is also a function of security in this country today! God forbid
that you don't have any job to do, you are left alone.
The economic system we practice is everybody for himself and God for us
all. No work at the end of the day, you are securing the people, physically,
socially and economically. For instance, Pa Rewane was driving a Benz few
years before his death, he stretched out about N100 to buy
newspapers. Somebody just snatched the money at Maryland junction and said I
have not eaten and you are buying newspaper! And the man said don't worry,
he called the person selling bread and said please give him enough. If not
that Pa Rewane was tactful, there would have been an altercation that
day. But the point is that this man in question cannot comprehend why
someone would be talking of newspaper when he has not eaten, all the man
wanted was bread.
What led to the 1789 revolution in France. They went to the king and
said, "we have not eaten! We are hungry. The king said go and take cake!
We don't have bread and you are talking of cake. So it doesn't matter,
even if Tafa sends the whole policemen to Lagos, there would be no
security.
So the security cannot be provided by Tafa, he can only do his best
according to his ability. He is a lawyer, he knows the jurisprudence of
life, not only law. He should realize that this is beyond physical
security. People want to feed their children, they want good place to lay
their heads e.t.c.
Good quality of life is the whole essence of government. If the quality
is good, the attitude of the people towards the government would change for
the better.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture, Alternative Revenue Earner in Ebonyi
Headline: Nigeria: Agriculture, Alternative Revenue Earner in Ebonyi
Body_Text:
Lagos — In a State that lacks Federal Government presence in terms of
investment, Ebonyi State is poised to make agriculture its alternative
source of income. Before the creation of the state in 1996, it was a general
knowledge that it produced the 'Abakaliki Rice'.
Apart from rice production, the State, in the past was also known for
producing and exporting palm kernel, palm oil, yam, livestock and poultry
products.
Shortly on assumption of office, the Governor, Dr. Sam Egwu visited
Israel for the purpose of attracting technical assistance in agricultural
development and food production. That relationship led to the signing of
memorandum of understanding with the state government.
It is believed that the administration, focusing on agriculture is to
ensure that the state regains its status as the food basket of the
nation. This is being done through the rehabilitation of poultry production,
large-scale oil-palm plantation and rice production, as well as provision of
agricultural inputs for farmers to improve their individual capacities.
In a recent interviwed with THISDAY, the Commissioner for Information
and Youth Orientation in the State, Dr. John Otu explained that the governor
has slightly shifted focus from investing on education as was paramount
concern when he came to power in 1999, to the development of agriculture and
industrial base for the people.
He said that Egwu's greatest achievement in his first term as governor,
was the mental and psychological liberation he gave the people of Ebonyi. He
stressed that most of the hawkers that are found on the streets of Abuja and
Lagos are back home, maximising the free education policy of the
government.
The aggressive move embarked by the government into developing
industrial base for the state, Otu said, is to absorb the citizens on
graduation from various studies.
It is obvious that the use of fertilizer by farmers is relatively new in
Ebonyi State, therefore, he said there is the need to acquaint the people
with modern techniques of application. Infact, with the total completion of
the State Fertilizer Blending plant, which would be producing about 40 tones
per hour, farmers in the state and its environs would be buying it in large
quantities and at subsidised rate, since it is the only blending plant in
the whole of south-east. Apart from that, it is also expected to shore-up
the state's revenue base.
Interestingly, Otu revealed that government has set up an
inter-ministerial committee to see that the blending plant comes on stream
soon and subsequently plans for its privatisation. When operational, it is
also expected that it would employ about 2,000 workers.
The influx of people to Ebonyi State is also attracting more business
men, as a result, rice millers are under pressure of increasing production
to meet demand. It is in appreciation of this that the state government, the
Commissioner said, initiated various measures aimed at ensuring the
mechanisation and mass production of this staple food. Government, he said
is also revamping the rice mill in other to modernise it and make the
'Abakaliki Rice' a global brand.
Otu disclosed that Chinese investors have acquired 10,000 hectares of
land to complement the state's agrarian drive in three geographical zones of
the state for rice planting. "Because Ebonyi is noted for rice production,
we want to optimise our potentials in that regard. The state is going to be
pacesetter in that crop the coutry", the commissioner boasted.
It was not only in rice production that the government has thrown its
weight at. Its agricultural programme also embarked on the modernisation of
the Nkaliki poultry. The project is about the largest in West Africa with a
capacity to producing 16,800 day-old chicks and 8,000 broilers a week.
The poultry, contracted to Dizengof, an Israeli company, would not only
be a source of revenue and foreign exchange earning for the state, but also
a major avenue for the employment of requisite manpower and to meet the
challenges of protein production of the country.
Touching on almost everything Egwu's government has also embarked on,
what may turn out to be the biggest palm plantation in Nigeria. The
construction of a standard oil palm nursery has been completed. This also
has the capacity of producing two million palm seedlings per annum. The
plantation, located at the Vertinary Investigation Centre (VIC) Ezzamgbo, is
equipped with the modern irrigation facilities.
The nursery is the first phase of the administration's massive oil palm
production pragramme. This is in addition to the 4,500 improved oil palm
seedlings developed by Ebonyi State Agricultural Development Programme
(EBADEP). It is believed that the oil palms, which were developed from high
yielding oil palm species from the National Institute for Oil Palm
Researchers (NIFOR) Benin, are being sold to farmers and various
agricultural agencies. Plans are underway for the establishment of some tree
nursery such as plantain, Banana, Ireculia SPP (Ukwa), Irvengia SPP (Ogbono)
and several other plants.
To fully tap the huge resources in the state, the Otu explained that due
to the huge deposit of limestone in the state, government is also interested
in having its own cement factory, outside the Nkalagu factory jointly owned
by the five eastern states. As a member of the government entourage to
China, the commissioner said a Chinese company has indicated interest of
carrying out a geological survey at the central zone of the state where
there is large deposit of limestone.
The Wanshi Cement Company from China, he said would not only invest on
cement production, but would delve into provision of social amenities in the
state. Otu added that the state prepared to turn the natural blessings
endowed in the state to wealth for the overall interest of the people.

Original_Search_Title: Ibi: the Unending Crisis of Bridging the Benue River
Headline: Nigeria: Ibi: the Unending Crisis of Bridging the Benue River
Body_Text:
The Nigerian drainage system presents one of the most outstanding
exhibits of the hydrological features of sub-Saharan Africa. The country
enjoys a unique advantage of the union of some of the most treasured rivers
in the continent: The River Niger and the River Benue. This is particularly
so because the River Niger, which emerges from The Futa djalon highlands in
Guinea, and runs thousands of miles through several West African countries,
eventually terminates in Nigeria, in a splendid confluence with River Benue
at the historical town of Lokoja.
It in true though, that these rivers cannot be taken for granted in our
treasury of natures endowment, but there is also the hard challenge of
enhancing lives among people who, by the mere presence of the rivers, seem
to have been isolated and left in perpetual difficulties. This, indeed is
the sad story of a lingering crisis in Ibi, where there is no bridge across
the River Benue.
Ibi is a cosmopolitan community in Taraba State, inhabited to varying
proportions by people from different parts of Nigeria. The major tribe is
however the Jukun who are the indigenes of the community. Due to the deeply
fertile and extensively arable land with which the area is endowed,
agricultural activities flourish in the area. Various kinds of cereal crops,
including maize, millet, guinea corn, and in fact yams among others, are
extensively cultivated. Ibi also happens to be one of the communities that
enjoys large scale fishing activities as a result of the vast deposits of
water from the Benue river which runs through the community. As a matter of
fact, Ibi, which is on the border of Plateau and Taraba states is cut into
two almost equal parts by the River Benue. There is the Ibi village on the
Western side of the river, and Ibi town on the eastern side of the
river. However throughout the length of the community, there is no place
where a piece of land joins the village and the town. The people have right
from the earliest times, been in the habit of using canoes and boats to
cross from one to the other part of the community.
Beyond Ibi, however, there are many states and communities in
north-eastern Nigeria separated from each other as a result of the coursing
water of the Rivers. The situation had since posed the challenge of building
bridges across the river at various locations in order to facilitate
movement and interaction among the states and the communities. The bridges
become necessary more so, in order to establish contact with, and enhance
the economy of the riverine dwellers which are the highly productive
agrarian type.
In line with this though, the federal government had in the 1970s
constructed a bridge across the river Benue at Numan in Adamawa State. Since
then, the two bridges have remained the only ones linking the separated
states and communities of eastern Nigeria.
By an estimate, the River Benue courses through a distance of some 2000
kilometres (1200 miles) from Adamawa to Lokoja. In between Numan in Adamawa
State and Makurdi in Benue States where one bridge each is constructed
across the River Benue, there exists a vast gap of approximately 1200
kilometres covered by the River Benue. Ibi community is located more or less
in the centre of this distance. By this is meant that a gap of not less than
600 kilometres exists between Ibi and Numan in Adamawa State, and
approximately the same between Makurdi and Ibi.
Ibi is therefore considered to be another major location at which a
bridge is supposed to be constructed across the river Benue. Interestingly,
the only federal road that links Taraba and Plateau states passes through
Ibi. But on getting to Ibi village, the road terminates at the bank of River
Benue, and continues without a bridge, from Ibi town to Wukari in Taraba
State. Essentially therefore, all vehicle and passengers going to Benue
State and some other North-eastern states, including those heading for the
eastern part of the country, and nearby places like Donga, Mutum biu, Zaki
Biam, Katsina Ala etc, have to stop over and be conveyed in ferries or boats
across the River Benue, and from Ibi village to Ibi town, from where they
would proceed to Wukari and then take on the various roads that link their
destinations. The only alternative to this route is for the vehicle to go
through Jos to Bauchi, to Gombe and on to Numan in Adamawa State, from where
they would turn back to Wukari in Taraba State to continue their
journey. This would require approximately 7 hours as against 3 hours 30
minutes needed to travel from Jos to Wukari through Ibi. In another sense, a
traveller from Taraba State cannot get to neighbouring Plateau State, except
he or she prepares to be ferried across the River Benue with all the
difficulties involved. Alternatively, such traveller must pass through Benue
State via the Makurdi bridge, proceed to Lafia in Nasarawa State, and
further through Akwanga and Southern Kaduna to get to Plateau State.
It is important to mention that if a bridge were to have been in
existence at Ibi, a traveller from Plateau State would not require up to one
minute to cross the river, and he would need an additional 25 minutes to go
from Ibi to Wukari from where he can proceed to other destinations.
Now around the river Benue in Ibi, many more people are engaged in
commercial boating and ferrying. Since there is no bridge, the people have
continued to invade the nearby forest where they source mahogany and other
hard woods with which they construct boats. At present, there are over 70
boats and canoes of different sizes and capacities, and about 5 ferries on
the river.
It would probably have made little or no sense at all for a traveller to
take the longer routes when they could easily be ferried or boated across
the river Benue from Ibi. There are however very serious difficulties and
risks associated with this option, and many travellers prefer to go through
the longer distances and hours to get to their destinations. Perhaps the
most discouraging of these difficulties is the time which commuters have to
waste before they are ferried across the river. There are very few vehicles
that have taken the Ibi route. So most times, a ferry would have to wait for
several hours before it gets up to 3 or 4 vehicles (cars and buses) to
convey across the river. And until the ferry is fully loaded, it would not
be released. This is the same rule that applies to the passenger boats. All
of these delays are accompanied with enormous crises of logistics. Apart
from the time factor, commuters are subjected to high ferry and boat charges
to cross the river with their vehicles and to cross in boats, as
individuals. The boats currently charge N80 per passenger, while ferry
charges range between N700 to N1000 to convey cars and buses across the
rivers. The economy, more particularly as it affects these riverine
dwellers, does not in any way support such an expensive transportation
system. The commercial drivers tend to transfer the burden of their ferry
expenses on the passengers, while the individual commuters bear the burden
on their own.
The river Benue has also in recent times witnessed massive accumulation
of sand dunes at various points in its course at Ibi. This has led to the
existence of cataracts on the riverbed which interfere with the movement of
ferries and boats on the river. Essentially the boats and ferries have to
establish routes within the river which are safer for their movements. These
cataracts however constitute serious threats to the boats and ferries which
themselves are always at the risk of being damaged in the water body due to
obsolete and over used engines.
Aside from these transportation difficulties, the absence of a bridge
across the river Benue at Ibi, has also impacted very negatively on
agricultural activities in the area. As was noted earlier, the people of Ibi
and neighbouring communities are large-scale cereal farmers and
fishermen. Large quantities of these agricultural produce are harvested each
farming season. However the major proportion of the products are either
wasted as a result of the lack of transportation facilities, or are sold out
at incredibly cheap prices to the few marketers who may summon the courage
to put their vehicles on the bad road, and bear the enormous cost of
ferrying the produce across the river or conveying them through the longer
routes to the markets. The vast and fertile arable land has also been left
to waste in greater proportion, as farmers are not encouraged by the
transportation problems. Also, as perishable agricultural products, fish are
sold at very cheap rates to prevent them from rotting.
One might wonder why since its construction in the 1970s, the Ibi
federal road has been left without a bridge across the river Benue. Daily
Trust gathered that there have been several proposals by successive
governments for the construction of the bridge. However, no government since
then has made any practical move to put the bridge in place. According to an
indigene of Ibi who spoke to Daily Trust, President Obasanjo had, during his
campaign for a second term, pledged that he was going to construct the
bridge "but it seems the president has forgotten his pledge."
The remains of the federal road itself is nothing to write home about,
as potholes have overtaken the major part of it. Daily Trust spoke to
Muhammed Nasir Waziri who is the chairman of the disciplinary committee of
the boat and ferry riders association in Ibi. According to him, the people
have been exposed to untold hardship due to the non-availability of any
bridge across the river Benue. "We have made several efforts to get the
government to sympathise with our plight, and put up a bridge here in Ibi,
but we have not succeeded."
According to Muhammed, some people hold the view that the people of Ibi
community are not interested in the construction of a bridge across the
river Benue, because of the commercial gains they are getting from ferry and
boat riding. He however refuted this claim, saying "We are not stupid as to
say that we are not aware of the gains that we can get if a bridge is
constructed here in Ibi. The community would receive a boost and we would be
able to have contacts and relationship with people from outside. So we are
very much in need of a bridge at Ibi here so as to ease the hardship we are
going through."
Daily Trust also spoke to one Muhammed Hussaini who is a commercial boat
operator. According to him, it is equally not true that the people of Ibi
have resisted attempts to construct a bridge across the river Benue. "People
are saying that we are performing ritual sacrifices to prevent the
government from taking any meaningful steps to construct a bridge in
Ibi. How can we be doing such things? When we know how we are suffering as a
result of the lack of a bridge here in Ibi," Hussain lamented, adding that
the people of Ibi are not only faced with the lack of a bridge, but that
they are also compelled to drink raw water directly from the river Benue as
a result of the damage which had since occurred at the water treatment plant
installed in the community by the government.
The story of the untold hardship being suffered by the people of Ibi and
their neighbours as a result of the lack of a bridge across the river Benue,
is as inexhaustible as the list of specific problems that emanated
thereof. It however remains a mystery as to why the Ibi federal road has
been abandoned at the very bank of the river Benue!

Original_Search_Title: Government to Review Kwara Chieftaincy Institution
Headline: Nigeria: Government to Review Kwara Chieftaincy Institution
Body_Text:
Lagos — A Committee to review the structure of the chieftaincy institution in
Kwara State is in the works.
Governor Bukola Saraki, while receiving the Emir of Ilorin, Alhaji Sulu
Gambari, in Council at the Government House, Ilorin yesterday said that the
exercise would be comprehensive and would address all grievances in the
State Council of Chiefs.
Saraki said he was aware that the last review was unjust and highly
politicised. "This administration is not interested in controlling the
affairs of traditional rulers". He told the Emir who had come with a large
entourage to pay the traditional Sallah homage to the Governor.
The Governor promised that Government would act as soon as the State
Council of Chiefs proffers its suggestions in the matter. He said the
government was no intention to interfere in the running of the Council, and
asked the royal fathers to educate Kwarans on the need to shun rumour
mongering and back-bitting.
Alhaji Gambari praised the administration for "taking Kwara out of the
woods", by restoring peace and harmony in the State.
"The first step you took in office testified that Kwara has entered a
new era with a new leadership", the Emir told Governor Saraki.
He pledged the unalloyed support of the Emirate and all Kwarans for the
policies and programmes of the State Government.
The Emir advised the Governor to enlarge the Back-to-farm scheme and
produce rice, yam and potato on a large scale.
The State Deputy Governor, Chief Joel Ogundeji; Chief Judge, Justice
Timothy Oyeyipo; Grand Khadi, Justice Muttalib Ambali; PDP State Chairman,
Alhaji Abubakar Zubair and Chief Imam of Ilorin, Alhaji Mohammed Bashir were
among dignitaries present on the occasion.

Original_Search_Title: Wildfire, Excessive Logging Eat Up 65 Sq Km of Nation's Forest Annually
Headline: Ghana: Wildfire, Excessive Logging Eat Up 65 Sq Km of Nation's Forest Annually
Body_Text:
Koforidua — Available statistics on the country's forest indicate that about 65
square kilometres of forests are lost annually, dueprimarily to wildfire,
poor agricultural practices and excessive logging.
The annual loss of timber, cash and food crops to bushfires alone is
currently estimated at 3% of Gross Domestic Product (GDP). These were
contained in a speech read on behalf of the vice president, Alhaji Aliu
Mahama, at the launching of government's plantation programme at Koforidua
on November 17.
According to the vice president, bushfires have therefore severely
reduced, not only the productive capacity of the forests but have also had
major impact on the other benefits of forests, including estimation of
premium timber species, loss of biodiversity, drying up of water bodies,
soil fertility and loss of tourist sites.
He observed, illegal logging by chainsaw operators and timer
concessionaires have led to huge loss of revenue to the state and excessive
environment degradation.
Consequently, the country is now in a very precarious situation that has
led to the shortage of timber for the wood processing industries and the
attendant threat to job security for workers in that sector, Chronicle
learnt.
"Apart from these concerns, illegal loggers have also recently hired
armed gangs to protect them while they undertake their nefarious activities"
he noted.
Alhaji Aliu Mahama, announced that the government was pursuing the
programme to establish about 100,000 ha of plantations over a period of five
years.
The Minister of Lands and Forestry, Prof. Dominic Fobih, stated that,
under the modified Taungya system, over 17,000 ha of plantations was
established last year, creating 76,000 jobs at the rural and local community
level.
Similarly, several tons of food crops such as maize, cocoyams, yams and
plantains were produced under this programme last year, he said.
According to Prof. Fobih, between January and June, this year, over
30,000 new jobs have been created in the area land preparation.
Earlier, in a welcome address, the Deputy Eastern Regional Minister,
Mr. Gustav Narh Dometey, observed that, quite often, the over-all social and
economic functions of plants are overlooked as the global environmental
situation deteriorates with incredible and amazing rapidity.
He said, the provision of open spaces, recreational parks and green
areas has the settlement planning of Town and Country Planning Department
but, very little effort has been put in by the district assemblies to
develop cities.
The Omanhene of the New Juaben Traditional Area, Daasebre Oti Boateng,
asked the Ministry of Lands and Forestry to ensure that, those given
concessions have a programme for reafforestation.
He also suggested that, farmers should be part of the programme to
ensure its sustainance as well as charged all Ghanaians to help protect the
forest from further depletion.
Ceremonial treeswere planted by various dignitaries to start the
programme at the Koforidua Sports Stadium.

Original_Search_Title: Church Donates Gifts to Less-Privileged Homes
Headline: Nigeria: Church Donates Gifts to Less-Privileged Homes
Body_Text:
Mountain of Fire and Miracles Ministries (MFM) at the weekend donated
gifts worth N2.5 million to inmates of less-privileged homes in Lagos.
The church made the donation through its welfare alms unit- "We Care
Ministry" and also feted the inmates with sumptuous dishes. The event took
place at MFM Onike-Iwaya Yaba, Lagos headquarters.
The inmates who had colourful wears made for them by the minister
laughed and danced across the expansive auditorium of the church. Some of
the inmates of the less-privileged homes who spoke to Eko Trust expressed
their happiness and one of them, Yinka Olaiya said, "I am happy. They gave
me two new clothes. I am wearing one and I have another one for Christmas."
The gifts include a bus to Arrow of God Home, Lekki, television, radio
sets, video CDs, cartons of milk, refrigerators, freezers, electric cookers,
bags of rice, bags of garri, tubers of yam. The statistics of the items run
into thousands.
Some of the recipients include Remand Home, Oregun, Boys Approved Homes,
Lagos State Government Little Saints Akowonyo, Boys Approved School, Isheri,
Rehabilitation and Vocational Training Centre, Isheri, The Good Shepherded
Orphanage Home, Idimu, Distressed Children Transit Home, Idi-Araba and
Remand Home, Agege.
Speaking to Eko Trust after the donation, which was witnessed by notable
church leaders, the coordinator of "We Care Ministry, Reverend Julius
Obileye, said the gesture was MFM's own way of practically ameliorating the
sufferings of the downtrodden.

Original_Search_Title: Traditions Die Hard: Can Arrests Stop Female Genital Mutilation?
Headline: Ghana: Traditions Die Hard: Can Arrests Stop Female Genital Mutilation?
Body_Text:
On September 20 of this year, police officers in Wa, in the Upper West
Region, arrested Fefe Dari for practicing female genital mutilation (FGM) on
three girls. Two days later, the 47-year-old woman pleaded guilty in the Wa
Circuit Court and received a prison sentence of five years. She is currently
serving this sentence in Tamale's prison.
A 1999 study by the Population Impact Project at the University of Ghana
estimated that 69.1% of women in the Wa district have had their genitals
cut. Despite this reported prevalence, authorities have arrested less than
10 people since FGM became illegal in 1994. Dari's arrest was the first in
the Upper West.
If Dari's arrest is an attempt to stop FGM, it raises questions about
how to effectively combat the practice: Why have there been so few arrests?
Is it fair to arrest someone like Dari? And, finally, what is the impact of
Dari's arrest on her community? Dari worked as a farmer in Billi-uu, a
remote village outside of Wa. She and her nephew, Kwesi Nabiaba, farmed
maize, millets, yam and groundnuts.
Dari was married and widowed twice. After her first husband died, she
married his junior brother who died four years ago. Dari has six children -
three girls and three boys. At the time of her arrest, she and her three
youngest children (ages three, five and nine years) lived with Nabiaba.
On September 19, Florence Ali, president of the Ghanaian Association for
Women's Welfare (GAWW), a non-governmental organization, was in Wa
conducting sensitization workshops on FGM. She received a report from a
nurse at Loggu Health Center, on the outskirts of Wa, that Dari had
performed FGM on three girls in Billi-uu on September 13. Someone from the
village had informed the nurse of the mutilation, prompting her to travel to
Billi-uu and verify the accusation.
The girls - who were three weeks, one and a half years, and five years
old at the time - are Dari's relations. The two oldest are her nieces and
the baby is Dari's granddaughter, the first child of her 16-year-old
daughter.
After receiving the report from the nurse, Ali notified the Women and
Juvenile Unit (WAJU) of Ghana Police Services in Wa. The next day, Ali and
police officers traveled to Loggu where they questioned Dari, who was there
for the market day.
According to Ali, Dari denied having performed the acts, saying that
someone from another village was to blame. When the police told Dari they
would detain her until she produced the real perpetrator, she confessed to
the crime.
Ali and the police went to Billi-uu. They walked the ten kilometers from
Loggu to the village, which is not accessible by car. They found the girls
and brought them to Wa District Hospital for treatment. "[The doctors] were
really very scared because they had lost a lot of blood," remembers
Ali.
On September 22, Dari pleaded guilty to "intentionally and unlawfully
infibulating the whole part of the labia minora and clitoris" of all three
girls. The maximum punishment for performing FGM is three years. Because
Dari circumcised three girls, Judge Alhaji Mohammed Ahmed Mustapha sentenced
her to five years of hard labor.
Residents of Billi-uu are angry with the nurses at Loggu Health Center
for Dari's imprisonment. They are angry because a member of their community
was taken away. They know that it was one of their own - a villager - who
reported Dari's acts to the nurses, but the authorities are protecting that
person's identity. The blame, therefore, falls on the nurses.
Hawa Yakubu, a nurse at the clinic, says that the villagers' hostility
towards them has affected their work. "Since the arrest, we haven't been [to
Billi-uu] because we are afraid," Yakubu explains. "We're supposed to go for
immunization programs, but we do not go. A man goes for us."
The nurses' experience illustrates one reason why the police have
arrested so few people for practicing FGM. People fear reporting cases to
them.
Daniel Dorledols, the public relations officer for the Ghana Police
Service in Wa, stresses the importance of keeping an informant
anonymous. "If the informant's identity is known," he explains, "the person
could be attacked." But is a guarantee of anonymity enough? An officer at
WAJU in Wa, who wishes to remain anonymous, recommends that informants
receive protection and a financial reward for their information.
Given the lack of resources to conduct intensive investigations, the
police often rely on informants to catch perpetrators of FGM. "The onus is
on the community members to report," says Marian Tackie, Executive Director
of the National Council on Women and Development, which is a department of
the Ministry of Women and Children's Affairs.
This need for insiders is a result of the law forcing FGM to be carried
out underground. Yakubu, the nurse at Loggu Health Clinic, explains one
tactic people use to keep FGM secret. "They used to let the child grow
[older before being circumcised]," she says. "But now that we've been
telling them [that it's illegal], they do it when [the child is] an infant,
so no one will notice."
Dorledols, of the Ghana Police Service in Wa, disagrees with Tackie on
the effects of the law banning FGM. He argues that the law, combined with
public education campaigns, has led to the near elimination of the practice
in his region. He says there have been so few arrests because there are so
few people performing FGM.
Pat Sutinga, a midwife and nurse in the children's ward of Wa District
Hospital, agrees that there has been a reduction in the number of FGM
cases. She says that after a mother gives birth to a girl in the hospital,
the nurses tell her that FGM is harmful and illegal. (A poster that reads
"Stop Female Genital Cutting" and shows a young girl with a bleeding vagina
is prominently displayed on the wall of the ward.) When the babies come back
for post-natal exams, she says they have not been circumcised.
Like Dorledols, Sutinga believes that public sensitization and the
threat of imprisonment have deterred some parents from having their
daughters cut. However, Sutinga admits that she is not able to examine all
girls in the region. Only nine percent of women in the northern regions give
birth with the assistance of a trained health care worker, according to the
United Nations Population Fund. If they do give birth in the hospital, most
villagers receive their post-natal care in local clinics, out of Sutinga's
reach. However, she says, the clinics send monthly reports to the hospital
without ever mentioning FGM.
"I agree that [Fefe Dari] did not follow the law," says her nephew,
Kwesi Nabiaba. "But it is that she was ignorant of the law."
In court, that logic was the basis of Dari's defense. She pleaded
guilty, but added that she did not know that FGM was a crime.
Dari's critics argue that she could not have been ignorant of the
law. Dorledols, Yakubu and Ali contend that people in the village know the
law. They point to the example of the Billi-uu resident who reported
Dari. "Because there was an informant from the village," explains Dorledols,
"that means that awareness was created."
Nabiaba disagrees: "The whole village was in darkness," he says.
Meetings with ordinary people in the region support Dari's claim of
ignorance. Waiting for a lorry at Loggu, two women from nearby Bintanga said
they had only heard of the law after Dari's arrest. "People were not aware
of the existence of that law," added Mohammed Seidu, a driver at the Wa bus
station.
Tackie argues that "ignorance of the law is no excuse." "Once there is a
law - whether you know about it or not, it is now a criminal offence," she
says. "Just like any criminal offence, you can be prosecuted, whether you
are ignorant or not, because you are inflicting something which is harmful
on another human being."
For others, it is not that simple. The law must be enforced, says Ali,
"but I kind of pitied [Dari] because it may be true that she really didn't
know the law existed."
Whether or not Dari was informed of the law, her arrest raises another
question: Is arresting people necessary to curb FGM or is educating people
on its harmful effects sufficient?
Ali says that stopping FGM is "a question of education." "Everywhere I
go," she explains, "[villagers] don't really know what is done to the woman
[when they are cut]. There was one instance when [a woman] said to me, 'You
see, they have been telling us that it isn't good, we shouldn't do it, but
we don't really know why. But now that you have taken your time to tell us
why, now I can also educate my clients and try to dissuade them from
practising it.'"
Most people interviewed felt educating people about the harmful effects
of FGM must go hand in hand with informing them about the law. And they
argue that the arrest of someone who performs FGM deters others from doing
it as well. In fact, Ali and Tackie hope to expand the scope of the
law. They sent a memo to the Attorney General asking that the law include
punishments for the girl's parents and other community members who
participate in the ceremony.
It is difficult - perhaps impossible - to discern the effects of Dari's
arrest on the prevalence of FGM in Billi-uu. Nabiaba, Dari's nephew, says
that the practice was common before the arrest, but it has stopped
since. "The arrest has scared people because there is a likelihood that
people will report you," he explains.
But it seems highly improbable that a custom would be entirely
eliminated in six weeks. "Traditions die hard," says Tackie.
Nabiaba does admit that the practice is engrained in their
culture. Asked why villagers perform FGM, he said: "It's something practised
by their great-grandfathers, their fathers. I don't know why they do it, but
it is a tradition."
Nabiaba has two wives and says that both of them have had their genitals
cut. All four of his children are boys but if he had a daughter, he adds, he
would have her cut.
If Dari's arrest has deterred others from performing FGM, it achieved
its desired effect. But there's also the chance it has forced the practice
even farther underground and people like Nabiaba are too scared to admit
that residents of Billi-uu still practise FGM.
There are some effects of Dari's arrest that are easier to measure.
Nabiaba says that since the arrest he has been "confused" and has lost
his appetite. He currently cares for Dari's three young boys, who are also
suffering. "They are at home weeping everyday," he says, "and I don't know
what to do."

Original_Search_Title: I Left Ghana Because of Rawlings - 1979 Victim Tells Commission
Headline: Ghana: I Left Ghana Because of Rawlings - 1979 Victim Tells Commission
Body_Text:
Mr. Daniel Ohene Adjei, another victim of the atrocities of the 1979
mutiny started weeping even before telling his story perhaps because just as
he was about to do so he had remembered the painful ordeal.
He told the commission that in 1979, as a trader who went to buy yam
from the producing areas to sell in Accra he had brought in some one million
tubers to the Timber Market when a military vehicle pulled up with some
soldiers in it.
One of the soldiers he said asked him for the price of a tuber of yam he
had picked to which he mentioned one hundred and twenty cedis. The soldier
said it was too expensive but he tried to explain to him that transport cost
and other incidentals are factored into the price of the commodity.
One of the soldiers he said pushed him at this stage and he
retaliated. He was hit and a struggle ensued but he was overpowered by the
soldiers who bundled him into their truck after a bad beating.
In the truck too they beat him until they reached Mitchel Camp,
Tema.
He was locked up in a guardroom for three days and for two days he did
not eat he told the commission. When he was released to go home he had no
money on him and he was half naked. A woman by the roadside he said offered
him one cedi for transport back to Accra and a dress to cover his
nakedness.
He could not go to find out about the status of his tubers of yam
because his father had discouraged him from doing so he told the
commission.
He then left for Nigeria because according to him he did not want to
have anything to do Mr. Jerry Rawlings whose regime was responsible for his
predicament.
He expressed gratitude to the government for making such a commission
possible but for which those of them with bottled-up pain would not have had
the outlet of airing them.
He added that he had forgiven those who wronged him after the
narration.
Proceedings continue today with the retired Major Boakye Djan and his
AFRC mates making a spectacular appearance which is expected to be hot.

Original_Search_Title: Swelling Agitation for the Hyppadec Bill
Headline: Nigeria: Swelling Agitation for the Hyppadec Bill
Body_Text:
The recent snappy tour to the riverine areas of Niger State by the
president, Chief Olusegun Obasanjo, to assess the damages caused by the
recent flood is a welcome idea.
Although the visit was brief, it will go a long way to provide hope for
the people of the area to the effect that the federal government has not
totally forgotten them.
Furthermore, the fact that the president could not visit the Shiroro
area where the flood wreaked havoc will give him an idea of the totality of
the catastrophe during the period the flood swept through the state.
It would be recalled that the disaster which is not peculiar to the
state alone, was a general phenomenon all over the Northern region which
regularly suffered from drought.
As can be seen from reports, Kaduna, Katsina, Jigawa and Kano states
were all affected by the flood in various ways.
In a chain of disaster, that assailed these states, nobody knew that the
flood would expand its frontier of destruction beyond these states, until it
suddenly reared its ugly head in Niger State.
Perhaps, the destruction caused by the flood in Niger State can be
described as more horrendous in scale, measured against what happened in the
other states combined.
In the first wake of the flood which occurred in September, thirty-six
(36) villages in Shiroro Local Government Area were submerged.
During the period, livestock, farm produce, houses and property worth
millions of naira were lost .
According to the village head of Galadima Kogo, Alhaji Aliyu Dada, fifty
people died in the flood. Two weeks later when another rain fell, eight (8)
people including two small children also died.
As would be expected from the state government, its legislative arm in a
motion appealing to the federal government to do something, specifically
urged her to hastily sign the HYPPADEC bill into law, so that the disaster
could be controlled in future. No doubt the flood has caused untold
disasters in the states agitating for HYPPADEC these being Niger, Kogi,
Kwara and Kebbi.
Although the President's visit has come and gone, the effect of the
disaster however remains, without eliciting either relief materials or
categorical statements from the president regarding his administration's
next move on the HYPPADEC bill, since it was passed to him for assent.
In the reports credited to him, the President said that he did not
believe that the HYPPADEC bill holds the key to the solution of the flood,
saying his position is based on lack of conviction that the 20-year-old dam
was responsible for the disaster.
But the contrary is the case, as investigations have shown that the dam
contributed in no small measure to the environmental disaster and human
suffering.
Those affected revealed that at the height of the rainy season when the
dam was at full capacity, the National Electric Power Authority (NEPA)
officials released the excess water stored, and the consequence was
immediate flooding of low lying areas.
They added that while this is an annual tragedy, this year's had been
compounded by the River Kaduna which courses through the area, so that when
the dam water was freed and eventually united with that of the river, the
result was the havoc that stared the people in the face.
Perhaps, the lack of adequate knowledge of the impact on the current
disaster informed the levity with which the government is handling the
matter.
In the attempt to unveil the causes of the flood, one came across
political equations impeding the realisation of HYPPADEC.
More potent in the factors inhibiting the assent of the bill by the
president is the personality tussle between IBB versus Obasanjo and
Atiku. In fact, it is being discussed in certain quarters that the HYPPADEC
bill is suffering because General Babangida is harbouring ambitions for the
2007 presidency.
As the saying goes: 'one gesture deserves another,' and since Babangida
was largely instrumental to Obasanjo's election after he was released from
prison by General Abdulsalami, who is also from Niger State, seeking for the
establishment of the commission should not have taken long as Obasanjo is in
power.
Before the soured relationship between the personalities served by
politics, there was a standing agreement between the president and
Nigerlites during the electioneering campaignsthat once the former is
elected, HYPPADEC as a body will see the light of day.
Whether the agreement was documented or whether a gentlemanly one, this
cannot be substantiated. But it would be remembered that the root of the
conflict between the state governor and the president during the latter's
first term, was not so much for seeking a second term. The problem was
traced to the president's stonewalling over the promise made by him to
create the HYPPADEC Commission.
The conflict at that time created the Kure and Mustafa Bello factions in
the PDP, when the latter was alleged to have been undermining the effort by
Kure to realise HYPPADEC in order to prevent him (Kure) from claiming credit
for election purposes in the bid for a second term.
It is almost five years now since the issue of HYPPADEC sponsored by
four states of Kebbi, Kogi, Kwara and Niger has been developing, but it
still remains remains a mirage.
The establishment of this important body has pitched the people of these
states against their leaders, especially Niger State which champions the
cause, questioning the rationale behind their overwhelming support for the
PDP government at federal and state levels.
Their angst has been concentrated on the two former leaders of Nigeria
who are citizens of Niger State for not using their influence to persuade
Obasanjo to sign the bill into law, since his office is being accused of
delaying the progress.
But for a very long time, the people will ever remain grateful to
General Babangida for not only completing the dam project initiated by
President Shehu Shagari, but for also expanding it.
Indeed the reason for the people's angst is not so much because the
president is withholding assent, but for the fear that the effort of those
who gave thought to the project might be in vain if the necessary measures
were not taken to ensure its safety, as it is believed that if the dam is
being used at its present low capacity, it could collapse.
The obvious fact is that it is not being fully utilised presently to
generate electricity, and the other purpose of its agricultural potential
remains completely neglected too.
This negligence is discernible at Gwada village, upon the borders of the
dam site where there is a vast land which can be transformed into an
agricultural treasure, but this has not been done.
In fact, the exploitation of this agricultural potential will generate
not only job opportunities, by extension it will boost the chances of peace
in the already restive country.
Having explained the factors militating against the realisation of the
enabling law that will create HYPPADEC, it would be of interest to make an
exposition on the sorrows and sufferings.
First, the majority of the people affected are Gbagyis, while others are
Hausas. The Gwaris are mainly farmers, while the Hausas are mainly
fishermen.
As earlier mentioned, there are thirty-six (36) settlements that were
submerged by the flood, killing people, destroying food crops, snuffing out
the lives of their livestock, and pulling down their abodes, thus making
them refugees in their own country.
The villages affected are Ibro, Kyaure, Galadima-Kogo, Maiyelu, Guni,
Lagado, Kafana and Alake.
Others are Laga, Dnasa, Layini Kpan, Shiroro Kanufe and Dnabuyi. These
are just a few of the villages whose names were made available to
journalists.
For instance, the village head of Galadima Kogo, Alhaji Aliyu Dada, told
reporters that 50 people were missing in the wake of the flood.
As for an education supervisor in the new Lakpma Local Government,
Alhaji Yahaya Idris, while underscoring the statement of the village head
over the statistics of those that lost their lives, he recounted in
sorrowful detail their ordeal as happened 20 years ago when the dam project
began.
"When the project began in the 80s," he said, "we were re-located to our
present villages. This was done so that the dam work could be successfully
executed," he added.
According to him, compensation then was so small that some of them
received a paltry sum of N300, the highest was N3,000. The education
