Coming nearer home to what he called Ani-Igbo, he noted with sadness that Anambra State has been mired in political crisis since the last elections, or rather since the previous four years. "The situation has taken a national dimension and should be of serious concern to everyone who cherishes the healthy political development of our dear country."
Still, beyond Anambra State, he raised some questions for Ndi-Igbo. "Why are we so deprived collectively and yet highly accomplished individually? Why are we not close to one another in the same manner that we easily make friends with people elsewhere? Why are we not serving ourselves as well as we have endeavoured to served our country?"
He extends the Anambra State crisis as a manifestation of the dilemma facing the entire Igbo nation. "We should reflect long and deep on our purpose as a people. We owe Nigeria a duty to contribute our quota to its development and growth and, in turn, expect a commensurate reward. But we cannot participate effectively in nation-building if we are disunited, if we lack focus and if we fail to pursue the common good. We will not gain the respect of others if we do not respect ourselves. We must therefore rise together as a people, sink our differences forgive one another, and declare and positively act to restore our dignity as a people in order to earn the respect of our fellow Nigerians and the entire world once more. That is why we saw no better reason than to dedicate this Ofala to Peace and Reconciliation in, and the well being of , Ani-Igbo as a basis for moving forward with the rest of the country in unity and strength. As we celebrate, we must pray for peace and reconciliation, not only in Ani-Igbo, but in our entire country."
He averred that no part of Igbo land aptly reflected the Igbo dilemma more than Onitsha where, in his words, "much wealth is created in the midst of abject squalor and not much is reinvested to raise the quality of life of the very people who toil so hard to create that wealth." He explained that aside Lagos, the businessmen and traders in Onitsha probably generate more money on a daily basis than in any other city in the country. "Yet the city is foreboding, filthy, undisciplined, overbuilt, and insecure because everyone cares only for his pocket and no one, not even the Local Government Councils, bothers about the general well being of the city. The city has thus merely become a cash cow that is fast being milked to death," he said.
He said though he and his people had consistently complained about the deplorable conditions of social amenities, roads, telecommunications, water, electricity, even security in Onitsha to the authorities since last year's edition of the festival, "Our only solace is that we have received sympathetic ears from the President and our Executive Governor in our dialogue with them. We know that they share our concerns and hope and pray that dialogue will progressively turn into cooperative action."
If prompt action is not taken to salvage Onitsha from its present state of rot, the Obi feared that city may increasingly become unable to serve Anambra State and Nigeria effectively unless immediate steps are taken to rehabilitate the city and new residential, industrial, and business areas created in neighbouring towns to suck some of the pressure of the city. "There is no doubt that this region of Anambra State offers great economic potentials for Nigeria, which can only be realised through far-sighted policies, plans and actions," he stressed.
At this juncture, he outlined some of the landmark achievements of his Obiship so far.
"At my first Ofala, I identified the need to build good relations between Onitsha and other parts of Igboland and Nigeria. I wish to re-state that commitment today. Indeed, the presence of our retinue of guests from all over Ani-Igbo and Nigeria today attests to our disposition to reach out. Onitsha is a cosmopolitan city that has welcomed all-comers for centuries. We will continue to actively build a more inclusive community based on mutual respect. I will continue to protect the interest of everyone and every organization within this kingdom of Onitsha ado N'Idu. My Ndichie and myself will be more actively in the community life of its various sections," he said.
Continuing, he disclosed that the past seventeen months of his reign have been extremely busy and rewarding. "We have started several initiatives reviewing our traditional governance system, evolving a blueprint for youth development, initiating a few programmes for community development, establishing a fund for manpower development, and embarking on the next phase of the development of this Ime-Obi (palace)."
According to the elaborate report in the festivals programme, the highpoint has been the expansion of the Obi-in-Council which was enlarged with the appointment of eight new Ndichies, including two Ndichie Ume. These appointments, according to him, reflect a more even spread and greater diversity of background and interest among Ndichie such that complementary spheres of responsibilities are now being assigned to them.
The monarch has also made some breakthroughs in the areas of peace and reconciliation. He has initiated several moves to address many of the knottiest disputes in various kindred groups and families with prospects of major breakthroughs. In the same manner, he is making headways in the area of communication. The Obi-in-Council is gradually developing a communications framework to ensure that Onitsha people everywhere are well informed about events and issues concerning our homeland. Hence, the publication of a monthly calendar of traditional and social events is now issued weekly by the Ime Obi. This calendar will become part of a proposed newsletter, which will eventually culminate in a website.
He evolved working committees to enable the Obi-in-Council focus attention on the pressing issues affecting the community. The working committees include: Local Government Review Committee to prepare the community's submission to the Technical Committee on Local Government Reforms in Nigeria; Land Review Committee to collate and review all contentious land matters affecting Onitsha and make recommendations to the Obi-in-Council; Environmental Sanitation Committee to evolve plans that would dovetail into the State Government's environmental programme including the issues of illegal structures and protection of open spaces; Security Committee to evolve a plan for communitybased vigilante programme working in concert with Nigeria Police and the State Security Adviser; Ime Obi Development Committee (Phase 1) to oversee the erection of the office complex, the Obi's reception room and a conference hall; Accounts Reconciliation Committee to examine past accounts and make recommendations to Obi-in-Council for improving transparency and accountability on communal finances; Ofala Steering Committee to have an equally successful Ofala in the years ahead; Youth Development Workshop Coordinating Committee; Manpower Development Trust Fund to provide bursaries for postgraduate studies to indigent but well qualified sons and daughters of Onitsha.
In the area of youth development, the 17-months old reign of Igwe Nnaemeka Alfred Ugochukwu Achebe, the Obi of Onitsha has made tremendous impact. A very successful youth development workshop held early this year (May 17). It was the centerpiece of the Obi's first anniversary on the throne.
While in education, during the year, computer laboratories were established in the following schools: Dennis Memorial Grammar School, Queen of the Holy Rosary College, and Christ the King College as part of the first phase of a pilot project by the Educational Tax Fund.
In the Diaspora, the Onitsha Ado National Improvement Association USA, on August 30 held a very rousing and well attended national convention and launching programme in Arlington, Virginia, USA with the Obi, accompanied by some members of the Ndichie, Agbalanze, and Otu Odu in attendance.
The Igwe's Obi-in-Council has also to reach out the business community - chamber of commerce, traders association, banks and major companies as stakeholders in Onitsha with the message that they have a duty to contribute to the solution of the city's problems.
The community's external relations has also improved considerably in the last 17 months. The Obi-in-Council has been able to opened good relations with several monarchies in the country, Benin, Ife, Kano, Asaba, Bonny, Kalabari, Nembe, Borno, Zaria, Achalla, Owerri, Rebisi (Port-Harcourt.), Calabar, Lagos, Opobo, etc.
Still, the Obi averred that most of the foregoing initiatives and challenges are at the early stages of implementation, adding that attention in the coming year will be devoted to driving them towards fuller accomplishment.
"Most of these initiatives are in various stages of implementation and the coming year will be devoted to pushing them to completion. Particular attention will be devoted to youth issues. We will also devote attention to the restoration and protection of open spaces.
"The challenges we face are enormous but we are not daunted. What we ask for are your material and moral support as well as your prayers. And I call on all who have a stake in Onitsha to join hands together with one spirit of brotherhood to continue to support me in this great and exiting task of restoring the dignity of a historic homeland and commercial centre," he said.
Surrounded by his colourful retinue the Obi retired to the Ime Obi while the Ndichie Okwa/Okwareze danced to music from the orchestra before the Igwe's second outing which was as graceful as the first. Shortly after he retired back into the Ime-Obi, the Ndichie Ume took to the dance floor with Egwuota (royal drums). Sounds of rattles, clanking of gongs and drums pervade the atmosphere as people cheered the chiefs and their troupes. Drumming, singing and dancing continue until another round of blast of the 'ogbunigwe' (cannon shot) heralding the third and last outing of the Obi into the square. On entering the square with his retinue, cheers and deafening ovation rent the air. Attended by his acolytes, two boys dressed in dull red or brown long gown with tall red caps, one on each side carrying state swords in their hands, the Obi stand in full elegance with his Abani and Nza. Walking majestically he acknowledge cheers from the spirited subjects as he exits bringing day one of the festival to a tumultuous close.
The second day of the Ofala known as Azu Ofala was no less festive. This time, the Obi appeared in a casual white damask. Surrounded by the Chiefs as in the day before, he sat on the throne receiving homage. Troupes from neighbouring towns and villages performed, women chant praises of the Obi, while acrobats and magicians entertain. The highpoint of the Azu Ofala is the parade of the Onitsha Age Grade Societies. As obtained in match pasts, the age grades appear in distinctive uniforms to entertain at the occasion.
Ofala has a curious an intriguing history. It grew out of mischief. A popular warrior in ancient Onitsha had eaten an ill-prepared yam and died with members of his family throwing the community into tragedy. After due consultations with the oracle, the diviners certified the crop edible, but with rules and regulations guarding the planting and eating of the crop. The head of the community who would have retired into seclusion, must eat the first of the yams upon his outing.
Hence, Ofala marks the annual emergence of the Obi from enforced isolation of communal mourning Inyepu Ukwu Na Nlo Eze, which is the high point of Onitsha ceremonial year. Ofala, then becomes a paradox. By this emergence, the Obi demonstrates that he has survived another year as a living atonement for the sins and misdeeds of Onitsha people, that he has mourned the community's dead and has been shown guiltless of their deaths, that he and the community have been filled with strength and richness of life and that he has foreseen the events of the year and found them to be good.
Ofala is a spectacular public outing of the Obi in the arena of his palace Ime Obi before a record crowd of his subjects most of whom had not seen him for the past one year. It is a festival at which the Obi presents himself to his people and exchange greetings with them.
Ofala marks the thirteenth day of Owuwaji and is celebrated over a two days period. The first day is primarily for the Obi and his Chiefs. It involves three outings by the Obi accompanied by his chiefs. Azu Ofala back of the emergence is held on the following day. At this time all the Obi's subject come forth to salute him.
The Onitsha ceremonial life involves a series of festivals, with each festival forming part of a continuum that helps to symbolically relate each aspect of social life to wider reality. Six main festivals are celebrated in one Onitsha year. The basic sacred divisions of the year are set by the phases of the moon. The Obi proclaims each of the twentyeightday lunar months Onwa and defines the ceremonial sequences in terms of the seven fourday weeks Oge Isa that comprise each month.
The six festivals are as follows: (i) Ifejioku: This is usually celebrated in January and is essentially a homage to the yam spirit with a view to establishing the presence of this guardian spirit of yams in each strip of farmland to be cleared and cultivated. It heralds the beginning of the harvesting of cocoyams. Hence it is also called Osisi Ahasi; (ii) Ajachi: Normally celebrated in June, it is a sacrifice to the Almighty and involves beseeching God to guide every adult in his labour and help his/her crops prosper; (iii) Umato: This is the first harvest festival celebrated in June at the time of the maize harvest. Umato is a sixteenday festival beginning with the exclusive celebration by the Obi for a fourday period, after which the celebration of the populace takes place. Umato entails a redistribution of new consumable wealth by the nobility to lesser mortals; (iv) Owuwaji: This new yam festival is normally celebrated in September and commences twentyfour days after the Umato, lasting for another twentyfour days. The Obi performs the ritual of eating the new yam after the various sections of Onitsha must have performed the ritual. The Obi must also during this period retire into a trancelike state for four days known as Inye Ukwu Na Nlo Eze; (v) Ofala, Obi's annual emergence from seclusion, having assessed his relationship with God and the spirits, after which his body is washed to signify the end of his mourning; (vi) Osisite: which comes nine days after the Ofala. This is the ceremony of cooking in the pot and signifies the end of the harvest cycle.
Every Onitsha indigene looks forward to these two days during the year, in which all have to place a respite from their endeavours and come to pay homage to their monarch. Ofala enables the people reaffirm their loyalty to the Obi.
Of particular interest at the festival are the submissions of the embattled Governor of Anambra State, Dr. Chris Ngige and his Abia State counterpart and friend, Orji Uzor Kalu at the event. While Kalu whose supporters nearly turned the arena to a political rally by displaying posters of "Orji Kalu for President, 2007" spoke in Igbo. He admitted that "Onitsha is home to every Ibo, because it accommodates all manner of people."
On his part, Ngige averred that the Ofala posses great tourism potentials waiting to be harnessed. "It's like a goldmine waiting to be tapped. What we are seeing here today compares credibly to the carnivals of Rio de Janeiro in Brazil. It has great tourism potentials which can be maximised."

Original_Search_Title: Bepanda : One Big Market
Headline: Cameroon: Bepanda : One Big Market
Body_Text:
Streets in Bepanda boom with business activities.
In the seventies and eighties, it was difficult for one to talk about residential areas in Douala without considering Bepanda. The neighbourhood was famous not only for the main Cameroon Telecommunication Centre and the Reunification stadium but due to its numerous attractive bars, beer parlours, night clubs, casinos and spontaneous events that kept the place booming. Today, though all these excitement has inexplicably died down, business activities have gradually taken centre stage with small and big business enterprises springing up here and there.
The Bepanda-Omnisport roundabout highly noted for its drinking and eating stalls after a thrilling football encounter as the number of customers increase. On such days, business activities begin timidly in the morning and enter high gear in the evening. As early as 7.a.m, activities such as the sale of second hand dresses, baked plums and plantains, meat, chicken, and oranges are on. Girls offering mobile telephone services are afoot. By midnight, the boom from the main bars such as "Cercle Sportif ", " Prolongation ", " Equipe ", "Jupiter Club", "Billionaire", engulf the environment. Just a few metres from the Reunification stadium is an extension of the Diedo market into Bepanda. The area is considered a foodstuff depot. Madam Helen Gwank, trader at the market explained that "most foodstuffs such as, vegeteble, plantains, banana, cocoyams, cassava, oranges, pawpaw, etc, are from either Pendja, Luom, Ekona, Mudika, Buea or Limbe. When the foodstuff is brought, it is retailed to the "bayam sellams" by the wholesallers. Some of these "bayam sellam" women spend the night in this area either waiting for suppliers from the "bush markets" or to secure their goods.
In other markets visited, the same scenario of small businesses implanted here and there is witnessed. At the Tonnerre roundabout, a giagantic story building on which is written "Bonjour Ancien" welcomes every visitor. Adjecent to this building is Madam Pauline, specialist in artificial nail fashion business. Though she did not get any formal training, the quality of her services are highly appreciated. Some of these services, whose prices according to her are moderate, include, placing of artificial nails, putting of nail varnish and nail decoration. She reveals that, it all started as a part-time business especially as many people did not consider it important. But today, it is a source of income to her and her family due to the fact that many people have realised its importance.
Elsewhere in the area, baked potatoes, cocoyams and yams are sold along the pavement. The rushed for the food is such that people are obliged to place orders in advance. A piece of baked yellow yam costs 200 to 500frs, a piece of white "calabar" yam between 250 and 1,000frs, while potatoes, white or yellow vary between 150 and 500frs depending on the sizes. However, along side these variety of foodstuffs sold in the area are many other small and big shops that trade in industrial goods such as, motor and motocycle spare parts, as well as construction, medical and communication equipment.

Original_Search_Title: Ogoja People Cry Out Over Bad Roads
Headline: Nigeria: Ogoja People Cry Out Over Bad Roads
Body_Text:
Cross River — The poor distribution of food items from the local areas to the urban
cities has been blamed on poor road network in some parts of the country,
particularly in the South East.
These were some of the views expressed by some community leaders in
Mbock area of Ogoja in Cross River State, at a function organised by the
sons and daughters of Ogoja in Lagos.
One of the community leaders who identified himself as Anthony Mabin
said, though the efforts of the Federal Govern-ment towards road repairs
could not be faulted to a great extent as it has been highly felt in some
areas, he however wondered why some highly commercialised roads such as the
one which linked Cross River and Eboyin states have not been repaired.
He said the roads and bridges have clamed so many lives in the recent
past.
"Majority of those who may have died on these roads through accident are
traders who may be on their way to sell some of their products in other
parts of the country" he said.
Mabin, a Police officer attached to the computer department of the
Nigeria Police, Oduduwa Street, Ikeja, further stated that part of the
reasons why food items, especially cocoa, yams, timber, rice and cassava
seem to be scarce in places like Lagos could be traceable to the bad
condition of the Federal roads in the country.
"I wish to appeal to the Federal Government, on behalf of the good
people of Ogoja to look into the state of roads connecting Eboyin and Cross
River States as it is presently the only quickest route from Cross River
state to Eboyin" he added.

Original_Search_Title: The Right People Must Be Put in Charge - Kalu
Headline: Nigeria: The Right People Must Be Put in Charge - Kalu
Body_Text:
DR. Kalu Idika Kalu former Finance and National Planning Minister
answers questions on the recurring fuel crises and points the way forward
just as he propounds ways to economic reforms that need to take place to
make up for the subsidy from oil if oil prices are to be kept as minimal as
necessary. Here are excerpts:
Uncertainties have enveloped the nation over the incessant increase
of petroleun products. It is a re-curing problem that seem to have defied
solutions. How would you propose that this problem be resolved?
I have said this several times and have been saying it for over eighteen
years. Since 1984, I have been talking about the need to have some economic
reforms, the need for reviewing the Land Use decree, the need for
macro-economic adjustments towards the exchange rate, the need to privatise,
the need to cancel the Enterprises Promotion Decree. Now, when we talk about
system price increase as deregulation, it's nonsense. The price increase in
petroleum products is not deregulation. Deregulation is not a situation
where competition is restricted and therefore an artificial price is
maintained because of the competition. Deregulation means where you allow
more competition and therefore prices should really come down.
What is happening in Nigeria now as a result fuel price hike is really
unnecessary. The price hike is not necessary and the consequence which was
the shelved strike could have been avoided if things are done properly.
I have been addressing this issue since the mid-80s. The mistake we made
was that before that time, I asked for a simple discreet devaluation. We
said no, we are afraid of the world devaluation. We said we wanted to do
second-tier foreign exchange market. Now, see where the mistake of the past
has landed us. You may not understand this, but as the exchange rate goes
down, when you now look at domestic prices including the price of oil using
international currency like the dollar, then, it is as if our price is
unusually low. The only way to get a handle on this is to go back to our
level of investment. That means you should mobilise funds that you need to
apply to different projects, raise the level of production and afterwards,
you can stabilise prices. You ensure the exchange rate is at that
point. Only then can you use that exchange rate to determine what the
equivalent international price of domestic products are. As long as the
exchange rate continues to go down, for instance, per litre is N11. Maybe
N11 was seven cents, then when N11 drops to N18, the seven cents now become
N5. When it goes to N30, that N30 becomes two cents. That is how the subsidy
keeps emerging.
There would not be need for this present problem if things were properly
handled in the first instance. What government should have done is to
explicitly own up that the oil industry is grossly mismanaged. The way to
correct this mismanagement is to have the right people to run the
industry. So many countries have refineries like we do and if you go to
these countries, the subject of discussion is not turn-around
maintenance. Of course, the refineries should be maintained. Deregulation
means that as we give incentives to investors, people can now come in and
invest in oil refineries.
Fuel price hike is not necessary. Left to me, we should go back to the
old price. We should identify the subsidy involved at selling at the old
price to those who are importing. Then, we should put that on the table. How
do we fund this subsidy? We should then begin to prune down expenditure in
other areas, prune down government expenditure, enhance production in other
areas for export and spread the funding of the subsidy over years. Is it not
strange that a country that is exporting over 2.2 million barrels of oil a
day is in this type of mess? The oil sector should have been deregulated a
long time ago.
Let me also say this, those who continue to blackmail the Nigeria people
by talking about the unity of the country should know that Nigerians have no
other place but Nigeria, but those so-called leaders who at every turn start
quoting keeping the country together as what they have achieved or what they
hope to achieve should know that we are about 125 million captives in this
country being blackmailed by them. It has to stop. We have to get back to
where we got it wrong and begin to get it right. Strike is the offshoot of
the peoples' frustration. It could be avoided.
This problem was not addressed during Nigeria's 43rd anniversary
speech. How come President Obasanjo was silent on this but he announced
other socio-economic reforms he intends to embark on?
The basic things he did through his speech was to encourage Nigerians to
be more optimistic and look forward to better times. That's what I got out
of his anniversary speech. He made mention of certain areas and looking at
it, I think this is not the period to sweep things under the carpet
especially in areas like agriculture. We talk about agriculture, we
romanticise. But the big question is, have we taken how much we got out of
the land? How much are we using to make agriculture what it should be? When
you talk of economic development, you talk of agriculture becoming more and
more modernized, what about movement of agricultural proceeds, as in
construction of roads, market facilities, farmers using the proceeds to
reinvest?
Then you talk about packaging too, talk about facilities that would
encourage the farmers to save money like credit facilities, extension
facilities, information provided to them as to how others who are producing
cocoa, groundnuts, palm-oil, yam are being funded and managed. Even
agriculture is a major industry. Then, there is the industrial sector like
solid minerals and the oil and gas, bitumen. All of these require a lot of
resources to develop. There are also the services sector, the
infrastructure, water, health, education, energy, transportation,
communication. These also require massive funding. Why are these sectors so
anaemic? We have not even started. Look at textile industry. We can export
all these and use the proceeds to fund the subsidy removed from oil. At this
time, Nigeria should be talking of what the IMF or the World Bank helped
them to do. But we have a mental block which has prevented us from doing
massive investment on top of the ones we had so that we can modernise
transportation, so that we can be talking about telecoms revolution instead
just installing three GSM companies. We must have companies that produce
chips, that produce the handsets, that produce the micro-waves and all that
go into making for a revolution in communication. We should not just be
talking about assembling Peugeot and others, we should be building the
electrical industry, the plastic industry, the leather industry.
We have all the raw materials but it required a large dose of
money. That large dose of money is not implicit in N70 billion budget or a
N100 billion. By the time you adjust the exchange rate for the policy that
we implemented, it depreciated the naira so that when you calculated our
budget and investments and savings into the real capital that we required to
make the kind of investment we're talking about, we find that we need to
break loose from this mental block and begin to see how the Russians and the
Chinese and the Brazilians and the Argentinians and the Vietnamese did
it. Many of them saved heavily, 25% of GMT but they also borrowed heavily
because they can identify the project against which they make the
investment. At this stage of Nigeria's history, we should get out of this
mental block where we say that people borrowed money and put in their
pockets. We should ask ourselves, how come other countries borrowed money
and utilise them? When are we going to stop giving this alibi? When are
going to grapple without debts and say yes, this is what we really owe,
these are the creditors we cannot pay and so, we need to be given a grace
period, a time-frame within which we can restructure this debt.
Now, we have so many projects that we have to implement to employ our
people, to feed our people, to clothe our people, to provide houses for our
people, to provide them with basic needs: clean water, medication in
hospitals and so on. If we take this loan, in seven years we should have put
that money including our own to such good use such that the load of the debt
is even lighter because of the growth out of which we repay the debt. This
has become difficult to understand. The professionals are so shallow by and
large and the people are at loss over the happenings including our own to
such good use such that the load of the debt is even higher because of the
growth out of which we repay the debt. This has become difficult to
understand. The professionals are so shallow by and large and the people are
at loss over the happenings in the country. Look at the industries. Where
are the Aladjas and the Ajaokutas?  Several years ago, we should have taken
one look at Ajaokuta and say no, this is no longer feasible. The technology
is ancient. With much money, within one year, we should start a modern steel
industry which will really now provide steel to industries that need steel
so that their own products would sell at the market. All these notion of
trying to refurbish a steel mill that is outdated is not necessary. All we
need is a contemporary framework that can be implemented, we have to decide
that we must put experienced people in the oil-sectors, the gas
sectors. These need a lot more fund than we can muster because we have to
totally restructure.
We still have geniuses in this country. How come they go abroad and
do very well?
We still produce skilled, technically proficient people with knowledge
of the mode of communication and operation. We are talking about the
computer and allied industry. If you go round various institutions in the
world, there are a lot of grounds prepared for these. We need to get
ourselves off our high horse, that taking loan is for poorer nations. We
should stop stuffing ourselves with funny notions of how wealthy we are, how
great we are. We should come down and plan very well. We need a
knowledgeable ministry of planning headed by a minister who knows what he is
talking about. The same thing for the oil sector. We need a cabinet which
reports to the people, which can lay out the plans of how Nigeria can
proceed, how we can turn the whole riverine areas of resources of our oil
into places like Dubai, not funny looking bungalows , funny-looking clinics
and ill-equipped schools. It's a travesty. It's unfair. It's unjust. The
funds are there. And above all, these resources constitute a heavy
collateral. When the cash flow can show they are viable, people will
rush. That is what globalisation is all about. That is what private sector
operation is all about. And private sector does not mean Nigerian private
sector. It's the whole global village where people can transfer funds,
invest in Nigeria from Hawaii, from many places, they don't even need to
know where these funds are located. But they need to know that the legal
framework is there, they need the knowledge that the cash and carry are
prudent and they are independent of any political manipulation in the given
country. These are the tasks that we need to address. Not general statements
of whether its agriculture, industry, power or whatever. We should get away
from what people want to hear. At this stage of Nigeria's history, we should
carry the flag which says law and order must be there, which says elections
must be free and fair, which says the people who deserves to be put in
positions should be the people there because its by their expertise and
merit and experience that they can expand the opportunity for others. We
need people who will fly the flag which says what is good for ten thousand
Nigerians is good for ten million Nigerians, what is good for one region,
that what's good for others, who must be totally blameless. I know its
almost impossible to get such leaders but at least elections can be free and
fair and just. That is what leadership is all about. I'm not just talking
about the presidency, I'm talking about all levels of leadership: in
parastals, in industries, universities and all levels of governance, so that
when something happens like we have the Bola Ige, Okadigbo cases, such
circumstances as bomb exploding and killing many people, somebody would come
out and tell the people what exactly happened, re-assert the rule of law and
get to the bottom of it all. If there are negative allegations being made,
such should be investigated. That is what a leader should be.
By this time, in a decent country, investigations on allegations as to
what really killed Okadigbo should have begun even independent of
government. Nothing should stop the police or the Ministry of Justice from
investigating the allegations and telling the public the outcome. The
Ministry of Justice, the Secret Service, the National Intelligence Agency,
these are Nigerians too. They should feel it the way the Igbos and non-Igbos
would feel it irrespective of whether a northern or westerner or easterner
was involved. The question of Okadigbo being Igbo should not even come
up. Before that comes up, it should already be known that somebody is taking
care to get to the bottom of it. At this stage in Nigeria, general
statements, soothing statements which people can puncture by the very music
they hear around them shouldn't be made. I'm not saying that the allegations
are true. What I'm saying is that behind the statements made, one can act
and get the substantive information of what really happened. That's really
beside the point. The issue is not an ethnic issue. It's being trivialised
when its made an ethnic issue. It's an issue of civil liberty and freedom
for all Nigerians and that is how it should be addressed.

Original_Search_Title: It's Sad That We Still Import Fuel - Sen. Olabiyi Durojaiye
Headline: Nigeria: It's Sad That We Still Import Fuel - Sen. Olabiyi Durojaiye
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Can you review the outcome of 2003 elections in the South-West zone?
That election has come and gone. It is now a matter of history. We
should leave what happened at the election to God and bar of history to
judge.
What do you mean by leaving it at the bar of history?
It was not the first time election rigging was done in this country and
we all know the consequences.
Do you have any regrets for not going back to the senate?
No, at all. No regrets whatsoever although sometimes as a person you
have nostalgic feeling that if I were there, I would have done so, and so
things in so and so ways.
But overall I have no regrets.
How do you see Nigeria at 43?
I have to thank God that we are still alive and still one country
despite all odds. It is however lamentable to reflect on so many missed
opportunities, bad mistakes and apparent bungling of affairs at all
levels.
What are some of the positive things you can point out that the
country has achieved?
Our founding fathers won independence on the platter of gold. We have
remained a united country despite a bitter civil war. We continue to
multiply in population. We have a few things - bounties of nature, near
deluge of oil and gas which many countries don't have.
Our country has gained more prominence in Africa and international
affairs. We have skilled manpower though unfortunately a good size of it has
emigrated overseas, others are still itching to go. However, the negative
developments are daunting and virtually dwarfed the positive
achievements.
How do you react to the recent fuel price hike?
The issue is not the hike in fuel price but the timing. It surprises me
and the fact that we still have to import fuel despite four refineries
installed at heavy costs. It is difficult to understand the rationale for
choosing this time around to hike the prices of petroleum products. It is an
embarrassing manifestation of disdain and lack of consideration for the
feelings of the masses to allow a price hike at a time Africans and the
world are focused on Nigeria.
You can as well react to the general strike over fuel price hike?
Strikes do not normally augur well for productivity because it means
withholding of services which results in starvation. But can anyone in good
conscience blame labour in view of this apparent provocation or
insensitivity on the part of government?
Labour appears to have been pushed to the wall.
What are your expectations from Obasanjo's second term government?
I have no personal expectations but my expectations for the people are
that the government should try to assuage sufferings, reduce the pains of
hunger by creating jobs for millions of qualified, healthy and capable
Nigerian youths who, being unemployed, are pushed to crime.
I expect this government to do something about making lives and property
safer in this country. Security of lives and property is rather poor.
These are my expectations.
How do you see the recent deaths of two of your former colleagues in
the Senate, Dr. Chuba Okadigbo and Professor Afolabi Olabitam?
Sad. I feel very sad. Okadigbo was a man of great glamour and charm,
radiance and brilliance. The same feeling of remorse and pain I have on
Okadigbo, I equally have on late professor Olabitam. Three of us, Dr. Femi
Okorounmu, Olabitam and myself, represented Ogun state and we earned the
joint accolade of being among the best state representatives in the
senate.
Late Senator Olabitam's quiet dignity, maturity and philosophical
approach to life and issues of life make him so respected and so sadly
missed.
In the case of Distinguished Senator Chuba Okadigbo, the glamorous "Oyii
of Oyi", not only the senate but the whole nation will miss his dynamic
contributions to national issues and his courageous fight to ensure that
democracy squarely rests on the principles of separation of powers.
As a prominent member of Yoruba socio-political organisation,
Afenifere, we wonder what the group is up to nowadays?
The organisation is well and kicking. The Yorubas need an organisation
like Afenifere always to look after their welfare domestically and in
relation with groups that are striving to form a modern nation -
Nigeria.
It is on record and commonly acknowledged that Afenifere was the largest
of the few progressive national groups that formed the National Democratic
Coalition, NADECO, which fought the military and secured democracy for this
country in the late 1990's.
It is also indisputable that Afenifere single-handedly toiled and
midwifed a national political organisation, Alliance for Democracy, AD.
All leading members of Afenifere and of the AD are well-appraised of my
personal opinion and strong belief that the focus and responsibility of
Afenifere should remain confined largely to the South-West zone of the
country and the Yoruba speaking people, both from within and outside
Nigeria. This is enough responsibility to task its energies and
resources.
The issue of building bridges across other nationalities within the
country with a view to participating in the formation of a national
government at the centre should be left to the AD or whatever political
configuration the AD may decide to work with.
As long as the Yoruba remain a part of this country we should play the
game according to the rules and join hands with other ethnic nations in
forming a modern, great and powerful national entity which Nigeria has not
yet achieved.
It is on record that NADECO of which Afenifere was a major and integral
part, mooted the idea of having a national conference whereby other national
units will come together and work out other modalities of building a modern
nation. That task has not yet been achieved and it is desirable that the
Yoruba join hands with others to build a virile nation - state,
Nigeria.
The AD did not go to Elections petitions tribunal despite its claim
that the elections in the South-West were rigged. Many people still cannot
understand why the party decided to let go?
Let me give you two proverbs in answer to your question. The Yoruba
proverb says, "it is not every wet cloth that you spread in the sun."
The Igbos say, "When grandmother's goat has eaten grandmother's yam,
what does she do - does she kill the goat?"
What happened to the AD - PDP accord?
That agreement has proved to be a fatal mistake but at the time it was
mooted, neither the movers nor those of us who consented to it, forsaw its
consequences. We must admit we all made a mistake.
Concerning 2007 elections, we wonder who between Atiku and IBB, the
Yoruba will back?
It is too early to talk about who will be president in 2007. There are
more pressing issues confronting the country today like finding employment
for the youths, ensuring stability, reducing corruption and so many economic
bunglings.
It is diversionary talking about 2007 elections at this moment, maybe,
in a year or two, the coast will be clearer.
But there are already steady campaigns for 2007 going on...
Who will be our president in year 2007 should not be our top priority
today. We have issues of unemployment, hardships arising from fuel price
hike which have adverse multiplier effects on people and every segment of
the economy as well as unwanton spread of corruption.

Original_Search_Title: Banda Chief Demands Probe Into Shooting Incident
Headline: Ghana: Banda Chief Demands Probe Into Shooting Incident
Body_Text:
Sunyani — Following the shooting incident at Banda-Ahenkro in the Wenchi district of the Brong Ahafo Region on September 15, this year, the paramount chief of the Banda traditional area, Nana Kwadwo Sito I, is pointing accusing fingers at the DCE for Wenchi, Mr. Joe Danquah, as the brain behind the incident.
The shooting incident in which 16 people were wounded, including the paramount chief, took place when the people of the area were celebrating their annual yam festival at Banda-Ahenkro.
The Kabronu clan, a faction in the Banda chieftaincy dispute, allegedly attacked the chief and his people as he was riding in a palanquin, on the way from the secret grove of the Banda stool.
But Hon. Danquah in an interview with The Chronicle said the chief was even lucky that he was not killed, because the security personnel had cautioned him not to visit the secret grove, because it is located at the area of the other faction at Kabronu, near Banda-Ahenkro.
In a petition addressed to the Minister of Justice and Attorney General, Nana Sito prayed that an committee be set up to investigate the matter.
According to the paramount chief, he has no confidence in the District Security Council (DISEC), because the DCE has a greater influence on the members of the DISEC.
Nana Sito in his petition stated that immediately after the shooting incident, it was reported to the police at Banda Gbao, but no arrest had been made and those who sustained injuries were not even invited for their statement to be taken.
He attributed the police inability to take action or cause the arrest of his assailants to the influence of the DCE on the police.
He stated that before the festival, he informed the Gbao police about his intention to celebrate this year's yam festival from September 12 to 17 and he was given the green light.
He added that even on the day of the shooting incident, the police were following them and saw the shooters, yet they failed to effect any arrest.
He said even though the DCE is aware that the Supreme Court had ruled in favour of him, he has refused to recognize him as the Paramount chief of the area.
He lamented that the DCE, being a Banda himself, has accepted the loser in the Banda chieftaincy dispute as a legitimate one.
The chief explained that on May 16, 2001, the Supreme Court gave its ruling on the Banda chieftaincy dispute and declared him the winner and the paramount chief of the area.
Later in interview with the paper to throw more light on the incident, Nana Sito disclosed that Hon. Danquah on several occasions prevented some of his sub-chiefs from responding to his invitations.
Nana Sito accused Mr. Danquah of posing as the paramount chief of the area and even resorted to signing some social responsibility documents supposed to be signed by he the chief.
He cited an instance when the DCE prevented members of an ad hoc committee on the proposed Tain district to be carved out of Wenchi district from attending a meeting at his palace at Banda-Ahenkro when it was the turn of his paramountcy to host the committee.
The said meeting, according to him, was held at Banda-Buasi. The reason for preventing the committee, as revealed to The Chronicle, was that the DCE warned them of possible clash in the town if they dare to attend the meetings there.
The DCE in a telephone interview with the paper denied the allegation, but said the chief was advised not to visit the secret grove, because of its location.
According to the DCE, the security personnel had foreseen the dangerous situation ahead of visiting the site, hence the warning.
On the Supreme Court ruling, Hon. Danquah said that there are many confusing documents from various factions in the Banda dispute, indicating that the court had ruled in favour of them. But he however referred this reporter to the Regional House of Chiefs to find out who is the paramount chief of Banda.
Hon. Danquah lamented that Nana Sito is playing politics with the Banda stool, which does not augur well for the people of the area, adding that the people of Banda need development, but in a place of conflict, it is impossible for development to take place.
The DCE advised the chief to refrain from politics to pave way for development at Banda.
On the chief's claim that the DCE is posing as the paramount chief in the area, Hon. Danquah said he is not interested in the Banda chieftancy, adding that "I am not belonging to any of the two royal families in the area, so I don't support any of the factions."
On the social responsibility that the chief accused him of signing, the DCE told the paper that he only appended his signature on the document as a witness and not as a chief.

Original_Search_Title: Violence Mars New Yam Festivalin Asaba
Headline: Nigeria: Violence Mars New Yam Festivalin Asaba
Body_Text:
Asaba — A woman was feared dead while many people were injured in an orgy of violence, which erupted in Asaba, the Delta State capital penultimate Sunday, when youths, who took advantage of the new yam festival marked in the town on the day, laid siege to it, burning completely a State government owned peugeot 504 salon car and vandalising many others.
Activities of the day, which climaxed the week long celebration of the new yam festival, referred to locally as ' Eze-Ugbo', had snowballed into mayhem, leading to a break down of law and order which saw the youths confronting and assaulting law abiding citizens and raping women.
The youths had invaded the works Department of the 'A' police station, where they picked iron rods with the intention to further unlease terror on innocent people on the street of Asaba but for the policemen, who resisted them.
Irked by the violent attitude and the invasion of thier station by the youths, the policemen confronted them, battled them to a stand still and succeeded in dispersing them after throughly tear gasing them.
A police source later confirmed the arrest of 19 of the youlths, condemning their attitude while saying that they went too far in thier invasion of the police station.
That Sunday, the youths were seen on Unebisi Road, armed with dangerous weapons, bottles and threatening innocent people, who scampered to safety for fear of being molested by them.
Reacting to the mayhem at a media briefing in Asaba, the State Commissioner of Police, Mr. charles Akaya, said the rampaging youths also dispossessed an inspector of police of his uniform.
He gave the number of the peugeot 504 saloon car burnt by them as DT18A19, belonging to the State Ministry of Economic Planning.
Akaya, who also cofirmed the arrest of 19 persons in connection with the mayhem, also stated that they would be charged to court after investigation was concluded into the incident.
He berated Asaba yoults over their conducts during such festivals, saying that it has become a common feature during any festival. He said the police would no longer tolerate such acts of lawlessness from any quarters.
Akaya warned that anybody caught unleashing violence on innocent people would be treated as a criminal, assuring that the police would always carry out its lawful duties of maintaining law and order.
In another development, the CP said youths of the town, who invaded Okwe, a surburb of Asaba, and distroyed properties penultimate weekend would be prosecuted.
Though he said no arresst has been made, he stated that the driver of the pick-up van seized by the youths and used for the invasion, was helping the police in the identification of youths involved in the act.
The commissioner further said on September 20, this year, "some youths of Umuaji, armed with dangerous weapons, as guns and matchets, went into Okwe and damaged people's building"
Explaining further, he said a pick-up van with registration No. AM 295 LND and driven by one Emmanuel, was commanded and used while the carnaged lasted, saying that the youths eventually abandoned the pick up vehicle and made good their escape
"This is a clear case of armed robbery and malicious damage. They will be located, arrested and prosecuted as such," the police boss declared.
Beside saying that investigation was being carried out by the police, with the assistance of the driver, Akaya assured that the perpetrators' would soon be fished out.
It would be recalled that the peole of Asaba and their counterpart in Okwe, both of Oshimili South Local Government Area of Delta State, have been at logger-heads over the ownership of parcels of land.
This culminated in a violent confrontation three months ago, and left on its trail burnt houses and several injured people on the side of Okwe.
During the crisis, non-indigenes, residing in Okwe village fled in their hundreds to take refuge at the Government Estate in the area.
Concerted peace efforts were, however, being made lately by the member, representing the council in the State House of Assembly, Mrs. Chinwe Monu-Olanrewaju and the Council's Chairman, Dr. Mordu Ononye, between the two communities.

Original_Search_Title: Refuse Takes Over Karmo Market
Headline: Nigeria: Refuse Takes Over Karmo Market
Body_Text:
Karmo market which is one of the busiest and cheap commodities market in
the Federal Capital Territory (FCT) has become a dumping place for refuse
especially after the weekly Tuesday market when the area is patronised by
all categories of traders and buyers from other surrounding villages and the
city.
With the site of the market in a village which lacks social amenities
and good roads, the market stretches along both sides of the road from the
last gate of Karmo daily market to the bus stop of old Karmo covering a
distance of two and a half kilometres.
The market, due to the density of the population of the village and the
number of visitors may be the dirtiest market in the city since traders not
only throw waste materials around the locality, but abandon their rotten
food stuff after the market, thereby creating stench and health hazards for
the residents of the area.
Apart from the motor park, a small space in which commuter buses
commonly called "molue" park after the daily business with residents, other
parts of the market is nothing to rejoice with in terms of sanitation and
plan.
The motor park which is mostly occupied by food sellers in carelessly
swept every Wednesday even as unsold food stuffs are piled under broken
vehicles at the park making the scene an eyesore especially after a heavy
rain.
While some visitors blame authorities for the deteriorating sanitary
condition of the market, others say the traders, most especially food
sellers are responsible for dumping left over foodstuffs along the
roadsides, though wheel barrow pushers abound on market days.
One of the visitors, Mrs. Jenet Ogbeche who said she lives in Area one,
Garki, not only expressed displeasure over the dirty condition of the market
but also complained of the food stuffs, the refuse and the sellers who
according to her, are not "hygienic" physically irrespective of the kind of
food they sell to people.
"It is a pity that the food sellers seem not to know what is
healthcare. They put their stuffs on refuse and sell to the people. But
unfortunately, people don't even mind from where they buy their items. Even
some of the sellers are so dirty that one would not doubt their food items
are unhealthy. Some even wash their yams, potatoes and fruits in the dirty
waters of gutters and drainage's," Mrs. Ogbeche observed.
Another woman who comes from Gwagwalada, blamed the traders for the
harzards even as she wondered what the money collected from traders was used
for.
"If the authorities, don't clean the market, does it mean the traders
can not clean where they put their commodities. If every trader cleans his
or her surrounding for the good health of the customers the market will
always be tidy," she opined.
But the authorities should do something better and faster. The market
has existed for decades and is supposed to be one of the most patronise in
the city since the rich come to buy their weekly needs from here," the woman
said.
Karmo market is not only famous for cheap commodities but also for
assorted materials because traders on weekly bases come from different
states of the country to sell and buy.

Original_Search_Title: The Search for a Righteous Man
Headline: Nigeria: The Search for a Righteous Man
Body_Text:
Lagos — Cont'd last week
There is also the frightening phenomenon of cultism and cult-related
violence which has claimed many lives and has refused to bow to threats and
pressure from the government and the authorities of the institutions. Part
of the explanation for its tenacity and resilience must be found in the
reluctance of the police and the judiciary to be diligent in arresting,
prosecuting and convicting these common criminals, either because they
themselves are members of senior cults or because they bow to the lure of
financial inducement from the well placed parents of the cult members.
The academic staff are no better, for they now participate in such vices
as grade fixing in return for financial inducement, extortion of money or
sexual favours from students, and compressing a whole semester's course into
a few lectures lasting not more than 20 per cent of the time allocated for
the course, thereby short-changing the students and the nation. The younger
lecturers, who tend to engage in these vices more, are also so deficient
intellectually that they often resort to plagiarism in their lectures,
hand-outs and even academic publications.
Popular Corruption:
Because we the elite have been so irresponsible in the brazen manner in
which we engage in corruption and other vices, we have emboldened the masses
to also emulate our bad ways. In the past, no villager would ever think of
'vandalising a public building or facility in his village simply because it
is left unattended. Now, the story is different: even building blocks are
removed from abandoned government building sites by villagers or local
people who should be the beneficiaries of the project. Their moral reasoning
is: everybody steals government property, so let us also have our share!
Another instance of popular corruption occurred recently when the
Nigerian Government sent an aircraft to evacuate stranded Nigerian citizens
from embattled Monrovia While most of the Nigerians seized the opportunity
to fly into the safety of home, some smart ones among them decided instead
to sell the boarding passes given to them for the flight after due screening
to some desperate Liberians who were trying to escape from the war in their
own country. Upon the plane's arrival in Lagos, Nigerian Immigration
officials stumbled upon the Liberians, and the puzzle was only solved when
the smart Nigerians at the other end, now sensing that Monrovia was becoming
too dangerous to live in, moved into the Nigerian Embassy Compound in
Monrovia and held the staff hostage, demanding to be repatriated home.
Indeed the tradition of honesty is fast disappearing even within family
relationships. How many people sitting in this hall today have been cheated
by their uncles, brothers or in-laws when they made the mistake of
entrusting the building of their village houses to such relatives, only to
be swindled?
Explaining Nigerian Corruption and Moral Decay
Corruption and moral decay in Nigeria can be explained by recourse to
several factors. In addition to those cited in the literature review, the
following perspectives are hereby offered:
Cultural Transition:
Nigerians, as citizens of modern nation-states, are still in transition
from a traditional to a modern socio-political system. Even in the West,
their present system took several centuries to evolve and become fully
ingrained in their psyche. It is too much to expect that within forty-three
years of taking our nation s destiny in our hands we could simply make a
complete transition to another system of moral values. This is why even
among our first and best leaders, there were allegations of impropriety. But
those leaders, may their souls rest in peace, were saints whatever their
faults may have been, compared to the present set of leaders, most of whom
are not mere sinners but devils in comparison. The late Prime Minister of
Nigeria, Tafawa Balewa, for instance, saved money for six years from his
salary as Minister of Transport and Workers in the colonial government
between 1952 and 1957 in order to be able to perform the Muslim
pilgrimage. Some of our present-day State Governors, who are in charge of
relatively tiny portions of what Balewa controlled in his time, now dip
their hands into their state treasuries and rake in millions of Naira to
sponsor themselves, members of their families and official parasites for the
pilgrimage thereby betraying the aspirations of millions of their citizens
who clamour for Sharia in order to return to what they perceive as an ideal
value system which guarantees equity to the ruled and selfless and honest
service from the rulers.
The tensions inherent in this transition from tradition to modernity are
visible in the reluctance of Nigerian leaders to relinquish power, because
deep in our psyche, we believe that between power and the powerful, only
death does them part. It is also apparent in the resistance of our leaders
to any checks and balances on the exercise of such power, especially where
restrictions are applied in their access to or control of public funds. Why
should any body tell the King what he can or cannot use the Kingdom's
resources for? This is in spite of the fact that the Nigerian constitution
is one of the most elaborate in the world in giving details of the principle
of separation of power.
Impact of Military Rule: Loss of Accountability and Job Security
Many of the values of the modem system of governance that we had begun
to imbibe were obliterated by the impact of military rule. The greatest
casualty of this was accountability. The post-colonial civil service was
well-trained to hold political leaders to account, and the civil servants
clearly told ministers and other political heads in writing that such and
such a thing that the had done or proposed to do was not in line with the
G.O. (Government Orders) and must be reversed or must not be done. The
political heads in turn respected the rules and were careful not to bulldoze
their way. However, with the mass purge of the civil service in 1975, which
was targeted at the so-called 'super- permanent secretaries', civil servants
became too timid to caution their political bosses. Accountability was dealt
the final blow that killed it' by the 1979 and subsequent Constitutions
which made political heads 'executive' and also politicised appointments
into senior posts in the civil service. Under the current dispensation, no
permanent secretary, accountant-general or auditor-general would, in his
right senses, dare caution or challenge any action of his political boss,
especially His Excellency himself" Another consequence of the mass purge of
1975 is the loss of job security in the civil service and the resultant
stampede by civil servants to steal as much as possible while still in
service since they could be sacked at any time 'with immediate effect'.
Impact of the Civil War
The Nigerian civil war did a lot of violence to our public morality. It
cheapened human life and took away the shock associated with its loss from
us. It created desperation, especially among the immediate victims of the
war, among us which made the end justify the means, which made survival the
first law of existence and materialism the reason for existence. Crimes and
vices which gained prominence as a direct consequence of this assault on our
collective moral code include armed robbery, prostitution, promiscuity,
advanced fee fraud, drug trafficking and official corruption.
Poverty, Insecurity and Underdevelopment
Nigeria's high level of poverty as discussed earlier is a consequence of
the greed, indiscipline and irresponsibility of the Nigerian elite. After
forty-three years of independence, and therefore forty- three budget cycles,
we are still undeveloped as discussed earlier because we do not use the
resources of the nation for the whole nation 'but for ourselves as the
elite. For example, an analysis of the 2()()3 budget proposals submitted by
the President to the last National Assembly shows that recurrent expenditure
is allocated 66.5per cent while capital expenditure is allocated only 35per
cent. Given our level of underdevelopment, the proportions should be
reversed. When the components of the recurrent and capital budgets are
further analysed, it is found their personnel costs, that is to say the
payment of the salaries and allowances of (Federal) civil servants (the
elite and the sub-elite) account for 45per cent of the whole budget and
67.5per cent of the recurrent budget. Overhead costs, on the other hand,
which should ideally be used to procure tools and materials to justify the
enormous wage bill, account for only 21.6 per cent of the total budget and
35per cent of the recurrent budget. Using this development model, it is
quite clear that we shall never reach the promised land of development.
Additionally, as discussed earlier, there is a wide gap between us and
the common Nigerians. We members of the Nigerian elite live sometimes more
comfortably than our counterparts of similar status even in developed
countries. Compare. for instance. all the palatial houses, the variety of
cars and personal staff of a Minister of the Federal Republic of Nigeria
with the single official car (no official house) of a British Minister. ()r
even compare the level of comfort of a Nigerian university vice-chancellor,
who lives in a free house and is fed at public expense in most universities,
and who has at least three cars and at least six servants paid for by the
university to at the end to his needs with a British vice-chancellor who has
only one official car, one official house and no more.
But the people who really live big are the politicians, especially when
they are in power. the top civil servants, the chief executives of
parastatals and the top members of the military and paramilitary elite. The
accomplished politician or other 'big man' usually has a palatial house at
his main base, which may be his state capital, a regional capital or Abuja
This costs about N25 million to build and furnish, complete with a stand by
Senator and all other accessories of comfort. He also has a befitting house
in is -village, if that is not his main base. This should cost about N 10
million. Then he has a guest-house in another major city (such as a regional
capital or Abuja if that is not where he is usually resident) which should
cost another N10 million. As for cars. there should be at least 7 of them: a
4-wheel drive for his long journeys- cost: N7 million: a posh car for normal
use in the town or city where he is ordinarily resident cost: N5 million;
Madam's car- cost: N3 million; a utility car for the family -cost: N3
million; 3 "olalnbo' cars for the teen-age children cost: N3 million. The
total capital cost of this life style is N66 million. This does not include
the cost of a house in London or New York, which is an optional extra The
recurrent cost of this life-style is estimated to be 141 million per month
to cover feeding, clothing, payment of staff salaries, utility bills,
children's education expenses, social and community taxes et cetera. This
comes to N12 million a year, to which we must add a N5 million provision for
trips abroad for medical check ups, relaxation or spiritual regeneration or
a combination of these. Thus the annual recurrent costs of maintaining this
lifestyle 'will be in the region of N 17 million. If this is added to the
capital costs, the sum total is N83 million for the first year. This money,
stolen (or extorted, if the official is in service) from the public treasury
in various ways, represents the annual income of more than 1665 Nigerians at
the generous rate of 140 per day.
The need to-achieve this kind of life-style is a strong driving force
and fuel for corruption. The need to mountain the life style once achieved
provides further impetus to accumulate more and more, helices the seemingly
insatiable appetite of members of the Nigerian elite for filthy lucre. The
example set by the top political and bureaucratic leadership senses as a
model for people lower down the socio- political scale. This explains why
almost everybody who has the chance steals and steals and steals! J P Clark
puts it graphically in his poem Lyong of the New Millionaires: L9O close to
the desert or forest, Out of which we all come. We fear forest or desert
will overtake Us in our new city stronghold.
So close to the desert or forest Out of which we all come, How shall we
find rest In our new beds of gold?
That land never knows peace uhere a few have too much and many none at
all.
The yam of this world is enough for all mouths which pay daily homage to
the god of the throat enough for the aged awaiting day's dusk and the young
peeping at tomorrow from mother's backs
But alas, men forge unequal knives a few slashing the yam with machetes
greedier than Esimuda's sword leaving mere peelings for the many others
Epilogue: The Road to Rectitude
The decline in public morality and the high level of corruption in
Nigeria are leading the nation to its own destruction. The responsibility
for the low level of morality in the conduct of our public affairs is that
of the elite collectively!! and the ruling elite specifically. Throughout
history and in all nations, the elite always set the rules of public conduct
and determine who gets what and how and who does what and how. Sometimes
they become over-selfish in skewing the rules completely in their favour and
pushing the masses to the wall. When this occurs. the masses, usually led by
a break-away faction of the elite, take over power, sometimes violently and
eventually a new set of elite take over from the discredited ones.
In present-day Nigeria, owing to the greed, indiscipline and
irresponsibility of the elite in slashing the biggest and best portions of
the 'national yam' for themselves, thereby creating hunger, frustration and
desperation among the majority of Nigerians, all the signs are that the
state is on the verge of collapse, unless something urgent is done to rescue
it. The collapse of the state may not take the relatively orderly form of a
revolution. It may simply take the form of violence, looting and anarchy
directed against symbols of the state and all members of the elite. Already,
the foot soldiers for such an eventuality exist in the form of ethnic
militia, area boys and other marginalised groups. During the recent
nation-wide strike against fuel price increases, some of these area boys
positioned themselves along the Kaduna-Abuja expressway and forced all
motorists to chant certain political slogans before allowing them to pass
through. Some of the young men also volunteered this warning to the
motorists in Hausa: 'Set us free before we come into the city of Abuja, for
we are vicious and wicked and our entry will be bloody.' In order to avoid
such a catastrophe, we must, as stakeholders in this country,, engineer a
change of attitude and a change in the ground rules by which the nation is
run through, among other things, the following:
Aggressive and well-co-ordinated poverty reduction, employment creation
and wealth-creation policies which would create more national wealth through
higher productivity and re-distribute it so that the inequality gulf could
be considerably narrowed
Deliberate policies to reduce conspicuous consumption and re-instate
discipline in national life
Improvement in the compensation packages of public officers from the
lowest to the highest to make them more realistic, including severance and
retirement packages in order to improve the sense of security of public
officers and reduce the temptation to corruption
Reduction in the size of the civil service workforce to one third of its
present level over a period of five years in order to have a smaller but
more efficient service which would also cost less to maintain, so that more
money would be spent for real development than for civil service maintenance
Further de-regulation, reform and transparent privatisation of key
sectors of the economy in order to reduce opportunities for public sector
corruption
Inforcement of sanctions against those found guilty of corruption. which
should include the seizure of their loot and appropriate additional
punishment, such as imprisonment
Repatriation of Nigerian looted funds from foreign countries (estimated
to be in the region of $100 billion compared to our total external debt of
about $29) billion) which should be used to liquidate the debt and improve
infrastructure nationally and also create employment for our people
The repatriation of the funds should be preceded by a thorough but
discreet investigation into what each government official has in his or her
foreign account, how they acquired it etc., after which they would be
confronted with the evidence and be given the choice to surrender the money
quietly, in return for protection from public exposure, or risk a full-scale
prosecution in the courts
However, all this and more cannot be done without an honest and
transparently clean political leadership, which, unfortunately, we do not
have in place at the present-time. The search for this kind of leadership,
within the framework of popular democracy and the rule of law, should be the
preoccupation of all of us.
Concluded

Original_Search_Title: Gov't to Gazette Nakivubo Swamp As a Protected Area
Headline: Uganda: Gov't to Gazette Nakivubo Swamp As a Protected Area
Body_Text:
Kampala — PROSSY Nalubwama was under siege. Her belongings were in ruins after the
floods poured into the house as she slept. She held on to a floating empty
jerrycan until she was rescued.
Her ordeal is not strange to the neighbours. They know that her house is
one of those that are worst hit by floods.
This scene in Kalerwe is common in Kampala's suburbs, which are prone to
floods. As the rainy season approaches, the tenants shift to escape the
menacing floods. New unsuspecting tenants usually replace them in the dry
season.
More than half a million people in Kampala, especially the low-income
earners, have been suffering quietly as a result of floods.
"We know about the plight of the people, but it is their fault," says
Robert Wabunoha, a senior environmental lawyer of the National Environment
Management Authority (NEMA).
Wabunoha says many people in Kampala have over the years built houses in
the valleys where water naturally flows. Because the water has nowhere else
to go, it floods people's homes.
Wabunoha says government has come up with new environmental laws to
protect the ecologically sensitive areas such as wetlands.
As the wetlands, which used to hold enormous quantities of water become
no more, the city has begun witnessing a catastrophe.
"It is becoming a routine to lose lives when floods hit the city," says
Paul Mafabi, the assistant commissioner in charge of wetlands in the
Ministry of Water, Lands and Environment.
Mafabi says most wetlands in Kampala have been cleared for human
settlement and industries.
In a recent tour organised by Shelter and Settlements, an NGO, the
low-lying Kifumbira slum that separates Mulago hill from Bukoto was found to
have countless pools of water. The pools provide a breeding ground for
mosquitoes and create a dirty environment that favours cholera. The area
suffered most from past cholera outbreaks.
This is because the latrines are built above water streams. During rains
the area residents usually open a hole to release faeces from the
latrines. The rain then washes away the faeces to streams, from where they
fetch water.
However, not many people have access to toilet facilities. Some defecate
in polythene bags, which they throw into the stream. This has earned buveera
the name: "flying toilet."
There are also heaps of unclaimed garbage because the crowded houses
leave only tiny passages, which can not be accessed by garbage
collectors.
Rwandume Mugizi, the KCC environment inspector, says Kampala has
swallowed up the greenery that once covered the empty hills and
valleys.
"We have a concrete jungle, which does not allow water filtration in the
soil and most of the water runs above the ground," he says.
Mugizi points out that most of the land in Kampala belongs to
individuals. This makes it difficult for the city council to plan and ensure
that houses are built to the required standards.
"The authorities plan for land that is not in their hands."
He says the lack of policies on housing has led to haphazard development
in Kampala and its suburbs. Mugizi also blames old laws that did not
recognise wetlands as important ecological areas. It is only in 1995 that a
law was put in place to protect wetlands. Henceforth it became illegal to
encroach on a wetland.
When the floods hit Kampala early this year, the former minister of
environment, Dr. Kezimbira Miyingo, issued a directive that all houses in
wetlands should be demolished.
But Phoebe Gubya, the KCC environment officer, complains about the
directive saying there is need for compensation. He says landowners claim
that they did not know they were building on wetlands.
However, Mafabi says there is no need for compensation for the houses
built after 1995 when a law was put in place to protect wetlands.
Mafabi says some of the wetlands such as the Nakivubo swamp, purify
waste water before it enters Lake Victoria. In so doing they protect the
lake from pollution.
"Soon the authorities will be mining water from filth as the mouth of
the lake is located a stone's throw away from Ggaba Water Works," he
says.
He says efforts are in advanced stages to gazette the Nakivubo wetland
as a protected area, and to stop fresh encroachment on it.
He says that after gazetting the Nakivubo swamp, the team will move in
to save Kinawataka wetland.
The war to protect wetlands has seen government taking contradictory
positions. As the government prepares to evict over 10,000 yam growers from
the Nakivubo wetland, a few rich owners of houses in Bugolobi who should be
evicted, have been left intact.
In a special report in The New Vision of April 25, it was reported that
the new proposed boundaries were being changed to favour the rich owners of
the houses.
Even the recent directive by Kezimbira came at a time when a
controversial shopping mall, Shoprite, was being put up at Lugogo play
grounds, which is a water way.
Mafabi says some politicians have abdicated from the noble cause of
protecting wetlands because they fear to make unpopular decisions.
"We are making a new law on wetlands to close the pitfalls," says a
source in the ministry of environment.

Original_Search_Title: Nutrition, Poverty And the Rights Issue
Headline: Nigeria: Nutrition, Poverty And the Rights Issue
Body_Text:
"HUNGER kills." This is the message a Lagos taxi driver conveys to the world through large, vivid inscription on a front panel of his rickety cab. As he meanders through the dusty streets of the city spreading his two-worded message, another set of inscriptions with the message that "Ignorance is death" is displayed. To the casual observer, these messages probably mean nothing more than the cab driver's own way of justifying his way of eking out a livelihood. But to the mind that is more discerning, the apparently simple messages connote weightier significance. Indeed, there is hunger as well as ignorance in Nigeria and the world at large. Both features combine to entrench under-nourishment, ill health, misery and death. Although, medical professionals and dieticians continue to maintain that a balanced, healthy diet is necessary for health maintenance, there is nevertheless a counter- argument that the majority of the Nigerian population cannot afford to eat a balanced, healthy diet or is unaware of the fact that certain healthful ingredients are limited or nutritionally unavailable to them.
To start with, nutritionists are worried that too many people are subsisting on variations of distorted diets which are either too little or too much in nutrient level, or with too great a loss of nutrients, thus making the individuals easy prey to ill-health and disease. "Nutrition is a key factor of good health," admits Dr. Rosemary Anyanwu, Nutrition Researcher at the Nigerian Institute of Medical Research (NIMR). "The effect of nutrition is not only on growth and physical development, but also on cognitive and social development. A malnourished child is not only more vulnerable to disease, its cognitive development will be in a peril, especially during the first three years of life. Stunted physical growth is closely linked to reduced mental development. Adequate nutrition begins with healthy eating habits and numerous studies have shown that increasing fruits and vegetables in diet can decrease risk of debilitating diseases such as cancer and heart disease."
Studies have shown that increasing fruits and vegetables in diet can decrease risk of cancer and heart disease. For calcium and protein needs, a few servings of dairy products, calcium supplements and /or alternative calcium-containing food sources are recommended daily. An array of whole grain products, such as the cereals, rice, yam, maize, potatoes or cassava are essential in providing various nutrients, calories and carbohydrates. Since each food has a slightly different nutritional profile, Anyanwu argues that consuming an array of different foods each day is recommended.
