Police Affairs Minister, Chief Broderick Bozimo spoke to Weekend
Vanguard in Benin City last weekend on the Federal Government's efforts to
checkmate the bunkerers and the new dimensions in the Warri
crisis. Excerpts:
Q: Why is it that the police cannot check oil bunkering in Warri
that threatens our economic revival as a nation?
The problem is not in Warri metropolis or Warri land but in the swamps
and in the riverine areas. It is not fair to say that the police is not able
to contain the crisis, that is untrue. The police have done very well at the
risk of their lives. You know how many policemen have been killed. These
things are happening in the creeks and not on land and those areas are not
sufficiently manned by police stations because they are in the swamps. There
are no roads to these places and so, we need to boost marine police. We need
to patrol the creeks, a combined effort of the marine police and community
youths who are to be screened and accredited by the chairmen of their
councils and traditional rulers. This team will patrol the creeks and once
you do that, you will be amazed.
Q: What about the issue of kidnapping, have you done anything about
it ?
Something was done in 1998 and there was no more kidnapping, no more
piracy. I have made these recommendations to Mr. President. I did that not
too long ago after meeting with the leadership of the ethnic groups. And I
want to tell you that without the police, that area would probably not be
part of Nigeria now because these youths are just doing whatever they like
but the police have kept things going with their presence.
Q: Is it not an indictment of the failure of the police that made
the government to deploy soldiers to Warri?
The militarymen have gone there to tackle the bunkering aspect of the
crises. From experience, government knows that the battle has assumed a
different dimension. You have criminals and international bunkerers who have
infiltrated the entire system. So, once you have peace in place, they will
make sure they torpedo the peace because when you are fighting, they are
underground bleeding the place, emptying the trunk line into tankers and
shipping them out. So, these are the things the military is focusing
on.
The intention really is to isolate bunkering, put an eye on them and
make sure that they don't run the Nigerian economy aground. The political
solution is being handled by the Danjuma committee, the State House of
Assembly, leaders of the ethnic groups, the governor, youths and all
others. The bottom line is the problem in the Niger Delta is about poverty
and marginalisation and government is gradually addressing them.
Q: Can that be the only cause of the recurring crises?
Every time we contain it, these people with vested interest, the fifth
columnists, they go there. If they strike and an Itsekiri-man dies, it is
the Ijaw who killed him and vice versa if it is an Ijaw man that dies. Let
me tell you, because of the concern over this problem, the entire Ijaw
leadership met recently about a month ago.
I was there, the Minister of Science and Technology, Prof. Isoun from
Bayelsa, the Minister of Transport, Senator Sekeibo from Kalabari, the
Bayelsa State Governor, Diepreye Alamieyeseigha who is the governor-general
of the Ijaw nation, his Delta State counterpart, Chief James Ibori, all
traditional rulers of note in Ijaw and the youths were all there.
At that meeting, we concluded that the struggle had been hijacked. The
whole idea was for the political liberation of the Ijaw in the Warri
South-West local government and we told them that when people go about
burning houses in the name of the struggle, that's rubbish and we resolved
at that meeting not to fight anymore. Do you know that as soon as we came
back, the bunkerers (we know some of them, the government knows them but
they are hiding, they are gradually going to take care of them) some of them
went round and started the crises all over. They just went and burnt one
village and attacked another and the fighting resumed again. If you notice,
they spread rumours that one ethnic group will attack tomorrow. They poison
the mind of our people. That is why the war is still on. It is not because
the Itsekiri, Urhobo and Ijaw hate each other. If we are not mad people and
I know we are not mad, is it not obvious that the burning and killing of
ourselves is a case of us cutting our noises to spite our faces? If we are
not idiots and I know we are not idiots, is it possible for us to go the way
we are going when others are building their societies?
Most of the houses burnt in Warri belong to people who have no idea of
what we are talking about. In the villages, old women who do not know what a
local government means, who have not left their homes for centuries, people
go and burn their houses. The Ijaw and Itsekiri people have decided to put a
stop to this. The leaders, I have been talking with them, people like Chief
Mabiaku, the Iyasere of Warri, Chief Ayomike, Chief E K Clark and others, we
are all worried because even though politically, we have divergent views,
that does not mean you should go and kill yourselves and burn what the other
people have laboured for. It is the fifth columnist, that is the oil
bunkerers who benefit when we fight. They are the people behind the crises
in Warri and the Niger Delta. You know that when two goats are fighting, the
third goat will carry the yam and run away.
If you know these bunkerers like you claimed, why has government not
arrested them?
It is our expectation that in no distant future, we will begin to unmask
these characters. We want to first recover their arms but you know that
these criminals are very fast. About a month ago, Mr. President directed
that the police should enter into the creeks and try to grab the
arms. Before we got there, a lot of them had escaped with the arms, that's
the report we got. So, that was not fully addressed. Efforts are in place,
however, to address that question very soon. There will be a policy
statement as to the retrieval of arms that are floating around the
Niger-Delta. That policy is being fine-tuned. There is surrender of
fire-arms order already made. But it has to be sensitive, the timing has to
be right.
When you announce that people should surrender their arms, the people in
the riverine area will see it as a joke if it is not worked out
properly. Things like, where do you surrender? If you are surrendering, what
is the consequence when you surrender? Issues like these are being worked
out. What if one refuses to surrender at all, a lot of things are being fine
tuned.

Original_Search_Title: Army Unit In Ketu to Remain
Headline: Nigeria: Army Unit In Ketu to Remain
Body_Text:
The Federal Government has directed that the military unit stationed at Mile 12, Ketu, Lagos, should remain there so as to ensure the security of persons and property in the area.
Vice President Atiku Abubakar gave this directive when the national president and founder of the National Traders and Market Leaders Council of Nigeria; a non governmental organisation (NGO), Alhaja (Dr.) Adetutu Odunsi, led a 140 man delegation on his office on a courtesy visit in Abuja today.
Abubakar stated that government would take steps to stop the incessant molestation of members of the association by some state government who accuse them of political partisanship.
Vice Preisdent, Atiku Abubakar said that government since its inception had given a lot of incentives to farmers through the distribution of fertilizer at subsidized rates. This, he said, has ensured a growth rate of more than 5% in the agricultural sector, adding that subsidy on fertilizer at present is put at 25%.
Abubakar told the delegation that President Olusegun Obasanjo recently approved the sum of Three billion naira (N3b) for the purchase of excess grains from farmers to ensure that farmers do not lose their crops and to motivate farmers to greater efforts in the next planting season.
Government, he said has also approved the sum of two hundred million naira (N200m) for the purchase of tubers such as yams and coco yams.

Original_Search_Title: Politicians Angry at UN Plan to Make Kenyan Commander of Peace Force
Headline: Nigeria: Reps Ask FG to Rebuff Okonkwo's Replacement As Ecomil Commander
Body_Text:
Abuja — THE House of Representatives Committee on Army has urged Nigerian defence authorities to stop the planned replacement of General Felix Okonkwo, the Nigerian commander of the ECOWAS Military Force in Liberia (ECOMIL) with a Kenyan General.Chairman of the House Committee on Army, Rep. Ado Dogo at a press briefing in Abuja yesterday, also revealed the discomfort of Nigerian troops in Liberia with the American imported meals they have been feeding on. The troops, he said, want local foods such as garri, yam.Mr. Dogo who just returned from an assessment visit of the Nigerian contingent in Liberia also disclosed logistical constraints facing the troops, notably finance and transport.
Observing that Nigeria's contribution of 1,580 officers and men to ECOMIL's total strength of 3310 officers and men should give the country a commanding role in the peace keeping operation, Mr. Dogo said:" Presently, General Okonkwo is the ECOMIL Commander in Liberia. He is expected to surrender such leadership to General Obande of Kenya when UN takes over the activities of ECOMIL in October 2003."The delegation learnt that General Obande is not in anyway better than General Okonkwo and moreover, Nigeria has the largest contingent in the peace keeping operation, why cant the country lobby for the retention of General Okonkwo as the Force Commander?"
Observing the discomfort of the Nigerian troops with American imported meals, the Committee chairman said: "The food presently supplied to NIGCON comes from the U.S. and most members of the contingent are usually not at home with it. They instead prefer home food such as garri, yams, apu etc. "Rep Dogo also disclosed other operational constraints facing the Nigerian delegation in Liberia such as transport and poor finances, but assured that the country's troops were giving a good impression of the country and its military even in the face of the limitations.

Original_Search_Title: Every Child Has a Right to Adequate Nutrition
Headline: Nigeria: Every Child Has a Right to Adequate Nutrition
Body_Text:
"Malnutrition is unacceptable .....form of deformity or another" - Late
Prof. Olikoye Ransome-Kuti, Former Minister of Health....
"Malnutrition is unacceptable because food is one of the basic rights of
the individual. Our children are ever in danger of malnutrition right from
the womb and this leads to one form of deformity or another" - Late
Prof. Olikoye Ransome-Kuti, Former Minister of Health
"DOCTOR, what can I do to make my child grow properly, I feed him
regularly, but instead of gaining weight and growing strong, he is lean and
weak. What can I do to make him grow well?" Ronke, a young nursing mother
asked during a recent public educational health programme held for mothers
in Ikorodu, a town on the outskirts of Lagos. The event, held at the
instance of a health-oriented public spirited organization, was coordinated
by a group of doctors and nurses from the community's local public health
unit. "How old is your child, and what kind of food do you give him?" one of
the doctors inquired. Ronke's reply was brief. "He turned one year just last
month and he eats the same food like every other person in the house,
whether it is beans, garri, yam or rice, we all eat the same food."
As she spoke, all eyes went to the child cradled in her arms. At a
glance, the child appeared healthy enough. The face was rounded and full,
the limbs seemed well padded and the abdomen was protuberant. But a closer
look quickly proved this initial impression to be false. What became
immediately striking was the expression of intense misery on the child's
face. This, coupled with the pale skin - wrinkled and spotted with rashes
that looked very much like scabies - the scanty hair and fullness in the
limbs arising from suspected abnormal retention of fluid by the skin
tissues, were enough to confirm that the child in question was far from
healthy.
A quick examination by the medical team was to reveal that a combination
of fluid retention, muscle weakness, and an enlarged liver, were
contributing to the child's protuberant belly. Beneath the waterlogged
tissues lay wasted muscles, as much of the child's body protein had been
used up in attempt to meet the child's own energy needs. Apart from the open
sores on the legs, the clincher to the child's true ailment was the
prolonged imprint left when the skin was squeezed between the thumb and
forefinger. "Madam, your child is malnourished," the doctor announced
apologetically. "He has developed kwashiorkor and is very sick. If you want
him to be well, you need to give him a proper diet of cereals, vegetables,
fruits, milk, meat, poultry, fish, peas, eggs, and others consisting all the
basic food groups that can provide the essential food substances."
While malnourishment (hidden hunger) doesn't kill outrightly, it
cripples. Malnourished children often suffer the loss of precious mental
capacities. They fall ill more often. If they survive, they usually grow up
with lasting mental and physical disabilities. Families suffer. The entire
community suffers. It is a waste of potential beyond measure. A consequence
of extreme poverty, in this sort of 'living death' called malnutrition, the
body gets food, but not enough of the right components to keep functioning
properly. A basic diet may have too little fat or carbohydrate to provide a
reasonable amount of energy. It may have too little protein to create
muscle, brains, or blood cells and may be deficient in minerals such as
iron, iodine, and zinc that when missing cause permanent impairment, just
like a shortage of vitamin A in childhood will cause permanent blindness, or
vitamins may be in too short a supply for the child's body to properly
rebuild its worn or damaged parts.
Dr. Rosemary Anyanwu, a nutrition expert says kwashiorkor is a disease
of poverty and ignorance, which has causes rooted in a combination of
factors that include diet, health, education, access to food and care. "One
of the consequences of inadequate nutrition is malnutrition - a dietary
condition caused by an insufficiency or excess of one or more nutrients in
the diet. A person is at risk of malnutrition if the amount of energy and/or
nutrients in that person's diet does not meet the basic nutritional needs. A
diet lacking in energy depletes the body's fat stores, followed by protein
from the muscles and organs, are used to provide energy. Children,
especially those under the age of five, quickly develop a condition called
protein-energy malnutrition (PEM) and the two most common forms are marasmus
and kwashiorkor.
"Kwashiorkor, like marasmus (which occurs when a child is weaned early
on to foods low in energy and nutrients), is a serious dietary condition of
children between the ages of ten months and three years, resulting from
severe malnutrition, and a lack of basic vital nutrients and a gross
deficiency of protein. In most instances, the severe lack of body weight of
the kwashiorkor child is often masked by water retention, which makes the
face moon-shaped and the belly swollen." Admitting that many uninformed
mothers unintentionally deny their children the ability of proper growth by
depriving them essential nutritional diets because of wrong feeding habits,
Anyanwu affirms that kwashiokor occurs when children are weaned and thus
deprived of the high nutritional value and protein content of breast milk."
It may also arise because the child's appetite is affected by other illness,
especially infections such as measles or gastroenteritis. Such children are
always hungry and, in the attempt to relieve this and to supply their energy
needs, their parents give them large quantities of carbohydrate which, on
its own, has low nutritional value. It is a vicious cycle."
Dr. Dorothy Esangbedo, an experienced pediatrician and endocrinologist,
notes that nutrition has significant effect on growth of a child. In her
view, it all begins at birth with breast feeding. Noting that a normal
healthy child grows in "spurts" throughout childhood, she says a
well-nourished child will grow about two and a half inches and gain about
two or thee kilos each year between the ages of two and five. "Adequate
nutrition begins with adequate brestfeeding. It starts with the mother. A
malnourished mother is most likely to breed a malnourished child. Usually,
by the 15th month of life, most infants are able to feed themselves without
help. Appetites vary with young children as well as adults, thus
necessitating promotion of a healthy pattern of eating rather than using
controlling techniques such as restricting food intake or pressuring smaller
children to eat more. Essentially, attitudes and habits formed during early
childhood years help establish lifelong health and nutrition habits."The
point here is that it is important to offer young children a variety of
foods within each food group over time, so as to provide them adequate
nutrients from a wide variety of healthy foods. But the issue is how many
mothers know this as a fact? Quite a few have questioned the rationale
behind the unequivocal assertion that adequate nutrition and health are
fundamental human rights, and others are compelled to ask what this really
entails. In essence, what are the primary links between nutrition and health
from a human-rights perspective?
In her own submission, Dr Janet Akindele, a sociologist, points to
discrimination and violence against women as the major causes of
malnutrition in children. Her argument is that being the principal providers
of nourishment during the most crucial periods of children's development,
women's vital caring practices to children's nutritional well-being
invariably suffer when the division of labour and resources in families and
communities favours men. "Inadequate access to good education and correct
information is also a cause of malnutrition. Without information strategies,
or better and more accessible educational awareness, the skills and
behaviours needed to combat malnutrition cannot be developed."
The issue of child malnutrition and women's rights has recieved leverage
in several areas. In sub-Saharan Africa, according to the World Bank, nearly
one third of children are malnourished, and 20 per cent of women are
underweight, with about 24 million low-birthweight babies are born every
year. Women who are usually underweight or overworked or were themselves
stunted by inadequate nutrition during their own childhood produce babies
with a greater risk of becoming malnourished.
Says Akindele: "The tragedy of childhood malnutrition is rooted in part
in the discrimination and disempowerment so many women endure. What
endangers women endangers children too." According to a UNICEF report, full
commitment to the rights of women is one of the best ways of protecting
children's well-being and nutritional development. It notes that to end
childhood malnutrition, factors like the mother's health, education,
literacy, and awareness of essential information about nutrition, diet,
child care, personal and environmental hygiene and the importance of
breastfeeding are required including her rights."
Of the nearly 12 million children under five who die each year in
developing countries mainly from preventable causes, the deaths of over six
million, or 55 per cent, are either directly or indirectly attributable to
malnutrition. Every year, in Nigeria, according to UNICEF, 12 million
children or one in every five children below the age of five, die from
preventable diseases while several millions more do not get the minimum
nutrients for healthy living. The result is severe malnutrition.
In the last decade alone, infant mortality rate in the country increased
to 128 deaths per 100,000 live births while under-5 mortality rate rose to
239 deaths per 100,000 live births. Available nutrition data from the
Federal Ministry of Health and the Federal Office of Statistics show that
today, 17 per cent of newborns have low birth weight, 11 per cent of
children under five years are malnourished while 45 per cent are
stunted. Goiter rate among children aged 6-11 years is about 22 per cent,
all indicative of a significant problem of nutrient deficiency.
Several human rights protagonists accept that the right to be free from
life-afflicting hunger is the most fundamental and the most obviously
universal of all human rights. They also concede that if for one reason or
the other, this human right expectation is not guaranteed, then every other
one is effectively made irrelevant.
According to the UN Institute for Food & Development Policy, the
agitation for adequate nutrition has always been high on the agenda of
citations in solemn declarations of political intent, yet this right remains
the most violated in practice. Statistics from the Institute show that this
human right is not realized in the case of over the world's 800 million
clinically hungry people nor the 120 million malnourished children under the
age of five.
In defence of the need to feed a child right, former Minister of Health,
late Prof. Olikoye Ransome-Kuti, once stated that "malnutrition is
unacceptable because food is one of the basic rights of the
individual". Olikoye, who headed a 25-member advisory committee on food and
nutrition to develop strategies to free the country from hunger and
malnutrition further noted that "our children are ever in danger of
malnutrition right from the womb and this leads to one form of deformity or
another". At the family level, the increased costs and pressures of
malnutrition-linked disability and illness is particularly devastating to
poor families - especially to mothers and society at large.
In similar vein, UNICEF's Executive Director, Carol Bellamy, noted
unequivocally that "the right to food is related to a variety of rights
which affect the ability of groups and individuals to achieve food and
nutritional security for themselves." Nutrition is a cornerstone that
affects and defines the health of all people, rich and poor. It paves the
way for growth, development, good health and aspiration to realization of
the fullest potential as individuals and societies. Conversely, malnutrition
increases the vulnerability to disease, ill-health and premature death.
Bellamy asserts that by the end of the 20th century, majority of the
countries of the world had ratified the 10 international conventions most
closely related to the right to food and nutrition, she said these include
rights of children to the highest attainable standard of health, and the
rights of women to be free of discrimination, and economic rights, including
food and shelter in peace as well as war.
But available data from most international agencies indicate that while
world ratification of most human rights treaties has been good, culminating
in the World Food Summit of 1996, where world leaders reaffirmed the resolve
to protect the right of every one to have access to safe and nutritious
food, the right to adequate food and the fundamental right of everyone,
notably children, to be free from hunger is far from achieved.
A recent research from the UK Institute of Child Health on the
nutritional status of children in Nigeria, revealed that there is a high
level of malnutrition in all children under 10 years old. Using a variety of
measurements, including height, weight and blood haemoglobin level, the
researchers, concluded that although the average child in Nigeria cannot be
said to be nutritionally neglected, his right to be adequately nourished is
not guaranteed and those with feeding difficulties have a higher risk of
malnutrition.
The conclusion of the research is that while more children under 10 can
benefit from education of parents and healthcare workers about their
specific feeding needs, such efforts would also improve the nutrition
prospects of whole families as well. Also data from the USAID Demographic
and Health Survey (DHS) in Nigeria conducted in 1990, showed that 43 per
cent of Nigerian children were stunted. The figure rose to 46 per cent in
1999 mainly as a result of inadequate politics, recurrent infections, and
insufficient maternal care.
Taking this up, Prof. Tola Atinmo, a professor of human nutrition at the
University of Ibadan, puts blame at the doorstep of bad government policies,
noting that as the economy is declining, the health and nutrition status of
the people is the first thing that suffers. Noting that there is a close
relationship between poverty and malnutrition, he argues that children from
poor families have an even higher risk of inadequate nutrition as a result
of financial constraints and certain social beliefs which affect the amount
and quality of food they receive.
"Nigerians don't spend enough time looking after their children," he
laments. "We still need to encourage women to breast-feed their children
exclusively for the first six months after birth. Many women also give their
infants supplements or water too early in life. These practices often lead
to infection or worse." Atinmo who was Principal Investigator of the in 1994
USAID-sponsored Nigeria National Micronutrient Deficiency Prevalence
Assessment research project covering 3,500 children. "Ten million children
were in jeopardy of vitamin A, iron, and iodine deficiency.
When you know these numbers, you can plan for intervention. For vitamin
A, you can use strategies such as supplementation or fortification of
food.While most children in poor households are more likely to succumb to
the ravages of problems arising from lack of safe water, inadequate health
care, measles, diarrhoea, respiratory infections, poor sanitation and
hygiene, their plight is further compounded by severe malnutrition."
Atinmo, also President of the Nutrition Society of Nigeria from 1986 to
1990, developed the very first draft of a food and nutrition policy aimed at
significantly improving the health of Nigerians. The document, which spells
out the nation's food and nutrition problems, and the corresponding food and
nutrition requirements, as well as tactics for overcoming malnutrition, was
a blueprint intended to be useful in developing a 10-year action plan to
improve nutrition in the country.
Unfortunately, the political situation in the country at that time
forced the plan to the sidelines and even though the new democratic
government is dusting off the files, there is yet to be significant
action. "We need to step up our education programs, so women can know what
to do and how to look after their children. This includes the need for
simple, low cost interventions such as educational leaflets on nutrition for
families and health workers showing how to feed a child correctly. This
doesn't always require a lot of money. It just requires having time and
information. Once you know how much people should consume for their age
group and their gender, you can figure out how much you need for planning
for agriculture and food production, as well as for imports of food products
in the country."
Fola Banjo, a nutritionist at the Nigeria Institute of Food Science and
Technology (NIFST), agrees that the high incidence of malnutrition in the
country dates back several years and has been aggravated in recent times by
the inflation. "The fundamental causes of malnutrition are poverty,
ignorance and inflation. Awareness and advocacy should be enhanced for
closer monitoring and reduction of existing disparities and violations of
child rights, especially with respect to the girl child, while the
legislature is being compelled to adopt the children's law which harmonises
all laws relating to children and incorporates the provisions of the
convention on the rights of the child already ratified by Nigeria and 190
other nations."
On the question of political instability, weak policy support and poor
funding that have characterised past government performance resulting in
apathy on the part of the target audience is paramount. This much was
acknowledged by President Olusegun Obasanjo, at special meeting to review
the status of the Nigerian child and trends in such status during the last
decade. At the event held in abuja at the instance of UNICEF and the
Nigerian Ministry of Women's Affairs and Youth Development, President
Obasanjo observed that "We realised that where national policy is not
grounded in democratic institutions, its formulation and implementation may
become unresponsive to the real needs if the people become alienated and
lose confidence.
Several world leaders agree with Obasanjo that the next step is for
communities to learn about human rights as related to their daily concerns,
making the connection to the struggles and aspirations of others, and
joining in action against the severe violations their governments impose on
human beings by not budgeting for "progressive realization" of economic,
social and cultural rights.
Indeed, the world is only beginning to take this next step. During
activities to mark the 55th anniversary of the UN human rights charter, Mary
Robinson, High Commissioner of Human Rights of the United Nations, strongly
advocated in a UNDP policy paper for specialized agencies, intergovernmental
and non-governmental groups to mainstream human rights and integrate human
rights in sustainable human development, regarding the right to food,
health, habitat and economic security.
The document emphasizes the legal obligation of all member nations and
of UN agencies such as the Food and Agriculture Organization, WHO, ILO, and
UNDP "to promote the progressive realization" of these rights through
development.

Original_Search_Title: Neighbours Turn Enemies
Headline: Nigeria: Neighbours Turn Enemies
Body_Text:
Communities in Kogi and Edo States go to war over a disputed
territory. Who owns the land?
In the best of seasons, the Ekpedos of Edo State and Ogoris of Kogi
State even called themselves brothers.
And as if to seal the claim of brotherhood, their ancestors have
often crossed the Great River Odobili to pick a wife. As a result,
not a few indigenes of Ekpedo in Edo State have Ogori blood running
in their veins. And vice versa. Such is the mixed blood that runs in
the body of Pa Weston Ajangbolo. But in a moment of fury, blood and
other affinities seem to lose their imports. And now Ogori people of
Kogi State and their Ekpedo brothers in Edo State have been on each
other's throat for months over a piece of land.
That was the case in the early hours of Wednesday, 2 July 2003, when
Ogori and Ekpedo communities went to war. It had started like a
scuffle. But once it began, the fires of war have raged on. Early in
the morning of that day, youths from Ogori allegedly invaded and
harassed people in Ekpedo. The hapless people saw tension in the air
and many of them ran for cover. Pa Ajangbolo's expensive edifice was
their choice. Apart from the share size of his house, the retired oil
company worker is also the national chairman, Ekpedo Development
Association. More important, the frightened natives had reasoned that
since Ajangbolo's mother was a native of Ogori, the invaders would
most certainly spare his house from attack. How mistaken they were?
By the time the rampaging youth returned with greater intensity,
Ajangbolo's immunity was violated.
About 200 youths soaked the house with fuel before setting it ablaze.
As people abandoned their houses and dashed for the forest, the
traditional ruler of Ekpedo, Oba John Afolabi Obabori, also abandoned
his throne and headed for nearby Agenebode to request for police
protection. None came on time. Next, he took his case to Auchi. Here
a detachment of policemen, joined by men from other locations hastily
responded to the call. However, it was a response that was late in
coming. By the time they arrived, Ekpedo in the evening, the town had
been reduced to a shadow of its old self.
In an inventory compiled by the Ekpedo community, the people claimed
their losses ran into millions of Naira. Seventy two houses were
torched in the town, 37 were completely burnt. Three supermarkets and
nine houses were vandalized. In the arson, two beer parlours and a
medicine store were affected.
Besides, Ekpedo people said they lost 37 motorcycles, 12 vehicles, 28
bicycles and several jerry cans of palm oil.
It was not an inventory they would stomach with calmness. Indeed, if
Ekpedo plight evokes sympathy, its revenge mission is no less
criminal. It started two weeks later. Days after the destruction,
governments of both Kogi and Edo States met in Benin city, the Edo
State capital. At the meeting attended by deputy governors of the two
states, it was resolved among others, "that there should be immediate
cessation of hostilities between the two warring communities." It was
also agreed "that the area in dispute should be declared a buffer
zone pending the final resolution of the dispute by the National
Boundary Commission." These were not to be.
The first sign of a fragile peace came on Wednesday, 16 July. Some
Ekpedo people entered the disputed territory and destroyed a farm
building belonging to one Ojo Shaibu, a tenant farmer on Ogori land.
The worst revenge was yet to come. It came on Sunday 20 July. As part
of efforts to maintain peace among the warring neighbours, leaders of
both communities agreed on encouraging peace missions. It was in that
spirit and to reciprocate an earlier gesture by his counterpart in
Edo State who had visited the Ogori community, that Azeez Idris King,
a House of Representatives member for Okene/ Ogori/ Magongo Federal
Constituency decided to visit Ekpedo. He went in company of Emmanuel
Olorundare, a veterinary doctor and an Ogori community leader, and
seven security operatives including the District Police Officer DPO,
Ogoni. As it turned out, Olorunfemi who was called out of a church to
join the peace mission did not return alive. The doctor was shot dead
and hacked to pieces by intemperate youths of Ekpedo. The car in
which he was riding with a police escort was smashed as the
operatives looked on helplessly. "It is sad to know that they could
kill somebody on peace mission in such a bizarre way in the presence
of policemen," Clarence Olufemi, a member of Kogi House of Assembly
told TheNEWS.
There are many accounts of how the war began. To the people of
Ekpedo, 20 June marked the beginning of the aggression. Some Ekpedo
women were said to have been harassed on a farmland by Ogori youths.
The women who were on their way to Ogori market were allegedly beaten
by their captors. But in Ogori, the more popular version is that some
Ekpedo youths stole some yam tubers situated along the border track.
In no time, the allegations snowballed into a big boundary dispute in
which Ogori is now being accused of harbouring territorial ambitions.
TheNEWS investigations revealed that both communities have lived
together peacefully as neighbours. So close were they, that they
hardly spare a thought on where to draw the line. "We live among
ourselves as if there was no boundary," said an aged man in Ogori. He
further claimed that Ekpedo people are settlers on Ogori land.
Ekpedo will hear nothing of this. People of this community argued
that the land on which they live belongs to them. Oba Obabori argued
that River Odobiri is the natural boundary between the two
communities.
The traditional ruler is armed with the memory of his late father who
he recalled would fondly point at the river as the boundary between
the two communities.
Indeed, Mike Oghiadomhe, deputy governor of Edo State, echoed the
same tradition in Abuja on Wednesday 23 July. During a special joint
meeting of officials of the two states with officials of the National
Boundary Commission in Abuja, Oghiadomhe argued in favour of using
natural objects as boundary. In his opening remarks, the Edo deputy
governor laboured hard to establish his case. A communiqué jointly
issued by members of the peace meeting recorded the deputy governor
of Edo State emphasizing that "as much as possible, boundaries be
made to follow natural features." But the regent of Ogori land and
his subjects insisted that the land has always belonged to their fore
fathers. In this claim they rely on a colonial instrument that
defines the status of the disputed territory. Kogi Deputy Governor
also argued in favour of the position. On his part, according to the
communiqué, Salawu stressed the need to take historical and customary
land holding patterns into consideration rather than basing
boundaries on arbitrary lines. He further recommended that the
perpetrators of the crisis be brought to book. And beyond this, he
urged the National Boundary Commission to expedite action on
demarcating all relevant boundaries in the state. He particularly
called on the commission to look at the sub sectors of the boundary
namely, Aduge/ Imoga Axis; Ogori/ Ekpedo axis and Aguda/ Ukpella
axis.
Salawu went into history to prove his case. "The claim of Kogi at the
Ogori/ Ekpedo axis is based purely on history. Ogori customarily
shares border with Bekuma, Ekpe (not Ekpedo and Ososo in Edo State."
He however urged the people to exercise restraints in their claim,
because in his words "war does no one no good." George Sakibo,
chairman, Federal House of Representatives Committee on Special
Duties who was also at the meeting and who has since conducted on the
spot, assessment of the crisis area also called for restraint. He
requested that in order to put an end to the loss of lives, the
boundary commission should put permanent marks between disputing
communities.
With all the arguments and particularly guided by the result of its
joint Field Team headed by I.A.A Adewole, Ag. Surveyor- General of
the federation, the Abuja meeting resolved in favour of the
instrument line, the Legal Notice No. 126 of 1954 "with slight
adjustment as far as practicable." The technical committee holds for
the Ogori/Ekpe sector, the instrument line boundary is from point
LN15 to point LN 17. This, experts argued has suggested that the
disputed territory belongs to Kogi. But the meeting agreed that "the
status quo shall be maintained along the disputed border areas until
the final determination of the boundary." Will the warring brothers
agree to sheath their swords?

Original_Search_Title: Political Definition of Niger-Delta
Headline: Nigeria: Political Definition of Niger-Delta
Body_Text:
The Niger Delta is home to more than 10 million people. Major states,
according to the 1991 census, recorded a population of 2,570,181 for Delta
and 3,983,857 for Rivers and Bayelsa States.
Before oil became the issue in Nigeria and unwholesome politics
influenced the true definition of the Niger Delta, it was less contiguous
with the old Ahoada, Degema, Opobo, Ogoni, Brass, Western Ijaw and Warri
Divisions. The agitation during and after the colonial era had always been
for the creation of a district or political region in this area in order to
allay the fears of ethnic domination by more populous ethnic groups.
Some people define the Delta in teams of its ethnography, as the region
occupied principally by the Ijaw peoples together with a variety fo smaller
ethnic groups including the Itsekiri, the Urhobo and Isoko peoples in the
western part of the Delta. This definition does not allow for a territory
with precisely defined boundaries, as the Ijaw people are to be found in
areas far beyond the Delta whilst there are other ethnic groups inhabiting
the interior extremities of the Delta.
On the other hand, many Nigerians perceive the Niger Delta as synonymous
with the oil-producing areas of Nigeria. In their own thinking, the Niger
Delta is the same as the oil-producing areas. In other words, the Niger
Delta includes the folio states: Abia, Imo, Edo, Delta, Rivers, Bayelsa,
Cross River, Akwa Ibom and Ondo. But an independent authoritative study by a
non governmental organisation (even though by extension Government was
involved), the Niger Delta Environmental Survey (NDES) in its first report
(1997) gave a cartographic, ethnographic and political setting. The NDES'
political definition of the Niger Delta is synonymous with the Government's
definition under the Oil Mineral Producing Areas Development Commission
(OMPADEC).
'The 1958 Nigerian Constitutional provision for the Niger Delta inter
alia states as follows:
To allay the fears of the minority indigenes of the Niger Delta and
address the development needs of the peculiar terrain of the Niger Delta,
before granting independence to Nigeria, the British Government proposed
that the Niger Delta be declared a Special Federal Territory.
Linguistically, ethnographically and culturally, the Niger Delta of the
pre-crude oil and gas era comprised a bewildering mix of ethnic
groups. Among these, the communities of the Ijaw (in Eastern, Western and
Central Niger Delta), the Ogoni, Itsekiri, Urhobo, Isoko, Ikwerre and Delta
Igbo hit more headlines than others did. These are the several communities
in the present-day Rivers, Bayelsa and Delta States as well as part of Ondo
State. Misconceptions concerning the correct focus of the Niger Delta made
more controversial, more complex and politically incorrect public debates on
effective answers to the vexatious questions involved. For example, while
presenting the Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) Bill to the Senate,
President Olusegun Obasanjo included Ondo, Imo, Abia and Edo
States. Furthermore, Governors of various states as far as Anambra were
jostling for inclusion in the Niger Delta. The episode became a comedy of
errors and it made nonsense of whatever good intentions the governors may
have had, on the issue.
But concerned people like Rev. Dadikumo Odondiri, the Secretary-General
of the Ethnic Nationalities, spoke out:
There is a specific geographical location referred to as the Niger with
a peculiar terrain and peculiar developmental needs. These states are
basically Rivers, Bayelsa, Delta, Akwa Ibom, and parts of Edo, not even
Ondo. We have a rural area (sic) in Cross River but the Niger Delta is a
specific area inhabited by the Ijaws principally, the Isokos, Urhobos,
Itsekiris, the Ogonis as well as some Kwale areas. These are the areas that
ought to benefit.11
The fact that, over the years, money from the Niger Delta has been spent
prospecting for oil in Sokoto, should not mean that Sokoto, if it has oil at
all, should be included as a member state of the Niger Delta
Commission.
In a paper submitted to the President of Nigeria by the World
Environmental Movement for Africa (WEMFA), the President of the body, Dr
Stephen Siniktiem Azaiki, stated inter-alia:
We have studied the position papers of the Bayelsa Leaders of Thought,
the Movement of Concerned People of the Niger Delta, Major Isaac Adaka
Boro's papers and they hold almost the same views as the report of the WEMFA
study on the Developmental Needs of 1995. It is our understanding that the
Niger Delta, of truth, historically and cartographically is the present
Rivers, Bayelsa and Delta States. But the geographical proximity and the
gross neglect of Akwa Ibom could earn it a place in the proposed Niger Delta
Commission.
The difference between Oil Producing States and the Niger States is
quite distinct and must neither be confused or used interchangeably. The
Niger Delta Development Commission appears to have confused the Niger Delta
with the Oil Producing States. Part 1 Subsection 2(1), which deals with the
establishment of NDDC, and the persons who shall be members of the
Commission clearly shows that what the Act intends to solve is the problem
of the oil-producing states and not necessarily that of the Niger Delta per
se.
Although the Niger Delta happens to fall into oil and gas producing
states, she has her own peculiar perennial problems and terrain, which had
been highlighted before oil and gas exploration in Nigeria. From the Berlin
Conference in 1885, the Willink Report of 1958, and the World Conference on
Environment and Development in 1978, it is clearly stated that neglect and
extensive environmental damage cause poverty. To adequately address the
problem, the 1999 NDDC Act should be re-captioned Oil Producing States Act
while a special bill should be articulated to reflect the age-long views and
aspirations of the Delta peoples. Let there be OMPADEC, which could admit
any oil-producing state even as far as Bauchi or Sokoto if oil is found in
their domain. The Niger Delta has a peculiar terrain and developmental needs
that deserve special attention and the understanding of all. Freshwater
swamp forests alone cover some 11,700km2 or about half of the delta, lowland
equatorial monsoon 7,400km2, brackish water 5,400km2, sand barrier lands 11
,400km all totaling 25,640km.2 Diverse animals and plant species, which are
native to the area, are now threatened with extinction due mainly to very
serious ecological degradation caused by over forty-five years crude oil
exploration.
Former Senator David Dafinone, an Urbobo leader, a staunch, active
spokesman of the Niger Delta, expressed the view that "any arrangement
making Abia, Edo, Imo and Ondo states part of the Niger Delta amounts to an
agenda to further marginalise the minorities."12
Before crude oil became the main source of Nigeria's revenue and foreign
income earner and hence an issue in Nigerian Politics, the Sir Henry Willink
Commission described the Delta Region of the southern minority zone as
"poor, backs Neglected." The Commission which was appointed by the British
Secretary of State on September 26, 1957 defined and recommended as follows:
"We were impressed, in both the Western and Eastern Regions, with the
special position of the people, mainly Ijaw in the swampy country along the
coast between Opobo and the mouth of the Benin River".
"We suggest that constitutionally it would be necessary to place on the
concurrent list a new subject, which might be. "The Development of Special
Areas"14 The commission said it all. The special area, mainly Ijaws residing
between Opobo and the mouth of Bomo River need special development.
Even so, the Commission wrongly assumed the projection that 10 years of
concrete development projects - physical environment, manpower and
industrialization - would bring the peoples region into the mainstream of
federalism and democracy. Forty-five years after being recognized as "the
goose that lays the golden egg," the people and territory of the southern
minority zone still remain "poor, backward and neglected." The plight of the
people of the Oil Rivers Protectorate (1891-93) and later the Niger Coast
Protectorate (1895) is much worse now than a hundred years ago. They are now
marginalise and oppressed by greedy, despotic big tribe hegemonies that
monopolize the reins of power. If the Niger Delta must be viewed as the
settlement of the Ijaw ethnic nationalities, it should embrace such states
as Rivers, Bayelsa, Delta, Akwa Ibom, Cross Rivers and Ondo States and no
more.
If the foregoing analysis of the definition of the Niger Delta comes
short of convincing, the following explanation may help us understand it
better and clearly too. When a river enters a lagoon its current is checked,
and it is obliged to flow more slowly. Consequently, it can no longer carry
all its sediments. Much of this is deposited on the bed of the lagoon. Thus
causes the formation of deltas.
Since the deposit of sediments is brought about by the speed of a river,
it is bound to happen when a river flows into the sea. If strong tidal
currents sweep the mouth of a river, much sediment is carried away and
spread over a large area of the seabed. But when a river enters a sea where
the tidal range (i.e. the difference between high and low tide) is small,
the absence of strong currents causes the sediment to be steadily deposited
at its mouth. As Pertins and Stembridge put it, "new land is built up, which
owing to its resemblance to the Greek letter (delta) is called a delta." 15
In the course of time, as more and more sediment is deposited, the delta
reaches out farther and farther towards the sea, and across it the river
cuts fresh channels to provide outlets for its waters. Such channels are
called tributaries. The Niger Delta, bulging out into the Gulf of Guinea
between the Bight (Bay) of Benin and the Bight of Biafra covers more than
10,000 square miles, and has a shoreline of about 200 miles of its fourteen
main tributaries. The tributaries chiefly used for shipping are the Forcados
and Bonny Rivers.
This is a thoroughly satisfactory explanation of the Niger Delta by
non-Nigerians and renowned scholars who wrote prior to the Nigerian Civil
War and pre-crude oil era. They explained the Niger Delta without bias and
that explanation should be upheld.
The Niger Delta is principally peopled by the Ijaw ethnic
nationality. As Sir Willink's report puts it, "The declaration of the Ijaw
is an opportunity of putting forward plans for their own improvement."
According to the erstwhile Rivers State Leaders of Thought, the peoples of
the enlarged Niger Delta are:
a. Ijaws of Western Ijaw Division,
b. Ijaws of Brass Division
c. Ijaws of Degema Division
d. Ogonis and Elemes of Ogoni Division
e. Ikwerres and other tribes in Port Harcourt
f. Etches, Ekpeyes, Ogbas, Egbemas, Engennes, and Abuas of Ahoada
Division,
and
g. Obolos and Opobians of Opobo Division.
In addition to the Ijaws of Western Delta are the Isokos, the Urhobos,
the Itsekiri and part of Kwale. Akwa Ibom State, on the eastern part of the
Delta, has been included due to similarities of terrain and backwardness she
shares with the core Deltans. Today, it is peopled by the indigenes of Ikot
Abasi, Oron, Mbo, Uqua Ibeno, Eket, Ibibio, Anang, Efik, etc.
The issue of population distribution or density, and the pattern of
settlement in the Niger Delta are largely determined by the availability of
dry land as well as the nature of the physical landscape of the region. The
Niger Delta region has a low relief and poor ground drainage. This factor is
responsible for the paucity of settlements of considerable size in the
heartland of the Delta. Large settlements are found in the interior parts of
the Delta where drainage conditions and accessibility are better. The
mangrove swamp zone, interspersed by islands of dry land, such as Port
Harcourt, Sapele, Ughelli and Warri, present habitable settlements located
at the head of the navigable limits of the coastal rivers or estuaries. On
the contrary, settlements at the sea front are made particularly difficult
by their poor accessibility to the major population centers in the
hinterland.
Another inhibiting factor is that most brackish water eco-zones are
impossible to live in. However, due to reclamation, dry land is created in
the fresh and brackish water eco-zones for urban expansion. For instance,
Nembe waterside in Port Harcourt, Warri, Nembe Town, Ogbia and Brass are
largely built on sand-fill. It is noteworthy that this is not a modem
phenomenon because nineteenth century European visitors to Warri commented
on the spacious land mass. Much of modern Sangana is built on the sand
dredged from the Sangana canal. Dredging, canalization and other human
activities affect groundwater quality and movement in certain areas of the
Niger Delta. In addition, due to the poor permeability of the sub-soil,
villages and bigger settlements often get flooded from the back-swamp side
before the river reaches its peak.
In general, it is not an easy task to give the exact up-to-date
population figures of African countries owing to unreliable statistical
data. However, available statistics show that the population of the
principal Niger Delta states in Nigeria have been on the increase. As the
1991 population census indicates, the overall figure for these states is put
at about 8.9 million
The consequences of oil and gas exploration arising from air, water and
environmental pollution may affect the fertility and life span of the
inhabitants in such a manner that fecundity may fall and the birth of
abnormal babies and plants may increase. Average life span of the
inhabitants may drop as well. At a Port Harcourt meeting of stakeholders
summoned by NDES in October 1995, a community leader from Warri drew
attention to the inability of oil operations to alleviate poverty. On the
contrary, he argued, oil boom has brought more doom than ever before. This
is a critical viewpoint that gives cause for concern. Indeed, malnutrition
is a major problem especially among children in the Niger
Delta. Water-related diseases and waste disposal practices constitute
serious problems throughout the area. A study of the six major causes of
death in Nigeria (measles, malaria, pneumonia, tetanus, dysentery and
tuberculosis) indicate that the coastal area constitutes a zone of
disproportionately high mortality proneness to these diseases. Other
diseases affecting the people of the Niger Delta include worm infestation,
gastroenteritis, hypertension and sexually transmitted diseases, especially
among adolescents.
It may be pertinent to add that the federal and state governments have
embarked on various projects like the Expanded Programme on Immunization
(EPI), Universal Basic Education (UBE), and increased Children Tax Allowance
to lessen the economic burden and reduce childhood illnesses and infant
mortality. Available evidence shows that maternal mortality is high-about
9.4/1000 births. It is equally observed that the majority of those who die
are illiterate mothers, especially those who do not have access to adequate
antenatal care.
The structure and age distribution of any population is of great
economic significance. This is because not all members of the population are
actively productive. Labour supply depends mainly on the productive sector
of the entire population. If a larger percentage of the entire population is
composed of children, it means that the government will have to provide more
for education and health care. On the other hand, if the entire population
comprises more of old people, the government has to provide for such
amenities required by old people like pensions, old people's homes. Between
children and old people are members of the working class. Understandably,
where the active youths constitute a sizeable proportion of the entire
population, the government has to provide employment opportunities, among
other things.
Owing to lack of reliable statistics, an analysis of the trend in
population growth and distribution is hampered by gaps in data. However, in
comparison to the 1963 census figures, the population growth trend in
Rivers, Bayelsa and Delta States is clearly appreciating. The regular influx
to the oil-producing states in search of employment opportunities, access to
education, social amenities, health facilities, potable water, etc may well
account for this. According to the 1991 census, the old Rivers State is
slightly more urbanized than Delta, with 31% versus 25% of their respective
populations living in urban areas. Population growth rates in both states
are estimated to be around 30%. Since oil drilling began in the 1960s,
migration into the region has greatly increased and there is high population
density on habitable land in the riverine and coastal areas.
Land and human population density are important in relation to
development pressures mounted on the Niger Delta. A map in recent World Bank
report defining the environment, and spelling development strategies for the
Niger Delta, shows a population density of 1.95 persons per hectare in
Rivers and Bayelsa states and 7.12 persons for Akwa Ibom. In truth, however,
the density of Ogoni land alone undoubtedly matches that of neighbouring
Akwa Ibom State. Rural population densities on limited islands of dry land
in the freshwater eco-zone appear to be equally high. The situation is made
worse because most of water eco-zone (about 20% of the Niger Delta) is
uninhabitable. The twin townships of Ogbolomabiri and Bassambiri, which make
up Nembe, are a good example. They are packed onto two largely sand-filled
islands surrounded by mangrove forests that virtually merge with the
sea. Available agricultural land is a few tens of hectares so that, apart
from local shellfish, all the required food has to be brought in from other
towns.
The major human occupations in the Niger Delta may be considered under
three main headings, namely primary, secondary and tertiary occupations. The
major traditional primary occupation includes farming and fishing, while
secondary occupations include industries like gin distillation, textile
weaving, boat carving etc. Tertiary occupation includes trade and commerce,
transportation etc. Even so, oil exploration by multinational companies has
since become the major production activity in the region.
AGRICULTURE
The Niger Delta contributes to the national economy not only in
petroleum and gas but also in agricultural production. The major
indigenously derived food resources are classified as follows:
a. Food crops: yam, cassava, maize, rice, cowpea, melon, groundnuts,
potato etc.
b. Tree crops: oil palm, rubber, coconut, raffia palm, cashew; pawpaw,
etc.
c. Livestock: goats, pigs, rabbits, fish, etc
This system is also known as subsistence crop farming. It involves the
traditional peasant who clears, cultivates, plants and harvests his land
through manual labour supplied by himself and his family members. Farm
yields are harvested, processed and stored to feed the family and, if
possible, provide seeds for the next planting season.
The size of the farm per household may range between one and three
acres, and it is usually cultivated with traditional farming tools like
cutlasses, hoes and machetes. One peculiar nature of the Niger Delta is that
the soil is rich and water is always available for cropping.

Original_Search_Title: Surinamese "Homecomers" At Elmina
Headline: Ghana: Surinamese "Homecomers" At Elmina
Body_Text:
A delegation of 102 Surinamese, on a "home-coming visit" have been honoured at a grand durbar at the Elmina Castle.
The Surinamese Minister of Transport and Communication, Mr. Guno Castelen and the Minister of Regional Integration, Mr. Romeo Van Russel, are leading a delegation that is made up of businessmen and entrepreneurs.
The visitors are to "reconnect with their roots and to commemorate the 140th anniversary of the abolition of slavery."
They were served with mashed yam with boiled eggs and were taken through the slave dungeons of the castle, as part of the welcome ceremony.
The District Chief Executive for Komenda-Edina-Eguafo-Abrem, Nana Ato Arthur, reminded Africans in the Diaspora to share their expertise with Ghanaians and to invest in the country.
"As brothers and sisters you should find time to return to your roots and share your experience and achievements with us", he said.
Nana Arthur briefed the delegation about a cultural heritage and management programme being initiated to facilitate the development of the district and called on the private sector, traditional councils, sister city linkages, especially those in the Diaspora, to help in its implementation.
Mr. Van Russel, a member of the delegation, thanked the people of Elmina for the warm reception and said Surinamese and Ghanaians are the same, but divided as a result of slavery.
He said the delegation will build and strengthen the new linkage it has recovered and ensure that it yields positive results for the two countries.
Nana Kodwo Condua VI, Omanhene of Edina Traditional Area, described the visit as a "spiritual and family reunion" that should serve as an avenue for the unification of Africans on the continent and those in the Diaspora.

Original_Search_Title: Warri War Hijacked by Criminals - Bozimo
Headline: Nigeria: Warri War Hijacked by Criminals - Bozimo
Body_Text:
Benin City — MINISTER of Police Affairs, Chief Broderick Bozimo says the Warri war
has not ended because the legitimate struggle of the people was hijacked by
charlatans, criminals, oil bunkerers, kidnappers and all manners of
people.
He told newsmen in Benin City, weekend, that "if not for the police and
other security agents, that area would probably not be part of Nigeria now
because these people are just doing whatever they like."
Chief Bozimo stated that every time the government and leaders of the
warring ethnic groups succeeded in restoring a semblance of peace to the
troubled city, "these people with vested interests, the fifth columnists,
start it all over with deliberate burning of houses and killing of the
people."
He cited an instance with the recent meeting of the entire Ijaw
leadership at Yenagoa, the Bayelsa State capital, attended by the Minister
of Science and Technology, Prof. Isown, Minister of Transport, Senator
Seikibo, himself, the Bayelsa and Delta state governors, all notable Ijaw
traditional rulers and youths.
Chief Bozimo stated that soon after the meeting where it was pointed out
that the struggle had misfired and there would be no more fight, the fifth
columnists went on a destruction spree.
He said that the "Itsekiri, Urhobo and Ijaw do not hate themselves as
the criminals want to portray them," adding "if we are not a mad people and
I know we are not mad, is it not obvious that the burning and killing of
ourselves is a case of us cutting our noses to spite our faces, if we are
not idiots and I know we are not, is it possible for us to go the way we are
going when others are building."
His words, "most of the houses burnt in Warri belong to people who have
no idea what the war is all about. In the swamps and villages, old women and
men who do not know what local government means, who have not left their
homes for centuries are killed."
Chief Bozimo stated that Ijaw, Itsekiri and Urhobo leaders were worried
by the hijack of the struggle as "even though, politically, we have
divergent views, but that our views are divergent do not mean we should go
and burn ourselves. It is the fifth columnists, the oil bunkerers who
benefit when we fight and when two goats are fighting, the third goat will
carry the yam and run away."

Original_Search_Title: The Marriage in Minna
Headline: Nigeria: The Marriage in Minna
Body_Text:
From all indications, Aisha Babangida was not the only bride in Minna
last Saturday. Nor was her lucky husband, Bashir Garba the only eligible
groom at the occasion. Brides and grooms of various shapes and hues were out
aplenty at the Babangidas' talk-of-the-town wedding.
Society weddings attract all kinds of people for all kinds of
reasons. Well-wishers, stargazers, onlookers, busybodies, freeloaders,
pickpockets, jilted rivals, irate lovers, hen-pecked husbands, distraught
wives, anxious spinsters, scheming bachelors, etc all have their place at a
big wedding, adding colour and glamour and possibly, fresh gossip
angles. Each comes with a mission that may or may not have anything to do
with the man and woman being joined in wedlock.
So, it should not come as a surprise that there seemed to have been more
than a social dimension to the well-attended event in Minna at which a
Babangida girl and an Abacha boy were joined together as man and wife. Let's
face it, there was no way that a wedding ceremony involving the child of
former President Ibrahim Babangida would have been solely or even mainly a
social event devoted simply to joining two young lovebirds in holy
matrimony. Given the emerging atmospherics of this interesting season, it
was clear even to the blind that this wedding ceremony was not so much about
matrimony as it is about politics.
For good or for ill, General Babangida is today seen by many as the man
to beat at the 2007 Presidential election. A big social event like his
giving of a child away in marriage was bound to attract all manner of
politicians.
Expectedly, many of them, cast in the mold of beautiful brides on the
lookout for a suitable groom, went to the event in the hope of striking some
interesting political liaisons. The prize catch, of course, was the father
of the bride. And many hopeful brides-to-be went out to Minna to display
their political ware last Saturday, in the expectant anticipation that they
could catch the fancy of the much-sought-after prime suitor. The spectacle
recalls a custom in my mother's village in the Urratta area of Owerri in Imo
State. Big occasions such as the New Yam festival, the coronation of a new
chief, Christmas or Easter often attract a lot of people back home from
wherever they might be sojourning. Many usually come home with the riches
that are emblems of their successes in the lands of their sojourn. Some also
come home to find suitable "unspoilt" brides with whom to start a home, as
families get anxious that their wards should not fall into the predatory
hands of big city girls. In the event, a practice soon developed amongst
mothers eager to find suitable suitors for their daughters.
What they do is that during these festivals, when the village would be
filled with all the nouveaux arrive, mothers would deck their daughters out
in their best apparels and take them on endless parades of the village,
under the guise of felicitating with neighbours. My grandmother once noted
with wry humour that such perambulations would often lead into the
homesteads of families with eligible but unattached sons. Here, mother and
daughter would linger for a while under one pretext or the other, or in some
cases mother would leave the daughter behind to return home to attend to
some urgent business or chitchat with the intended groom's mother. In the
interim, some kind of rapport is meant to develop between boy and girl. Many
interesting liaisons, not all of them leading to marriage, are known to have
resulted from these parades. Quite a few have ended in bitter quarrels and
hot family feuds, especially in cases where the male party in question stood
accused of leading the lady on by "obtaining under false pretence". In some
cases where matters get out of hand, aggrieved families are known to resort
to the desperate expediency of shotgun weddings, often with unpredictable
results. Politics is usually about marriages of convenience. And it does
appear that the season of political marriages is now upon us once again as
people gear up for the next phase of the battle for the Nigerian
presidency. The marriage in Minna was thus largely about pairing up - brides
out in search of suitors; presidential aspirants in search of vice
presidential hopefuls; and vice versa.
The curious thing about the event, though, is that the real brides
failed to show up. Of course all manner of pretenders and interlopers were
out in full flight, struggling to be noticed and courted. Some of the
defeated Alliance for Democracy, AD, governors belong in this category. They
should be quite conversant with yet another marriage-related custom, this
time from Yorubaland.
People familiar with how traditional engagement ceremonies used to be
conducted amongst the Yoruba would recall the playful drama that often
ensued at such events. After the groom's family had stated their mission,
the bride's people are required to produce the bride so that the groom would
certify that that indeed was the lady he sought. Often, the bride's family
would produce all manner of people covered in veil - from wizened old women
to heavily pregnant housewives and even infants. Creative families have been
known to produce men dressed up as women. Of course, the groom's family
would refuse to accept these unlikely "brides" until the genuine material
was produced, usually after a lot of toing and froing, and some disguised
extortion.
So where were the real brides last Saturday? They were in Ota having a
meeting with the President, who increasingly might turn out to be the most
critical matchmaker in this game as the Yoruba decide whether to go to Minna
or Yola, or neither.
The message sent by the absence of the Odu'a governors from Minna cannot
be lost on the initiated, including the father of the bride. The message is
clear: hold your territory and let those interested in your support come to
you to ask for it. Don't go offering yourself. That could smack of begging,
or worse, prostitution. Those who are now busy trying to use Babangida to
even scores with Obasanjo over the outcome of the last elections had better
be warned. They are about to miscalculate again. The man in Minna knows the
difference between genuine and fake brides.
As for Vice President Atiku Abubakar, Babangida's presumed main rival
for 2007, the work that needs to be done keeps piling up by the day. Does
Atiku have marriageable daughters? We ask this question because it seems he
needs to stage his own show very soon. Now we hear another of Babangida's
children, this time his first son, is set to marry in December in Maiduguri,
right in the heart of the North East that is said to be Atiku's
stronghold. What does that tell you? Sounds to me like carrying the battle
into the enemy's territory. For sure, things can only get more
interesting. May God grant us long life and the good health to witness it
all.

Original_Search_Title: Mrs. Asmah Advises Young Industrialists
Headline: Ghana: Mrs. Asmah Advises Young Industrialists
Body_Text:
Mrs. Gladys Asmah, Minister of Women and Children's Affairs, yesterday
appealed to young industrialists to put their rich ideas into the production
and processing of local products.
"You might not be a scientist but you can do something to benefit the
entire society, particularly in your area of specialization."
Mrs. Asmah's remarks were made during interactions with industrial
studies students from the University of Ghana at the Food Research Institute
in Accra.
The aim was to introduce the students to the country's manufacturing
avenues and also take advantage of the opportunities in the private sector
especially in the area of food processing.
Mrs. Asmah said over the years, research findings had not been fully
utilized to boost the production base of the country because "they always
remain on the shelves".
She said with adequate planning and efficient time management the
country could use its own resources to develop.
"We have to start thinking of building the foundation for food
processing and adopt steps to curtail post harvest loses to ensure food
security," she explained.
Dr. W. K Amoa-Awua, Deputy Director of the Food Research Institute, said
the institute trained most of the small-scale manufacturers in the
country.
He said the institute was the brain behind most of the processed foods
such as yam fufu, banku mix, cocoyam fufu, fermented maize and maize meal on
the local market.
"We offer the producers the technology by training them and in certain
cases assist them in the production as well."
Dr Amoa-Awua said the institute had manufactured a pilot malt plant that
could be used in the breweries for large-scale production after its capacity
has been expanded.

Original_Search_Title: Economic and Physical Toll of Vandeikya Flood Disaster
Headline: Nigeria: Economic and Physical Toll of Vandeikya Flood Disaster
Body_Text:
Several weeks after flood from the Cameroon Mountains washed five Benue villages, the cries still echo. Our Benue Correspondent, Hir Joseph writes.
The three-day long heavy down pour which led to an unquantifiable disaster in the lives of members of five communities in the Vandeikya local council of Benue State cannot be forgotten in a hurry. The flood disaster which happened along the flood plains of Aya and Be rivers and beyond (both up and down stream), was a massive discharge of water that carried with it heavy objects from the upland Cameroon Mountain range, through the famous Obudu Valleys in the neighbouring Cross River State. The prolonged down pour which peaked on July 20 saw the reported case of death of a girl named Awuese Akpenwuan in the Gube Mbatenda Mbayan community, and a neighbour by name Iorkuma Mir who died while trying to recover a flowing mattress from the massive collection of water .
Although it washed away their homes and main economic power, the yet to be harvested crops as well as livestock, the people of the five Benue Valleys did not witness much death as did the victims in the neighbouring Cross River communities. Unlike in Benue where the swirling waters arrived by daybreak, the people of Cross River were awakened at midnight by the incident. Reports say many lives were lost in the waters even as three flowing corpses were recovered at Mbaduku, a Benue district, and identified as the remains of people from Cross River.
Although only two lives were lost to the flood in the five affected Vandeikya (Bebue) communities namely, Dagba and Mbazerem in Mbaduku district as well as Agera, Agidi-Mbakena and Gube in Mbayongu district, the level of damage has left the victims not any better than their late kinsmen: the over 3,000 victims whose houses and livelihood were washed away by the flood are presently taking refuge in open churches and primary school buildings in villages far away from theirs.
Save for the relief materials offered them recently by the outgoing Hon. Joseph Anakaa led Vandeikya local government transition committee, the over 3,000 flood victims would have all been corpses by now. With no means of sustenance, the victims live only by what concerned sympathisers offer them. What that means is that they now spend long days thinking where the next meal will come from. Living a life of want and an abject lack of housing and clothing, the victims who were thrown out in the cold by the July 20 flood disaster, had at the time of our visit, cried out for further assistance, having almost exhausted the food stuff supplied by the local council administration.
They specifically mentioned that basic infrastructure lost to the flood would mean that they will for long be cut off from the necessities of life. According to them five bridges across the rivers Be and Aya were completely damaged, a total of 52 compounds destroyed, economic trees ravaged, domestic animals perished, food stuff, yams, cassava and fadama farms washed away, while deep gullies such as never previously seen in the area have now emerged. These they cried out, will take them years to recover, particularly as the destruction of bridges will now cut them off from markets and neighbouring villages.
To address the issue whole-heartedly, Benue governor, Mr. George Akume paid an assessment visit to the five affected communities where he personally took note of the extent of damage done with a view to effectively planning and executing the physical reconstruction of bridges, culverts, feeder roads and other infrastructure. The governor who also assured that relief materials would be provided for victims, therefore ordered the immediate constitution of an ad-hoc committee on whose report and recommendation, the state government will act, as well as solicit the assistance of the President Olusegun Obasanjo-led Federal Government.
The ad-hoc committee which was set up by the state's Ministry of Water Resources and Environment promptly submitted their report and recommendations on July 25. In it the committee which visited all affected areas, put the value of domestic and household items as well as farm crops at "millions of naira."
Made up of a chairman, Iorshagher Daniel, a secretary, Alabar Terumbur and member, Nathan Ichor, the committee confirmed that the flood occasioned a marine destruction of infrastructure especially bridges and culverts. The bridges, according to their report, are at Agidi-Mbakena, Gube in Mbatende, Kyundu, Gusha and Ageva in Mbayongor district, as well as Dagba and Gbagboese-Gbeiu in Mbaduku district. The committee also noted that deep gullies have developed around culverts, and major roads have been washed away.
For immediate measures therefore, the committee recommended, apart from relief for household items, the reconstruction and rehabilitation of culverts and bridges as well as roads.
The committee also recommended the commissioning of a study to establish other causes that might have given rise to the flood, with a view to averting further disasters.
The committee also recommended the immediate equipment (funding) of the State Emergency Management Agency (SEMA), with necessary tools and machinery to respond swiftly to disasters within the state.

Original_Search_Title: NNPC/Shell Oil Pipeline Ruptures, Explodes in Ogoniland
Headline: Nigeria: NNPC/Shell Oil Pipeline Ruptures, Explodes in Ogoniland
Body_Text:
Crude oil spilling from a ruptured Shell Petroleum Development Company
(SPDC) joint venture oil pipeline burst into flames in Ogoni land, scorching
yam fields and spreading thick, black smoke for miles, residents and
activists disclosed last week.
Residents of Gio, in Khana Local Government Area of Rivers State, 40
kilometers east of Port Harcourt, said they first learned of the spill when
they awoke to an explosion early Friday and saw flames leaping from the
pipe.
In a bid to put out the blaze, Shell workers raced to the scene but were
denied access by angry villagers who demanded compensation for the spill, a
company spokesman who spoke on condition of anonymity said.
The spokesman explained that Company officials were negotiating with the
community to repair the leak, but this had not achieved any results as at
press time.
Ledum Mitee, leader of the activist Movement for the Survival of Ogoni
People, disclosed that the flames had died down but that rolling smoke made
it difficult for nearby residents to breathe.
Crude was also flowing into nearby streams, polluting drinking water,
Mitee said.
Mitee blamed the spill on corroding pipelines that have not been
maintained since Shell's pullout.
Shell suspended drilling in Ogoniland since 1993 as a result of protests
by villagers who accuse the oil giant and the Nigerian government of
colluding to cheat them of oil royalties.
Shell pipelines continue to transport crude through Ogoniland, a region
with about 500,000 people in Nigeria's swampy southern Niger Delta.
The cause of the latest spill has not been determined as the area
remains inaccessible and the strike action embarked upon by Shell workers
makes it difficult to reach concerned officials for their immediate
response.
While reacting to the development, the company's External Relations
Manager, Don Boham disclosed that the leaking pipeline has been clamped and
that efforts are being made to ascertain the degree of damage to the
surrounding farmlands.
On the issue of compensation, he pointed out that a different asset team
would be in-charge of that , adding that as soon as the degree of damage is
ascertained and those affected identified, steps would be taken to remedy
the situation.
It would be recalled that Shell senior staff union Petroleum and Natural
Gas Senior Staff Association of Nigeria (PENGASSAN) embarked on a strike
action last week to protest what they described as the company's EP
Globalisation policy.
The workers are also protesting the relocation of the SAP server to the
Hague , pointing out that this was unacceptable since it would put vital
information regarding the country's petroleum sector under the control of
foreigners.
The striking workers are also of the opinion that the Globalisation
policy of the company would cost Nigerian staff their jobs , while making
the same jobs available to foreigners .While speaking on the development,
the Lagos Chapter Chairman of the union, Celestine Ebigwu noted that
Europeans were using the issue of Globalisation to solve their unemployment
problems at the expense of Nigerians.
The union chairman also disclosed that the company has started bringing
