in non-technical expatriate personnel to do jobs which were hitherto
reserved for Nigerians and lamented government's lack of concern over the
development.
In Brunei, the policy of Shell Globalisation was rejected by the
government , the chairman disclosed , adding that the situation remains
unchanged in Nigeria because government has failed to live up to
expectation.
Meanwhile , the federal Government say about 300,000 barrels of crude,
more than one-tenth of Nigeria's exports are stolen daily by criminal rings
who syphon the fuel from pipelines using everything from buckets to
sophisticated pumps.

Original_Search_Title: Shocking Price Hikes
Headline: Zimbabwe: Shocking Price Hikes
Body_Text:
Harare — PRICES of basic goods have shot up incredibly to beyond the reach of
many over the past few months, in violation of Government price controls
with retailers blaming suppliers.
Despite the Government's warning that it had not changed its position on
price controls, many shops have raised prices far above the listed prices
Most shops have either ignored or dismissed the Government's warning
that those found overcharging would meet the full wrath of the law.
The retailers say that they continue to apply their standard mark-ups
for each category and blamed their suppliers for the rapid increase in
prices.
"Suppliers caught us by surprise as we had ordered goods at low prices
only to receive the goods at the high prices on delivery so we were left
with no choice but to shift the burden onto the consumers," said a manager
with a supermarket in down town Harare.
A loaf of bread now costs between $850 and $1 000 while the price of a
250-gramme packet of tea leaves is ranging between $800 and $1 000.
Milk prices have also gone up with a 500ml packet selling for $520. The
milk has, in fact, been repacked into 300ml and 750ml packets which cost
$450 and $750 respectively.
Early this year, Dairibord Zimbabwe Limited was fined $1,5 million for
selling milk in smaller packets without approval from the Ministry of
Industry and International Trade.
A kilogramme of beef now costs between $5 500 and $10 000, salt is
selling for as much as $1 700 for a kilogramme, a 2kg packet of rice now
costs $6 000 while a 750ml container of cooking oil costs close to $4
000.
A bar of soap now costs between $2 235 and $4 000, up from about $600
four months ago, while prices of bath soaps range from $2 500 upwards.
Many families no longer buy bath soap opting to use laundry soap.
A 100ml toothpaste tube is selling for more than $4 800 in some
supermarkets, while a 100ml bottle of Vaseline costs over $1 500.
The price of fresh and dried kapenta or matemba, which used to be quite
affordable and was the source of protein for many families, has shot up and
now costs $7 500 for a kilogramme. A 500-gramme packet of soya-meat chunks
costs $1 215.
In January a family of six required $77 768 worth of groceries a month,
but this figure shot up to more than $88 578 in February, $107 304 in March
and $132 226 in April.
By May the same family needed $181 260 and in July it needed $213 489
against a minimum wage of $47 000 a month.
These runway prices of basic commodities have seen a lot of people
change their lifestyles dramatically in order to survive.
A balanced diet is something that only a few can afford, while the rest
settle for whatever is available.
Gone are the days when families sat down to Sunday breakfast with
cereal, tea, juice, toast, egg, bacon, baked beans, sausage and tomato as
only a lucky few can manage tea and bread.
Some families have even cut down on washing clothes and do it only once
a week to save soap.
Washing powder now costs between $9 500 and $12 000 a kilogramme and
fabric softeners that are equally expensive are now considered a
luxury.
The survivor on the street these days is the man who can think of
alternatives.
Many people said they had now found alternatives for bread in sweet
potatoes, pumpkins, yams and porridge while others said they had to contend
with going about their business on empty stomachs until they returned home
for supper.
Traditional food that had been replaced by exotic and fine foods is
slowly coming back into many homes and this food has crossed the suburb
divide from Mbare to Borrowdale.
Bread has become part of the luxury foods for many families who now
prefer sweet potatoes, yams and pumpkins that nutritionists describe as
healthier.
People are now opting for chicken intestines and gizzards as well as the
legs and heads, while some families have become vegetarians because they
cannot afford to buy meat.
There has also been an increase in the number of people who bring packed
lunches to work. Both men and women said they now opted to bring food from
home as lunch in town and the industrial area was unaffordable.
The price of a plate of sadza in town ranges between $1 400 and $5 000
while take-aways sell at between $3 000 and $20 000.
There is also an increase in the number of people who have turned to
fruits as a result of the hardships.
Bananas and oranges are the most popular. They cost between $30 and
$150.
Many found buying fruits said they had realised that fruits were more
affordable and healthier.
"It is better to eat a fruit at lunch then have supper at home. Fruits
are healthier than all these fast foods anyway and if life has become this
hard, we better turn to them," said Ms Elizabeth Chikwanha of Eastlea.
Another culture that has cropped up as a result of the biting prices of
basics, is the culture of bargaining.
Consumers said they had since done away with impulse buying and always
looked around as many shops as possible before buying something.
This is, however, being hampered by the use of plastic money and people
end up buying from wherever they can use their cards.
Despite the increases, the director of price controls in the Ministry of
Industry and International Trade, a Mr Ndlovu said price controls were still
in place but they had been placed in two categories. These are the gazetted
goods and the price monitored goods.
Mr Ndlovu said commodities like maize and wheat together with flour and
mealie-meal were under strict control and the Government gazettes their
prices.
Under price monitoring, manufacturers and the ministry agree on a price
the commodity is supposed to be sold at.
He said commodities such as soap and cooking oil have been dropped from
the first category where the Government used to gazette their prices.
However Mr Ndlovu could not be drawn into commenting whether this meant
all the recent price increases have been approved by the ministry, referring
all questions to the Secretary for Industry and International Trade who
could not be reached for comment.
Efforts to get a comment from the Consumer Council of Zimbabwe's acting
executive director, Mrs Rosemary Mpofu, were fruitless as she was said to be
out of office and did not reply messages left with her secretary.
"An average person now needs as much as $200 000 for groceries that do
not even last a month and imagine with the kind of salaries that we earn,
how are we expected to survive," said an angry customer in a supermarket
along First Street.
Economic analysts say with the present rate of inflation of 399,5
percent, employees would need a salary and wage adjustment of not less than
700 percent.
Of the 399,5 percent year-on-year rate of inflation in July 2003,
increases in food prices accounted for 156 percentage points while non-food
items in the Consumer Price Index accounted for 243,5 percentage
points.
Food inflation prone to transitory shocks stood at 480,8 percent
gaining, 46,8-percentage points on the June 2003 rate of 434 percent.
The increase in month-on-month inflation in July 2003 was accounted for
by increases in the average price of beverages, bread, cereals, meat, rent
and rates.
The month-on-month food inflation stood at 17,8 percent in July 2003
dropping 6,6 percentage points on the June 2003 rate of 24,4 percent.
The month-on-month non-food inflation stood at 16,5 percent dropping 3
percentage points on the June 2003 rate of 19,5 percent.
The increase in year-on-year inflation was largely accounted for by
increases in the average price of oils and fats, beverages, milk, cheese,
eggs, meat and public transport.
Pushing inflation to such high figures is demand-pull inflation as well
as cost-push inflation.
Demand-pull inflation is emanating from the scarcity of certain
commodities thereby pushing prices up according to the simple law of supply
and demand.
The manufacturers of the commodities that are increasing prices when
they please are mainly driving cost-push inflation.
Government has therefore been urged to seriously look at the situation
and come down hard on the overcharging manufacturers.

Original_Search_Title: PM's Office Probes Pay to Ghost Luweero Claimants
Headline: Uganda: PM's Office Probes Pay to Ghost Luweero Claimants
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Government is querying claims by people who say they supplied the National Resistance Army (NRA) with various items during the five-year guerrilla war.
Among the claimants is a man who is demanding payment for his hippo, which the NRA supposedly ate in Kapeka, Luweero.
Sources said President Yoweri Museveni was unhappy with the inflated claims worth sh6.7b submitted by an earlier committee chaired by Stanley Mugwanya.
He set up a 20-man committee of bush commanders and former contacts chaired by Maj. Jacob Asiimwe, which has reduced the claims to sh2.5b.
Following an investigation by the Prime Minister's office, a claim of sh2b lodged by a city lawyer on behalf of five Luweero war claimants has been dismissed.
The New Vision could not establish how much has already been lost through such fake claims.
Erick Muhwezi of Muwhezi, Bamwite and Co Advocates had in the documents claimed that his clients supplied the NRA in 1985.
The claimants were, Yafesi Paskali (sh466m), Ali Ladha (sh441m), Antonio Zambe (sh435m), Lozio Kasereka (sh571m), Mugisha Kangye (sh117), of Ruhinda, Bushenyi and a Rwandese company, Comptoir Textile Rwandis (sh158m).
On verification by a select committee vetting the debts, it was found that the claims were fake and the purported suppliers were non-existent.
"Their files were dismissed because we found them fake and the lawyer failed to produce the claimants," said Maj. Matthias Sewakambo, a member of the committee.
But when contacted, Muhwezi maintained that his clients were genuine.
"They are scattered in Congo and Kasese. I have not yet been told to bring the witnesses but I am ready to produce them," he said on phone.
Zambe claims commander Saleh Rufu a.k.a. Salim Saleh, on October 17, 1985 took his six trailers carrying 228,270 litres of fuel in transit from Mombasa to Butembo in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC). He said Saleh promised to pay for the fuel.
Saleh's aide, Capt. Juma Seiko, dismissed the claim saying Saleh's debts were documented.
The in-charge fuel at the time was Col. Eric Mukasa, who was not available for comment.
Comptoir claims NRA in 1985 confiscated its containers loaded with second-hand clothes (coats and trousers) destined for Rwanda and it had not been paid to-date.
Ladha claims NRA confiscated 1,360 assorted bales of second-hand clothes and one truck carrying 455 bales of shoes. He claimed the goods were confiscated from Masaka while in transit from Mombasa to Bukavu in the DRC.
Kasereka claims he supplied timber to the NRA in Kasese. When he was interrogated, he said the late Col. Serwanga Lwanga received the timber.
The ex-NRA officers however, say the date Kasereka gave was when NRA overran Lubone Dairy farm near Fort portal and no NRA fighters were in Kasese then.
"By the time we overran Lubone, Serwanga Lwanga was in a Tororo Prison. He never came to Kasese and we took a whole month before entering Kasese," said Capt. John Kaye, who was one of the fighters.
Kaye said the NRA was not interested in timber.
Muhwezi reportedly confessed before some of the committee members that the claimant had given him false information.
Sources said Muhwezi failed to produce the claimants when asked to do so by the former Luweero Triangle minister, Tim Lwanga.
Former ethics and integrity minister Miria Matembe and the Inspector General of Government, Jotham Tumwesigye, also wrote to the PM's office stopping the payment after they received reports that the claimants were fictitious.
Mugisha Kangye claimed drugs worth sh117m were taken from him by the late Col. Frank Guma between Fort Portal and Kasese in 1985.
Investigations, however, revealed that Guma was the director of finance based in Luweero.
Committee members said some of the false claimants had been calling them promising hefty kickbacks if their payments were cleared. Payment is made through the justice and constitutional affairs ministry.
Some of the claimants have re-submitted their claims each time a new minister for Luweero is appointed or a new probe team is named, according to Sewankambo.
There is also evidence in the files to show that some of the people camped at Wankulukuku were paid and signed declaration forms saying the government owed them nothing.
Luweero Triangle minister Prof. Ssemakula Kiwanuka is vexed over the matter.
He said he had contacted the Police Criminal Investigations Depart-ment and the IGG's office to arrest the culprits.
"It is disgusting and we are taking some measures. We will take action against those who were paid but continue coming back," he said.
He said his office was also considering publishing all the names of the beneficiaries against their payments, to show the public how much has been paid.
Yasin Sejjamuge of Bukomero sub-county, Kiboga district, received sh3m on November 20, 2002 and signed documents saying he had no further debts with the Government.
He is the same person leading the group of claimants camped at Wankulukuku stadium.
On February 17, 2003 he submitted another claim of sh50,901,000 for fuel allegedly supplied.
A lady, Ida Nakku, is demanding for 10 cows after receiving sh1, 080,000 for her original claim.
Kiwanuka said one Mugwanya was paid sh54m but continued going to his office for more money.
The New Vision has also seen documents showing that Sheik Hassan Sendegge had initially filed a claim for 43 cows but is now demanding payment for 402 cows, which he claimed the NRA ate when they occupied his Kasibiriti farm for nine months.
Hope Matsiko of Ngoma, Luweero claimed for 30 cows. Records show she was paid for 15 cows. She filed another claim of 35 cows in 2001.
In 2003, on the strength of a purported letter from former ISO chief Brig. Henry Tumukunde dated March 28, 2003, she filed a claim of 100 cows. After 10 days she filed another claim of 85 cows.
There were also forged claims amounting to sh1,075,770,000, which have been dismissed.
These are cases where LCs signed for over 100 non-existent claimants in one area alone.
In Kapeka, Semuto and Wakyato alone, 172 ghost claimants were found on the list presented by the LCs. Other files rejected are fraudulent and exaggerated cases.
In Kitega Lugallu, Mubende district where the NRA fighters allegedly stayed for a day, one Leonard Kizza claimed to have supplied, 1,750 bags of posho, 600 bags of maize, 550 bags of beans, 300 bags of yams, 1,640 bags of Irish potatoes, 15 acres of cassava, 12 acres of sweet potatoes, 60 goats, 65 cows, 20 pigs and 21 sheep.
The Government is not paying for property looted, destroyed, or abandoned during the war.
But there are claims for iron sheets, sofa sets and radios.
The committee has also declared invalid some recommendations from some UPDF commanders collaborating with Luweero peasants to defraud the Government.
There are also forged signatures of dead commanders and ex-NRA men who left for Rwanda.

Original_Search_Title: Volta NPP Power Game Ends With Much Disenchantment
Headline: Ghana: Volta NPP Power Game Ends With Much Disenchantment
Body_Text:
Hohoe — It was a congress with a difference. There were no banners in the town
to, at least, inform people about what function it was, no invitation to the
media and no radio announcements were made.
But there were buses loads of bespectacled macho men. And many District
Chief Executives in the region- the good, bad and questionable- all showed
up from far and near; while yam sellers and other wayside traders living
right there enquired from pressmen what the drumming and horn tooting were
all about. They apparently had no prior information. For those who were
fortunate to get the programme, the venue indicated was E.P. Secondary
School, even though they were right in the premises of a teacher training
college.
Such was the organization of the Volta regional delegates conference of
the New Patriotic Party (NPP), which took place at the St. Theresa's
Training College here at Hohoe last Saturday, that the enthusiasm that was
ignited earlier in the day quickly evaporated like a morning dew.
Of course there were also allegations of fat white envelopes,
reminiscent of the black polyethylene bags described by Dr. Charles
Wereko-Brobby, the step aside Volta River Authority (VRA) Chief Executive,
at the congress grounds.
But at the end of the day, Mr. Lord Commey, National Organizer of the
NPP , managed to return to Accra with a list of nine names under his armpit
for all his "toil", thanks to the 'cooperation' of Mr. Atta Yeboah, Deputy
Regional Director ,of the Electoral Commission (EC).
And at the end of the day-long exercise, the 'big nine' names that came
up as the new regional executive of the party, have come to seal (at least
for now), the political power game, with its ethnocentric connotations, that
had rocked the region in recent times.
Mr. Emmanuel Ken-Wuud Nuworsu. finally emerged as the substantive
Regional Chairman, massively defeating his only remaining opponent, M
Emmanuel K. Asidigbe. Other new executive members are Christian Kofi Glakpe
(1st vice chairman), Madam Nancy Tsiportu (2nd vice), Emmanuel Elvis
Mesre-Boahene (secretary), Ken Ayim (assistant secretary), Nana Okyere
Mantey (treasurer), Johnson Avulete (organizer), Madam Grace Mensah (women
organizer) and John Reagan Tepretu (youth organizer).
Suspiciously, all those who won the elections were conspicuously
positioned at the bottom of each ballot paper.
This raised a lot of eyebrows in the hall. However, the disenchantment,
as noted by "The Chronicle", had more to do with the naked abuse of
incumbency and power than the poor hospitality accorded the observers,
activists, police and media men; as not even sachet water was served all
through the twelve hours of heat, sweat and noise that engulfed the assembly
hall.
At a point, one of the contestants for the chairmanship position,
Mr. Samuel Tsahey, felt he had no option than to announce his withdrawal
from the race, just minutes before voting was billed to have begun.
He cited irregularities like manipulation of the delegates' list,
saying, "The whole business was being handled by anonymous people". Indeed,
so glaring were some of the accusations that not even Lord Commey's
oratorical skills could not disabuse the minds of many a participant in the
hall.
When later The Chronicle contacted him for his views, Mr. Tsahey
explained that the list of 80 delegates who were to vote had been hidden
from him, even though he applied for it long ago; and alleged that the ones
for Ho West, Hohoe North and many other constituencies were compiled a few
minutes to voting time.
"The NPP in the region has become a private company; owned by Tommy
Amematekpor and run by Ken-wuud Nuworsu", he charged.
Mr. Tsahey argued that since Nuworsu, the incumbent, was also a
candidate, it was not prudent on the part of the national executive to have
given him the job of compiling the list of delegates; adding that there
could have been a committee for that job instead.
As if to corroborate the claims, another activist, from Nkwanta
constituency, (name withheld), said he heard Mr. Seshie Torblu, executive
member of Ho central, shouting that anyone who contested against Nuworsu
would get only 18 votes.
True to his word, Emmanuel L.Asidigbe, who vied with him, polled 18; an
indication, (according to the young man), that the game had been played in
the boardroom long before it was staged in the open.
On his part, Mr. Nuworsu refuted all the allegations leveled against
him.
He said he was not responsible for the compilation; neither did he hide
it from anyone.
"Ask anybody, it was the constituency chairmen who brought their own
lists to the regional office; and we forwarded them to the electoral
commission", he stressed.
He also contended that the EC official was simply following the list he
brought from Ho, and that there was no way he could have changed it at the
congress grounds.
Other guests of honour present were Hons. Kwasi Owusu Yeboah and Kofi
Dzamesi, Regional and Deputy Ministers respectively, Mustafa Hameed,
National Youth Organizer, Alhajis Maiga and Tanko, both of the Nasara Club,
Mr. Obed Okujeto, patron, and Mrs Agnes Okujeto.
In another development, a petition, filed against Nuworsu by Dr. Samuel
Ohene. Party member with number 0086226 is likely to throw a spanner into
the wheel of events in the near future.
The petition dated August 27, this year, and copied to the National
Chairman, Secretary and Council of Elders argued that recent publications in
"The Heritage" and "Independent" newspapers were so serious that they should
be looked into before he was allowed to stand.
According to Dr. Ohene, the same allegations, as published, have been
repeated to him directly by known NADMO employees. He said he was also aware
that the Serious Fraud Office, SFO, has completed investigations into a case
in which the new Regional Chairman and NADMO Regional Director, sold many
application forms and charged fees for them.
"The whole party will be exposed to ridicule and contempt when these
issues become subjects of criminal or civic litigation very soon- as I
believe they surely will", the petition stated.
Although the petition was dated some three clear days ahead of the
congress it is not known whether the authorities received it to consider the
contents. But whatever the case may be the concerns raised can have serious
political implications if the charges were true. What is left now is for a
litigant to prey on the "golden opportunity".

Original_Search_Title: NRC Hears Story of Old Woman Who Refused to Strip Naked
Headline: Ghana: The Most Disgraceful of Them All
Body_Text:
It was for a justifiable reason that General Emmanuel Erskine described
Case No. 2 at yesterday's sitting at the National Reconciliation Commission
as one of the most brutal acts of inhumanity to be heard at the place.
When Madam Ama Mainoo, a woman on the threshold of 90 got midway with
her story of how soldiers in the heady days of the chaos which visited Ghana
in the 80s, some people who sat in the public gallery had tears in their
eyes.
She was 69 and a seller of utensils and yam. She was also a farmer and
one day when she noticed some four soldiers standing by her wares at the
Koforidua market, she asked whether they wanted to buy some of the items but
this drew their anger and one of them slapped her. She was then told that
she was under arrest and given another slap with one of them calling her a
witch.
At this stage she demanded to know whether she had raised a loan from
the government and this again attracted another slap for her.
She was asked to carry the utensils she was selling on her head but she
complained that she could not do so and this drew another slap for her and a
shove with the butt of a rifle.
At this stage she requested that they kill her but they took her
together with other women who were selling various items at the market to a
place called Club. In the vehicle which conveyed them to the place, one
pregnant woman who sells tuna had one of the fish forced into her mouth, the
sight of which she added made her to fall into unconsciousness.
At the club she said one soldier started removing her wares one by one
into an inner room and she asked whether they were being bought and this too
got her another slap.
They were made to march carrying their wares on their heads, towards the
market she said. The weight of the items was too much for her and so she
fell down on a number of occasions she told the commission.
At the trotro station in Koforidua, she was asked to strip naked but she
pleaded that she had an in-law there and could therefore not do that.
Then began the selling of her items at reduced prices. She again asked
whether the proceeds were going to be given to her but was told that they
were going into the government coffers.
It was not over yet as one soldier insisted that she strip but she asked
for death instead.
She was forced onto a table she narrated and asked to strip and this
time she removed her outer cloth but the soldier asked her to do likewise
for her pants but this she stood her grounds and refused.
She told the commission that she was given seven lashes of a flexible
wire rod and from this she had a swollen neck and was admitted at hospital
for forty days. At the end she shed tears copiously as she sought assistance
from the commission.
ERSKINE: This is one of the most brutal acts of inhumanity that our
people committed on our own people. A woman born in 1914 and 69 at the time
of the act, this is where the inhumanity comes in.
This is the most disgraceful petition to have come before this
commission and this country. Let's hope that never again will this happen in
this country.
We keep saying this all the time. Those soldiers who committed those
acts could have been her children.
If they are still alive and listening they must be ashamed of
themselves. Those serving should stay away from engaging in this kind of
thing. SYLVIA: You are a very brave woman and we are happy you are here. ENO
AMA: It is God who made it possible.
SYLVIA: We have heard about atrocities committed in Koforidua including
the beating of women and thanks to God that you maintained your dignity by
not removing your pants. ENO AMA: It was God who gave me the power.
SYLVIA: It is a shame that she insisted on her right yet she was beaten
by people who said they had come to fight for human rights.
ENO AMA: One soldier who raised objection to the maltreatment was
slapped by his colleague. SYLVIA: Were they on drugs? ENO AMA: I can't
tell. SYLVIA: Without the influence of drugs or alcohol they could not have
behaved the way they did. ENO AMA: I agree. A man born of a woman won't
behave the way they did.
PALMER-BUCKLE: I commend you for standing up against military and police
brutalities and commend you also for even coming here.
As the chairman rightly said it is our duty to sympathise with her. We
want to thank the church women and the market women at Asokore for the
support they gave her at the time.
If she has not yet gone to her maker it is because perhaps he wants her
to tell her story. If there is anything we can do to help her we will do so
when we meet at Koforidua. I'd like to meet her children at Asokore. Let her
leave her particulars with our counselors.
UBORR: Those who sought to disgrace you have rather disgraced themselves
because whereas you have been able to tell your story they cannot dare.
When the female commissioners examined her for evidence of what she went
through they reported seeing black spots on her lower back and concluded
that there were marks from the lashes she received from the soldiers.
Earlier one Edward Charles Sagoe a one-time cinema owner in Koforidua
had also appeared to tell the commission of how his Datsun Car was seized by
a group of armed soldiers and he has since not recovered it.
He told the commission that when he went to Burma Camp to search for the
car he was warned never to come there again if he did not want trouble.
Mr. Sagoe was a popular man in Koforidua and he owned the Rio
Cinema. Sitting continues tomorrow.

Original_Search_Title: For a Just Reform
Headline: Nigeria: For a Just Reform
Body_Text:
Lagos — THE recent decision by the National Council of State to set up a
technical committee to recommend ways by which the bastardized local
government system in Nigeria could be reformed and restructured, is no
doubt, an idea well conceived. However, in keeping with the maxim that "he
who comes to equity, must come with clean hands", this writer firmly
believes that the Obasanjo-led government should be able to convince
Nigerians at the end of the day that he and indeed his party - the
"almighty" Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), are not going to use the
so-called reforms of the local governments to play further on the already
ravaged collective psyche and intelligence of the Nigerian populace.
The growing fear of a hidden agenda that belie the so-called reforms
cannot be swept under the carpet, especially when one looks back into what
the local government system has suffered in the hands of the thirty-six
state governors in the country since the expiration of the first fourth
public dispensation in May 2002. It is story replete with bastardization,
frustration and hopelessness. You need to see chairmanship and
councillorship aspirants in the country who have been endlessly tossed,
dribbled and siphoned, and you begin to wonder why those innocent Nigerians
are subjected to such inhuman and ungodly treatments.
It is an incontestable truism that the problem with the local government
system is not caused by non-performance by elected council authorities but
it is a product of the over-bloated selfishness of power-hungry and
money-conscious state governors who have constituted themselves colossuses
in our political landscape. The deliberate shift of elections into the 774
councils in the country in preference for Transition Committees, is a case
study of the governor's blatant display of greed and avarice. This, no
doubt, puts to question the sincerity of purpose of the state governors and
their cohorts at the States' Electoral Commissions.
Unfortunately, neither the States' Houses of Assembly - bodies
constitutionally charged with the direct legislation over the Councils - nor
the Obasanjo-led Council of State that has just woken from its slumber,
could ask questions, intervene or constitutionally stop the governors from
making further caricature of the local government system. But what do you
expect from most Houses of Assembly that have become stooges and toothless
bulldogs in the hands of state governors? Does the kettle call the pot
black? Or, would Obasanjo swear that he does not know before now the
illegalities that have bedeviled the local government system? Who is
cheating who?
The claim in some quarters that non-performance on the part of Council
Chairmen is the cause of the planned reforms, does not hold any water. The
non-performance in the Council areas is unarguably caused by our state
governors. While this writer does not intend to hold brief for the Council
Chairmen, after all they are no saints, it remains obvious that the
large-scale corruption going on in our local government areas is
masterminded by the state governors who have seen in the local governments,
veritable avenues for siphoning public funds. In fact, in some states, the
local governments are extended ministries and parastatals that are at the
mercy of wolves in the garb of governors. To say that the governors see in
the local government councils, veritable goldmines, is not an
overstatement.
Whoever mooted the idea of Joint Account for states and local
governments. The so-called state and local governments' joint account system
serves as the final deathblow on the financial independence of the local
government system. Elected authorities of the local government areas are now
at the mercy of the governors who play god on them. Yes, agreed that section
169 of the 1999 constitution as amended in 2001 provides in subsection 6 for
the running of a "State Joint Local Government Account", the same
constitution, in sub-sections 7 and 8, empowers the states to distribute the
amounts standing to the credit of the local governments accordingly.
But what do we witness in our states? - a brazen display of executive
recklessness. The truth is that our governors have gone-haywire and no one
appears ready to stop them. They appear to be wielding enormous powers in
the control of the finances of the local governments such that an
institutionalized confederation of looting appears to be taking place. Yet,
the respected Indian nationalist, Jawaharlal Nehru had forewarned that "evil
unchecked grows, evil tolerated poisons the whole system." Who should
therefore, blame Council Chairmen if they emulate their "looting
masters".
Before the expiration of the tenure of the immediate past local
government councils, attempts were made in some states by council
legislators to remove council chairmen found to have violated constitutional
provisions but their efforts proved abortive, no thanks to strong backings
received by this offenders from their "partners in crime-governors". Yet,
any keen followers of events in the local governments will agree with this
writer that capital projects, and indeed meaningful developmental
programmes, stopped in so many councils as soon as the so-called joint
account system came into operation. The governors proverbially hold the
knife and the yam. They decide which local government area gets what. The
rest gets into their private pockets and businesses.
This writer happens to come from one of the local government areas in
Abia State where in the past one year or thereabout, the local government
authority has not embarked on any capital project. The very last capital
project executed by the local government council was the one done by the
elected council chairman whose tenure ended in May 2002.
Restructuring or reforming the local government system is not a bad
idea. However, methinks that the reformation should start first by taking
away the management of the council from the state governors. If the councils
cannot be made autonomous then, they should be returned to the Federal
Government for proper management. In addition, the Joint Account System
should be abolished as the aim has been defeated. Allocations should come
directly to the councils from the federal government via the Revenue
Mobilization, Allocation and Fiscal Commission. The governors are hawks and
wolves, who see in the Councils, ready preys to feed on. They should no
longer be allowed to manage our local government areas.
The National Assembly on the other hand, should be guided by national
interest, put the Supreme Court ruling at the back of their mind and give
appropriate legal backings to the afore-mentioned suggestions. Again, there
is nothing wrong with the 1976 reforms and the Ibrahim Babangida
modifications. Any attempt to scrap the council areas will not only be
retrogressive but will certainly breed uncontrollable social chaos.
Imah wrote in from Mbala-Isuochi, Abia State.

Original_Search_Title: Banks And Sierra Leone Border Reopen
Headline: Liberia: Banks And Sierra Leone Border Reopen
Body_Text:
Monrovia — Small signs are emerging that life in the Liberian capital Monrovia is
beginning to return to normal following the signing of a peace agreement on
18 August aimed at ending 14 years of civil war.
Banks have begun to reopen for the first time in nearly three months and
the government is talking of reopening schools on October 1.
Overland travel to Sierra Leone has also become possible for the first
time in six months, even though this involves a precarious 90 km trip
through territory controlled by the Liberians United for Reconciliation and
Democracy (LURD) rebel movement.
The main border crossing at Bo Waterside reopened to pedestrians last
Saturday, although it remains closed to vehicles for the time being.
Commercial banks in Monrovia closed on 6 June as the Liberians United
for Reconciliation and Democracy (LURD) rebel movement launched the first of
three attacks on the capital. However, the state-owned Liberian Bank for
Development and Investment reopened its doors this week following the
signing of a peace agreement on 18 August.
Information Minister Reginald Goodridge told IRIN the Central Bank of
Liberia (CBL) would reopen on Monday. The two other main banks in Monrovia,
Ecobank and Tradevco Bank and International Bank, were expected to follow
suit soon after, he added.
The closure of the banks hampered what little commercial activity could
still continue after LURD overran the northwestern suburbs of Monrovia and
pounded the city centre with mortar bombs and rockets. Relief organisations
were forced to fly in large quantities of dollars in cash to continue paying
their staff and buy in essential services.
Education ministry officials told UN and relief agency representatives
on Wednesday they were hoping to resume normal school classes on October 1,
two weeks before acting president Moses Blah hands over power to a new
broad-based transitional government.
However, the reopening of schools, most of which have been turned into
temporary shelters for thousands of people displaced from their homes by the
recent fighting in Monrovia, has not yet been officially announced.
Aid workers quoted education ministry officials as saying that schools
would be reopened in phases because of the still volatile security
situation. Those in Monrovia, where the presence of a West African
peacekeeping force affords better public safety, would be the first to
resume classes.
The bridge over the Mano river to Sierra Leone at Bo Waterside was
reopened to pedestrians even though the territory between the frontier and
Monrovia remains firmly under LURD control.
Jacques Klein, the UN special envoy to Liberia met Sierra Leonean
President Ahmad Tejan Kabbah in Freetown earlier this week to ask that the
border be reopened to vehicle traffic as well to facilitate humanitarian
access.
The United Nations is keen to bring food and other supplies into Liberia
overland from Sierra Leone and would like to resume the repatriation of the
13,500 remaining Sierra Leonean refugees in the country by road.
Until now the refugees have been repatriated by sea. But the
Guinea-registered ferry that was being used, the MV Overbeck, suffered
engine failure at sea on Sunday, shortly after leaving Monrovia with nearly
200 people on board. It was finally towed into Freetown on Thursday after
drifting on the high seas for five days.
Sierra Leone closed its border with Liberia in February after LURD
fighters seized control of all of western Liberia, pushing several
demoralised hundred government troops into Sierra Leone to seek refuge.
But last Saturday border guards began allowing pedestrians to cross the
international bridge freely in both directions on Saturday.
An IRIN correspondent who visited Bo Waterside on Thursday was told by
Liberian and Sierra Leonean officials that more than 500 Liberian refugees
had returned to Liberia since then.
At least 75 came home on Thursday carrying mattresses and other
personnel belongings.
Varney Tombekai, one of the returnees, told IRIN: "I am very happy to
return home. It is better to live in your country than being a refugee. I
can now see peace in Liberia. "He said many of the Liberian refugees
sheltering in the nearby Sierra Leonean towns of Zimmi and Jenema were eager
to return.
Liberia's northern border with Guinea remains inaccessible from Monrovia
because of recent fighting near the LURD-held town of Gbarnga which
straddles the main road to Guinea. However, many people have begun to
transit through Sierra Leone to Guinea instead.
On Thursday, a few Liberians were simply crossing into Sierra Leone to
purchase food and other commodities like soap, milk, and sugar among
others.
"Thank God, we can now do our businesses again. I am appealing to the
Sierra Leonean government to open the borders to cars so we can bring more
goods and food to Liberia and our people can visit some of their relatives
inside Sierra Leone", a female trader said.
Food remains scarce in the LURD-controlled towns and villages along the
90 km road between Monrovia and the Sierra Leone border. Several villagers
told IRIN they were living on yams and cassava growing wild in the bush, but
these were becoming difficult to find.
Relief agencies are gradually establishing a presence in the
area. Medecins Sans Frontieres has opened a mobile clinic in the town of
Tubmanburg, 60 km northwest of Monrovia and the UN children's Fund (UNICEF)
has resumed the immunisation of children in the area.
UNICEF and its partners have mounted a seven-day campaign targetting
42,000 children, who have had no access to immunization facilities since
February. Many of them have been displaced with their families to Tubmanburg
from other parts of western Liberia.

Original_Search_Title: Increase in Price of Fuel - Inflation Rate Shot Up to 14% in June
Headline: Nigeria: Increase in Price of Fuel - Inflation Rate Shot Up to 14% in June
Body_Text:
In apparent reflection of the sharp increases in the prices of goods and
services occassioned by the increase in pump price of petroleum products,
rate of inflation shot up to 14 per cent at the end of June, from 10 per
cent in May. This represents a major draw back to the quest for a single
digit inflation rate which is one of the objectives of the Federal
government for fiscal 2003.
It would be recalled that the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) in the
communique issued by its monetary policy committee for the Month of July,
had predicted a modest rise in the inflation rate in June and July. The
expected rise according to the apex bank is driven by the effect of the
crises that followed the hike in the petroleum products on transportation
cost.
Consumer Price Index (CPI) figures released on Tuesday by the Federal
Office of Statistics (FOS), indicated that the composite consumer price
index recorded 5450.1 in the month of June, 2003. This showed a significant
increase of 5.1 per cent over 5184.9 recorded in May, 2003. Similarly, the
FOS report also showed that All Items Index rose by 10.9 per cent in the
second quarter of 2003 as against 0.4 per cent decrease observed in the
first quarter of 2003.
The report attributed the general increase to a rise in the prices of
fuel, food items, clothing materials, transport fare and services across the
country. This affected both the urban and rural indices which rose by 5.8
and 5.0 per cent respectively when compared with May, 2003 indices. FOS also
reported that in the twelve months to June, 2003 the average consumer price
level went up by 13.9 per cent, higher than 8.7 per cent observed by making
a similar comparison in May, 2003 while the urban and rural indices showed
increases of 14.3 and 13.8 perc ent respectively over the same period.
On the percentage change in the average composite CPI, the report stated
that, "the percentage change in the average prices of goods and services for
the twelve-month period ending June 2003 over the average of the previous
twelve-month period was 10.1. This means that a fixed market basket of
consumer goods and services purchased with N100.0 during the twelve-month
period ending June 2002 sold for N110.1 on the average for the corresponding
period ending June, 2003. The percentage change also indicated a small
increase when compared with that of May, 2003."
Similarly, "the composite food index increased by 4.1 per cent in June,
2003. The rise in the index was largely due to the increase in the prices of
stables and proteins such as rice, plantain, yam tuber, beef, and
fish. Edible oil also showed marginal increase over previous month's
prices. The level of the composite food index was higher than the
corresponding level a year ago by 6.2 per cent. The average annual rate of
increase in the index was 6.0 per cent for the twelve- month period ending
June, 2003."
However, the All Item Less Farm Produce index which excludes the prices
of agricultural produce rose by 5.7 per cent in June, 2003 against May, 2003
figure, while in the twelve months to June, 2003 the index went up by 21.7
per cent and the average annual rate of rise of the index was 16.6 per cent
for the twelve month period ending June, 2003.

Original_Search_Title: KCC Razes House Built in Wetland
Headline: Uganda: KCC Razes House Built in Wetland
Body_Text:
Kampala — KAMPALA City Council (KCC) has demolished a building in Bugolobi to halt
encroachment on the Nakivubo swamp, which filters wastewater before it
enters Lake Victoria.
KCC law enforcement department and the Police also arrested the site
engineer, Waliggo. He was charged in a city court. The building was earlier
razed twice.
Phoebe Gubya, the KCC environment officer yesterday said Nakawa division
carried out the demolition of the house without her endorsement.
She said a recent ministerial directive to protect Kampala's vital
swamps could not be executed because it did not provide for compensation of
the land and house owners.
Paul Mafabi, the assistant commissioner in-charge of the Wetlands
Inspection Division (WID), blamed KCC for laxity in enforcing the law. He
said KCC continued to approve building plans in the wetlands.
"We will break down more houses. But we have to begin by discouraging
new encroachment on the swamp," Mafabi said
He said plans were underway to gazette the Nakivubo swamp as a protected
area for purification of water.
Mafabi said the swamp was being destroyed by industries, human
settlements and growers of yams.
The new swamp boundaries have been adjusted to favour rich property
owners who have encroached on the natural boundaries of the swamp at
Bugolobi.
He said the Nakivubo swamp had suffered encroachment over the years and
its distance from the lake has declined from 4.8 kilometres to three. "If
nothing is done, we shall soon be mining water from filth because Ggaba
Water Works is three kilometres from Murchison bay," Mafabi said.

Original_Search_Title: Labour Leader Commits Suicide in Ado-Ekiti
Headline: Nigeria: Labour Leader Commits Suicide in Ado-Ekiti
Body_Text:
Ado-Ekiti — Tragedy struck in Ado-Ekiti weekend when the Chairman of the newly
inaugurated Trade Union Congress (TUC) in Ekiti State, Mr Olu Johnson,
allegedly committed suicide by hanging.
Mr Johnson who was the deputy director of the Public Complaints
Commission in Ekiti State, was sworn in along with four other officers last
Tuesday to run the affairs of TUC, an alternative union to the Nigeria
Labour Congress (NLC).
The new chairman, according to reports, hanged himself inside his
bedroom in the early hours of Saturday after eating a meal of pounded yam
prepared by his wife.
Late Johnson, a father of six was living in a flat around Omolayo area
of Ado-Ekiti with his second wife, after he divorced his first wife who had
four children for him.
THISDAY gathered that the deceased on the fateful day had kept the rope
used to draw water from the house well in a secret place and later sneaked
into the bedroom to hang himself with it, when his wife and children were
having their bath.
The wife on returning to the bedroom after her bath found the dangling
body of her husband on the rope tied to the ceiling fan. It was gathered
that the police were immediately contacted after which the body was removed
and rushed to the state Special Hospital, Ado-Ekiti where he was confirmed
dead.
Several posers have been raised over what could have pushed the Deputy
Director who is presently on level 15 to hang himself.
None seemed to offer satisfactory explanation for the Ado Ekiti tragedy;
as suicide was a very uncommon crime in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Lessons From Aba, Political Mustard Seeds
Headline: Nigeria: Lessons From Aba, Political Mustard Seeds
Body_Text:
So, even before the moves to reassert the Igbo's right to his
entitlements in Nigeria, the major players in the team were discussed and
analyzed. As politicians, none was wont to allow the other the right of way
in what they thought were leadership matters. It was here that Ukiwe came in
handy. As a soldier, he was not really one of them. He had not been involved
in the party politicking that had bruised most of them. On that score, he
was "acceptable" to Mbakwe as he was to Dr. Okigbo. In spite of himself,
Ukiwe began paying courtesy visits to both sides anytime he was in the
East. In time he had built up so much rapport that when he hosted the
ice-breaking meeting In December 1988, both homeboys and "Lagosians" freely
jostled shoulder to shoulder. Then as Mbakwe and Okigbo alternated as hosts,
they used virtually the same "good offices" and their interpersonal
communication increased. Added to this, was the absence of emphasis on who
was "the leader", and you got the unfettered freedom with which these
sensitive men of timber and calibre shared jokes, plans and pounded yam with
egusi soup.
The effect of the new-found harmony among the Igbo elite was
electrifying. Exchange of visits increased, telephone calls among these men
quadrupled. Homeboys were encouraged to call on "Lagosians" each time they
were in Lagos. The Lagos boys were similarly required to visit or call on
the homeboys whenever they were in the East. Even before this point was
reached, a little known group in the North had formed and rebuilt esprit de
corps as well as faith in the Igbo spirit.
With headquarters in Kaduna and led by a resilient, highly mobile and
persuasive organizer, Chief Ezenwa Odimuko, this group broke into the
national scene with their Idea of a new and all-embracing new yam festival
christened Iri Ji Ndigbo. This was to be an annual event whose focus was
cultural revival and spiritual reawakening. In the words of the chairman of
the Igbo Cultural Association, Chief Odimuko, the festival was to serve as a
"forum of cultural homecoming for Ndi Igbo ... and an occasion for the rest
of the country, nay the world, to come and share in the experience of an
aspect of the culture and tradition of Ndi Igbo". After a great deal of
"legwork" and homework, the association planned the kickoff festival for
October 1986 to be staged at Enugu, the capital of the former Eastern
Region. Commodore Ukiwe, then Chief of General Staff, was billed to grace
the occasion as special guest of honour. It was the Commodore who in fact
guided the association as they tried to invite the then Nigerian president,
President Babangida to the occasion. To blunt any misgivings about the
political undertones of the event, the association was advised to add
"Agricultural Show" to the title of the festival.
This slight emphasis on agriculture and food production was meant to tie
in with the government's agrarian drive and provide cover for other less
than global aims of the organizers: Everything worked well including the
rally of traditional rulers, the renewed pride of the people in themselves,
the revival of brotherhood among the Igbo elite, the involvement of all and
sundry. But one crucial thing went wrong. Just as the D-day was around the
corner, Commodore Ukiwe was removed as Chief of General Staff in
circumstances that served to remind the Igbos that their reintegration into
and full participation in the Nigerian polity was still a
dream. Nevertheless, Igbos milled to the occasion and imbibed the full
lesson of the low political blow. Altogether, a total of four festivals were
staged between Enugu and Owerri (1986-89). 'The association lent
considerable support to major cultural outings in the Port Harcourt and
Aniom axis of the Igbo nation. It also spawned similar organizations all
over Igbo land, e.g. the Bendel East Cultural Association (BECA) which
became a major plank in the demand for Anioma State.
However, the association suffered one other setback which had a
debilitating effect on its activities. As it recorded one success after
another, improved upon its organization and spread its revivalist messages,
the association became even more ambitious in its aims. In 1987/88 it
embarked on an ambitious plan to build an Igbo Cultural Centre towards which
end, a mammoth fund raising programme was scheduled for Aba. Aba of course
is the cradle of Igbo industry and cultural socialization.
The Enyimba spirit was what successful Igbo leaders had cultivated and
it had invariably buoyed them to the pinnacle of peoples power. Chief Sam
Mbakwe and Chief Ojukwu could write volumes on it.
An elaborate programme was crafted for this purpose. As if to make up
for the 1986 spoiler, Commodore Ukiwe again was to feature prominently at
the Aba extravaganza. Igwe Osita Agwuna was to deliver the keynote
address. Chief Sonny Odogwu was to lead the Igbos from across the Niger to
the event. Chief Emmanuel Aguma and Obi Chukwumela Nnam Obi were to swamp
the venue with the Port Harcourt Igbos. Cultural troupes from across
Igboland and guest troupes from our brothers in the East, West and parts of
the North were also invited to the occasion. As usual, Chief Odimuko and his
men were all over the place briefing traditional rulers, organizing groups,
predisposing launchers, sprucing up the venue and generally tidying up the
programme.
Once again, just as D-day approached, the association's executive split
right down the middle. The secretary was pitted against the chairman in what
seemed an unnecessary power struggle. In less than no time the other members
of the executive lined up behind the combatants and the fire went out of
hand. The apple of discord obviously was thrown in from outside and just a
day to the event proper, the police moved in to outlaw the event. According
to the police, the secretary had written them to say that the split in the
executive was such that bloodshed was inevitable if the event went on as
scheduled. Of course, every effort to persuade the police that the contrary
was the case failed, with the result that troupes from across the Niger,
keynote speaker, guest, of honour arrived for a nonevent. The damage was
irreparable, the loss in material terms was innumerable, but the blow on the
resurgent Igbo spirit was incalculable.
Thereafter, we set out to establish the source of the friction and
repair the damage. Eze Isaac Ikonne, Enyi 1 of Aba, set the ball rolling in
the effort to reconcile the chairman and his secretary but to no
avail. Since the secretary remained adamant to peace moves and chose to ally
himself with the Eze Igbo in Kaduna at that time, we decided to administer
the strong medicine. I went with the chairman to the secretary's home
village and reported his obvious collusion with officialdom to sabotage the
Aba launching, whereupon his traditional ruler sent word to him to report
for arbitration. He failed on two occasions to heed the call of his own
royal father.
Thus, when eventually a formal "hearing" of the case came up under the
good offices of Eze B. Enweremadu, with the traditional rulers of the
chairman and secretary present, the secretary easily lost both face and
case. But in the spirit of brotherhood the two officers were made to shake
hands, share food and drinks and were under obligations to repair the damage
done by cooperating to execute the forthcoming Iri Ji festival and relaunch
the cultural centre fund-raising before new elections.
The lessons of the disaster had remained instructive to many an
organizer, i.e. that external forces using state coercive apparatus could,
at any given time throw a monkey wrench in the works to frustrate and
forestall a genuine patriotic move. But most importantly, it showed how the
many traitors in our midst then could be dealt with through good case-making
at their home bases using the traditional institutions for both arbitration
and sanction.
One natural consequence of the coming together of the Igbo elite was a
realization that a movement forward was impossible without moving along with
our brothers in the East. Paradoxically this came at a time the Igbos had
been politically truncated into two states while the so-called minorities
had been rewarded with three states. For quite sometime, many Igbo speakers
at meetings had this peculiar problem of reference. If these brothers were a
minority in the dim or recent past, are they still a minority with three
states? Could you now refer to them as the new majority? This dilemma first
surfaced in 1989 when the former Cross River State gave birth to Akwa Ibom
State. When the Igbos got nothing and the then Head of State stated with
finality that he had written the final chapter in the state creation book,
the gut feeling among th Igbos was to start complaining. But good sense
prevailed. If you protested against this blatant and deliberate injustice,
it might look like you were begrudging the geopolitical gain of Akwa
Ibom. So the Igbo leadership swallowed hard and sincerely rejoiced with our
neighbours in Akwa Ibom. This turned out to be a good political
investment.
But providence was also on the side of the East. A small political
mustard seed, The Oriental Club, had blossomed into an awesome political
tree. It was made up of young and energetic easterners who had lived through
the tortuous past and had seen through the divisive tactics of those whose
stock-in-trade had been to divide the East and subjugate it. Members were
also broad-minded enough to admit that blunders had been committed in the
past by easterners against easterners. Whether these mistakes were those of
the head or heart, members got them resolved. It was time to forgive and
forget so that the East could regain its balance and reclaim 1ts rightful
place in the leadership of the nation. Chief Jacob U. Esuene, former
military governor of Cross River State, was the leading light of the
movement with his home in Eket as the meeting point. The club had done so
well that some Igbo political drifters even tried to identify with it. It is
to the club's eternal credit that it laid the foundation for the eventual
rise of what turned out to be The Eastern Forum (Here we mean the real
Eastern Forum not the counterfeit one by some meddlers and their fellow
travellers). The club took yet another credit - that of reaching out across
the Niger for a genuine handshake with our brothers in the West (Here again,
this is not to be mistaken for the much-vaunted handshake across the Niger
by some "armchairists").
Thus the atmosphere was most auspicious when leaders of the East began
to meet in Enugu. At first,' it appeared there were two meetings, one
holding at Ezeogo Dr. Akanu Ibiam's Onikepe Lodge and the other at Dr. Pius
Okigbo's residence. Although they had nothing but respect for the Ezeogo,
most non-Igbo leaders in the East seemed somehow more comfortable with
Dr. Okigbo as host. Although there was no rivalry between the two hosts, it
only explained how the parallel meetings evolved.
As stated earlier, one ex-sergeant, Chris Okigbo, had been in touch with
former head of state, Gen. Obasanjo and Ezeogo Ibiam for the express purpose
of strengthening the bonds of friendship between the East and the
West. After Obasanjo's delegation was hosted at Dr. Ibiam's house, a return
delegation from Dr. Ibiam enjoyed Obasanjo's hospitality. In an effort to
drum up a bigger and better host community in the East, good old 'Chris
Okigbo frantically reached out to Igbo and non-Igbo leaders alike for a
meeting at Ezeogo's Enugu residence to which only Mazi S.G. Ikoku and Chief
C. C. Onoh were the only notables. The meeting was adjourned for two weeks
for poor attendance.
On the other hand the forum which met frequently at Dr. Okigbo's house
was always well-attended. But in view of the fact that a major delegation
was soon to visit the East, it was decided to unify the meetings;
Dr. Okigbos was to swallow Dr. lbiam's. At the time, Dr. Emmanuel Nsan,
former health minister in the Buhari regime, was coordinator for Cross River
and Akwa Ibom. A phone call got him one night at his clinic and informed him
that the meeting at Dr. Ibiam's had been cancelled; it was to hold at
Dr. Okigbo's on a new date. It took him sometime to come to terms with so
sudden a change but he nevertheless complied. So when none of our brothers
turned up at Dr. lbiam's for the second time, that venue wound up and
Dr. Okigbo's picked up even more steam.
From that point on, everything moved along well-oiled wheels and
rails. It was there, that the East took the momentous decision on collective
identification with a political tendency during Gen. Babangida's ill-fated
political associations. Final ratification of the resolve was expected at
Eket when, the night before, Babangida diabolically disbanded the thirteen
political associations and put in their place his two self-crafted National
Republican Convention (NRC) and, Social Democratic Party (SDP). Even so, the
East was coming of age.
It may be in order at this stage to relate an event during a forum
meeting at Dr. Okigbo's, that once more illustrates the so-called "monkey
wrench" hypothesis, to wit, that when a suffering people are about to
consummate a patriotic self-redeeming move,external forces will use state
coercive instruments to stultify the move.
At last the big delegation of the Egbe Ilosiwaju Yoruba was to be
received at Dr. Okigbo's. Unlike their previous visit when they were met at
Dr. Ibiam's, the heavyweight delegation was accommodated at the Zodiac
Hotels. This time around, Chief R.B K. Okafor was very prominent in both
delegations. His involvement with the Zikist Movement, the National Council
for Nigeria and Cameroun (N.C.N.C) and the Yoruba West spanned over four
decades. Both the West and the East knew him well and accepted his middleman
role, banking on his strengths and discounting his weaknesses.
By 9 a.m. that eventful morning, Dr. Okigbo's compound was already
filled to capacity with guests and hosts alike. New, not so new, expensive
and not so expensive cars covered every parking space. Dr. Okigbo and Chief
Matthew Mbu were to pilot the day's affairs but Mbu was not there at the
start of deliberations, although word had gone round he had arrived Enugu
the night before. The meeting took off with Gen. Olutoye, J. S. Olawoyin and
Chief Kola Balogun leading other western notables.
The issue of enmity between the East and the West and its translation to
every facet of national life had been hammered home in earlier
contacts. After the introduction of people present, the meeting began with a
discussion on other aspects of East-West cooperation. The telephone rang. It
was Dr. Okdigbo's wife. She sounded urgent and frantic; she wanted to speak
to none but Dr. Okigbo, who took the call with practised brevity. He had
hardly resumed his speech when the phone rang again. It was Chief Mbu, who
also wanted to speak only to Dr. Okdigbo. Taking the handset, Dr. Okigbo
said brusquely "We have started. Where are you? Why don't you come here
quickly and take your seat..." The next few seconds were
dramatic. Dr. Okigbo dropped the receiver, lost colour and stammered to the
house, "Gentlemen, that's Chief Mbu. He informed me that State Security
Service (SSS) people are set with jeeps and lorries to storm this place... I
am afraid we have to leave ..." He did exactly what he said. Chief
R. B. K. Okafor and Chief Kola Balogun at first tried to reason out the
scenario but soon joined the pandemonium that marked the exit and flight
from the meeting venue. With incredible and lightening speed our guests left
Dr. Okigbo's compound, checked out of their hotel rooms and had crossed the
Niger bridge.
Soon the room was empty and as I tried to piece together what could
happen next, suddenly one man ran back into the room. It was Prof. Ben
Obumselu, Dr. Okigbo's confidant, who was taking the minutes of that day. He
was looking for his minutes papers which we located at the rest
room. Obviously he had dashed into that sanctuary but changed his mind and
fled without those vital "incriminating evidence." Yet many questions
remained unanswered. Who was afraid of East-West entente? Who let loose
these security agents on a friendly gathering? Was intimidation their best
way of making amends...?
The advent of Gen. Babangida's political puppetry did slow down to some
degree the political momentum in the East. For one thing, it did provide
ample room for political drifters, fence-sitters and gamblers to identify
themselves. But as some of them had privileged information on the unfolding
new inclinations in the East, their activities tended to put some vital
political machinery in reverse gear. At times, valuable time was lost trying
to contain their nuisance value. For how else could one explain, for
example, the mischief of Chief Arthur Nzeribe in forming what he called
CARIA (an acronym for Cross River, Anambra, Rivers, Imo and Akwa Ibom
States) when in fact the all-embracing Oriental Club had reconciled and
reunited the people of the East. Mercifully, Chief Nzeribe's schemes and
irritations eventually came to grief. There was also the cleavage between
Chief Sam Mbakwe and Senator Emeka Echeruo which threatened to bring down
the roof of the Oriental Club. Although this particular misunderstanding
appeared to border on personality clash, its roots seemed to reach deep down
into the First Republic. Nevertheless, Dr. Pius Okigbo and other eminent
personalities did their best to put out the fire. There were indeed other
flash points in the East at the time that claimed inordinate amounts of
valuable time and tended to push the main issues to the back burner.
Such was the state of affairs in October 1990 as the leaders of the East
trooped to Lagos for Commodore Ukiwe's 50th birthday. After the glamour and
revelry that attended the big party, the lgbo leadership converged at
Ukiwe's Lagos residence the following day to take stock. The brief meeting
rose with the then famous one-liner - first things first. That meant that
until the Igbos got justice through more states, all talks of transition was
hot air. The leaders then resolved to sit again in two weeks' time at the
Owerri residence of Chief Emma Adiele. Chief Adiele's Owerri residence had
been the venue of several crucial meetings and the chief an enthusiastic
host. Even when he was out of the country, he still provided all the hosting
facilities needed for a successful outing. That meeting was more or less a
business meeting. Although it was Monday, members turned up promptly to
deliberate on several issues among which were:
(a)The structural defects of a lopsided federation which had handicapped
Igbos; and the need for Igbos to go for real political power;
(b)The need for a conflict resolution forum to avoid the ugly situation
in the Second Republic that fouled up relations between lgbos in rival
political camps, e.g. NPP's Nwobodo NPN's Ekwueme;
(c)Discuss and note information from a highly reliable source that the
North (or part of it) had a permanent plan to dominate and even Islamize the
country;
(d)The need to keep Dr. Azikiwe, Dr. Ibiam, and Chief Osadebay and Obi
Chukwumela Nnam abreast of developments to which end Chief Mbakwe and
Prof. Anya, Chief S.G. Ikoku and Dr. Mang Obasi were to liaise with Zik and
Ezeogo Ibiam; Col. Nwawo and Dr. Azinge with Osadebay while Chief
R.B.K. Okafor should keep in touch with the Oba of Ogbaland

Original_Search_Title: Adm Human Rights Diary
Headline: Ghana: Adm Human Rights Diary
Body_Text:
Far From Home: Children in the Big City
Whether it is street kids or trafficked kids, we hear a lot about the
welfare of children.
Although intentions might be good, often these efforts to help children
underestimate their abilities and resourcefulness.
When taken out of a traditional family context, they build new
ones. Nobody says this is the best way for a kid to grow up. It isn't. But
not all Ghana's children are living in the best possible world.
More than 40 percent of Ghana's population is under the age of 15 years
of age.
Children are scrambling in Ghana for a piece of a not very big pie.
Here are two stories, about a boy and a girl, trying to do the best they
can in a difficult situation. No moral judgments, just tough kids in a tough
situation, acting with dignity.
Meele is a girl who likes to talk to her friends. When I first meet her
she is eating mashed beans out of a shopping bag, but that does not stop her
from taking part in the conversation at hand.
Like thousands of other Ghanaians do every year, Meele has migrated from
rural area to the big city in search of work.
As she waits in the Agbobloshie Market, on the outskirts of Accra, there
isn't much to be found on this particular day. She works as a porter,
carrying on her head everything from garbage cans to peanuts for as little
as 500 cedis.
Meele is lucky today. A man asked her to carry his gerry-can full of
gasoline some 2 kilometers through the market, a trip good for the
relatively large sum of 3,000 cedis. After this trip, Meele goes back to
waiting.
She rejoins her friends, a group of four other girls sitting on the pans
they use to hold their loads.
From dawn until dusk, they sit at this particular T-junction in the
market. A broken table lies sideways nearby, offering a barrier of sorts
against the elements - the sewage ditch, the cars, the hawkers.
Amidst the monotony, Meele and her friends are loyal and considerate to
each other. When one of Meele's friends needs help placing a crate of
tomatoes on her head, the rest of the girls are there almost instantly to
help.
These girls seem to be Meele's entire support network in Accra. She has
no siblings, and her parents live some 300km away in Tamale. She has not
spoken to them since she left Tamale four months ago.
The other girls are also from Tamale. Some of them have been in Accra
for one year. Meele is the newest to arrive in Accra of the bunch, and one
of the youngest, at 15 years old.
As an expression of the strong ties of friendship the girls maintain,
they defer most solicitations to Meele in order for her to get the most
experience and make the most money.
On this particular day, that money amounts to 5,000 cedis. Except for
the handful of times Meele goes out on a job, the girls spend the day
sitting on their pans.
They say they enjoy sitting out in the sun. They smile and joke with
each other, comment on the passers-by, and tell stories. They sit in an
enclosed circle, often seemingly oblivious to the commotion that engulfs
them.
Tamale may be far away geographically, but these girls seem to be doing
a good job of recreating it in Accra.
Meele and her friends may not have the benefits that come from having
lots of money, but they certainly enjoy the benefits that come from being
mature and resourceful.
At around 3pm, they leave their spot to pray. The mosque is conveniently
located across the street and down an alley. After washing their feet, they
enter a nearby mosque for the afternoon service.
The mosque is connected to a goat farm - the prayers and livestock make
for an interesting mix of sounds.
They do not hang around the mosque afterwards to chat - they leave to
retake their positions on the street almost immediately. Although they may
not live the conventional family life, they still live conventional routines
that may not be all that different from their routines in Tamale.
If Meele misses her parents or hometown, she does not show it. She
always appears independent, street-smart, and assertive - an impression that
was confirmed by a talk with a woman selling yams behind the girl's
circle. According to her, foreigners passing through the market see the
girls and often offer their "help" and "assistance" to them. The girls
always refuse, the kiosk owner says.
In an ideal world, no child would have to leave a life in rural area
with parents for a strange city. But in Ghana, such a life in a strange city
is often better than the rural life that preceded it. Many children like
Meele are capable and resourceful.
They are not the victims that they are sometimes treated as. They are
proactive kids making the best of a situation.
If you asked Meele, she probably wouldn't say she thinks her life is
perfect. But she probably wouldn't cry about it, either.
Tawia, a 13 year old boy from Bolgatanga, works filling tin cans with
kerosene in the Agbobloshie Market. He has a happy demeanor as he makes his
way down the market street, going to the kiosks of his customers with his
bright smile and his gerry-can.
Tawia's work process actually involves two trips - one time through to
fill his customer's tin cans full, and then another round later in the
evening to collect the money. As Tawia explains, he needs to give his
customers time to earn the 1,000 cedis he charges.
Along his way filling up the cans he meets Kwadjo, a boy around his age
with his same job. They exchange a couple of friendly greetings before
continuing on their respective routes. Tawia explains that they each have
their own dedicated customers, so there is no competition between them.
After he is done he goes back home for the night. He lives a few paces
off from the main market road with a group of girls he introduces as his
sisters. They sleep together in a room the size of some bathrooms.
There seems to be no space for possessions of any kind. Their clothes
seem to suspend themselves on the wall. Tawia's room-mates sell tea and rice
in the market.
Since his mother is dead, he says, the four girls Tawia lives with
function as a sort of collective mother.
They seem happy and supportive of him, asking him how his work was. One
of them, Janet, tells me Tawia enjoys his work, since he is happy to
contribute what he can to their funds. Judging from Tawia's genial attitude
towards his customers and room-mates, one cannot help but agree.
For all the appreciation shown for Tawia's work, it was surprising to
find out he takes home 5,000 cedis per day. Although the amount may be
small, the fact that it is acknowledged by those who support him perhaps
teaches Tawia some of the valuable discipline that goes along with doing a
job well.
This discipline, in turn, helps Tawia do his job better. Some people in
the market would shout things to him, both flattering and insulting, when
they saw a reporter accompanying him. Tawia would give them a slight smile,
but not the dignity of a response.
Perhaps this summer job has given him another beneficial quality for
someone becoming an adult: thick skin.
Unlike many of the other children who work in the market, Tawia's job is
temporary. He came to Accra one year ago to live with his sister and to
attend school. Now that school is out for the summer, he is helping to
defray the cost of his education that his sister paid.
Often people complain that children should not work. To be sure,
obviously certain kinds of labor are inappropriate for children.
Still, other types may, in fact, be quite suitable and practical for
children to perform.
In this case, Tawia is performing the task happily, with no exceptional
risk to his health, and for the good purpose of continuing his
education.
It appears to be money well spent. Tawia is nice, articulate,
hard-working, and judging by how he is treated by his customers, very
well-liked. One customer was at pains to endear himself to me, as if I were
friendly and nice by my mere association with Tawia.
Tawia seems ambivalent towards his life before coming to Accra. The only
time he seemed sad was when his mother was mentioned.
Like many children in Ghana, he does not live a charmed life. But he
knows how to look after himself and he knows he is loved.
For Tawia, at least, that seems to be enough.

Original_Search_Title: Caging Anti-Democracy Friends
Headline: Nigeria: Caging Anti-Democracy Friends
Body_Text:
Benin-City — Once again, Nigeria_s countless efforts at cultivating a stable, civil democratic rule is heading for the crucibles. The indicators for this avoidable looming abortion of democracy are familiar. In the past, these indicators conspired to rub this emerging great nation of good governance, democratic rule, social justice and enforcement of citizens_ fundamental human rights. Indeed, the force of poverty, marginalization, corruption, arrogance of power, lawlessness, hunger, violence, thuggery and assassination are again roving the political airwaves, and leaving on the tracks ominous signals, which if not addressed in the true spirit of social justice and the rule of law are capable of jeopardising the sustainability of democracy in the country.
It is against this background that every stakeholder in the polity has a self-imposed responsibility to call the Ngigegate scandal by its very name; civilian coup which is a treasonable felony. Such grievous crime against the on going democratisation process should not be swept under the carpet, without recourse to the rule of law and the spirit and letters of the constitution and ensure the safeguard of the rule of law and not the rule of man or rule of some pristine crude and uncultured godfathers, it behoves on the State to immediately arrest and prosecute all the suspects in the failed coup attempt to force Dr. Christopher Ngige out his office as the Executive Governor of Anambra State.
The urgent need to prosecute all those involved directly in the civilian coup attempt is not necessarily for the purpose of identifying the victors and the victims in the _Ngigegate scandal_ but for the sole purpose of establishing the fact that Nigeria as in the rest parts of the world where decent humanity lives, it is the rule of law that prevails and not the rule of man. It is for this objective re-establishing the fact that rule of law governs humanity that the Nigerian State together with the rest of society should insist on enforcing the law and arraigning all suspects in that saga before a competent law court as specified by the constitution. To do otherwise is to encourage subterfuge and lay the groundwork for the subversion of the fourth republic, and by extension the democratisation process.
Thus, the recent declaration by Chief Audu Ogbeh, the National Chairman of the ruling Peoples' Democratic Party that the dramatis personae allegedly linked to the failed coup in Awka, who the party had earlier expelled from its fold can be pardoned and re-absorbed into the party as soon as they apologise for their alleged crime against the Nigerian State is rather begging the question, and indeed very unfortunate. The PDP and its leadership stand to garner flourishing credibility if the rule of law prevails on this matter, and surely the party and its leadership will be helping to build a virile, credible and formidable democratic Nigeria. It is not a matter of some persons, who have money, playing godfatherly roles in the business of politics. There is nothing absolutely wrong in godfatherism in any secular venture. Even in the advanced democracies of the world, i.e. the United States, Britain, Canada, Germany, France etc, political gladiators do not fly in from the sky. They are products of the societal melee that create those who have mega bugs, who sufficiently generate interest in politics, which is the art and science of the sharing and allocation of societal collective resources. And so, for the common good of the godfather, the godson or goddaughter and the entire society, godfatherism blossom to recruit and create new political (ruling) elites, who then occupy positions of authorities, when they become holders of the knives and the yams. So, really, the issue is not godfatherism as such. The issue in the Anambra political debacle is that desperadoes on both sides of the divide are involved in a ruffor-ruffor fight for the soul of Anambra state. These desperadoes, in the process of sorting themselves out infringe on the constitution and laws of the body-polity. This is the crux of the matter. The law should take it course. And let heavens fall.
