It would be recalled that Chronicle in its 23rd June edition, published a story on its back page headlined "Imminent clash over boundaries".
The paper quoted the Nkoranzahene as saying that the Mos are taking his land and also described the Mohene as a self-styled chief in the Mo traditional area.
The chiefs and their subjects regretted that some seven disgruntled persons, spearheaded by the district chief executive for Kintampo, Mr. James Kwabena Bomfeh, are pushing the family head of the omanhene to stand against him.
The Mos called on the government to caution the DCE, himself a Mo, in his involvement in the Mo chieftaincy issues.
They warned that if Mr. Bomfeh is interested in the Mo chieftaincy, he should relinquish his position as district chief executive.
The MTC recalled that during the honourable regional minister's visit to the Kintampo District, the DCE made the Mohene and his sub-chiefs organize a durbar in the honour of the minister, but the minister failed to come to them.
The minister and his entourage, according to the traditional council members came to New Longoro, the seat of the Mohene, but never stepped at the durbar grounds on the advice of Mr. Bomfeh.
The MTC in a unanimous decision called on the government and the Brong Ahafo Regional House of Chiefs to bring the Nkoranzahene to order, since a statement of such nature should come from some one like him.
They recalled that if the Nkoranzahene knows that the Mohene is a self-styled chief why was he invited to attend the Nkoranzahene's yam festival last year at Nkoranza.
On the demarcation of boundaries between the Mos and the Nkoranza traditional area, the MTC has agreed that the government should look into it quickly to avoid any future clash.
The Mos said where the Kintampo market is located belongs to them, but the Nkoranzahene's representative at Kintampo, being the Kyeremakomahene has been taking royalties from the traders at the market, which they said must be stopped with immediate effect.
The warned that if it continues they may run out of patience it is important that the government take a critical look at it. The MTC also deliberated on issues affecting their culture and Deg Pedia festival, which is slated for October 28 this year.
According to the omanhene, the Mos have a lot of cultural activities that need to be showcased to the world. Nana Elaje said the Pedia festival, which means yam festival, would attract tourists to the area, in order to create revenue for the district.
To get the Mo traditional area to develop; the MTC has appealed to all Mos far and near to come together to build a formidable Mo state.

Original_Search_Title: Nigerian and French Production Companies Team to Produce Film on Ken Saro-Wiwa's Lemona Tale
Headline: Nigerian and French Production Companies Team to Produce Film on Ken Saro-Wiwa's Lemona Tale
Body_Text:
Lagos — The African cinema industry recorded a major landmark on Monday with the
signing of a co-production agreement between Nigeria-based Capitol
International Productions Limited and France-based Akangbe Productions to
produce a screen adaptation of Ken Saro-Wiwa's last novel, Lemona's Tale in
a 90 minute feature-length film. Tunde Okoli who was at the occasion reports
Two pan-African film production companies (Albert Egbe's Capitol
International Productions Limited, based in Nigeria and N'Diagne
Adechoubou's Akangbe Productions, based in France) are coming together to
co-produce what promises to be a milestone in African cinema. The
co-production pact, is aimed at bringing Albert Egbe's screen adaptation of
the slain human rights and environmental activist, Ken Saro Wiwa's last
novel, Lemona's Tale to the big screen with the production of Lemona, a 90
feature film.
Using Wiwa's main character's name in the novel, Lemona as its titled,
Lemona is an international full-length feature film project to be shot on
35mm celluloid. It is based on the last novel the internationally renowned
author and civil and environmental rights activist wrote while in prison and
published after his death in Europe.
The screen play is written by Ken Saro Wiwa's personal friend and
professional colleague - Albert Egbe popularly known as "MR. B" of the late
1970s down early 1990s TV Comedy series, Basi and Company, written, directed
and produced by Ken Saro Wiwa.
A gripping story of life trials, Lemona tells the intriguing story of
the journey of a strong-willed woman, Lemona through the turbulent tides of
life. Born and raised in poverty with very little education, Lemona is
determined to get out of the poverty spiral, make something of herself and
get the good things of life. In pursuit of her destiny, Lemona meets several
encounters, some good, some bad some disastrous. Lemona, the protagonist, is
variously described "beauty queen", "Africa's Mona Lisa", "the enigma",
"queen of the night", the "serendipitous one".
The story is also factual about the background of the Nigerian society
at that time and the cross - over period into post Independence era. I
recall some of the factual events of the time, for example, the
constitutional conference held in London between Nigeria politicians and the
British Government as a prelude to the granting of Independence.
October 1, 1980 is the 20th anniversary of Nigeria's Independence from
British colonial rule. Following a presidential pardon, Lemona, beautiful,
elegant, 48 years old, but looking several years younger, steps out of the
Port Harcourt prison gate into a black limousine. Freedom at last after 23
years in jail for killing her white lover, John.
"Lemona" in Dukana language South Eastern Nigeria, means "lucky
encounter", or "happy meeting". Once out of prison Lemona's sole purpose in
life after 23 years of "silence" in the seclusion of the prison, is to find
her daughter, delivered in prison some 29 years earlier. With the help of
the since retired prison controller, Fidelis Chukwu, Lemona embarks on the
mission to find her daughter.
Unknown to Lemona, Fidelis Chukwu who has a long - lasting grudge
against the trial judge, sets Lemona up, in a meeting with the trial judge,
now a supreme court judge, Kole Bamidele. But his wile, Elsie, childless,
who is privy to the meeting, comes in and angrily confronts her husband
about Lemona and their illegitimate child. In a wild rage she attacked her
sick husband and strikes him in the chest with a club-like statuette, one of
the ornaments in the living room. He falls and hits his head on the
staircase and dies in a pool of blood. Elsie turns her fury on Lemona. They
both land on the floor and Lemona manages to wriggle out to reach the fallen
statuette and strikes Elsie several times with it. Elsie dies.
Lemona is condemned to death by hanging. On her last day on earth she is
visited by Olu Bamidele, 22 years old, whose parent Lemona killed. She is
the first visitor Lemona had in years.
To Ola, Lemona now tells her story against a backdrop of what is
happening in Nigeria, which spans the colonial and post independence
years. Her early life in Dukana as a poor but happy 13 year old child of a
single mother, the yam festivals, her time at school an out of it, her stay
with the Mana's as a nanny or house girl, her return to Dukana after
two-and-a-half years to her dying mother, her encounters on return to Port
Harcourt, with Mama Bomboy, then Maybel, a street-wise "business
woman". Donatus Adoga, general manager of United African Company, whose
mistress Lemona becomes, Edoo kabari, a young graduate in English, who
looses his life for the love of Lemona, and finally, the English engineer,
John Smith, with whom Lemona falls in love for the very first time.
Their affair lasts for nearly three years. Nigeria's independence is
round the corner. Expatriates are being encouraged to take an early
retirement with mouth-watering compensation. John Smith goes on holidays to
England.
Lemona dreams of going to UK with John as his wife. But John returns
after one-month holiday to announce that he has married Charlotte in the UK
and they are planning for her to join him in Nigeria.
"Hell hath no fury like a woman scorned". In her fury Lemona attacks
John with the carving knife at dinner. By sheer accident, the knife
accidentally lands on John's chest. John collapses and subsequently
dies.
In shock Lemona offers no defence, despite efforts by her lawyer. The
judge, Kole Bamidele, who is taken by her beauty, reluctantly commits her to
prison custody, pending, trial. He then hatches a plot with the prison
controller to secretly get Lemona pregnant by him and so escape the
hangman's noose. Lemona is not a willing participant. The result of the
strange encounters in the night is Ola Bamidele.
Before reluctantly releasing her child for "proper up-bringing" several
years ago, Lemona had marked the child for future recognition, by biting off
the tip of the child's little finger. By this revelation, mother and child
recognize each other.
After a heart-rending re-union, Ola leaves her mother. Lemona is hanged
at 6 am the following morning.
Egbe said his adaptation of Wiwa's Lemona's Tale in Lemona stems from
his fascination with the author's tales and thematic focus. "Apart from the
personal relationship, which I had with Ken Saro - Wiwa, I come from another
part of the Niger Delta area of Nigeria, with similar cultural and
environmental background as Wiwa's Ogoni. So his stories touch my
heart.
"I see my adaptation of his work "Lemona" into a feature film as my
contribution to keeping alive the memory of a man widely regarded as a hero
by his people in fighting for justice and human rights."
For him, the story itself is not only fascinating, but also reminds him
of childhood days, the storytelling under the moonlight, the festivals and
life in the village during the colonial era of the pre 1960 years.
For N'Diagne Adechoubou's of France-based Akangbe Productions, the
choice of Lemona is important both from the point of view of fiction and
cinematography for Lemona is a story about diabolical beauty. Is beauty a
source of misfortune? The film project is already an attempt to answer this
question.
He explained that the appeal of Lemona as a film subject is in the
confrontation of emotions and a disturbing riddle: "Is Lemona Ken Saro-Wiwa?
From start, we agreed that the choice of Albert Egbe as scriptwriter was a
good choice: he is an experienced Nigerian filmmaker and very conversant
with Ken Saro-Wiwa's imaginary world. After reading his script, we are more
than ever eager to be involved in the production of this film. The artistic
and creative team working with Balufu Bakupa Kanyinda and Albert Egbe is a
promise of quality."
He added that the personality of the author/director, Balufu
Bakupa-Kanyinda, is also of interest as far as the film project is concerned
for his approach of film narration is convincing and does not leave the
audience insensible He has both an artistic and technical capacity that
enables him to direct as well as serve the film while inspiring his team
with a collective and beneficial impulse.
The Beninois is optimistic that the project will come out with a world
class film. "Albert Egbe is entrusted with the casting. As a producer and
artistic director, he has good knowledge of sets, sceneries and talented
actors in Nigeria. We are convinced that the production of Lemona will be
successful and that the final result will be an excellent film."
The director of the film, Balufu Bakupa-Kanyinda, recalled how he
ignited the ember that is setting the creative fire of Lemona after a chance
meeting with a Nigerian journalist in Europe. He found in Wiwa's revolution,
a strong link to the ideals and political activities of the slain Burkinabe
and African revolutionary leader, Thomas Sankara. The Congolese who
confessed that he had never heard of Ken Saro-Wiwa before fall 1991 said he
immediately went after Wiwa's books soon after the encounter. "After the
interview, I read Ken Saro Wiwa's Sozaboy. It's a great novel written in
English, but not British English."
Two years later, through some Nigerian friends, he got other books by
Ken Saro-Wiwa including Lemona's Tale. He fell in love with the novel right
from page one. "The story is a man's prophetic projection of his own
destiny. Ken Saro-Wiwa tells the story of Lemona's fateful life, which ended
with her condemnation to a death sentence by hanging. I thought of Pouchkin
who died in a duel with swords like the hero of his novel, Eugene
Oneguine. Between Lemona and Ken Saro-Wiwa stands Africa, an Africa that is
secreting fatal tragedies."
For him, Lemona's Tale is a profound story that tells Africa, the Africa
of Nigerians. It is a metaphor of human destiny given through a woman's
particular destiny. "While reading Lemona's Tale I could see vividly bright
film scenes. It was like a bitter scream bringing to cinematography a touch
of its intriguing beauty. In the name Lemona" there is "Mona Lisa", as well
as bitterness. There is also the silt (limon) that fertilizes poor
soils. Ken Saro-Wiwa is Lemona. They were both condemned to death by hanging
in the same Port-Harcourt jail. They were the same age. Lemona is a riddle
that gives scope to a good film plot," he enthused.
This love for the tale propelled him further into kick-starting the
project. In 2000, at a television workshop in Bamako, Mali, after the
screening of the movie: Twins of the Rain Forest, he was moved to approach
the film's scriptwriter, Albert Egbe who was present at the screening. "I
told him about Lemona's Tale. He had never heard of it because the book had
not been distributed in Nigeria yet... I suggested that he should buy the
film rights and write the script."
He added that in the novel there is a sort of twilight beauty which is
worth narrating, a moving story that can provide the support for a
light-hearted, poetical film. "Albert and I talked about it in Paris,
Libreville, Cape Town, Lagos... Lemona is the link between us. When driving
through Lagos, I was often distracted enough to look for Lemona among female
faces. And I met her. She is a beautiful and fantastic actress."
An eager Egbe did not waste time in writing the script. At the close of
the Pan-African Film Festival (FESPACO) February this year, in Ouagadougou,
Burkina Faso, Egbe handed over the Lemona script to Balufu. "It was an
unforgettable occasion," he said.
He said the production companies are making the film now, because
beautiful Lemona's Tale is the story of a thwarted destiny. "It's a story
for cinema. The film I intend to make will bear the stamp of this destroyed
beauty. This story of beauty crushed by the dramatic happenings of life is
universal. Lemona's story ends up with her hanging. However there is also an
auspiciously hopeful, happy ending: her meeting with her daughter. It's a
film that tells a story about memory and its transmission. From mother to
daughter. It's a story about the viciousness of human life," he said.
Earlier on, Egbe explained that the entire film will be shot on
locations in Nigeria. However, it is not going to be an entire Nigerian cast
affair. "We are trying to do an African international film. The film will be
shot between March and April on locations in Lagos and Port Harcourt. Though
we are using mainly Nigerian actors, we are going to involve actors from
other African countries." He named South-Africa-based Nigerian actor and
filmmaker Akin Omotosho, son of Nigerian writer and scholar, Professor Kole
Omotosho, London-based Nigerian actress, Rosemary Dacosta who has been
pinned to play the lead role, Lemona. Lagos-based Richard Mofe-Damijo and
Liz Benson as some of actors and actresses who will be acting in the
film.
The film's director, Balufu is optimistic that there are very talented
actors in Nigeria who are up to the demands of the dramatization of this
tragedy. "I'll have to sound true in this tragedy that appeals to the
masses. It's a film that opens onto the world. It requires a strong and
creative narrative structure, and a dynamic rhythm to sustain it."
For Egbe, the planned trans-Africa co-production of Lemona is expected
to return Nigeria to the heart African cinema. "We want to be in the
forefront of the revival of celluloid in Nigeria. With this project, we are
working at the revival and the reawakening of the cinema culture in the
country, and we are approaching this from the pan-African perspective," he
said.
What is more, Africans from different regions and countries are taking
active part in the execution of the Lemona film project. As Balufu from
Congo, (Central Africa) is set to Direct the filming of Lemona, Abraham
Haile Biru from Ethiopia (East Africa) will be on photography as the
project's Director of Photography. While Egbe's Capitol International
Productions will arrange the shooting of the film in Nigeria and also
produce and distribute the film in Nigeria and Anglophone West-Africa,
France-based N'Diagne Adechoubou's Akangbe Productions will overlook
post-production in Paris or South Africa and will be responsible for the
distribution of the film in Europe and Francophone West-Africa. Canada-based
Ken Saro-Wiwa Jnr. son of late Saro-Wiwa is also co-producing and will be
responsible for the distribution of Lemona in Canada, US and probably, the
Americas as the film is expected to be dubbed and subtitled in French and
Portuguese.
Other co-producers include UK-based Ghanaian, John Akonphrah who is the
head of the British Film Institute and Joel Phiri, a South African-based
Zimbabwean who in conjunction with Akin Omotosho will be responsible for the
distribution network in Southern Africa region.
Born Kenule Benson Tsaro-Wiwa in Bori, Rivers State, to Jim Beesom Wiwa,
a businessman and community chief, and a farmer, Saro-Wiwa was educated in
Government College, Umuahia, where also Chinua Achebe and Elechi Amadi
studied.
His first novel, Sozaboy, was published in 1985. The antiwar novel was
written in 'pidgin' English ("rotten English") - an English-based dialect
spoken by many Nigerians. Its title meant "soldier boy". The story was
partly based on Saro-Wiwa's own experiences and was about a young man who
serves as a soldier during the Biafran War. Saro-Wiwa dedicated Adaku and
Other Stories (1989) to his sisters and depicted in its stories "the
condition of women". Lemona's Tale (1996), published posthumously, examined
also the role of women in changing society.
During the civil war he chose the Nigerian side and was the
administrator for the oil depot at Bonny Island. He then served in the
Rivers State Cabinet as a regional commissioner for education. In 1973 he we
was dismissed due his opinions about autonomy for Ogoni people. Through his
writings Saro-Wiwa became increasingly involved in activities, which brought
national and international attention to the campaign of the Ogoni
people.
In 1990 Saro-Wiwa founded the Movement for the Survival of the Ogoni
People (MOSOP). A more radical youth, also founded by Saro-Wiwa, was
reputedly engaged in sabotage against Shell forcing the company to cease
operations in Ogoniland in 1993. The Nigerian government decided to break
MOSOP and Saro-Wiwa was arrested and a number of his supporters and
relatives were slain at Giokoo.
A multi-faceted individual, Saro-Wiwa is an accomplished television
producer, writer of satirical novels, children's tales, and plays. He was
set up and imprisoned along with other eight Ogonis in a mock murder trial
in 1994 on the orders of the late maximum ruler Sani Abacha. Despite wide
international protests, Saro-Wiwa was hanged with other eight Ogoni rights
activists in Port Harcourt, on November 10, 1995.
A strong believer in the power of the 'pen'. In a letter he wrote while
in prison and published in May 1955 in the Mail & Guardian, Saro-Wiwa
stated: "Ultimately the fault lies at the door of the British government. It
is the British government which supplies arms and credit to the military
dictators of Nigeria, knowing full well that all such arms will only be used
against innocent, unarmed citizens." In another letter he concluded that:
"The most important thing for me is that I've used my talents as a writer to
enable the Ogoni people to confront their tormentors. I was not able to do
it as a politician or a businessman. My writing did it. And it sure makes me
feel good! I'm mentally prepared for the worst, but hopeful for the best. I
think I have the moral victory."
What is more, his son, Ken Wiwa jnr. depicted in his book In the Shadow
of a Saint (2001) his relationship to his father. "He usually opened the
proceeding, droning about his politics, trying to drill his values into me,
sprinkling the lecture with his favorite phrases: 'Hard work doesn't kill';
'To whom much is given, much is expected'; 'In Nigeria, the only wrongdoers
are those who do no wrong'; 'To live a day in Nigeria is to die many times.'
He usually ended with the clincher that the ball was in my court, or with
the reminder that he had given me the best opportunities in life."
With what Egbe described as "a fairly substantial fund" which the
producers hope to source from funding agencies around the world, the film's
producer, Albert Egbe is optimistic that the film will be ready early enough
to be part of the 10th anniversary of the gruesome murder of Kenule
Saro-Wiwa on November 5, 2005.
Egbe is enthusiastically positive that the project will be a resounding
success. Corro-borating his co-producers' assertion, he averred that Lemona
"promises to be a great African movie."

Original_Search_Title: Nigerians Pay More for Foodstuff
Headline: Nigerians Pay More for Foodstuff
Body_Text:
The recent hike in the price of petroleum products from N26 to N34 by
the Federal Government has begun to spill over to the prices of food stuffs
in the country, even as 80 per cent of the populace are living below the
poverty line, i.e., on less than US$1 per day.
Vanguard findings showed that the prices of different brands of 50 kg
bags of rice ranged between N4, 200 - N4, 600 while Tin sales of N1, 100 -
N1, 200.
On a similar note, 100 kg bag of brown beans sells for N7, 500 while the
same size of white sells for N6, 000. Tin of both brown and white sells for
N950 and N800 respectively.
The bigger size of the Abuja species of yams which is the most common in
the market sells for N400, while the medium and small sizes sells for N300
and N200 respectively.
A 100kg bags of gari sells for N4, 500, while the Tin sells for N550. A
module (olodo) of eguisi (melon) is N1, 000 while the hand peel-made sells
for N1, 200. Also, a module of Ogbono, is sold for N2, 000, while a derica
cup is sold for N550.
Specifically, Vanguard surveyed traders at various markets on Lagos
Mainland. The foodstuff dealers say they are still trying to come to terms
with the additional hardship the petroleum product prices have spilled on
food items. They blame the rise in cost of food stuff on transportation
which has more than doubled following the increase in pump price.
A majority of the traders who spoke to Vanguard warned that Nigerians,
especially, the masses, should be prepared to adapt to the situation of
things because " nobody is sure of what the future economy has in the store
with the high prices of goods and services."
According to them, one fact we cannot run away from is that the cost of
living has gone up for the masses.
A spokesman for the dealers, Mr. Genesis Okorie said that after the
strike action the market prices of commodities have not been
stable. According to him, dealers are not yet sure of the next rate of
increases on purchases.
The reason for the fluctuation he said is that majority of the dealers
additions to the old price depend on the rate at which goods were purchased,
and the cost of transportation.
He complained that government is not helping matters for the survival of
the Nigerian masses. He added that for them in the business, things have
been low key in the past months of the political campaign, coupled with
government coming with an increment in petroleum products, then the
strike.
Mr. Barnabas Odife, at Iddo market said that the action of the
government in increasing petroleum products prices is a sure signal of the
masses to depend less on the government for their survival.
According to him, if the government has the interest of the masses at
heart, it would have considered the negative implications on the poor masses
before effecting any increase in less than a month of assuming office. The
commodity dealers appealed to the federal government to help the masses by
ensuring that its policies are fine-tuned to lessen their negative
impact.

Original_Search_Title: Spotlight: Lake Victoria Swamp Crops Poisonous
Headline: Uganda: Spotlight: Lake Victoria Swamp Crops Poisonous
Body_Text:
Kampala — RESIDENTS of Kampala, Entebbe and Jinja will be courting death if they
continue eating yams and vegetables cultivated in city swamps since they
contain dangerous chemicals, a report has warned.
The report released by Karin Ljung, a Swedish researcher, said high
concentrations of chromium, cadium, lead and zinc are absorbed into the
crops. The concentrations are higher than those tolerated under the
guidelines of the World Health Organisation.
"This is alarming. These metals are among the most hazardous to humans
and the environment," said the report.
Bernard Kiremire, an associate professor in the department of chemistry
at Makerere University, said the report confirms their study done five years
ago. He said they exposed pollution in Nakivubo swamp and "the yams that
have disastrous consequences."
He said concentrations of heavy metals exceeding certain levels have
short and long term effects on people.
He pointed out that cancer, ulcers, kidney and liver failure and bone
damage are some of the effects of the toxic chemicals when they are ingested
by human beings. Others include abdnominal pain, diarrhoea, convulsions and
renal failure.
Kiremkre referred to the Nakivubo swamp as a natural filter that cleans
impurities in wastewater from Kampala before releasing it into Murchison
Bay, the mouth of Lake Victoria.
However, cultivators have destroyed some of the vast papyrus vegetation
with a network of roots that hold the poisonous metals in the swamp,
Kiremire said.
But Ljung's study revealed that metal concentrations coming from Kampala
were reduced by 99% after passing through the swamp. Unfortunately, his
investigations revealed that yam tubers retain the chemicals and that they
are unfit for human consumption.
Kiremire said the source of pollution was effluent (wastewater) from
industries, used oil and fuel filters from garages, petrol stations and the
uncollected garbage in the city.
The report said tests done on water and plant samples from different
areas of the channel and the swamp revealed that the concentration of
poisonous metals was highest in Nakivubo swamp and Lugogo, where the swamp
is used for farming.
Over 10,000 yam growers have pitched camp in Nakivubo swamp.
In Jinja, plants at Kirinya and Walukuba contained high levels of
metals, which exceeded limits accepted for human consumption.
The report pegged results in Jinja, where the soil has been
contaminated, to the former copper smelter, which used to refine copper ore
from Kilembe mines.
Kiremire said low-income earners cultivate yams, dodo (a vegetable),
finger millet, cowpeas, green pepper and sugarcane on the soils that contain
the heavy metals.
The Entebbe International Airport is believed to be a source of petrol
that contains lead, which spills into the nearby waters, according to the
report.
Kiremire proposed proper treatment methods in the areas where waste is
produced and increasing awareness and public concern.
He said law enforcement should be strengthened because pollution effects
are seen after a long time.
Dr. Aryamanya Mugisha, the National Environment management Authority
boss, said yam growers would soon be evicted.
"Plans to gazette the Nakivubo swamp are in advanced stages and we will
not bargain with the yam growers," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Mole Game Reserve Renders Communities Poor
Headline: Ghana: Mole Game Reserve Renders Communities Poor
Body_Text:
Tamale — Displaced communities from the Mole Game Reserve are living in perpetual
fear and hunger as their livelihood has been jeopardized and they feel
hunted by game wardens.
Hunting of wild animals, farming and gathering used to be their way of
life until the government decided to turn their land into a game reserve in
1958. Since then, suffering, fear and hunger have become the lot of these
people who once lived in communities within the reserve: Kunkua, Gbantariga,
Dushie, Bugubeli, Jang, Belipong and Salakowo.
Today, inhabitants of these villages have become settlers in other
villages outside the reserve - Laribanga, Kanato, Kabampe, Grupe, Seyin,
Murugu and Nabori.
At Laribanga, the chief, Alhaji Ibrahima Awudu Jaa, recounted the bitter
experience he and his people went through, experiences, he said, which are
still prevailing.
"We lost our crops; millet, maize, cassava, yam and even mango
plantations. Now we farm about ten miles away from the reserve. Yet we don't
harvest much because of incessant invasion of our farms by elephants. Those
who are eager to farm have to sleep in their farms to scare away the
elephants, all day."
"We don't have clean water to drink because the elephants have muddied
the dug-outs and ponds from which we drink. We travel to Damongo, 24
kilometres away, to buy water at ¢7,000 per drum."
The chief said because of the menace by the elephants, most of the youth
have abandoned farming to seek greener pastures in cities in the south.
"About 70 young men have left. Those who do well in the cities have set
up one trade or the other. This has encouraged more of the youth to
migrate. The girls also go there as porters. "The youth who stay behind,
resort to poaching but because they are being shot at by wardens, they are
also leaving gradually.
Most families here depend on their emigrant relatives to feed. Our wives
cannot go to the reserve to pick sheanuts and dawa dawa. Even outside the
reserve, the elephants feed on the fruits."
The chief added rather dejectedly: "We are waiting for government to
decide whether we should die for the game reserve to exist."
The people of Kabampe, like Kanato, are hemmed in by the game reserve
and a forest reserve. They cannot farm in both areas. According to the
chief, Iddisah Anumu, "Farming is our major occupation. We cannot farm in
the reserves. So we farm around our households. We have done that for so
long that the soil is no longer fertile. How does government want us to
feed? We are suffering too much. That is why our boys go to the game
reserve."
In November last year, 32-year old Braimah Saaka sneaked into the game
reserve to poach. Unfortunately, he was sighted by six game wardens. As he
turned to run, he was shot in the back and killed.
In spite of their travail, the communities are not organised enough to
advocate for policies that would improve their livelihood.
"You see the issue has been politicised. When you talk about the plight
of the people they brand you as 'opposition'; you want to spoil the image of
the party in power," says a retrenched administrative officer in
Laribanga. "But we have formed a community-based organization to look for
ways to help our people."
At Kabampe, a teacher, Iddisah Saaka, thinks their assembly member
should be the right person to convey their plight to the district
assembly. "But since we voted him into the position, he has not set foot in
this village. He is always at Laribanga. We want our own electoral area,
separate from Laribanga, so that we can effectively present our case at the
assembly."
Indeed, past governments have ignored the displaced Mole communities for
quite long. Consequently, they have little or no hope in any
government. However, the district chief executive for West Gonja,
Mr. Zakaria Yakubu, who sympathises with them, gives them a ray of
hope.
"The assembly is doing all it can to assist them overcome some of the
problems they encounter. Leaving the people alone without assistance from
the government or responsible agencies would be tantamount to asking them to
go into the game area and hunt," he said.
In this connection, the district assembly, together with the Village
Infrastructure Project (VIP) is to construct a dam each at Laribanga, Murugu
and Nabori to supply the people with good drinking water and also for
irrigation purposes in the dry season.
He explained that the provision of the irrigated dams would engage the
people in farming during the dry season, which is usually the period for
hunting.
Also, two boreholes are to be provided the Murugu community, which is to
benefit from a new six-unit classroom block being constructed with
assistance from the Canada Fund.
The DCE added that the assembly would assist the hardworking women who
engage in gari (cassava) processing and other income-generating activities
with soft loans from the Assembly's Common Fund to improve their lot.

Original_Search_Title: Poor Security Hinders Efforts to Help Displaced People in Monrovia
Headline: Liberia: Poor Security Hinders Efforts to Help Displaced People in Monrovia
Body_Text:
Abidjan — Displaced people in the Liberian capital Monrovia continue to be plagued
by frequent incidents of rape, abduction armed robbery and looting, World
Vision said on Wednesday.
The UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA) also
reported continued looting and a general climate of insecurity in the city
of one million people. Like World Vision, it said displaced people living in
camps on the edge of the city and others forced to flee their homes by two
rebel attacks last month were the hardest hit.
World Vision said, aid agencies had stopped distributing food to
displaced people at camps in Monrovia's northern suburbs because of
lawlessness in the city. "Hundreds of thousands of displaced people in
[these] camps, will soon face starvation if a peaceful solution [to the
Liberian conflict] is not reached immediately," it warned.
OCHA said in a situation report on Tuesday: "Many of Monrovia's
internally displaced persons continue to suffer from shortages of food,
clean water and basic health services. Abuses against civilian populations,
especially those living on Monrovia's outskirts, are reported regularly."
It added: "Systematic looting of houses at Brewerville continues as
evidenced by a flood of new merchandise on sale at Duala Market, which
includes items like bath-tubs and toilets seats." Brewerville is a
northwestern suburb that saw heavy fighting during two rebel assaults on
Monrovia last month.
There has been no fighting in Monrovia since rebel forces abandoned
their latest attempt to capture the city two weeks ago. However, the
violence against civilians by gunmen fighting for President Charles Taylor
has continued, despite repeated government warnings that looters would be
dealt with harshly.
OCHA said the UN World Food Programme, non-governmental organizations
and Liberian officials, had begun to distribute food to more than 180,000
desperate people in Monrovia.
"But food insecurity among many internally displaced persons continues
to be critical. Because of a lack of security, the displaced fear to venture
into the villages to look for cassava, bush yams or any other food," OCHA
said.
Clean water was being delivered to only 35 camps. At some private
companies were charging displaced persons 10 Liberian Dollars (US 13 cents)
for five gallons of water.
"Continuous displacements and looting of relief items have made it
difficult for the humanitarian community to scale up their
support. Comprehensive assessment and disease surveillance has not been
undertaken to determine the extent of morbidity and mortality as well as
violence and other abuses," it said.
Although attention has focused on the humanitarian crisis in Monrovia,
there is little information about the situation in the interior, much of it
occupied by rebel forces, where a further two million people live. OOCHA
noted that because of the civil war had lost access to over 70 percent of
Liberia's population.

Original_Search_Title: Afenifere Never Supported Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: Afenifere Never Supported Obasanjo
Body_Text:
Lagos —  The leader of Afenifere, Chief Abraham Adesanya, was cornered at the
socio-political group's headquarters in Lagos by a crop of journalists in
the heat of the fuel price hike crisis.
Excerpts:
Today marks the beginning of an indefinite strike action called by
the labour movement in the country, as a result of the hike in the prices of
petroleum products. What is your view about the present state of things?
The PDP government spent a lot of money during the last election to
solicit for people's support. They used money to buy electorate's votes. The
news that came to me from all over the country was that in many places, they
were distributing money, even when the voters were on the queue, waiting to
exercise their franchise .
How can that happen and you would expect the government not to go
into debt?
How do they get this money back, except you ask the people to pay
more. You use one glorious name to ask people to pay more, like increasing
petrol price. The proceeds will obviously replenish government purse, the
amount they have indiscriminately spent. The pity of it is that, it is not
only people who got this money from them that are now paying back. You and I
will now be paying back.
If you choose the wrong person and he is treating you in a way that he
knows, you deserve the government you have chosen for yourself.
From the situation of things at the moment, it is believed that
people are ready to continue to stay at home if only to demonstrate that
they object to the increases. How do you assess the reaction of people so
far?
Well, the reaction of the people is what you yourself has been
seeing. It is something you can point to, it is something you can hear. But
from the newspaper reports I have read, the PDP said they are going on. They
are the people who got us into this debt. So, they say we must go on with
what it takes to make us run into this debt, which you too must pay. And how
do they do that? Increase in fuel price. Even if you don't have a car, when
the price is increased, you are also to pay. You will pay. I will also
pay. The market woman will pay, the farmer will pay. That is the evil of
voting for the wrong people, the wrong party.
Well, so far, Mr. President has said the decision is irreversible.
What is your stand on this?
How can somebody say the action he takes on behalf of his principal is
irreversible? Isn't that a case of the tail wagging the dog? The agent
saying that well, you my principal , you are talking rubbish.
But sir, Afenifere, which you head, supported Chief Obasanjo in the
last presidential election. Why this sudden turn-around to now label him as
anti-masses?
What has that got to do with the petrol issue? The point you must note
is that Afenifere never took any official stand. You wouldn't see it in the
handout of Afenifere that, this is what they are going to do about
Obasanjo.
It is a question of guess work. And if that were so, then we must reap
the benefit of what we sowed.
The UAD (United Action for Democracy), has said that civil
disobedience will be a continuous exercise until this present government
falls. Do you support this view?
Well, I wouldn't know. But Yoruba have a saying that "Ohun ti eniyan o
ba ni je, enu e lo maa fi ko" (whatever somebody will not eat, he will
reject with his mouth). If they feel that what they are getting, they don't
deserve it, it is their duty to make a change.  But what you sowed you will
reap.
That is the immutable law of karma. If you plant orange, you don't get
to your farm with a view to harvesting mango. Or you plant cassava, you go
back there with a feeling that you will harvest yam.  You are wasting your
time.
The argument has been propounded that owing to the virtual absence
of a viable opposition, this has given Mr. President more than enough
latitude to do whatever he desires without being checked. Do you share this
opinion?
I wouldn't know. But what I know is that those people, either
representing us in the National Assembly, at the states assemblies, the
local governments, they are deemed to be the servants of the people, the
agents of the people. The people are the principal, those they put there are
their agents. Can a river rise higher than the sun? When your agents tell
you that he is higher than you are, then there is a coup. Your agent is
saying that well, what I say must be carried out, no matter what you
think.
When a servant is telling his master such a thing, where do we then go
from there.
In those good old days, when the civil servants or the government wrote
a letter to you or me, do you know how they usually ended it?  "Your
obedient servant". But nowadays, they are not our obedient servants, they
are your masters. If it is not so, how can people do something , take for
example, this fuel price increase and you seek the opinion of the high and
the low, including the medium, and they say this thing is not right, and you
say no, I will force it down your throat. May the good Lord deliver us.
As a leader, what will be your reaction to the move being made by
the PDP-controlled Federal Government to restructure the local government?
The PDP wants to be the master of all. How can any government come
around to say it is appointing some people to go and do a council reform or
whatever they call it? They are the people to appoint those who will do it,
and when they sit and do it and realise that it is not right for you, like
this increase in fuel price, they say that is what they want to do whether
it is right or wrong. We are all powerless.
We have said, let the people come together and decide on what to do.
They are now saying they are going to appoint some people to sit over the
sensitive issue, and come back with their report. Is that the democracy for
which many of us paid with our own blood?
When we are asking for the national conference, you remember the time
we've been asking for it, some obas, traditional rulers came together to say
they must do something about this matter. Representatives went to Abuja to
talk over this matter. There are six zones in the country, four of these
zones agreed that we should have national conference. The remaining two
didn't say there shouldn't be, but they lied and said well, we didn't know
this is what we were coming to discuss. So, we still have to go back to our
people, and the other zones took them on. For example, they knew of that
conference before we in the Afenifere knew about it. And these are the same
people who said, let us go back to tell our people. In spite of that, four
zones agreed that we should have national conference. Two said no or half
no. And these two won the battle. Even our president never liked it.  They
never took any step to carry it out. And do you people still feel proud that
we are practising democracy in this country?
In the light of all these sir, people out there are calling for the
resignation of Mr. President from office. What is your stand on this?
Which people are you talking about?
The fuel price hike protesters?
Oh well, that is their opinion.
Alright then, what is your own opinion sir?
My opinion?
Yes sir?
Let me think about it. I don't talk before I think. I think before I
talk. I never thought of resignation before I came here, but now that you
are telling me that some people are saying so, what is my own opinion. Isn't
that what you are asking?
Those who are saying resign, must have thought over it for some time.
They must have slept over it. You haven't allowed me a minute to think over
it and you are asking for my opinion.
Would you say you are disappointed in the government of Chief
Olusegun Obasanjo as a person?
As a person, I don't like this petrol hike. I don't like him saying that
well, the will of the people should not prevail. I don't like it at
all.
But so far, you don't think he is taking the right step?
If I think he is taking the right step, I wouldn't have said the hike in
the prices of petroleum products should be reversed.
Recently, Chief Obasanjo made public, his decision to make one of
your own, Chief Cornelius Adebayo, a minister in his new cabinet.  What is
your reaction to this?
Well, if Obasanjo is of the view that he wants to take some opinion,
that certain opinion should be represented in his government, who am I to
say he shouldn't. To get these people there is one thing, to consider what
they say there, is another. So, let us wait and see.
The AD has said Chief Adebayo is on his own if he takes the
appointment.
So, what is the position of Afenifere on the issue?
What I understand is that Chief Adebayo was taken to represent the
North-Central. Well, I don't think I will be wrong if they think the
North-Central from where he comes, should be represented and they take
him.
One thing I must make you all understand is that I have implicit
confidence in C. O. Adebayo, absolute confidence. I know Adebayo is not
somebody who picks up appointment just because of money.

Original_Search_Title: Mile 12 Market: Its Health, Traffic Menace
Headline: Nigeria: Mile 12 Market: Its Health, Traffic Menace
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Mile 12 market along Ikorodu-Ketu road, Lagos State is known for its
cheap fresh fruites, vegetables and other food items. But many are not aware
of the deplorable environment and the danger it poses to free flow of
traffic, writes Agha Ibiam
If one takes a trip around Nigeria to take a census of markets,
especially food stuffs, Lagos would probably rank the highest despite the
fact that 80 per cent of the food consumed are produced outside the
state.
The population explosion in the state, which became more noticeable in
the early 1980's, has also aggravated the demand for the explosion of the
markets. But the most disturbing about having these pockets of markets is
the conversion of the roads as markets. Reasons adduced by the traders are
not far-fetched. They range from the high cost of renting shops and payment
other sundry bills from the local government authorities. Other reasons
include high level of poverty, lack of job opportunities and the tendency to
care for the ever-increasing extended families.
The hazards the conversion of roadsides to markets constitutes to
motorists can not be over-emphasised. Most of the traffic jams experienced
by motorists on major Lagos roads could be traced the dangers of these
roadside markets as vehicles no longer use normal lanes to discharge and
pick passengers.
The Mile 12 International Market, located at the out-skirt of Lagos is a
case in point. It is the major market that supplies the Lagos residents and
its environs consumable goods.
Established in 1975, the market was established to argument the
existence and reduce the traffic jam along the Iddo Market.
The scanty population then, made it unnoticeable until 1983, during
President Shehu Shagari's tenure when it became fully established. Before
this period, the Mile 12 market was said to be situated at Maidan market
named after the village. Stretching from Iyana School Bustop, extending to
the nooks and crannies of Kosofe and to Mile 12 under-bridge.
The foodstuffs come mostly from the northern, eastern and middle belt,
(Benue) parts of the country. Yam for instance is brought from Abuja, Zaria,
Ogbomosho, Abakaliki and Igbura in Benue State. While other perishable goods
like tomatoes and pepper are brought in from Jos, Plateau State; Abeokuta,
Ogun State; Ede in Osun State and Ajase in Benin Republic. Palm oil comes
mainly from Ekiti-Pupa, Ekiti State and Umuahia, Abia State.
The problems of Mile 12 Market are of two folds. One is the problem it
constitutes to the environment. The second reason is the traffic jams the
market is causing as a result of its location. THISDAY checks revealed that
most traders prefer using the service lane and the first few lock-up shops,
located just opposite the pedestrian bridge to sell their goods. Even the
bridge is now a market on its own as pedestrian scarcely makes use of
it.
Apart from two or three lock-up shops opposite the bridge, the rest are
not adequately used. THISDAY reliably gathered that miscreants perpetrate
virtually all manners of evils there, especially at nights. Most of the
shops built by various individuals during the time of Col. Olagunsoye
Onyinola (rtd) as governor of Lagos State are dilapidated due to
abandonment. Most of the shops are uncompleted and poorly planned.
Interestingly, some of the abandoned shops have been turned into
residence. They have also become a hideout for miscreants, used as joints
for smoking Indian Hemp, abode for criminals. Others are also used as cinema
houses and brothels. It was reliably gathered that the markets are sometimes
used as petrol depots, especially during scarcity of petroleum product
Cocaine business equally thrive at the abandoned shops.
Mile 12 market can be a gory site. Even pigs known for dirty places will
detest being nurtured at the Mile 12 Market environment. It therefore
borders health officials how consumable foods items like fish, meat
tomatoes, vegetables, onions, pepper amongst others are displayed and sold
openly there. During and after the rain season, the market remains a no-go
area. The epidemic the entire market is causing to its environs, according
to some residents cannot be explained.
For many, the entire market is an eye sore. THISDAY could not hold his
breath while touring the market as repugnant odour oozed out from different
directions. The swampy, muddy ground is about two to three feet deep in some
parts of the market. Observers have therefore wondered how traders manage to
carry out their commercial activities in such a place. It is therefore a
strange sight seeing people wearing rain boots. It also creates economic
activities as young girls and boys wait to wash people's feet for a token of
about N5 to N10.
THISDAY asked one of the traders why the market is in such a messy
situation. He quickly said that the Lagos State Government and the council
should be held responsible. Government, he said makes a lot of money from
the traders and the trailer drivers through all sundry taxes and levies
without develop the market. It has appeared as if most of the traders are
accustomed to the dirty environment. The bad terrain is also a problem to
trailer drivers. The trucks do not have good and spacious packing spaces,
thereby using service lanes, which compound traffic problems.
According to a medical doctor who operates within the axis of the
market, who spoke to THISDAY on condition of anonymity, said most health
hazards exist as a result of its closeness to residential quarters. Some the
health hazards include road accident, cholera, malaria, typhoid fever and
other water borne diseases.
He noted that road accident is frequent at Mile 12 Market. And when a
case is beyond their treatment, it is referred to Orthopedic
Hospital. Cholera, the said is also very rampant due to wastes dumped
indiscriminately. The decomposed wastes emit odour that causes stomach
disorder and other undiagnosed ailments.
"Most of the people when admitted, do not even know what their problems
are. You have to subject them to serious interrogation and test before
getting a clue. But as medical practitioners, we understand that the
environment contributes to most sicknesses they suffer," the doctor
said.
About the traffic jam, the Iyaloja of Mile 12 Market, Alhaja Iyabo Ahmed
argued that it is not the fault of the traders who use the service lane, but
blamed it on commercial buses that stop incessantly to drop and pick
passengers.
She said the local government officials apparently because of the petty
levies they are charged permit the traders that sell at the roadside. This
she said has equally encouraged arrogance on the part of the authority's
officials. "Both my team, the Hausa and the Igbo Yam Sellers Association,
have in the past attempted to solve the problems of the traffic jam. But the
more we try to proffer solutions, the more unrecognised we become to the
council. They prefer levying the people who sell along the service lane, toe
trailers that are packed indiscriminately and in the process make huge
amount of money for their selfish interest", she said.
The man-hour and loss of lives recorded along the Ikorodu--Ketu road
cannot be quantified. There are some instances the traffic jam stretches
from Owode-Onirin spare parts market. At times, motorists spend about two to
three hours between that short distance. This is mostly witnessed when
trailers bring food items from various states.
THISDAY had observed a situation where a young sick girl of about 14
years almost lost her life in an attempt to conveying her to a hospital in
Lagos. Perhaps her consolation was the consistent words of sorry she
received from her mother and co-passengers. There have been other worst
cases.
Weekends are considered as worst days on that road. There are some
Sundays when ordinarily the road suppose to be free, but to the chagrin of
the road users there will be unprecedented traffic jam. The situation is
made worst when the military men, LASTMA officials and the traffic warders
who supposed to control traffic, concentrate only in extorting money from
bus conductors. The uniform men often use miscreants to make this brisk
business.
A senior police officer in Ketu division who again appealed for
anonymity explained that the traffic jam at the market is as a result of the
inaccessibility of the service lane to motorist, especially the commercial
buses.
This he said has compelled traders and trailers to take over the lane to
sell and discharge their goods. But of note he added, is that most drivers
are impatient, pointing out that if the service lane had been made
motorable, their effort of controlling traffic, especially the commercial
drivers would have been more easier.
The officer did not however absolve his men of extortion. "Our effort to
rid the police of bad eggs is our obligation. We are aware the N20.00 they
collect from motorists, we will do something in that regard", he
stressed.
The other problem of Mile 12 Market, the Iyaloja said is the acute lack
of water, light, toilet facilities, security and inaccessible road due to
the swampy nature of the place. Like other markets in Lagos, her expectation
is to see the market built like the Tejuosho or Iponri markets. Even as
traders pay the council ticket, ranging from N5.00 to N10.00 daily, while
shop owner's pay about N200.00 monthly, the Iyaloja said there was nothing
to show for it.
She rather argued that government could decongest the market by making
another place available to them.
An official of the Nigerian Market Women and Men Association of the
market however proffered a solution to the traffic hold-up. He suggested
that the trailers could be made to pass through Obanta road, linking Maidan
to Dada Street. Another route is from Dada Street to Adebinpe Street, which
takes them off to Kosofe-Iyana school and off to Ketu, enroute to the
express.
The Lagos State Government recently inaugurated mobile courts within the
metropolis to prosecute traffic offenders. The Federal Road Safety Corps
(FRSC), the report said would concentrate on federal roads, while the ones
raised by the government would concentrate on state within the
metropolis.
But it appears that the more laws are made, the more motorists disobey
it. They argue that the state should brace up to that challenge as more
lives and man-hour is lost on Lagos roads.

Original_Search_Title: Petrol Costs N400 Per Litre in Port Harcourt
Headline: Nigeria: Petrol Costs N400 Per Litre in Port Harcourt
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — The on-going nationwide industrial action called by the Nigeria Labour
Congress (NLC), yesterday in its eighth day crippled economic and social
activities in Port Harcourt, Rivers State capital as state government and
private offices remained shut.
Only few vehicles plied the roads because of scarcity of fuel.
Since workers of all petroleum stations in the city joined the strike
action, premium motor spirit (PMS) popularly known as petrol and other
petroleum products became very difficult to come. Investigations by THISDAY
showed that the commodities could be found with black market operators.
As a result, a litre of petrol yesterday sold for N400 in the black
market.
Prices diesel and kerosene also went up by as much margin a situation
which forced many motorists to pack their vehicles.
People who depended on electricity generating sets for power supply to
their houses or private businesses were forced shout of operation as a
result of fuel scarcity.
The acute scarcity of petroleum products, apart from forcing a few
commercial vehicle operators to double their fares also pushed up prices of
food stuff like garri, rice and yam beyond the reach of many people.
A tuber of yam that used to cost between N100 and N150 now sells for
between N200 and N350 while a cup of rice that use to cost N100 now sells
for N200.

Original_Search_Title: Millions Lost in Mbale Storms
Headline: Uganda: Millions Lost in Mbale Storms
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE heavy rains ravaging most parts of Mbale and Sironko districts have
destroyed crops worth millions of shillings, leaving farmers stuck.
The Mbale LC5 secretary for production, Michael Namundi, said on
Saturday that the most affected area was Buwabwala sub-county, where
landslides also killed four people.
He said domestic animals, including goats, pigs and cows were also
killed.
"There is an eminent danger of famine in the district unless solutions
are put in place as soon as possible," Namundi said.
He said bananas were the main food crops in the region but storms had
left most banana plantations destroyed.
In Sironko district, the most affected areas were Zasui, Bumasifa,
Butandiga sub-counties.
Namundi said it is high time people started planting wind resistant
crops such as cassava and yams to avoid being victims of famine bananas are
destroyed by storms.
The district environment Officer, Paul Mwambu, said there was a high
rate of tree cutting, a practice that has left crops and the soils exposed
to wind, and landslides.
He advised residents to plant trees and urged the local leaders to lead
the campaign.

Original_Search_Title: Mistaken MP Ken Lukyamuzi Flees Own Rally
Headline: Uganda: Mistaken MP Ken Lukyamuzi Flees Own Rally
Body_Text:
Kampala — RUBAGA South MP John Ken Lukyamuzi on Sunday fled from a rally he had
called at Nateete Police grounds in Rubaga Division Kampala, when he saw a
team of Military Police pass by, apparently on a different mission.
Lukyamuzi, clad in a cream suit, abandoned other MPs and Justice Forum
leader Kibirige Mayanja and fled with part of the crowd and hid in a nearby
yam garden. He suspected the Military Police had come to arrest him.
The Military policemen were chasing Joseph Lubega, a driver of taxi UAE
458T which was involved in an accident with an army vehicle on Masaka
road.
They ironically caught up with him a few metres from the public
rally. The embarrassed Lukyamuzi re-appeared after 30 minutes, with his
cream suit heavily caked in mud. He said experience had taught him to become
wary of security at such gatherings and would not take chances.
"As a leader, I have to lead even in running for dear life. But today, I
was going to mobilise security for my guests," a jocular Lukyamuzi told the
crowd that was slowly regrouping after the fiasco.
Present was Mbarara Municipality MP Winnie Byanyima, who told Ugandans
not to be coerced into endorsing the third term.
"I am back to work with other Ugandans to block a constitutional change
that will give Museveni another term," she said amid cheers.
Aswa county MP Reagan Okumu, Jacob Oulanyah (Omoro County) Ssebuliba
Mutumba (Kawempe South) and Nsubuga Nsambu (Makindye west) also
attended.
Lukyamuzi told the gathering that he was going to spearhead a motion
demanding President Museveni to present a comprehensive programme on how he
would effect peace talks with LRA leader Joseph Kony.

Original_Search_Title: As Fertiliser Scarcity Deepens Hope Dashes for Kuje Farmers
Headline: Nigeria: As Fertiliser Scarcity Deepens Hope Dashes for Kuje Farmers
Body_Text:
As the present farming season continues to appear bleak following
absence of political will by government to combat the prevailing fertilizer
scarcity to avert the grave threat to food security in the country, farmers
in Kuje Area Council (KAC), are banking their hopes only on the Area
Council.
Like in all area councils of the territory, agriculture is the main
occupation of the over 250,000 natives of Kuje, even as the current
statistical survey of the FCT agriculture extension service division of the
department of agriculture show that communities in the council provide over
30% of the food grown in the FCT.
The council's capacity in agriculture produce has qualified it as the
food basket of the territory, according to inquires which farmers in the
area attributed to support from the council.
According to them, the council has over the years promoted the
development of agriculture through regular provision of imputs like
fertilisers, improved seedling, herbicides, pesticides and tractors at
subsidised rates in addition to extension services as well as gari pressing
plants, rice and groundnut milling industries.
But as the period for fertiliser application is gradually elapsing
without the commodity in sight, Kuje farmers have turned their attention on
other alternative arrangements, banking their hopes solely on the council's
efforts.
One Zafania, a farmer in Rubochi, one of the popular towns in Kuje told
our reporter that even sale of the commodity through open market which was
always available at out-throat rate is not easy to come by this year.
Another, one Malam Bako Abdul who owns several hectares of yam in
Dafara, a small farer settlement farm said in the past years, getting
fertilizer was nothing difficult since the council management always procure
and sell out the commodity at subsidise rate, but expressed predicament that
almost one full month into the fertilizer application period, farmers are
still stranded as neither open market dealers nor the area council has said
nothing relating to the issue.
Like his counterpart in Rubochi, Malam Abdul expressed helplessness,
saying that he has confidence that the council management will still provide
the broad shoulders on which they have always leaned on during tilling,
planting and harvesting seasons, saying in Hausa that "our chairman must not
forget us, we have always praised his gesture towards promoting the
development of agriculture at the grassroots."
We got as June/July, the period for application of fertiliser on yam
which is already shooting roots long beyond responding to chemicals. We also
got that soyabeans and rice for which fertilizer applications come during
the month of August have already started shooting out leaves for those who
planted earlier.
The chairman, Alhaji Jibrin Wowo, in a short telephone interview told
our reporter that its administration has not forsaken farmers this farming
season, saying that providing boost to farmers has always been his priority
to since his people and predominantly farmers.
He however, expressed the same predicament that like all other councils,
Kuje Area Council has not seen any supply, assuring that all hands are on
deck to supply of the commodity and to reach the people through their
council wards.
Alhaji Wowo also said the council's tractors hiring scheme in very much
effective for farmers to take advantage at mass productivity.

Original_Search_Title: North West : Education Boss Salutes Bilingualism In St. Bright School
Headline: Cameroon: North West : Education Boss Salutes Bilingualism In St. Bright School
Body_Text:
"St Bright Bilingual Nursery and Primary School in the Up Station neighbourhood of Bamenda is the only true or credible bilingual institution in the North West with kids who study all the subjects both in French and English". The observation was made by Amina Tumenta, Inspector of primary and nursery education, in appreciation of the impact the institution is creating within the education family. The event was the graduation of pupils from St Bright Bilingual Nursery and Primary School, when the inspector revealed that in appreciation of merit, the school recently lifted awards for good performances as they emerged with the third over all best FSLC results in the province at the end of course examination last year.
Parents and observers who filed out for the graduation were impressed that beyond academics, St Bright school operates a school farm featuring flowers, a vegetable garden and a crop rotation area where yams, beans and corn are grown. Also present is a bee farming section with some hives. The assistant Head teacher, Nkeng Grace told participants at the event that the institution is a model in values and rise above cultural, religious, political and tribal barriers. She hailed well wishers and staff who make St Bright emerge as a model in North West schools and revealed that with 150 pupils on roll for a 15 man staff strength; the school graduated nine grade six pupils and 16 from nursery two to primary one. The school also take credit with a 100 percent success score in the Common entrance for its pioneer batch with all the pupils passing in list A. Meanwhile, the proprietor, Mrs Agartha Forsah sounds off that the sky is the limit for the institution in the years ahead.

Original_Search_Title: Development is Being Destroyed By Subsidies, Mali President Tells Congressional Committee
Headline: Mali: Development is Being Destroyed By Subsidies, Mali President Tells Congressional Committee
Body_Text:
Washington, DC — U.S. and European subsidies and tariffs "support injustice," Mali
President Amadou Toumani Toure told the House International Relations
Subcommitteee on Africa, Tuesday, summarizing written testimony that he
presented for the record.
Toure said he was representing all African nations and the devastating
effect of subsidies on Malian cotton illustrates the harm that agriculural
subsidies - now totaling more that US$300bn in the United States and Europe
- are causing to agriculture across the continent. "We have decided to pull
the alarm bell."
Toure is the first Afican President or head of state to testify before
the subcommittee. "We needed to bring some weight to this," said one member
of the president's party. Agricultural issues, a key element in the
political structure of a small group of powerful legislators, are not
usually taken up by the Africa subcommittee.
But there is a crisis, said Toure. Agriculture subsidies that keep
prices artificially low contribute significantly to the continued economic
deterioration in Mali and other cotton-producers in Africa. There have been
"serious consequences on our economies," he told the lawmakers in his
written testimony. "Mali lost 1.7 percent of her GDP and 8 percent of her
export receipts; Burkina Faso lost 1 percent of her GDP and 12 percent of
her export receipts; Benin lost 1.4 percent of her GDP and 9 percent of her
export receipts.
Follow us on WhatsApp | LinkedIn for the latest headlines
And low prices for agricultural products lead to rural depopulation
which, in turn, leads to urban unrest and "breeding grounds for terrorism."
African cotton producers have a competitive advantage but "international
trade rules, as defined by the World Trade Organization, are biased" by the
substantial subsidies granted to European, American and Chinese cotton
producers. - in 2001 estimated to be, US$700m for Europe, US$2.3bn for the
USA and US$1.2bn for China.
Also testifying Tuesday was the Secretary-General for the Common Market
for Eastern and Southern Africa (Comesa), Erastus J.O. Mwencha, who told the
subcommitteee that "obstacles" to the export of fresh fruit to the United
States were "considerable."
Last year, said Mwencha, the U.S. Department of Agriculture approved
just 45 items of fresh products from all 54 nations of Africa. In the Comesa
region, said Mwencha, "only four products had been approved and these are
onions and pineapples for Kenya, Snow peas for Zambia and yams for
Madagascar."
Opening the hearing, subcommittee chairman, Ed Royce (R-California)
noted that "by one estimate, the elimination of subsidies and other
protections in developed countries would allow African countries to triple
their net agricultural trade."

Original_Search_Title: Cross River Boosts Tourism Development
Headline: Nigeria: Cross River Boosts Tourism Development
Body_Text:
Calabar — That the Cross River State government is currently making tourism
development a priority projects no longer news. What is actually spectacular
is the height it has reached in ensuring that the dream of re-inventing
tourism in the state is achieved to the fullest.
Daily Trust gathered that in the course of re-inventing tourism in the
state emphasis will be focused on the implementation of an Integrated
Tourism master Plan "that will give the state a unique tourism flavour
referring to what for now is fondly referred to as the "Sacred city of
Norgarah." "The vision of the sacred city of Norgarah is conceptualised and
facilitated by KPMG Professional Tourism Developers, and is aimed primarily
at establishing Calabar as a trade and distribution hub in West Africa,
while at the same time providing a unique tourism experience that will start
to inform the growth of the tourism sector in Nigeria," reports Clement
Umina, a photojournalist for environment and development, eco-tourism
developers with the state government.
Mofinews, a local tabloid published by the state Ministry of Finance
once disclosed that the city will play a complementary role to the Calabar
Free Trade Zone, as well as divert traffic from notable world commercial
centres to Dubai, Bankgok etc following the arrangement.
Unima also mentioned specifically that the state secured the support of
the federal government by way of a guarantee to rise through a bond, N4.6
billion from the Nigerian Capital market to further upgrade the Ranch Resort
at Obudu and other noticeable tourist sites including implementation of the
masterplan.
Meanwhile, the decision to locate the project in Cross River according
to an official statement in Calabar, is obvious from the standpoint of its
strategic location in the West African sub-region, where exists a rich
attraction of natural, cultural and historical resources, unique attractions
of unexplored pristine environment-clean state and a pro-investment
government.
Daily Trust learnt also that the project implementation is to be carried
out in four phases and will involve two sites requiring a vast total land
area including reclaimed swamp.
"Phase one of the project design will see the putting in place of a
warehouse, entertainment strip, accommodation, leisure activities, cultural
and agricultural tourism.
"Phase two will involve accommodation/conferencing, leisure activities,
cultural and agricultural tourism and accommodation. Phase three will dwell
more on exclusive accommodation, eco-tourism and agricultural tourism, while
four will centre on eco-tourism and demand build up" said an official
statement in the tourism department.
Accordingly, the project master plan as best understood, will achieve
the status of the New Partnership for African Development (NEPAD) in its
short term projection, while the long term plan will design a link with
other tourism infrastructure, and educate local population as well as
develop resorts.
On how the proposed master planning implementation will achieve
dividends for the local populace and from sack huge investments, vis-à-vis
the global competitive trend in tourism, Clement Umina, an environmentalist
emphasised that the state has a distractive excellent cultural and
environmental feature and or treasures, "that make it different from
others."
According to Umina "this may be a cultural activity like the New Yam
Festival celebration, the boat regatta, a place like the relics of
Othu. Amsu in Abi, or the Obudu Ranch which has been the number one selling
point of the state and the nation as a whole."
Umina also borrowed from a World Bank Urban Programme Team leader for
Europe and Central Asia Region, stating that "what makes a particular place
special and possible to mark as a tourist destination, is not necessarily a
fanciful location that has abundant artificial or natural beauty with very
upscale accommodation, but a place of scenic beauty, for what it is, a place
with no artificial adornments."
Indeed he concluded that Eco-tourism will be the best option on tourism
for Cross River, going by the scenic beauty which the cultural and
environmental treasure of the state endows.
As he puts it "Eco-Tourism appreciates the potentials for balanced
activities. It pertness the well trained international tour operator, the
weather tourist and the local people who have spent all their lives living
in these choice locations. It increases attention for environmental
concern.
"Moreover aggregate data shows that more tourists now prefer the
tranquil, peaceful homestead of the rural village than the bustle of the big
cities," even as he countered that foreign exchange is bound to buttress,
the economic potentials of the people in this regard.
Meanwhile, the state government has in the past four years prioritised
the development of tourism in the state, such that it currently embarks on
campaigns at conserving the natural habitat in keeping with the
implementation of the master plan.

Original_Search_Title: South West : Farmers Exhibit Know-How at Agric Show
Headline: Cameroon: South West : Farmers Exhibit Know-How at Agric Show
Body_Text:
Mamfe (Manyu) The Manyu Divisional Chief Town, Mamfe, was in a two day festive mode recently as food crops and life-stock farmers throughout the South West Province converged to exhibit their best products at what was termed a "mini-agricultural show with a provincial touch".
The Mamfe show, organised by the South West Provincial Delegation of Agriculture was the third after Tombel in Kupe Manugouba and Menji in Lebialem Divisions.
The best species in food crops and fruits like cocoyams, plantains, yams, cassava, potatoes, oranges etc and livestock like goats, fowls, fish and pigs were the biggest pullers of the anxious crowds. Almost everything exposed at the Mamfe show especially the oranges, garri and eru were all bought even before the close of the first day, except the barely eleven months old pig which probably, because of its extraordinary 400 kg weight chased away many a prospective buyer. But this pig did not see the end of the show as it was slaughtered and displayed in pieces for easier purchase at the Mamfe central market.
Opening the show, the First Assistant S.D.O for Mamfe, Mr. Nwafua Lawrence Forwang said it was to limit the headaches of inadequate mechanisation, lack of capital, preservation facilities and low quality production that government, through various action plans was training and employing technicians to assist farmers, encourage them through best farm competitions and agro-pastoral shows.
On his part, the chief organiser of the show, South West Provincial Delegate of Agriculture, Mr Mboge Mboge George, saw the objective of the show as one aimed at bringing the farmers closer in order to learn from each other. Announcing a CFA 250,000 government support to young farmers and a farmers' grant of CFA 4 million, Mr Mboge said much more was on the way in form of inputs under the HIPC programme.
The Provincial Delegation of Livestock, Animal Breeding, Fisheries and Industries, co-organisers of the show, was represented at the occasion by Mr Njoume Abrie Njumbe, Animal Industries Engineer. The mini agro-pastoral shows, which have a provincial touch, come up twice in the year in two different divisional headquarters.
The second and last for this year has been slated for Mundemba in Ndian Division in November.

Original_Search_Title: Agricultural Expert Warns Government
Headline: Ghana: Agricultural Expert Warns Government
Body_Text:
Ghana will continue to stay HIPC if it continues to rely on the
production of roots and tubers such as cassava and yam alone.
There is therefore the need for new agricultural policies that will
encourage more people to invest in the production of fruits, seeds and
vegetables.
The general manager (GM) in charge of Agric Division of Dizengoff Ghana
Limited, Mr. Aviram Tal, made this observation in an interview with
Chronicle in Kumasi. According to him, Ghana could export new fruits and
vegetables to earn more income if proper attention and focus is shifted to
its production.
Asked about his views on Agriculture in Ghana, he observed that agric in
the country today, is an improvement over the previous years.
He commended the government for his bold initiative on the mass cocoa
spraying exercise and described it as an excellent and effective decision
any government could take.
Aviram Tal however pointed out that lack of knowledge and access to good
source of information on the part of the farmers are some of the problems
associated with agriculture in Ghana.
"Ghana is suffering from a lot of fake agricultural materials smuggled
from Cote d'Ivoire," he revealed.
The GM explained that most of the chemicals that are smuggled into the
country from neighbouring countries, particularly Cote d'Ivoire, do not
contain the right proportion, rendering them ineffective for agricultural
activities.
These smugglers he said, tend to cheat the farmers and impede the
government's agricultural sector developmental policies, leading to a
perennial failure in achieving agricultural target by the state.
He described as unfortunate the fact that despite his numerous notices
served to the appropriate quarters; the necessary intervention has not been
made.
Aviram Tal stressed the need for farmers to be educated on agricultural
materials in order to identify the right ones to use to ensure effective
farming.
According to him, it is the objective of the Dizengoff Ghana Limited to
standardize agricultural activities in the country to maximize the
production of food and cash crops, adding, it is also their aspiration to
look for specific inputs to solve specific problems in the agric
sector.
The group therefore aims at providing enough fungicides to help curb the
numerous cases of fugi diseases which is the biggest problem confronting
Agriculture in West Africa.
As a means of ensuring effectiveness in agriculture, Dizengoff Ghana
Limited has been organizing a series of workshops for farmers throughout the
country to educate them on better product protection and uses.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Goods, Services Up in Adamawa
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Goods, Services Up in Adamawa
Body_Text:
Yola — PRICES of goods and services have jumped up by about 200 per cent in
Adamawa State few days after the Federal Government announced a hike in
price of petroleum products. Vanguard investigation in Yola, the state
capital and its environs indicated that transport fares on intra and inter
state routes have risen by between 100 per cent to 150 per cent depending on
the distance.
For instance, Yola to Jalingo which use to cost N250 has now risen to
N400 while Yola to Numan, a distance of about 54 kilometers which hitherto
cost N80 has now been increased to N150. Yola to neighbouring Gombe which
hitherto cost N300 has now been increased to N450 by motorists. Long
distance journeys were mostly effected by the hike. For instance, Yola to
Kano, Yola to Jos which use to be N1,500 and N1,100 have now attracted
N2,000 and N1,500 respectively. The worst hit are prices of food
items. Prior to the increase of petroleum products, foreign rice which was
being sold at N4,500 per 50kg bag has now jumped to between N5,000 and
above.
Prices of other staple foods, like garri, yam, cassava, beans and even
vegetables have also recorded sharp increases thereby making life more
difficult for the ordinary Nigerian. Speaking to Vanguard over the fuel
hike, a trader Mallam Hamisu Modibbo described the increase as
unrealistic. "By this increase, the PDP led federal Government has buried
Nigerians alive as no ordinary citizen can be assured of his or her future,"
he feared. Some civil servants who spoke to Vanguard on the issue regretted
that the new hike has further compounded the plight of the average Nigerian
worker as salaries and other take emoluments have been rendered
useless.

Original_Search_Title: Minister to Open Fruit Packing Plant in Western Cape
Headline: South Africa: Minister to Open Fruit Packing Plant in Western Cape
Body_Text:
Pretoria — Agriculture and Land Affairs Minister Thoko Didiza will this afternoon
open a fruit packing plant that is set to create job opportunities for the
communities of Swellendam in the Western Cape.
The project involves the production and packaging of Persimmon, a
deciduous fruit that is exported mainly to countries such as Japan, America
and Europe.
Persimmon is a sallow fruit almost similar to an apple thus often
referred to as the 'Apple of the Orient.'
Agriculture and land affairs spokesperson Nana Zenani said South Africa
would take advantage of a window opportunity as a seasonal counter-producer
and supplier of the fruit for international markets.
Producing and selling Persimmon was expected to generate approximately
R390 million in foreign exchange, said Ms Zenani.
She said the total crop produced all over the world was around 2.2
million tons with some of the top producers like Israel growing 30 000 tons,
Spain 3 000 tons and now South Africa entering the market.
'By the year 2010, South Africa will produce 30 000 tons,' and she added
a total of 160 hectares of land had been planted in the Western Cape by a
select group of farmers.
The previously disadvantaged communities have been allocated a 25
percent stake in land and the packaging-house as an empowerment initiative
to ensure inclusive involvement and ownership.
'The project will provide around 30 permanent workers with early entry
into the project with good return potential,' Ms Zenani said.
She said the Industrial Development Corporation (IDC) and Kay-Yam
Limited jointly funded the project.
It would see a greater development with the diversification of South
Africa's agricultural portfolio through the introduction of new varieties of
Persimmon, said Ms Zenani.
She said Persimmon farming would also see the completion of a value
chain of the fruit's production in the country, empowerment and co-ownership
in the project by the workers.
